quotes.quote: If one's bank does not employ an individual who understands the above paragraph, Anthropic will sell you artificial cognition for $20 a month.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie responds to a retraction request from Anchorage Digital CEO Nathan McCauley regarding McKenzie's earlier essay on debanking. McKenzie methodically dismantles each of McCauley's objections—regarding Silvergate's solvency, omission of regulatory guidance, and the definition of debanking—finding that none identify actual factual errors. The piece doubles as a meditation on the power dynamics between banks and writers, arguing that retraction demands from well-resourced financial institutions carry implicit legal threats that chill speech. McKenzie also highlights the irony that McCauley's own bank signed a consent order with the OCC for BSA/AML deficiencies while criticizing McKenzie's analysis of compliance failures at other crypto banks. The essay concludes by contextualizing Anchorage Digital as a trust company that cannot take deposits, and situating the retraction demand within the crypto industry's broader pattern of preferring to be judged on potential rather than track record.
quotes.key_insight: A retraction demand from a bank CEO that fails to identify a single factual error reveals more about the crypto industry's desire to suppress unfavorable analysis than about any deficiency in that analysis.

quotes.quote: Their equity is already vapor. They are leveraged 35:1 against their risk-on assets alone.
quotes.summary: McKenzie analyzes Tether's November 2022 attestation and argues that, even taking their own numbers at face value, Tether was again insolvent. With only $250 million in equity against $68 billion in liabilities and 12.86% of reserves in risk-on assets, a mere 2.86% impairment would wipe out their cushion — an impossibility to avoid given Bitcoin's 10%+ decline. He notes their accountants' careful disclaimers about extraordinary market conditions, their historical largest counterparty Alameda Research being vaporized, and the mystery of where $120 million/month in Treasury interest income disappears to. The piece frames Tether as the largest fraud in history, drawing explicit parallels to Madoff's unraveling.
quotes.key_insight: Tether's own published attestation, even if taken entirely at face value, mathematically demonstrates insolvency given any non-trivial market decline — their 0.36% equity cushion cannot survive the 12.86% of reserves held in risk-on assets.

quotes.quote: I described Tether as 'the internal accounting system for the largest fraud since Madoff.' This remains true.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that Tether's own quarterly reserves report from March 2022 proves it became undercollateralized during the May 2022 crypto selloff. With only $162 million in equity cushion and nearly $5 billion in volatile crypto and investment holdings, a mere 3.27% decline in those holdings would wipe out all equity — and Bitcoin alone fell 36% in that period. He demonstrates that even under the most generous assumptions and taking Tether's own numbers at face value, the math is inescapable. The essay is written explicitly for journalists and lawyers, with archived documentation and a cryptographic hash to prove the claims weren't edited after the fact.
quotes.key_insight: Tether's own published financial attestation, taken at face value with generous assumptions, mathematically proves its reserves became undercollateralized during the May 2022 crypto crash due to a reckless decision to back fixed liabilities with volatile assets and a wafer-thin equity cushion.

quotes.quote: A 3.27% decrease in the value of these investments wipes out all Tether equity and causes their tokens to be undercollateralized.

quotes.quote: This improves the margin on currency purchases from about 70% to about 85%, at a stroke, which is an incredible 20% lift for one engineering sprint.
quotes.summary: Apple's new policy allowing app developers to collect user contact information in-app and pitch payments out-of-app will have a much larger impact than most people expect. Game developers, who generate 60% of App Store revenue, are uniquely positioned to exploit this because they already have sophisticated systems for incentivizing user behavior through in-game rewards. Games like Genshin Impact can trivially offer small in-game rewards for providing email addresses, then redirect purchases to web storefronts at a ~10% discount, improving margins from 70% to 85%. McKenzie predicts with 90% confidence that major games will implement this within six weeks, and the ripple effects will extend to SaaS businesses and other platform ecosystems.
quotes.key_insight: Apple's seemingly incremental steering policy change will trigger massive payment behavior shifts because game developers already possess the sophisticated reward systems needed to redirect billions in transactions away from app stores.

Game developers, who generate 60% of App Store revenue, are uniquely positioned to exploit this because they already have sophisticated systems for incentivizing user behavior through in-game rewards. Games like Genshin Impact can trivially offer small in-game rewards for providing email addresses, then redirect purchases to web storefronts at a ~10% discount, improving margins from 70% to 85%. McKenzie predicts with 90% confidence that major games will implement this within six weeks, and the ripple effects will extend to SaaS businesses and other platform ecosystems.
quotes.key_insight: Apple's seemingly incremental steering policy change will trigger massive payment behavior shifts because game developers already possess the sophisticated reward systems needed to redirect billions in transactions away from app stores.

quotes.quote: The core insight of VaccinateCA on Day 1 was that, if doses were actually being injected, that the person actually doing the injecting both a) knew whether or not they had more doses and b) to whom they could administer them. Essentially all other data in the ecosystem were stale or lies.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes VaccinateCA, a non-profit he led that expanded into Vaccinate The States, building the country's largest public dataset on COVID-19 vaccine availability. The project started as a hackathon-style effort where volunteers called pharmacies directly to gather ground truth about vaccine stock, then evolved into a data broker between community efforts, government agencies, and large publishers like Google Maps. McKenzie argues that the government's software procurement processes were structurally misaligned with the speed the pandemic demanded, and that a scrappy volunteer effort filled a critical gap. He calls for continued urgency in vaccinating the remaining half of America and helping other nations.
quotes.key_insight: When government software procurement moves at bureaucratic speed during a crisis, scrappy volunteer efforts that prioritize ground truth over official data systems can fill critical gaps and save lives.

quotes.quote: It took America two days—two days—to develop a covid-19 vaccine. That is a triumph worth celebrating. Next time, we should match our world-leading scientific expertise with the relatively pedestrian web application the country needs.

quotes.quote: I pick a peer group wisely because you're giving them write access to both your conscious thoughts and your entire worldview.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on four years at Stripe, describing how his role evolved from hands-on Atlas work to cross-functional publishing and developer experience. He details the organizational challenges of hypergrowth, where the company grew from ~650 to ~3,000 employees while constantly reinventing its processes. He argues that Stripe's economic model is uniquely powerful because it compounds on the success of its customers, who themselves compound on their customers' success. The piece also candidly addresses his struggle with depression in 2020, the surprising value of corporate infrastructure he once dismissed, and how working at a prestigious company reshaped his network access in ways that feel meritocratically uncomfortable.
quotes.key_insight: Stripe's economic model is recursively compounding — it indexes on the success of customers who themselves index on the success of their customers — and this structural advantage, combined with relentless operating cadence, explains why insiders consistently underpredict the company's trajectory.

quotes.quote: There is a meritocracy in our class system and a class system in our meritocracy, and I feel that this is underexamined because the nuances discomfit both people who understand the nature of merit and those who understand the nature of class systems.

quotes.quote: It may not have been anyone's job to wake up every morning, assume Plan A was no longer viable, and search for proof that that had happened.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reveals that he organized an anonymous working group in Tokyo that, by March 25, 2020, had concluded Japan was experiencing a widespread coronavirus epidemic despite the public consensus that Japan was handling the situation well. The group pre-registered their findings via cryptographic hashes on Twitter, then circulated a white paper through economist Tyler Cowen and other channels to accelerate policy response. Their core prediction—that Japan would face a public health crisis in April comparable to peer nations—proved correct, with Japan eventually declaring a nationwide state of emergency. McKenzie reflects on the institutional failures that allowed the epidemic to go undetected, particularly how Japan's cluster-based testing strategy (Plan A) was structurally incapable of detecting its own failure due to insufficient testing of asymptomatic patients outside known clusters.
quotes.key_insight: Systems designed to monitor a specific strategy can be structurally incapable of detecting that strategy's failure, and institutional inertia means no one may be assigned the job of looking for evidence that the current plan has already collapsed.

quotes.quote: The definition of 'cryptographically secure' rounds to 'Even with hundreds of millions of dollars of computer time and the smartest mathematicians on the planet, it is impossible to produce two documents with the same hash, or to go from a hash to the document which produced it.'
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains the practice of 'dropping a hash' — publishing a cryptographic hash of a document to prove you possessed it at a specific time without revealing its contents. He walks through the properties of cryptographically secure hashing algorithms like SHA-512, explains why they are considered unbreakable by modern science, and shows how security researchers use this technique to claim credit for discovering vulnerabilities while allowing companies time to fix them. The post is explicitly written as a canonical reference for non-specialists, filling a gap he noticed in available explanations.
quotes.key_insight: Dropping a hash lets anyone prove they possessed a document at a specific time without revealing its contents, using the same mathematical guarantees that secure the global economy.

quotes.quote: It is not magic. It is not simply a shibboleth or ritual unique to the security community. It is a reproducible technology, available to anyone, that makes guarantees backed by the present limits of human understanding of the world we live in and the math which governs it.

quotes.quote: The governmental and media consensus is that Japan is weathering covid-19 well. This consensus is wrong.
quotes.summary: This white paper, primarily authored by Patrick McKenzie as part of a pseudonymous Tokyo working group, argues that Japan's official covid-19 case counts in March 2020 were massively understated, possibly by 5X or more. Using multiple lines of statistical evidence—Diamond Princess asymptomatic rates, Singapore airport detection data, and case fatality ratio modeling—the authors demonstrate that Japan's cluster containment strategy had already failed. The paper warns that absent aggressive intervention, Japan would face a national health crisis with over 500,000 infections by end of April 2020. It directly challenges the governmental and media consensus that Japan was weathering the pandemic well, calling voluntary behavioral changes insufficient.
quotes.key_insight: Multiple independent statistical analyses converge on the same conclusion: Japan's official covid-19 numbers were a gross undercount, its cluster containment strategy had already failed, and the country was weeks away from a public health crisis it hadn't yet acknowledged.

The paper warns that absent aggressive intervention, Japan would face a national health crisis with over 500,000 infections by end of April 2020. It directly challenges the governmental and media consensus that Japan was weathering the pandemic well, calling voluntary behavioral changes insufficient.
quotes.key_insight: Multiple independent statistical analyses converge on the same conclusion: Japan's official covid-19 numbers were a gross undercount, its cluster containment strategy had already failed, and the country was weeks away from a public health crisis it hadn't yet acknowledged.

quotes.quote: Tether is the internal accounting system for the largest fraud since Madoff.
quotes.summary: McKenzie presents a comprehensive exposé of Tether and Bitfinex as interconnected components of what he calls the largest fraud since Madoff. After Bitfinex lost banking access following a 2016 hack and Wells Fargo cutting ties, they intensified use of Tether as a pseudo-dollar system and routed funds through Crypto Capital Corp, a money launderer whose principal was skimming 10% of deposits. When CCC's accounts were frozen and funds embezzled, Tether became insolvent but survived through increasingly desperate measures including paying withdrawals with cryptocurrency, using employee friends' personal bank accounts as money mules, and raiding Bitfinex customer funds. The piece traces how each operational crisis necessitated a new layer of fraud, deepening the hole and creating systemic risk for the entire cryptocurrency ecosystem.
quotes.key_insight: Tether and Bitfinex form a self-reinforcing fraud where each crisis demands a deeper deception, creating systemic risk for the entire cryptocurrency ecosystem while the community collectively looks away because acknowledging the fraud would collapse the asset prices sustaining their businesses.

quotes.quote: Stocks, bonds, mutual funds, branch offices, call centers, blah blah blah, it all exists to justify the only pricing page that matters, and all the verbiage on the pricing page is about how much you pay the customer.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie dissects how discount brokerages actually make money, revealing that the vast majority of revenue comes from net interest on customer cash balances rather than commissions or the controversial 'payment for order flow.' He walks through the revenue breakdown of Schwab, TD Ameritrade, E*TRADE, and Interactive Brokers, showing that net interest accounts for 49-67% of revenue while payment for order flow is typically 1-8%. He argues that public outrage over payment for order flow is misplaced, since it actually results in better execution prices for retail investors, while the real hidden cost is the interest spread brokerages earn on idle cash. The piece concludes that commissions will go to zero, roboadvisors are a bad business below scale, and technologists holding concentrated stock positions should investigate securities lending.
quotes.key_insight: The outrage over payment for order flow is misplaced because it represents a tiny fraction of brokerage revenue and actually benefits retail investors, while the real hidden cost — the interest spread on customer cash balances — goes almost entirely unnoticed.

quotes.quote: Stripe is a celebration of the written word which happens to be incorporated in the state of Delaware.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on his first two years at Stripe, explaining why he chose to work there instead of running his own business. He frames the decision around leverage — Stripe's scale allows his work to impact far more businesses than he could reach independently. He describes his unusual role as a senior individual contributor, his work on Stripe Atlas, security red-teaming, internal writing, and mentoring. The post also covers the challenges of hypergrowth, remote work from Japan, and how working at a large organization changed his views on ambition and impact.
quotes.key_insight: Joining a high-growth company trading autonomy for leverage can multiply your impact far beyond what solo entrepreneurship allows, but it requires accepting organizational overhead and the constant recalibration that hypergrowth demands.

quotes.quote: Mediocrity doesn't happen because a villain kills good work then cackles maniacally. BigCo doesn't set out to be mediocre… BigCo just has an awful lot of people with an awful lot on their plate, and many of them are incentivized to make locally-optimal decisions at the expense of competing global goals.

quotes.quote: There is a real sense that your children moving to Tokyo causes them to lose connection with their culture and that the rewards from the national enterprise aren't being allocated fairly.
quotes.summary: Japan's Furusato Nouzei (Hometown Tax) system allows taxpayers to redirect up to 40% of their residence tax to any city of their choice, receiving a 1:1 tax credit. Created to address the imbalance between Tokyo (which absorbs educated workers) and rural regions (which pay to educate them), the system evolved far beyond its original intent. Cities began offering gifts in return for donations, leveraging Japan's gift-reciprocation culture, which escalated into a competitive bidding war where towns offer near-cash equivalents worth up to 50% of the donation. Participation exploded from 33,000 people in 2008 to 2.2 million in 2016, with $2.5 billion donated, creating a thriving intermediary industry. Despite the gaming, the system broadly achieves its goals of redistributing resources and reconnecting migrants with their hometowns.
quotes.key_insight: Japan's Hometown Tax evolved from a modest redistribution scheme into a competitive marketplace because gift-reciprocation culture and unfettered donor choice created incentives that turned tax policy into e-commerce.

Participation exploded from 33,000 people in 2008 to 2.2 million in 2016, with $2.5 billion donated, creating a thriving intermediary industry. Despite the gaming, the system broadly achieves its goals of redistributing resources and reconnecting migrants with their hometowns.
quotes.key_insight: Japan's Hometown Tax evolved from a modest redistribution scheme into a competitive marketplace because gift-reciprocation culture and unfettered donor choice created incentives that turned tax policy into e-commerce.

quotes.quote: It's a great game theory problem: unless a supermajority of cities collectively agrees to limit gifts to a token number, it's strongly in a city's interest to duck the central government's questionnaires and not express any objection to the status quo.

quotes.quote: Mean words cannot hurt a bank. Threats cannot hurt a bank. Paper trails, though, are terrifying to regulated institutions.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a comprehensive guide to resolving identity theft and erroneous credit report entries, drawing on his unusual hobby of ghostwriting hundreds of dispute letters from 2004-2007. He explains how credit reporting agencies and banks operate, their incentives, and their regulatory obligations under the FCRA and FDCPA. The core strategy is to present yourself as an organized professional building a paper trail, communicate only in writing, and leverage the fact that regulated institutions fear well-documented complaints far more than angry phone calls. He walks through the exact sequence: file a police report, write the bank, then the CRAs, using calm professional language that signals awareness of legal deadlines and willingness to escalate.
quotes.key_insight: Regulated institutions don't fear anger or threats — they fear calm, organized professionals with paper trails who understand legal deadlines and will escalate to regulators without hesitation.

quotes.quote: You never want identity theft treated like a customer service problem. Their CS department is scored on number of tickets resolved per hour, and each rep's incentives are simply to classify you as something requiring no followup and get you off the phone.

quotes.quote: People who can file a regulatory action while being emotionless about it are terrifying, because they suggest that their day job is e.g. administrator for a hospital, that they're very comfortable with pushing papers around government agencies, and that they will remember deadlines, keep copious records, and consult with other professionals where appropriate.

quotes.quote: I buy and operate really, really miniscule businesses and I grow them to 'tiny.'
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews Jonathan Siegel, a Tokyo-based entrepreneur who buys and operates small software businesses as a portfolio. Siegel describes his evolution from building products (with a ~4% hit rate) to acquiring existing businesses, starting with Exceptional and Airbrake in the error-monitoring space. The conversation covers the practical economics of selling small SaaS businesses, including seller financing, typical valuations, and why founders often sell despite ongoing revenue. They discuss separating creative passion from profit-driven discipline, the challenges of scaling teams, and how Silicon Valley culture distorts founders' definitions of success, causing them to reject life-changing acquisition offers in pursuit of billion-dollar outcomes.
quotes.key_insight: The most reliable path to building wealth through software isn't chasing unicorn outcomes but acquiring and growing small existing businesses while keeping your creative passions separate from your profit-driven discipline.

quotes.quote: A business failing because the market wasn't ready for it is natural. A business failing because it cannot get a checking account or because it operates in a jurisdiction where incorporation requires a bribe, on the other hand, is a bug.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on a tumultuous 2016 that saw three major transitions: the shutdown of Starfighter (his CTF-based recruiting startup), joining Stripe to work on Atlas (a product to help entrepreneurs worldwide incorporate businesses), and selling Appointment Reminder to pay down $120,000 in debt accumulated during the Starfighter years. He candidly discusses how personal burn rate, leverage, and family obligations shaped his decisions, and explains why he chose employment at Stripe over restarting consulting or launching a new SaaS. The post captures the emotional and financial realities of transitioning from indie software entrepreneur to employee at a fast-growing company.
quotes.key_insight: The arc from indie entrepreneur to Stripe employee was driven not by ambition but by the hard math of leverage, debt, and family obligations—and by finding a mission (reducing friction for new businesses worldwide) that offered more impact than any solo venture could.

quotes.quote: It is not widely appreciated how fragile companies are in the early days. They grow into self-perpetuating institutions filled with skilled professionals, armies of lawyers and accountants, and the resources to solve problems great and small… but they don't start that way. The world expects them to be mighty oaks, but they all start as seeds in the wind.

quotes.quote: I knew nothing — nobody had ever told me that 'creating intellectual property' is something that you don't need to ask permission to do and my background therefore suggested it was either forbidden, risky, or risked being forbidden.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces he's joining Stripe to work on Atlas, a product that aims to make company incorporation and back-office operations as simple as spinning up a cloud server. He traces his journey from a Japanese salaryman building Bingo Card Creator to becoming a prominent voice in the small software business community. He argues that the number of internet-enabled businesses will inevitably explode due to falling costs in payments, distribution, and manufacturing, and that Atlas can remove the remaining logistical barriers preventing entrepreneurs worldwide from starting companies. He frames the opportunity in terms of macroeconomic impact, noting that interventions early in the business funnel could show up in global economic indicators.
quotes.key_insight: The barriers to starting internet businesses are falling so rapidly that the main remaining obstacles are logistical and informational — incorporation, banking, bookkeeping — and products like Atlas can remove those barriers at scale to unlock an explosion of new businesses worldwide.

quotes.quote: I work at Stripe; I work for the Internet. If you're on the Internet, I work for you, too.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces he's joining Stripe to work on Atlas, a product that aims to make company incorporation and back-office operations as simple as spinning up a cloud server. He traces his journey from a Japanese salaryman building Bingo Card Creator to becoming a prominent voice in the small software business community. He argues that the number of internet-enabled businesses will inevitably explode due to falling costs in payments, distribution, and manufacturing, and that Atlas can remove the remaining logistical barriers preventing entrepreneurs worldwide from starting companies. He frames the opportunity in terms of macroeconomic impact, noting that interventions early in the business funnel could show up in global economic indicators.
quotes.key_insight: The barriers to starting internet businesses are falling so rapidly that the main remaining obstacles are logistical and informational — incorporation, banking, bookkeeping — and products like Atlas can remove those barriers at scale to unlock an explosion of new businesses worldwide.

quotes.quote: Some people would feel overwhelmed if asked to write Ruby, negotiate with vendors, and construct lifecycle email sequences — that sounds to me like a fulfilling way to spend any given Tuesday.

quotes.quote: The real driver there is the fact that a SaaS business has that recurring income and that baseline. From a buyer's perspective, worst case scenario, even if you can't grow the business or even if it starts to decline, you've still got that baseline of subscribers.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews Thomas Smale of FE International about the process of buying and selling online businesses, drawing on Patrick's experience selling Bingo Card Creator through the brokerage. The conversation covers how SaaS businesses command a ~25% valuation premium over other online businesses due to recurring revenue predictability, with typical multiples of 3-5x annual seller discretionary earnings. They walk through the full acquisition process from initial consultation through prospectus creation, buyer matching, due diligence, contract negotiation, and handover. Patrick emphasizes the importance of preparing months in advance by separating shared infrastructure, documenting code, and keeping financials clean. The episode also reveals that many small SaaS buyers are non-technical, and that working with a reputable broker provides critical value when deals hit unexpected snags.
quotes.key_insight: Selling a small online business successfully requires months of unglamorous preparation — separating infrastructure, documenting code, cleaning financials — and a structured process with a trusted broker who maintains backup buyers and standardized terms to navigate the inevitable complications.

quotes.quote: It always amazes me how many small business owners run businesses and they have no idea what they've got going in or out each month.

quotes.quote: This question doesn't sound like, 'Hey, I'm trying to cheat you out of $100,000' but they're trying to cheat you out of $100,000 when they ask this, and they know this.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews salary negotiation expert Josh Doody about tactical approaches to negotiating compensation at every stage of employment. They cover how to deflect the 'what is your current salary' question without lying, why you should always negotiate upward from any offer, how to trade across multiple compensation axes (salary, vacation, equity), and how to build a formal written case for raises at your current company. The conversation emphasizes that negotiation behavior signals competence to employers — confident professionals who hold boundaries are perceived as stronger candidates. McKenzie estimates his original salary negotiation blog post has generated over $2.3 million in added annual salary for readers.
quotes.key_insight: Salary negotiation is not adversarial — it's a professional competence test where holding your boundaries and refusing to volunteer information signals exactly the kind of confident, trustworthy employee companies want to hire.

quotes.quote: No matter what their offer is, I will negotiate upwards.

quotes.quote: Every great developer you know got there by solving problems they were unqualified to solve until they actually did it.
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains the fundamentals of stock exchange mechanics and algorithmic trading for generalist programmers, using Stockfighter (Starfighter's programming game) as the vehicle. He walks through order books, limit orders, market makers, spreads, liquidity, and risk management, then describes how Stockfighter lets developers practice these concepts via a REST API without risking real money. The post doubles as both a trading primer and a product pitch for Starfighter's hiring-through-gameplay business model. He argues that most web developers underestimate their ability to tackle problems like algorithmic trading, and that the barrier is psychological rather than technical.
quotes.key_insight: Algorithmic trading is far more accessible than most programmers assume—the core data structures and algorithms are straightforward, and the real barrier is the belief that finance is too complex to approach.

quotes.quote: I have a sneaking suspicion Wall Street invented many of these words to give customers the impression 'This is all really, really difficult — pay us money and the complexity goes away.'

quotes.quote: Starfighter doesn't ever intend to build gates (gates close; that's what they are for). We dynamite gates.
quotes.summary: McKenzie details the design and engineering behind Stockfighter, a programming game built by his company Starfighter to improve engineering hiring. The game casts players as Wall Street traders who must write code to interact with a realistic stock exchange simulation via REST APIs. He explains why they chose a finance setting (it naturally encompasses diverse engineering challenges), how they built a high-performance stock exchange in Go capable of processing orders in 100-200 nanoseconds, and how simple trading bots produce surprisingly complex emergent market behavior. The piece covers their tech stack decisions (Go for performance-critical exchange code, Ruby for game logic, React for the trading UI, NSQ for messaging), level design philosophy emphasizing specified goals with unspecified solutions, and their commitment to browser-based accessibility to avoid gatekeeping.
quotes.key_insight: A realistic stock exchange simulation built with simple components produces emergent complexity that naturally tests the full spectrum of engineering skills — from systems programming and distributed systems reasoning to algorithm design and data analysis — without artificially constraining solutions.

quotes.quote: Compensation negotiations are presently like a stock exchange where only your counterparty can see the ticker and order book. You'd never send an order to that exchange — it would be an invitation to be fleeced.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares his complete salary and consulting rate history to support the #talkpay movement, arguing that information asymmetry in compensation negotiations systematically disadvantages employees. He traces his journey from $10/hr order entry through Japanese salaryman wages to consulting rates of $30k-$50k per week, demonstrating that the difference between low and high compensation is rarely about skill but about positioning, packaging, and market knowledge. He argues that well-compensated professionals have a moral obligation to share compensation data to help those earlier in their careers. The post also covers equity valuation, business sales, and negotiation tactics.
quotes.key_insight: The gap between low and high compensation is primarily about information asymmetry and self-positioning, not skill — the same person can earn 50x more by targeting higher-value work and overcoming the discomfort of charging what they're worth.

quotes.quote: I was nearly exactly the same level of capabilities at $30k a year as I was at $30k a week, I just got better at organizing the business to expose me to opportunities to capture the better rate.

quotes.quote: We're not here to fix the technical interview: we're here to destroy it, and create something new and better in its place.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces Starfighter, a company cofounded with Thomas and Erin Ptacek that will publish free-to-play Capture The Flag programming games designed to teach rare programming skills and serve as work-sample tests for hiring. The business model is contingency recruiting: companies pay Starfighter a fee when they hire players who demonstrate exceptional skill through the games. McKenzie argues that traditional tech hiring is fundamentally broken, filtering on resumes and keywords rather than ability, and structurally excluding qualified candidates. Starfighter aims to replace this with direct skill measurement, connecting top-performing players with CTOs rather than HR inbox black holes. The post also notes McKenzie is selling Bingo Card Creator and transitioning away from self-employment to focus on Starfighter full-time.
quotes.key_insight: The tech industry's reliance on resume filtering and whiteboard interviews over work-sample skill assessment represents both a massive market inefficiency and a structural barrier to entry that a well-designed CTF platform can profitably dismantle.

quotes.quote: The build-versus-buy decision for anything at a SaaS company which isn't something you actually sell should come down to 'Can I do this before lunch?' If yes, build. If not, buy or adopt OSS.
quotes.summary: This podcast episode features Jay Winder, CEO of MakeLeaps (a FreshBooks for Japan), discussing his experience raising a $750K seed round through AngelList syndicates after bootstrapping for four years. The conversation covers how AngelList's syndicate mechanism dramatically simplifies angel investing logistics, reducing transaction costs for both entrepreneurs and investors. They discuss negotiation tactics around standardized deal terms and the power of negotiating from abundance. The episode also explores Japan's underdeveloped SaaS ecosystem, the cultural tendency of Japanese companies to build rather than buy software, and the counterintuitive social proof that comes with funding announcements versus bootstrap success.
quotes.key_insight: AngelList syndicates are transforming seed-stage fundraising by collapsing transaction costs, standardizing terms, and enabling founders — even those outside Silicon Valley — to negotiate from strength through oversubscription.

quotes.quote: Most data which will be new to your system is not actually new. It presently exists somewhere in the customer's organization.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie details the design and implementation of CSV/Excel upload for his SaaS product Appointment Reminder, a feature he delayed for four years due to its estimated $100K engineering cost. By leveraging two open-source libraries—SheetJS for in-browser spreadsheet parsing and Handsontable for grid editing—he reduced implementation to 3.5 days. The post covers the technical architecture including client-side parsing, Redis-backed job processing, feature flags for gradual rollout, and the UX challenges of column mapping and error handling. He concludes with a meditation on OSS sustainability, arguing that SaaS businesses should pay for commercial licenses rather than relying on the donation model.
quotes.key_insight: The combination of client-side spreadsheet parsing and pragmatic engineering tradeoffs can reduce a $100K feature to days of work, but the real insight is knowing when to ship imperfect software behind feature flags rather than waiting for perfection.

quotes.quote: I assessed this feature as probably costing $100,000 in engineering time to implement well, so I punted and implemented a quick 'concierge upload feature.'

quotes.quote: Since failure is not an option, given that we're fathers, fatherhood succeeds where a bunch of New Year's resolutions in the previous years did not.
quotes.summary: Patrick and Keith discuss how having families has transformed how they run their businesses. They cover updates on their respective products (Appointment Reminder and Summit Evergreen), then dive into how children create forcing functions for better time management, delegation, and productivity. The conversation touches on Silicon Valley's youth-obsessed culture, Japan's structural problems with work-life balance and declining birth rates, and the importance of tracking key business metrics. Both argue that family constraints actually improve business outcomes by forcing prioritization and delegation rather than endless tinkering.
quotes.key_insight: Family constraints are not obstacles to business success but forcing functions that drive better delegation, prioritization, and ultimately faster growth than the unconstrained solo-founder lifestyle.

quotes.quote: Menial tasks are easy to do, and they certainly feel like you're winning when you're doing them, which is the most toxic thing ever.

quotes.quote: Is it weird to say that is distinct from actually wanting the money? I wouldn't mind a little bit more to establish more of a buffer for my family, but other than that, money qua money doesn't buy me anything I don't already have. It might as well be WoW gold.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie's 2014 year-in-review reveals his businesses grossed roughly $200,000 with $120,000 in profit across Appointment Reminder, Bingo Card Creator, and productized consulting. He candidly admits that Appointment Reminder, despite being his main focus since 2010, hasn't reached the $500k/year level he expected, largely because he doesn't find the problem space exciting. BCC declined 33% in revenue on autopilot while he struggled with burnout and health issues. His biggest regret is failing to ship a pre-sold conversion optimization course, which blocked his entire product pipeline. He moved his family from rural Ogaki to Tokyo, finally hired help for AR, and decided definitively against raising venture funding.
quotes.key_insight: Passion for the problem you're solving matters more than the business opportunity — without it, even a smart operator will chronically underinvest in a viable product.

quotes.quote: The salaryman/employer relationship is best characterized as 'You swear yourself to us, body and soul, and in return we will isolate you from all risks.'
quotes.tone: candid, witty, deeply informed, affectionate yet unflinching
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a comprehensive insider's guide to doing business in Japan, drawing on his decade of experience as a foreign entrepreneur. The essay centers on the salaryman system as the defining institution of Japanese professional life, explaining how it creates a near-total dependency between employee and employer that pervades housing, relationships, social life, and identity. This system makes startups extraordinarily risky in social terms, constrains hiring, and shapes how software is sold (high-touch, relationship-driven). Despite these challenges, McKenzie argues Japan represents a massively underserved market with weak competition, where even adequate technology feels like wizardry from the future. He balances frank discussion of systemic racism and bureaucratic friction with genuine affection for Japan's culture of personal service and relationship-based trust.
quotes.key_insight: Japan's salaryman system creates a total social institution so powerful that opting out carries enormous personal risk, but this same rigidity leaves the market massively underserved by technology, creating extraordinary opportunities for those willing to navigate the cultural complexity.

quotes.quote: Most people want to become wealthy so they can consume social status. Japanese employers believe this is inefficient, and simply award social status directly.

quotes.quote: Much of the time, if there's an onboarding issue in the user experience, it's a lot of times derived from the way that the teams were split up to begin with.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews Samuel Hulick, a user onboarding expert, about how SaaS companies fail at onboarding and how to fix it. They discuss how organizational structure (Conway's Law) causes onboarding gaps, with 40-60% of free trial users never logging in a second time. The conversation covers lifecycle emails, product tours, empty states, and the importance of guiding users toward accomplishment rather than just showing features. The second half shifts to how Samuel built his reputation as 'the user onboarding guy' through public teardowns, grew an email list to 11,000+, and earned $70K from a self-published book. Both emphasize the power of building assets, owning your niche, and shipping things rather than perpetually preparing.
quotes.key_insight: The fundamental feedback loop of successful SaaS companies is doing unscalable things — like live chat and manual customer interactions — to fuel the scalable improvements in onboarding, lifecycle emails, and product experience that serve the thousands of customers who never talk to you.

quotes.quote: You do lots of stuff that doesn't scale, and then use the stuff that doesn't scale as fuel for the mass-scalable approaches that affect the rest of the customer base that doesn't talk to you.

quotes.quote: All things are worth what someone will happily trade you for them.
quotes.summary: McKenzie uses an elaborate Harry Potter fanfiction dialogue to explain how the Stellar cryptocurrency protocol works at a conceptual level. The piece walks through fundamental concepts of money, debt, trust, IOUs, gateways, currency exchanges, and distributed consensus by having Hermione, Harry, Ron, and a Defense Against the Dark Arts professor (representing Bitcoin maximalists) interact with the 'Stellarmus' system. Along the way, it contrasts Stellar's trust-based consensus model with Bitcoin's proof-of-work system, highlights the counterparty risks inherent in any financial system, and skewers Bitcoin maximalist ideology. McKenzie concludes that Stellar is an interesting proposal that may someday matter, while reaffirming his skepticism of Bitcoin.
quotes.key_insight: Every financial system — including supposedly 'trustless' cryptocurrencies — ultimately requires trust in someone, and the honest question is not whether to trust but whom and how much.

quotes.quote: No one is worth unlimited trust, young Master Potter, not even a goblin.

quotes.quote: Galleons are a currency, Master Potter. They happen to have a bit of gold in them, to be true, but the real magic of them — a strikingly ordinary kind of magic — is that the inhabitants of Wizarding Britain want to have them.
quotes.summary: McKenzie uses an elaborate Harry Potter fanfiction dialogue to explain how the Stellar cryptocurrency protocol works at a conceptual level. The piece walks through fundamental concepts of money, debt, trust, IOUs, gateways, currency exchanges, and distributed consensus by having Hermione, Harry, Ron, and a Defense Against the Dark Arts professor (representing Bitcoin maximalists) interact with the 'Stellarmus' system. Along the way, it contrasts Stellar's trust-based consensus model with Bitcoin's proof-of-work system, highlights the counterparty risks inherent in any financial system, and skewers Bitcoin maximalist ideology. McKenzie concludes that Stellar is an interesting proposal that may someday matter, while reaffirming his skepticism of Bitcoin.
quotes.key_insight: Every financial system — including supposedly 'trustless' cryptocurrencies — ultimately requires trust in someone, and the honest question is not whether to trust but whom and how much.

quotes.quote: If you actually want to convince somebody to sell somebody on something, a phone call or in-person meeting are still a lot more richer environments, more successful environments, to make that happen.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews Steli Efti, founder of Close.io, about high-touch software sales for SaaS businesses. They discuss how engineers are socialized to avoid sales but can actually excel at it by reframing sales calls as customer development conversations. The episode covers practical tactics for getting started with inbound sales, handling rejection by tracking 'noes' as progress, qualifying leads by asking what it would take to become a customer, and pipeline management. Steli also shares the story of pivoting from a successful 60-person sales consultancy to focus entirely on Close.io's SaaS product, driven by the software's natural momentum and the consulting business's painful cash flow dynamics.
quotes.key_insight: Engineers who reframe sales as structured customer development — asking questions, qualifying intent, and creating outcomes rather than 'maybes' — can dramatically increase SaaS revenue without becoming the manipulative salespeople they fear.

quotes.quote: Instead of focusing on the 10 wins, what you can do is focus on the losses, and say, 'I know that for every nine noes I get, I will get a yes.' I'm not trying to earn the success, I'm trying to work my way through the failure.

quotes.quote: Someone out there will eventually crack the Da Vinci code of getting scalable customer acquisition for SaaS businesses. And that person is going to be a billionaire.
quotes.summary: This podcast episode covers launching new products, with Patrick McKenzie and Keith Perhac discussing creditcard.js as an example of a well-scoped developer product, Keith's new SaaS Summit Evergreen for dripping online courses, and concierge onboarding as a powerful conversion tactic. They explore tiered SaaS pricing strategies, the importance of aligning price with customer success, and why B2B dramatically outperforms B2C for small software businesses. The conversation also covers using Standard Operating Procedures to delegate repetitive tasks, insights from MicroConf on scaling businesses, and the practical realities of customer acquisition costs and lifetime value calculations for SaaS.
quotes.key_insight: The highest-leverage activities in a software business are concierge onboarding to maximize conversion, thoughtful tiered pricing to capture customer surplus, and systematizing repetitive work through SOPs before automating it.

quotes.quote: In a world of infinite choices, if something isn't made exactly for me, then I'm gone.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie presents a detailed walkthrough of conversion optimization for SaaS businesses, using Freckle (a time-tracking tool) as a live consulting case study. He covers the fundamental SaaS equation (traffic × conversion rate × ARPU × retention), then systematically analyzes each step of Freckle's signup funnel — from homepage H1 copy to pricing page design to the signup form itself. He demonstrates how to craft headlines that address specific customer objections, why buttons should look like buttons with benefit-focused copy, and how pricing pages should stomp objections rather than list features. He also emphasizes charging more as the easiest lever, shares how adding a $200 plan doubled his own revenue, and argues that in-app onboarding tours are critical for B2B SaaS retention.
quotes.key_insight: Conversion optimization starts not with A/B testing buttons but with mapping your funnel numerically to find the real bottleneck, then building a mental model of why prospects leave at that step.

quotes.quote: We were all racing against for loops and the prize for 2nd place was 'Our customers' security gets horribly abused.'
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that the Heartbleed vulnerability's unprecedented rapid response was largely due to superior marketing: a memorable name, professional logo, dedicated website, and clear writing. He contrasts this with previous critical vulnerabilities like CVE-2013-0156 (Rails YAML deserialization) that failed to spur action because they were communicated through obtuse CVE numbers and plain-text mailing list posts. The post breaks down exactly why each marketing element worked — the name was emotionally evocative and technically accurate, the website copy was tight enough for executives to grasp in 30 seconds, and the logo conveyed danger and professionalism. McKenzie concludes that the open source community's cultural resistance to marketing is actively harmful, because getting people to patch systems is itself an engineering problem.
quotes.key_insight: Getting people to patch critical vulnerabilities is a coordination problem that demands the same engineering rigor as writing the fix itself — memorable names, clear writing, and professional branding aren't vanity, they're the delivery mechanism for security.

quotes.quote: This makes marketing an engineering discipline. We have to get good at it, or we will fail ourselves, our stakeholders, our community, and the wider world.

McKenzie concludes that the open source community's cultural resistance to marketing is actively harmful, because getting people to patch systems is itself an engineering problem.
quotes.key_insight: Getting people to patch critical vulnerabilities is a coordination problem that demands the same engineering rigor as writing the fix itself — memorable names, clear writing, and professional branding aren't vanity, they're the delivery mechanism for security.

quotes.quote: Tarsnap (the business) is run with less seriousness than a 6 year old's first lemonade stand.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie writes a detailed, unsolicited-but-permission-granted consulting workup for Tarsnap, Colin Percival's encrypted backup service. He argues that while Tarsnap has world-class engineering, its business operations are so poor they actively undermine its engineering mission. McKenzie proposes repositioning from 'backups for the truly paranoid' to 'backups for servers of serious professionals,' replacing metered picodollar pricing with a 3-tier SaaS model ($50/$100/$500/month), redesigning the amateur-looking website, and improving onboarding. He contends that charging more would benefit both Colin and his customers by funding critical improvements like recurring billing, monitoring, and business continuity planning.
quotes.key_insight: Brilliant engineering undermined by amateurish business practices doesn't just leave money on the table — it actively harms the product's users by starving the business of resources needed for critical features like recurring billing, monitoring, and business continuity.

quotes.quote: I anticipate that phone call to take 5 minutes or less. $2,400 is approximately equal to an entire month's profits of BCC. Any questions?
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews his 2013 business year across four lines of business: Bingo Card Creator (maintenance mode, sales down 23%), Appointment Reminder (revenue doubled), consulting (quit mid-year), and productized consulting (~$62k revenue). Total disclosed Kalzumeus Software revenue was ~$112k with ~$60k profit, though Appointment Reminder numbers are kept private and now dominate his income. The year was marked by a severe cashflow crunch caused by poorly timed consulting exit coinciding with tax obligations, capital investments, and seasonal lows, which contributed to serious health problems. Despite the difficulties, he successfully transitioned from consulting to productized consulting, outsourced BCC operations, and set ambitious goals for 2014.
quotes.key_insight: The most dangerous moment for a solo entrepreneur is when strategic transitions collide with financial obligations, because running multiple businesses on cash-basis accounting without a balance sheet means you can't see the cliff until you're already falling off it.

quotes.quote: The Internet has a short temper and a long memory.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Nathan Barry discuss the strategy of 'teaching as marketing' — building an audience and credibility by publicly sharing expertise, then monetizing through productized knowledge (books, courses, email lists). They cover how Nathan launched multiple successful books without being 'Internet famous,' how tiered pricing packages can double or triple revenue compared to single-price offerings, and why email lists are the most valuable business asset for independent creators. The conversation emphasizes that you don't need celebrity status to build authority — consistent teaching creates perceived expertise that translates directly into sales.
quotes.key_insight: Building an audience through consistent public teaching creates authority and trust that converts directly into product sales, and smart packaging with tiered pricing lets customers self-segment so you capture value proportional to what each buyer receives.

quotes.quote: The first way to never get screwed about a $10,000 check is to not really need it.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Keith Perhac discuss strategies for transitioning from consulting to product-based revenue streams. Patrick explains why he wound down his consulting business after a Fortune 500 engagement collapsed and took months of his pipeline with it, highlighting the fragility of consulting income. They cover multiple productization models: retainer agreements with existing clients, licensing pre-written code, creating info-products (courses and books) from consulting expertise, and building products for other companies' platforms. The conversation emphasizes that retainer agreements would have been 'transformatively good' for Patrick's consulting business, and that even 90% of paid info-product content may be freely available but the curation and packaging create enormous value.
quotes.key_insight: Consultants should systematically convert one-time engagements into recurring revenue through retainer agreements, licensed tools, and info-products extracted from their existing expertise, because consulting without residual income is inherently fragile.

quotes.quote: If you are a W-2 employee, you have one boss. If you move into consulting and have three clients, who are each responsible for about one third of your billing, you now have three bosses.

quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Keith Perhac discuss strategies for transitioning from consulting to product-based revenue streams. Patrick explains why he wound down his consulting business after a Fortune 500 engagement collapsed and took months of his pipeline with it, highlighting the fragility of consulting income. They cover multiple productization models: retainer agreements with existing clients, licensing pre-written code, creating info-products (courses and books) from consulting expertise, and building products for other companies' platforms. The conversation emphasizes that retainer agreements would have been 'transformatively good' for Patrick's consulting business, and that even 90% of paid info-product content may be freely available but the curation and packaging create enormous value.
quotes.key_insight: Consultants should systematically convert one-time engagements into recurring revenue through retainer agreements, licensed tools, and info-products extracted from their existing expertise, because consulting without residual income is inherently fragile.

quotes.quote: If any server operated by your company is on an unpatched version of 2.3, that server will be compromised by the adversary.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues urgently that businesses running Rails 2.3 applications face certain security compromise since Rails Core has dropped support, and presents Rails LTS — a commercially supported fork by German consultancy Makandra — as the practical solution. He explains that upgrading to Rails 3/4 is prohibitively expensive for many businesses, self-maintaining a fork requires expertise most companies lack, and relying on informal forks like GitHub's offers no guarantees. McKenzie bankrolled Rails LTS as its anchor customer with $10,000, negotiating a model where patches are open-sourced after a 10-day delay. The post systematically dismantles objections to paying for the service, framing it as insurance rather than paying for code.
quotes.key_insight: When open source projects drop support for versions your business depends on, paying for commercial maintenance is not a luxury but a basic cost of doing business — far cheaper than the inevitable security breach.

quotes.quote: You're not really paying money for OSS code which already exists, you're buying the guarantee that new code gets written at non-deterministic times in the future before the lack of it causes an emergency for your business.

quotes.quote: Your customers do not perceive the value of your applications as being dumb telephone pipes. If they wanted dumb telephone pipes, they could get them from AT&T for like $14 a month.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie presents a comprehensive guide to selling software to businesses, covering low-touch, high-touch, and hybrid sales models. He argues that SaaS companies should price based on customer perceived value rather than cost, use tiered pricing with strategic plan names, and offer annual billing to improve cash flow and reduce churn. He demonstrates how product tours, lifecycle email campaigns, and drip sequences can dramatically improve conversion rates. He also shares how he personally won enterprise hospital contracts over an 800-pound gorilla competitor by investing time in relationships, and teaches specific tactics for navigating purchasing departments and enterprise sales cycles.
quotes.key_insight: Software companies massively underprice by anchoring to their costs rather than to the business value customers receive, and the hybrid sales model — combining low-touch self-serve with high-touch enterprise engagement — is the most underexploited opportunity in SaaS.

quotes.quote: A job is a system that turns time into money, and a business is a system that turns systems into money.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie's Microconf 2012 talk covers systems-based marketing for software companies, aimed at engineers who'd rather build than sell. He argues that engineers have a natural advantage in building scalable marketing systems because they can exploit platforms like Google, Twitter, and app stores programmatically. The talk covers practical tactics including SaaS pricing strategy (charge more, segment by customer value, name plans carefully), automated marketing email sequences that educate then sell, and optimizing the first-run experience of software to dramatically improve activation rates. He also briefly discusses his consulting practice, where he commands $20,000/week by framing his work as revenue generation rather than hourly development labor.
quotes.key_insight: Engineers have a unique advantage in business because they can build automated systems for marketing, sales, and customer activation — the companies that win are the ones that systematize everything from email sequences to first-run experiences rather than doing marketing manually.

quotes.quote: My definition of traction, if I can throw it out there, is evidence that you can capture value at a rate that moves the business forward.
quotes.summary: This podcast transcript covers the story of Stormpulse, a bootstrapped weather risk management SaaS that grew from a side project to being used in the White House Situation Room. Patrick McKenzie interviews founder Matt Wensing about the company's transition from freemium (6.5M free users) to a paid B2B model, their experience raising angel funding outside Silicon Valley, and the mechanics of selling enterprise software. The conversation covers convertible notes, the role of social proof in fundraising, why most people shouldn't angel invest, and how founders have unique advantages in enterprise sales. McKenzie and Wensing both emphasize that B2B software is chronically underpriced and that founders should charge far more than their instincts suggest.
quotes.key_insight: Traction isn't vanity metrics — it's evidence you can capture value at an accelerating rate, and the path from bootstrapping to funded growth requires mastering social proof both for fundraising and enterprise sales.

quotes.quote: I want to disabuse you from that notion, right now. For the vast majority of people listening to this, angel investment is not a good idea.

Patrick McKenzie interviews founder Matt Wensing about the company's transition from freemium (6.5M free users) to a paid B2B model, their experience raising angel funding outside Silicon Valley, and the mechanics of selling enterprise software. The conversation covers convertible notes, the role of social proof in fundraising, why most people shouldn't angel invest, and how founders have unique advantages in enterprise sales. McKenzie and Wensing both emphasize that B2B software is chronically underpriced and that founders should charge far more than their instincts suggest.
quotes.key_insight: Traction isn't vanity metrics — it's evidence you can capture value at an accelerating rate, and the path from bootstrapping to funded growth requires mastering social proof both for fundraising and enterprise sales.

quotes.quote: Fire four HTTP requests at every server on the Internet: if the server is added to your botnet, it was running a vulnerable version of Ruby on Rails.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive breakdown of the January 2013 Ruby on Rails YAML deserialization vulnerabilities that allowed remote code execution on virtually any Rails application connected to the internet. He argues the severity is apocalyptic and that thousands of applications will be compromised for years, not just those under active development but forgotten staging servers, localhost instances, and downstream dependencies. He extends the analysis beyond Rails shops to show how any startup consuming services from Rails-based providers is also at risk. The post concludes with practical advice on security incident response, maintaining application inventories, and preparing for what he predicts will be an ongoing cascade of related vulnerabilities.
quotes.key_insight: Catastrophic framework vulnerabilities don't just threaten direct users — they cascade through the entire ecosystem of services, dependencies, and shared credentials, making every startup's security posture only as strong as the weakest link in its supply chain.

quotes.quote: Profits roughly tripled from ~$70k to ~$200k, exclusive of Appointment Reminder.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie's 2012 year-in-review details how his profits roughly tripled from ~$70k to ~$200k across multiple business lines. Bingo Card Creator had its best year despite maintenance mode, driven by A/B testing and Stripe integration boosting conversion rates. His consulting practice grew by raising rates rather than hours, charging five-figure weekly rates for SaaS companies. He launched a productized consulting video course on lifecycle emails that earned ~$60k against a $20k target. Appointment Reminder quadrupled its monthly revenue run rate and landed first enterprise contracts, though it continued to receive less attention than planned. He closes with 2013 goals including systematizing sales, potentially hiring, and wrestling with whether to go all-in on AR.
quotes.key_insight: Tripling profits came not from working harder but from relentlessly optimizing conversion rates, raising consulting rates, and productizing expertise — demonstrating that leverage beats effort at every stage of a software business.

quotes.quote: The notion of spending months of work with a publisher to write a book that would likely fail to earn-out a $5,000 advance seemed to be a silly thing to do just to put "published author" on my resume.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces his book on conversion optimization, SEO, and A/B testing for software companies, published through Hyperink. The book compiles approximately 20 of his best blog essays with 4 new essays, covering selling software, increasing conversions, and SEO strategy. He explains he avoided traditional publishing because the economics didn't make sense, but Hyperink's more equitable royalty model and modern workflow convinced him. The book targets solo software entrepreneurs, SaaS marketing/engineering teams, and anyone who wanted his blog distilled into a curated collection.
quotes.key_insight: Traditional publishing economics are misaligned with niche technical expertise, but curating existing high-value content through modern publishing platforms can create a viable book without the typical author exploitation.

quotes.quote: Programmers build tools. Business consultants solve problems.
quotes.summary: This podcast transcript features Patrick McKenzie, Keith Perhac, and Brennan Dunn discussing how freelancers and consultants can dramatically increase their rates by repositioning themselves as business consultants rather than commodity programmers. They cover the mechanics of scaling from solo consulting to a multi-employee consultancy, including the harsh realities of cash flow, payroll obligations, and the gap between charge-out rates and employee costs. The conversation also explores using drip email campaigns and content marketing to build trust with prospects, making sales discussions feel like foregone conclusions rather than pitches. Throughout, they emphasize that the difference between low-rate and high-rate consultants is not skill but how they communicate value.
quotes.key_insight: The gap between low-rate and high-rate consultants is not technical skill but the ability to position yourself as someone who delivers measurable business outcomes rather than selling hours of labor.

quotes.quote: It is almost surreal how the people charging three times as much are not three times as better developers. The reason for the huge discrepancy is how they communicate their value and how they refuse to allow fear and uncertainty to rule their businesses.

quotes.quote: You can price it for free or you can price it for dear, but never price it for cheap.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Ramit Sethi argue that underpricing products and services attracts pathological customers who demand more while perceiving less value. Drawing from their own experiences — Ramit going from a $4.95 ebook to $12,000 courses, Patrick 7X-ing his consulting rates — they demonstrate that higher prices correlate with better customers, fewer support headaches, and greater perceived value. They discuss the psychology behind why engineers and freelancers chronically underprice, including fear of rejection and anchoring to hourly wage mental models. The core thesis is that pricing is strategic: it signals what market you play in, what customers you attract, and how seriously your work is taken.
quotes.key_insight: Pricing is not just a revenue decision but a customer-selection mechanism: higher prices attract customers who perceive more value, demand less support, and create better outcomes for everyone involved.

quotes.quote: If you are genuinely creating value for businesses, you are no longer in the 'trade defined small units of time for defined small units of money' business model. This is what most employees do. This is how you have spent most of your life working. You have left that life behind.

quotes.quote: 85 percent of the work is done before you ever sit down in the room.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Ramit Sethi discuss practical strategies for acquiring first consulting clients, emphasizing that most business is won through relationships and research long before any formal pitch. They argue that engineers systematically undercharge and fail to communicate in terms clients care about — business outcomes rather than technical details. The conversation covers how deep customer research reveals the exact language and pain points that make sales inevitable, why charging too little destroys credibility, and how to position yourself as someone who understands the client's business at a visceral level. They stress that the gap between commodity freelancing and premium consulting is primarily about repositioning and communication, not technical skill.
quotes.key_insight: Premium consulting is sold by demonstrating deep understanding of the client's business problems in their own words, not by showcasing technical skills or competing on price.

quotes.quote: You don't get 7x the value from server monitoring if you increase your server fleet by 7x, you get (at least) 50x the value.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that SaaS companies drastically undercharge by using linear per-unit pricing when customer value scales exponentially with usage. Using Server Density as a case study, he shows how switching from per-server variable pricing to a tiered plan model ($99/$299/$499) doubled revenue per visitor cohort. The old $11/server model attracted price-sensitive hobbyists who generated disproportionate support costs, while the new tiers segmented customers by business seriousness. He emphasizes that most customers don't care about price, and those who do are disproportionately terrible customers. The piece advocates for radical pricing restructurings over incremental tweaks.
quotes.key_insight: SaaS pricing should scale with customer value, not usage units — switching from linear per-unit pricing to tiered plans segments away pathological customers and captures the exponential value serious businesses derive from your product.

quotes.quote: We sell products which have margins that come very close to 100%, and saving pennies on transactions to spend tens of thousands on integration costs or to shave full percentages off our conversion rates is absolute madness.
quotes.summary: McKenzie details his experience integrating Stripe as a payment processor for Bingo Card Creator, praising its API design, developer experience, and support quality. He then walks through a series of A/B tests comparing Stripe's on-site credit card checkout against PayPal/Google Checkout off-site options, finding that customers overwhelmingly prefer credit cards (90% vs 5% each for PayPal and Google Checkout). The breakthrough came not from checkout design alone but from reducing the free trial limit from 15 to 8 cards, which combined synergistically with the redesigned Stripe-powered checkout to produce massive revenue gains. Year-over-year sales increased 38-108% across several months. He credits the combination of good infrastructure (Stripe), professional design, and rigorous A/B testing for the results.
quotes.key_insight: Developer experience and end-to-end control of the checkout flow matter far more than transaction fees, because the revenue gains from better conversion optimization dwarf any savings on processing costs.

quotes.quote: Email is the best broadcast channel ever invented for business purposes.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that email is the most effective broadcast channel for business purposes, yet most geeks and software developers dramatically underutilize it due to misguided psychological blocks. Drawing on his own experience launching a mailing list and six years of blogging, he demonstrates that email vastly outperforms blog posts and RSS feeds in open rates, click-through rates, and actual behavior change. He shows how email keeps leads warm, builds trust through drip campaigns, and can extract meaningful revenue from existing customers. The post doubles as both a persuasive essay and a launch announcement for his own training email list.
quotes.key_insight: Email massively outperforms blogs, social media, and RSS for driving actual behavior change and revenue because it lands in a workflow people are already committed to reading, rather than competing with thousands of equivalent-looking items in a feed.

quotes.quote: People are statistically speaking terrible at introspection.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that email is the most effective broadcast channel for business purposes, yet most geeks and software developers dramatically underutilize it due to misguided psychological blocks. Drawing on his own experience launching a mailing list and six years of blogging, he demonstrates that email vastly outperforms blog posts and RSS feeds in open rates, click-through rates, and actual behavior change. He shows how email keeps leads warm, builds trust through drip campaigns, and can extract meaningful revenue from existing customers. The post doubles as both a persuasive essay and a launch announcement for his own training email list.
quotes.key_insight: Email massively outperforms blogs, social media, and RSS for driving actual behavior change and revenue because it lands in a workflow people are already committed to reading, rather than competing with thousands of equivalent-looking items in a feed.

quotes.quote: If you routinely hang around people who run businesses, your perception of the world is going to get influenced by people who run businesses, and if you're routinely hanging around people who will perpetually have a business some day you have a risk of that warping your perspective on things.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie, Keith Perhac, and Amy Hoy discuss the trajectory from consulting to product businesses, pricing strategies for SaaS companies selling to businesses, and the lifestyle benefits of running your own software company. Amy describes building Freckle and her 30x500 course after leaving consulting, emphasizing that developers and designers absolutely do buy software despite conventional wisdom. The conversation covers why SaaS pricing grids should include high-tier enterprise plans, why selling to businesses beats B2C, and why competitors validate rather than threaten a market. They conclude with a philosophical discussion about work-life balance, arguing that the culture of overwork is self-imposed and that product businesses can generate substantial revenue on remarkably few hours of actual work.
quotes.key_insight: Product businesses selling to other businesses on value — not cost — can generate life-changing revenue on remarkably few hours of work, but founders must overcome both cultural pressure to overwork and the instinct to underprice.

quotes.quote: Anybody who's doing a SaaS pricing grid where the top price tops out at $20 or $30 should really put anything you need to just get an enterprise level and price it at $250 or $500. This is free money.

quotes.quote: The first rule of A/B testing is that everything you know is wrong.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes commissioning a full redesign of Bingo Card Creator and A/B testing it against the old version. The redesign dramatically improved trial signups and user success rates, with 45% more visitors successfully creating bingo cards. However, sales remained completely unchanged because fewer users hit the trial limitations that trigger purchases. The result illustrates why big-bang A/B tests are frustrating: when dozens of changes ship together, isolating the cause of any observed effect is nearly impossible. He also notes that users credited the purely cosmetic redesign with imaginary performance improvements and new features.
quotes.key_insight: Improving user experience and success metrics does not automatically translate to improved revenue — a redesign that made 45% more users succeed also removed the friction that caused them to pay.

quotes.quote: Teachers are so successful with the newly redesigned BCC that, out of any 100 using the software, less of them decide to purchase it.

quotes.quote: I've probably lost in excess of 100,000 readers over the years for the blog being down.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie explains why he stopped self-hosting his WordPress blog and moved to WPEngine's managed hosting. After years of fighting Apache KeepAlive issues, WordPress security vulnerabilities, server crashes during traffic spikes, and routine maintenance that consumed billable weeks of his time, he was convinced by WPEngine founder Jason Cohen's technical expertise and the service's superior infrastructure. The migration resulted in faster page loads, zero downtime during high-traffic events, and freed him to focus on his actual businesses instead of server administration.
quotes.key_insight: The opportunity cost of self-hosting infrastructure you don't need to control vastly exceeds the dollar cost of paying someone who has solved the problem properly.

quotes.quote: If it weren't the best blogging software available I'd take a hammer to my backups then burn the shards and bury them on sanctified ground so that they never troubled the world again.

quotes.quote: We overwhelmingly suck at it. We have turned sucking at it into a perverse badge of virtue.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that engineers are terrible at salary negotiation and have turned this incompetence into a misguided badge of honor. He frames negotiation as a brief, high-leverage activity worth potentially $100,000+ over a career, using the analogy of reading poetry while standing on one foot for $100,000. The post systematically dismantles psychological barriers to negotiation, explains how employers actually think about compensation (in terms of fully-loaded costs where $5,000 is trivial), and provides tactical advice including never giving a number first, using the employer's own language, and trading across multiple dimensions of compensation. McKenzie emphasizes that negotiation should only happen after receiving a 'Yes-If' — agreement in principle that you'll be hired if terms are reached — and that the process is far less adversarial than most candidates imagine.
quotes.key_insight: Salary negotiation is a brief, high-leverage skill that engineers irrationally avoid, yet the asymmetry between what $5,000 means to you versus your employer means even modest negotiation effort yields outsized career-long financial returns.
