quotes.quote: I forecast 50% growth, reasoning 'Hey, most of the systems work pretty much without my intervention, so I think the overall growth of the Internet plus a few A/B test means, oh, 50% or so.' It mostly tread water.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie's 2011 annual report covers his best year ever, breaking $100,000 in total sales across Bingo Card Creator, consulting, and Appointment Reminder. BCC was in maintenance mode for most of the year but remained roughly flat at ~$23k profit. Consulting generated $55k in revenue but consumed far more time than expected due to prospecting overhead and travel. Appointment Reminder grew despite neglect, with McKenzie weighing whether to take outside investment or grow organically. He candidly discusses how disorganization and lack of focus prevented him from executing on his primary goal of building AR, and closes with a personal engagement announcement.
quotes.key_insight: A software business built on durable, automated systems like SEO and email marketing can sustain itself with almost no active work, but growing beyond that plateau requires the focus and discipline that consulting income and life logistics constantly threaten to consume.

quotes.quote: SEO, AdWords, email marketing, and the usual scalable marketing stuff continued to be my bread and butter even when I was too lazy to actually cut and butter bread.

quotes.quote: My first guesstimate at my rate, back in 2010, was $X. It turns out that I could do just about as much work as I wanted regardless of whether I charged $X, $2X, or $5X.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie's 2011 annual report covers his best year ever, breaking $100,000 in total sales across Bingo Card Creator, consulting, and Appointment Reminder. BCC was in maintenance mode for most of the year but remained roughly flat at ~$23k profit. Consulting generated $55k in revenue but consumed far more time than expected due to prospecting overhead and travel. Appointment Reminder grew despite neglect, with McKenzie weighing whether to take outside investment or grow organically. He candidly discusses how disorganization and lack of focus prevented him from executing on his primary goal of building AR, and closes with a personal engagement announcement.

quotes.quote: Twilio turns every phone into a smartphone.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares hard-won lessons from running Appointment Reminder, a production Twilio application, covering testing, security, deployment, and architecture. He opens with a passionate case for Twilio as transformative technology that turns every phone into a smartphone, then pivots to the painful realities of building production telephony apps. The core of the presentation addresses a catastrophic incident where a queue failure caused 600 phone calls, 400 SMSes, and 200 emails to blast 15 people in five minutes. He advocates modeling phone calls as state machines, using staging servers, implementing strict security controls, and designing systems to fail closed rather than fail open.
quotes.key_insight: Production telephony applications require defensive engineering far beyond typical web apps because bugs don't just show error pages — they blast real people's phones at 3 AM.

quotes.quote: The end-game for Zynga's take-over-the-world vision is the human race slaved to artificial dopamine treadmills. The endgame for Twilio's vision is that every $2 handset in Africa is the moral equivalent of an iPhone.

quotes.quote: If there are 10 people in a fight and only one of them understands how to use science, I already know who's going to win.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Keith Perhac discuss their experiences working in tech in Japan, covering the stark differences in how programmers are valued compared to the US, the broken social contract of lifetime employment, and Japan's lagging adoption of modern web practices. They pivot to evangelizing A/B testing as a transformative business practice, sharing concrete examples of massive conversion lifts from simple changes like headlines and button design. The core argument is that A/B testing is criminally underused despite being nearly free to implement, and that the skill of conversion optimization is far more valuable than most engineering work. They also discuss how job titles matter enormously — calling yourself a 'system engineer' instead of 'programmer' can double your perceived value.
quotes.key_insight: A/B testing is so cheap and so powerful that failing to do it continuously is one of the largest unforced errors a business can make, yet most companies — even sophisticated ones — leave enormous gains on the table.

quotes.quote: You want to not look like an ad at the first glance, but to look like an ad on the second glance.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his experiment with image ad blending on BuySellAds, where he designed a banner ad for Bingo Card Creator that visually matches the host site's content style. The technique exploits banner blindness by making ads look like native content rather than traditional advertisements. He placed a $135/month ad on BusyTeacher.org targeting teachers and achieved a 2.2% click-through rate, far above banner advertising norms. The cost per acquisition dropped to roughly 8 cents versus 30-40 cents on Google AdWords, making the economics dramatically better. He reflects on the ethics of the approach and concludes it's aggressive but honest. He wishes more inventory were available in his niche and plans to pursue direct ad buys with smaller sites.
quotes.key_insight: Designing image ads that blend with a site's visual style defeats banner blindness and can dramatically reduce customer acquisition costs compared to standard ad networks.

quotes.quote: If you're not technically inclined, you think 'Hmm, ghosts in the Googles. Oh well, here is my username and password again.'
quotes.summary: McKenzie walks through debugging an extremely subtle bug in Bingo Card Creator where a Rails security upgrade silently changed CSRF protection behavior from throwing exceptions to quietly resetting sessions. This interacted with his A/B testing library's humanity-check AJAX POST, creating a race condition that sporadically logged users out and wiped referrer tracking data. The bug manifested differently in development vs. production due to timing differences in JavaScript execution, making it nearly impossible to reproduce consistently. He traces his debugging journey from noticing anomalous Halloween sales stats to spelunking through Rails source code across version changes, ultimately finding a one-line fix. The post serves as a meditation on how modern web applications sit atop layers of complexity that are usually invisible until they break in unexpected ways.
quotes.key_insight: Silent behavioral changes in framework security patches can create race conditions that are nearly impossible to reproduce in development, causing subtle but widespread damage that only surfaces through anomalous analytics data.

He traces his debugging journey from noticing anomalous Halloween sales stats to spelunking through Rails source code across version changes, ultimately finding a one-line fix. The post serves as a meditation on how modern web applications sit atop layers of complexity that are usually invisible until they break in unexpected ways.
quotes.key_insight: Silent behavioral changes in framework security patches can create race conditions that are nearly impossible to reproduce in development, causing subtle but widespread damage that only surfaces through anomalous analytics data.

quotes.quote: Bingo Card Creator is not terribly complicated software when compared to most applications, but it sits on top of other pieces of code which collectively are orders of magnitude more complicated than any physical artifact ever created by the human race.

quotes.quote: If you call yourself a programmer, someone is already working on a way to get you fired.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that engineers fundamentally misunderstand how their careers work because educational institutions fail to teach business realities. The core thesis is that engineers are hired to create business value—increasing revenue or reducing costs—not to write code, and they should position themselves accordingly. He covers salary negotiation, networking, the irrelevance of academic credentials, the overvaluation of startup equity, and the critical importance of communication skills. The post systematically dismantles engineer-culture shibboleths like technical stack identity, modesty, and the belief that merit speaks for itself. McKenzie contends that framing yourself as a business-value creator rather than a programmer is the single most important career move an engineer can make.
quotes.key_insight: Engineers are not hired to write code—they are hired to create business value, and the most successful ones learn to describe themselves and their work in those terms rather than in technical jargon.

quotes.quote: It does not matter to the company that the reporting form is the world's simplest CRUD app, it only matters that it either saves the company costs or generates additional revenue.

quotes.quote: The world isn't a meritocracy.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on how running his own business fundamentally changed his psychology, decision-making, and self-confidence. He traces his journey from being a praise-seeking student to an unhappy Japanese salaryman to a thriving independent software business owner. The post argues that career choices exist in a vast multi-dimensional preference space, not on simple linear trajectories, and that most constraints we accept about work are negotiable social conventions rather than laws of nature. He shares how business ownership taught him to value his time, charge appropriately for consulting, negotiate confidently with anyone, and ultimately optimize for happiness. He concludes by explaining why he turned down the idea of taking investment, recognizing it would move him away from what actually makes him happy.
quotes.key_insight: Running a business reveals that most career constraints are negotiable social conventions rather than fixed rules, and the key skill is knowing what actually makes you happy and having the confidence to optimize for it.

quotes.quote: The way we work is, essentially, arbitrary. 40 hour work weeks for middling-good salaries are no more a law of nature than 90 hour workweeks for middling-poor salaries.

quotes.quote: Most engineers severely undercharge for their products.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie recounts his journey from underemployed technical translator in rural Japan to running a profitable software business (Bingo Card Creator) in just 5 hours per week. He details how he leveraged content marketing, Google AdWords Conversion Optimizer, and systematic optimization to grow a hobby project into a real business. The presentation covers his transition from salaryman to full-time entrepreneur, supplemented by consulting income. The tactical half offers five actionable strategies: charge more, build web apps, make your work publicly visible, master SEO, and optimize everything through A/B testing. He emphasizes that multiplicative improvements across the funnel compound dramatically over time.
quotes.key_insight: A solo software business can be grown to substantial revenue in just 5 hours per week by systematically applying content marketing, conversion optimization, and automation — treating every improvement as a multiplier rather than an addition.

quotes.quote: Almost all SaaS firms find that they make huge portions of their revenue from the topmost plan which is bought by people spending other people's money, but instead of optimizing for this we optimize for charging 'fair' prices as determined by other software developers who won't pay for the service anyway.

quotes.quote: Every business is an iceberg: of the value created by the business, much of it is hidden within the company or (at best) exposed to existing customers, and only a small portion peaks above the waterline, outside of the existing community around the business.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces he will be speaking at MicroConf, a conference for small software businesses organized by Rob Walling in Las Vegas. He offers a promo code and a free ticket giveaway for someone with an existing small software business. He previews his talk concept: an iceberg metaphor about how businesses hide most of their value below the waterline, and how marketing and SEO are about maximizing the visible portion. He emphasizes his eagerness to personally connect with attendees and give advice about their software businesses.
quotes.key_insight: Most businesses fail to surface the value they create — marketing is the discipline of making more of that value visible to people outside your existing community.

quotes.quote: Both traditional marketing and SEO revolve around maximizing the visible bit of the iceberg.

quotes.quote: Picture me in a freezing, pitch black apartment at 4 AM in the morning crying in between calls to apoplectic customers of customers.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a six-month update on Appointment Reminder, his second SaaS product. Despite slow customer acquisition (~50 trials, ~10 paying), unit economics are strong with costs well below revenue per customer. He recounts a harrowing production incident where a queue failure led to hundreds of duplicate calls blasting customers' phones. He also discusses discovering the white-label sales channel, learning painful lessons about high-touch B2B sales, and deciding to drop his lowest pricing tier after realizing some prospects aren't worth pursuing.
quotes.key_insight: Building a SaaS business with strong unit economics is the easy part; the hard parts are incident response discipline, learning high-touch sales processes, and having the judgment to disqualify prospects who will never be profitable customers.

quotes.quote: A business is defined both by what it does and what it does not do.

quotes.quote: Failure to deliver reminders in a timely fashion is not nearly the worst possible failure case.

quotes.quote: Quite a lot of the reporting on Japan is the equivalent of someone ringing up Mayor Daley during Katrina and saying 'My God man, that's terrible — how are you coping?'
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie, a software business owner living in central Japan, wrote this post to counter sensationalized English-language reporting about the 2011 Tohoku earthquake and tsunami. He emphasizes that Honshu is enormous — comparable to the distance from Chicago to New Orleans — and that Tokyo suffered negligible damage despite dominating coverage. He details Japan's extraordinary disaster preparedness systems, from university emergency notifications activated within 90 seconds to trains stopping safely at 150+ mph. While acknowledging the devastating loss of life from drowning, he argues the earthquake was a triumph of engineering: buildings stood, trains stopped, factories didn't explode, and nuclear plants performed largely as designed. He closes with practical advice against well-meaning but counterproductive foreign volunteering.
quotes.key_insight: The 2011 Japan earthquake was not a story of lucky survival but of decades of meticulous engineering, preparation, and procedural discipline that converted what could have been a civilization-ending catastrophe into a serious but survivable disaster.

quotes.quote: We're landlocked so, unless the mountains fall into the sea, tsunamis are not an issue for us.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie posts a brief personal update following the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami in Japan. He confirms he and those close to him are safe in Gifu, far from the epicenter. He thanks readers for their concern and redirects charitable impulses toward disaster relief organizations, noting that future disasters will likely strike less-prepared countries than Japan.
quotes.key_insight: Disaster relief donations have their greatest impact when directed toward future incidents in less-prepared countries, not necessarily the headline event.

quotes.quote: Bingo Card Creator is not a form of gambling — it is a form of software which helps elementary schoolkids learn to read.
quotes.summary: McKenzie details his ongoing frustration with Google AdWords' ad approval process, which flags his educational bingo software as gambling due to keyword-based blacklists. The review process introduces unbounded delays—ads can sit 'Under Review' for weeks—and the only reliable workaround is filing support tickets that trigger automated brush-off responses. He argues the system punishes compliant advertisers while being trivially circumvented by bad actors. Despite paying Google $10,000 annually, he finds their customer service essentially non-existent, and the lack of viable competitive alternatives in CPC advertising leaves him with no leverage.
quotes.key_insight: Google's ad approval system creates a Kafkaesque experience where compliant advertisers are punished by processes designed for bad actors, while the lack of competition means customers have no recourse except public shaming.

quotes.quote: Customer acquisition is one of the easiest places to screw up as a startup, particularly for technical founders (who, in their previous lives, have probably never had to do it for anything).
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that startups should treat customer acquisition as a hackable system, not just focus on building better products. He observes that incumbents are optimized for existing adoption cycles but often can't adapt, creating openings for startups to innovate on how they sell rather than just what they sell. Using examples like freemium models, monthly billing, and SEO expertise, he shows how changing the acquisition method can be as disruptive as changing the product itself. He advocates for treating marketing strategy with the same rigor as product development, iterating and measuring systematically.
quotes.key_insight: Startups can outmaneuver incumbents not by building better products but by hacking the customer acquisition cycle itself — innovating on how you sell is often more important than what you sell.

quotes.quote: We have the Blue Book to tell you what a ten year old beater is worth down to the dollar, there are entire industries devoted to assessing every type of security to determine their valuation, and the closest thing most students have to insight on the degree selection process is getting advice from Aunt Sue.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes a data project he built to quantify the financial value of different college degrees by major. He combined Bureau of Labor Statistics salary data with Mechanical Turk crowd-sourcing to map occupations to degrees, then calculated net present value estimates for each major. The project was commissioned by an online education publisher as lead-generation content. He argues that the lack of transparent salary-by-major data from colleges is a deliberate information asymmetry that serves institutional interests, and that technical degrees perform very well while humanities and caring professions perform poorly.
quotes.key_insight: Colleges maintain deliberate information asymmetry about salary outcomes by major because transparent data would undermine their ability to price all degrees identically, and the government data that could fill this gap requires significant technical effort to make actionable.

quotes.quote: No college will actually do this because transparency goes directly against their interests: if all degrees from a particular institution are valued at 'An uncertain, but certainly large and roughly constant number', then the standard practice of pricing them all identically makes sense.

quotes.quote: Self-employment is awesome. There are occasionally challenges to it, but since I spent a few years dipping my toes into the water prior to doing it full-time, I have had very little of the 'Uh oh, I might not be able to make rent' uncertainty that a lot of folks report.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews his 2010 business year, marking his transition from Japanese salaryman to full-time self-employment as the best decision he ever made. Bingo Card Creator grew 38% in sales to $42,590, driven by deprecating the desktop version and leveraging email marketing, while requiring minimal maintenance. He launched a consulting practice earning $45,000 from three clients, though juggling consulting with product work proved far harder than expected. Appointment Reminder finally launched in December after six months of delay, already attracting an acquisition offer he declined. His combined businesses generated roughly $60,000 in profit, validating his leap to independence.
quotes.key_insight: Transitioning from employment to self-employment succeeds best when you spend years building revenue streams gradually, but the real challenge isn't making money — it's managing your attention across consulting, product maintenance, and new product development without letting any single activity crowd out the others.

quotes.quote: I went 'full time' and work less than ever. This is mostly because BCC is mature software.

quotes.quote: Prior to doing so, my workflow generally involved a whole lot of bubble gum, duct tape, and praying.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that essential engineering practices like staging servers, version control workflows, and automated deployments are rarely taught but dramatically improve software quality. Drawing from his experience at a Japanese megacorporation, he provides practical guidance for small teams to implement staging environments that mirror production, git branching strategies that decouple development from deployment, and deploy scripts that include sanity checks and rollback capabilities. He emphasizes that these practices should be documented, scripted, and reproducible rather than cobbled together manually. The post advocates for incremental improvement rather than perfection, noting that every step along the path yields clear benefits.
quotes.key_insight: Professional engineering practices like staging servers, version control workflows, and automated deployments aren't luxuries for big companies — they're essential infrastructure that small teams can and should implement incrementally, with each step yielding immediate benefits.

quotes.quote: Setting up a staging server should be easy. If it is not easy, you already have a problem in your infrastructure, you just don't know it yet.

quotes.quote: If I manage to bring my Slice to its knees, it will be because my revenue has hit several million dollars a month. That will give me many attractive scalability options, such as hiring someone to care about scalability while I sip chilled juices on the beach of a tropical island.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie documents the progress of building Appointment Reminder, his SaaS product for reducing no-show appointments. He covers his Rails deployment stack choices (VPS over Heroku, pragmatic technology selections), reports 70% code completion with core SMS and phone reminder loops working via Twilio. He describes outsourcing wins including Fiverr for voice recordings and a virtual assistant for bookkeeping, and shares critical customer discovery insights from phone conversations that revealed a broader market than expected, particularly service providers who travel to customers.
quotes.key_insight: Talking to actual customers reveals market segments with far more acute pain than you imagined, and the intensity of that pain directly determines your pricing power.

quotes.quote: While my life is firing on all cylinders, happiness does not write jQuery or Rails code.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents the early development process for Appointment Reminder, his SaaS product for automating appointment reminders via phone, text, and email. After six months of procrastination following the MVP, he set an arbitrary end-of-November deadline and broke the work into half-day mini-quests to maintain momentum. He walks through his technology choices (Rails, jQuery, SASS/HAML), his ruthless feature scoping process where he cut anything not needed on launch day, and his incremental coding approach of getting visible progress quickly. The post serves as a real-time case study in how a solo developer ships a product by cutting scope aggressively and maintaining forward momentum.
quotes.key_insight: Shipping a product as a solo developer requires ruthless scope cutting — if a feature won't be needed on launch day, it's out of scope, and momentum comes from breaking work into small units of visible, measurable progress.

quotes.quote: Deadlines tend to coerce me into doing my best work. Specifically, deadlines with subgoals that have small units of measurable accomplishment work best for me.

quotes.quote: Big mistake: my customers empirically do not feel the same way.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie recaps his best-ever October for Bingo Card Creator, hitting $6,024 in monthly sales and crossing $100,000 in lifetime revenue. He details a multi-pronged Halloween promotion strategy: an SEO mini-site that drove underwhelming conversions, email marketing that delivered extraordinary 1,000% ROI on a $32 spend, and an A/B tested discount that surprisingly failed to move the needle for new customers. A critical SSL migration mistake during the month caused AdWords conversion tracking to break, costing thousands in wasted ad spend and disrupted campaign optimization. Despite the self-inflicted wounds, he views the month as a major success and plans to systematize seasonal promotions for future holidays.
quotes.key_insight: Email marketing to an existing customer list can deliver orders-of-magnitude better ROI than SEO or paid acquisition, but only when the list is fresh and the offer is time-pressured against the actual buying window.

quotes.quote: Very little I have done has scalable 1,000% ROIs on marketing spends.

He details a multi-pronged Halloween promotion strategy: an SEO mini-site that drove underwhelming conversions, email marketing that delivered extraordinary 1,000% ROI on a $32 spend, and an A/B tested discount that surprisingly failed to move the needle for new customers. A critical SSL migration mistake during the month caused AdWords conversion tracking to break, costing thousands in wasted ad spend and disrupted campaign optimization. Despite the self-inflicted wounds, he views the month as a major success and plans to systematize seasonal promotions for future holidays.
quotes.key_insight: Email marketing to an existing customer list can deliver orders-of-magnitude better ROI than SEO or paid acquisition, but only when the list is fresh and the offer is time-pressured against the actual buying window.

quotes.quote: Implementing SSL in Rails was very painful and resulted in my site being down or unusable for a large portion of my customers for much of the day.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie documents his painful experience implementing SSL encryption for his Rails application, prompted by the release of FireSheep, a Firefox addon that trivially compromises web session cookies on shared wireless networks. He walks through the technical steps: configuring Nginx for dual HTTP/HTTPS, scrubbing absolute URLs, using Rails plugins for SSL requirements, and dealing with IE-specific download bugs. The process caused significant downtime and he admits he should have used a staging environment first. He urges other Rails developers to test FireSheep against their own admin sessions and implement SSL immediately.
quotes.key_insight: SSL implementation is far more complex than flipping a switch — it requires coordinating server configuration, asset hosting, session security, and browser-specific workarounds, but the alternative is leaving every user session open to trivial hijacking.

quotes.quote: My theory was that my customer's didn't store anything security critical in their elementary school bingo card games, but the increasing amount of information available to the admin plus a customer support story from this morning convinced me that compromise would be a Very Bad Thing.

quotes.quote: Ideas that spread, win.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie recounts his experience at the Business of Software 2010 conference, synthesizing lessons from speakers including Seth Godin, Dharmesh Shah, Eric Ries, Scott Farquhar, Jason Cohen, Paul Kenny, Rob Walling, Peldi from Balsamiq, Eric Sink, Dan Bricklin, Derek Sivers, and Joel Spolsky. The talks covered themes from tribal marketing and customer happiness metrics to acquisition mechanics and the ethical responsibilities of founders. McKenzie weaves his own insights throughout, particularly around A/B testing, charging more, and the compounding nature of career experience. Joel Spolsky's closing argument — that founders have ethical obligations in how they deploy the leverage their businesses create — left a lasting impression on McKenzie, challenging his view that keeping a company small is a morally neutral lifestyle choice.
quotes.key_insight: The most important assets in a software business are no longer technical — they are community, trust, and the ethical choices founders make about how to deploy the leverage their businesses create.

quotes.quote: The bottom line is currently there is nothing that you cannot do to someone's Diaspora account, absolutely nothing.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie dissects critical security vulnerabilities he discovered in Diaspora's source code upon its pre-alpha release, detailing three classes of flaws: missing authorization checks, mass assignment vulnerabilities, and NoSQL injection. He demonstrates how each flaw could be trivially exploited to gain full control of any user's account, overwrite encryption keys, or extract private data from the database. McKenzie argues that releasing the code publicly was irresponsible because real users inevitably signed up to public nodes despite alpha disclaimers. He concludes that the Diaspora team lacks the security expertise to ship safely and that their codebase remains vulnerable even after patches.
quotes.key_insight: Security is not a feature you sprinkle on top — authentication without authorization, mass assignment defaults, and string interpolation in database queries are fundamental architectural failures that no amount of encryption can compensate for.

quotes.quote: This is what kills most encryption systems in real life. You don't have to beat encryption to beat the system, you just have to beat the weakest link in the chain around it.

quotes.quote: If you obsess over your YSlow and/or Page Speed performance scores, you might be wondering how to get your Rails app to automatically crunch Javascripts and CSS files without actually requiring cumbersome rake tasks to be repeated during every deploy.
quotes.summary: A brief technical tip on how to automatically minimize JavaScript and CSS files in a Rails application. McKenzie points readers to a monkeypatch approach that integrates asset minimization into the normal Rails asset pipeline without requiring manual rake tasks during deployment. The post is essentially a four-step guide linking to external resources for the implementation details.
quotes.key_insight: Asset minimization in Rails can be automated via a simple monkeypatch rather than requiring manual build steps during deployment.

quotes.quote: When I started this blog four years ago, I never thought it would be seen by more than a few dozen people, so I never planned any sort of information architecture to it.
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces a curated index of his best 72 blog posts, organized by category. After four years and 500 posts totaling 300,000 words, he recognized the blog had become unwieldy for new readers. With help from a Hacker News volunteer, he grouped his best work by topic and linked related posts together, particularly experiment/results pairs. The post serves as a brief meta-announcement directing readers to his new 'greatest hits' page.
quotes.key_insight: A prolific blog eventually needs curated information architecture to remain accessible to new readers, and the effort to organize your best work is itself a valuable marketing investment.

quotes.quote: 500 posts (300,000 words!) and several hundred thousand readers later, it is unwieldy to get to the good stuff unless you have been following along for the last couple of years, or have a week free.

quotes.quote: If you haven't figured out the significance of it originally being written in Financier instead of in Geek, please, think it through.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie translates Paul Graham's essay on emerging trends in startup angel investing from 'Financier into Geek.' He explains how convertible notes with rolling closes are replacing traditional fixed-valuation equity rounds, walking through valuations, discount mechanisms, and valuation caps with simple numerical examples. The key innovation is that early-committing angels get better terms, breaking the deadlock where investors all wait for social proof before investing. McKenzie notes this shifts the primary negotiation point from valuation to cap size, allowing founders to offer different 'price points' to different angels.
quotes.key_insight: Convertible notes with rolling closes and tiered valuation caps break the social-proof deadlock in angel investing by rewarding early-committing investors with better terms, shifting the negotiation from valuation to cap size.

quotes.quote: They exist like the Greek gods: you didn't ask for them but they are there, there is no negotiating with them, and prolonged association means you are likely to get your dignity violated by a bovine.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on the psychological difficulty of adjusting to self-employment after a lifetime of externally imposed schedules. Despite having complete freedom over his time, he finds the lack of structure disorienting and struggles with the feeling that he isn't being productive enough. Paradoxically, data shows he's actually getting more done than ever—running more A/B tests and increasing sales. He attributes the disconnect to decades of measuring productivity by hours worked rather than output. He explores hacks like getting an office for mental separation and using a gamification app to maintain discipline.
quotes.key_insight: The hardest part of self-employment isn't doing the work—it's the psychological disorientation of losing externally imposed structure, which can make you feel unproductive even when data proves you're accomplishing more than ever.

quotes.quote: Gainfully employed young men should be working at 2 PM on a Wednesday. I was having a late lunch and reading a novel at the coffee shop.

quotes.quote: That's the thing about schedules: once you have one everyone else needs to, too, preferably as close to yours as possible.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on the psychological difficulty of adjusting to self-employment after a lifetime of externally imposed schedules. Despite having complete freedom over his time, he finds the lack of structure disorienting and struggles with the feeling that he isn't being productive enough. Paradoxically, data shows he's actually getting more done than ever—running more A/B tests and increasing sales. He attributes the disconnect to decades of measuring productivity by hours worked rather than output. He explores hacks like getting an office for mental separation and using a gamification app to maintain discipline.

quotes.quote: Market seasonality has non-trivial impact on many software businesses.
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains how market seasonality significantly impacts software businesses, using his own Bingo Card Creator's dramatic sales swings as a case study. He advocates measuring year-over-year rather than month-over-month to avoid mistaking seasonal dips for declining traction. The post covers practical strategies including cash flow management during off-seasons, using slow periods for major engineering changes, and preparing marketing campaigns months before peak seasons. He also demonstrates how the same seasonal data can be presented to investors as either stagnation or growth depending on framing.
quotes.key_insight: Seasonal businesses should measure year-over-year, use off-seasons for risky engineering and new projects, prepare marketing months before peaks, and carefully frame seasonal data when communicating with stakeholders.

quotes.quote: If Thomas and I were bigwigs at a multinational firm with entire departments devoted to preventing anything from getting accomplished, that disagreement might have been settled by a great deal of harumphing. However, we're engineers, so we made a wager.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes an A/B test between his custom logo and a 99designs off-the-rack logo, sparked by a friendly wager with Thomas Ptacek of Matasano. The test ran sitewide for two weeks with 17,000 real users, and his original custom logo won decisively with 11.05% vs 9.92% conversion to free trial, statistically significant at 99% confidence. He argues this demonstrates that logos can potentially add or subtract 10% from a software company's enterprise value. The post emphasizes that A/B testing should be cheap and easy to run, requiring minimal code changes, and that most software companies never bother to test something with such significant business impact.
quotes.key_insight: Most software companies treat their logo as a one-time decision, but A/B testing shows logos can swing conversion rates by 10% — making cheap, low-friction testing infrastructure one of the highest-leverage investments a small software business can make.

quotes.quote: I can't possibly stress enough how valuable it is to have your A/B tests require only a single line of code. You'll test more frequently, you'll test more diligently, and you'll discover more valuable things about your business.

quotes.quote: People treat SEO like it is black magic, but at the core it is very simple: Content + Links = You Win.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie presents an advanced SEO framework for software companies, arguing that the core equation is simple: Content + Links = Success. He advocates treating company websites as first-class software products deserving continuous improvement, and demonstrates how scalable content generation through CMS systems, outsourced writing, and automation can turn SEO into a predictable revenue channel. Using his own Bingo Card Creator as a case study, he shows how paying a freelancer $3,000 over several years generated over $20,000 in sales in one year alone. He also covers linkbait strategies, particularly open source software as a uniquely effective link-building tool for developers.
quotes.key_insight: Software companies should treat SEO as a scalable, automatable revenue channel by combining systematic content generation with strategic link-building, turning small investments in outsourced content into compounding long-term returns.

quotes.quote: The site dies nearly every time I get Reddited — with only about 10,000 visitors each time, which shouldn't be a big number on the Internet.
quotes.summary: After Patrick's viral 'Falsehoods Programmers Believe About Names' post brought 100,000 visitors and crashed his server, he diagnosed the root cause as Apache's KeepAlive setting. With KeepAlive enabled at 3 seconds, each of his 20 worker processes spent most of its time idle waiting for follow-up requests, reducing effective throughput from 60,000 to roughly 380 requests per minute. Turning KeepAlive off immediately fixed the problem. He documents the debugging journey and shares specific Apache configuration recommendations for memory-constrained VPS environments.
quotes.key_insight: Apache's KeepAlive setting, a sensible default for normal traffic, can reduce server throughput by 99% during traffic spikes on memory-constrained VPS instances by holding worker processes idle waiting for follow-up requests that never come.

quotes.quote: If you're running a memory constrained VPS and get hit by a huge wave of traffic, KeepAlive will kill you.

quotes.quote: Anything someone tells you is their name is — by definition — an appropriate identifier for them.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie catalogs 40 false assumptions that programmers routinely make when designing systems that handle personal names. Drawing on his experience living in Japan and working with global enterprise systems, he argues that no computer system he has ever encountered handles names correctly. The post systematically demolishes assumptions ranging from names being ASCII-compatible to people even having names at all. McKenzie frames proper name handling as both a technical challenge and a matter of identity and respect. The piece became one of the most widely referenced posts in software engineering, spawning an entire genre of 'falsehoods programmers believe' articles.
quotes.key_insight: Names are so culturally diverse and personally fluid that virtually every structural assumption a programmer might encode into a system will be wrong for some real person, and getting names wrong means getting identity wrong.

quotes.quote: I have never seen a computer system which handles names properly and doubt one exists, anywhere.

quotes.key_insight: Names are so culturally diverse and personally fluid that virtually every structural assumption a programmer might encode into a system will be wrong for some real person, and getting names wrong means getting identity wrong.

quotes.quote: I have lived in Japan for several years, programming in a professional capacity, and I have broken many systems by the simple expedient of being introduced into them.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie catalogs 40 false assumptions that programmers routinely make when designing systems that handle personal names. Drawing on his experience living in Japan and working with global enterprise systems, he argues that no computer system he has ever encountered handles names correctly. The post systematically demolishes assumptions ranging from names being ASCII-compatible to people even having names at all. McKenzie frames proper name handling as both a technical challenge and a matter of identity and respect. quotes.quote: I love when the market gives me exactly what I want.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers that his earlier intuition about bots not affecting A/B test results was mathematically wrong. Through algebraic analysis, he shows that bot traffic artificially depresses z-scores, potentially turning statistically significant results into null results. He implements a JavaScript-based bot detection system for his open source A/B testing library A/Bingo, requiring clients to perform arithmetic via AJAX POST requests — something resource-intensive for bots but trivial for browsers. The system retroactively scores participation when a visitor first proves human, integrating cleanly with existing test tracking.
quotes.key_insight: Bot traffic doesn't harmlessly wash out of A/B tests — it mathematically depresses z-scores, and JavaScript execution challenges are an effective, low-friction way to filter bots without burdening real users.

quotes.quote: Bots do bork statistical significance testing, by artificially depressing z scores and thus turning statistically significant results into null results at the margin.

quotes.quote: Essentially, I'm A/B testing dropping a profitable product which has a modest bit of traction and thousands of paying customers.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his most ambitious A/B test: removing Bingo Card Creator's downloadable desktop application entirely and funneling all users to the web version. After finding that 78% of sales come from web app users while the desktop version generates 10x more support emails, he built an A/B test where half of visitors see a site where the download never existed. He expects a 25% sales increase by converting download users to web signups, which convert at twice the rate. The test was carefully designed to not disrupt existing customers, with override mechanisms and strategic summer timing.
quotes.key_insight: When a legacy product generates disproportionate support costs and lower conversion rates than its replacement, A/B testing its complete removal can reveal that killing it actually grows the business.

quotes.quote: I get literally ten support emails about the downloadable application for every one I get about the web application, and one of the first things I suggest to customers is 'Try using the web version, it will magically fix that.'

quotes.quote: Two clients not at their appointments means two massage therapists not seeing anyone, and that costs them $2 a minute in direct economic losses.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie unveils his second product, Appointment Reminder, a web application that automates phone, SMS, and email reminders for service businesses with appointment no-show problems. He identifies a massive underserved market of small service providers (hair salons, massage therapists, tutors) who can't afford the $300+/month enterprise healthcare-focused solutions. Using Twilio's API infrastructure and his B2C marketing skills, he plans to offer the service at $9-$79/month, deliberately pricing to enable the pitch that it pays for itself after preventing one no-show. Following Lean Startup methodology, he built only a demo as his MVP rather than a complete product, reasoning that making someone's phone ring from a web interface is the key selling moment. He plans to leverage SEO and AdWords while avoiding enterprise sales cycles.
quotes.key_insight: You can validate a SaaS product by building only the demo that sells it — if the magic moment is making a phone ring from a web browser, everything after that is out of scope for your MVP.

quotes.quote: Given that it was about 2 in the morning when I recorded it due to time zone differences, sometimes my ability to speak in coherent sentences leaves a bit to be desired.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces his interview on Mixergy with Andrew Warner is now live after being rescheduled due to technical difficulties. The interview covers his experience running Bingo Card Creator while working full-time as a Japanese salaryman, his early traction strategies, and hints at an upcoming new product. He notes the interview was recorded at 2 AM due to time zone differences between Japan and the US, which affected his coherence at times.
quotes.key_insight: Building a software business while working full-time in Japan meant navigating not just limited hours but practical challenges like recording interviews at 2 AM due to time zone differences.

quotes.quote: Everything he says about customer service, building remarkable products, early marketing, and competition just knocks it out of the park.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recommends a Mixergy interview with Peldi Guilizzoni of Balsamiq, praising his insights on customer service, product development, early marketing, and competition. He uses the post to introduce readers to Mixergy, Andrew Warner's interview series with successful internet entrepreneurs. McKenzie highlights several favorite interviews including ones with Wufoo and Kissmetrics founders. He also mentions that his own Mixergy interview is forthcoming.
quotes.key_insight: Curated interviews with successful software entrepreneurs are a high-value resource for micro-ISV founders seeking actionable marketing and product advice.

quotes.quote: Most of them are inspiring, and many have killer, actionable tips that you can use in your businesses.

quotes.quote: The person who signs up for Dropbox through your referral link gets a better deal than they would have gotten from the homepage, and you also get a free bonus yourself. That is marvelous, marvelous psychology there.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes implementing a Dropbox-style two-sided referral incentive for Bingo Card Creator, where both the inviter and invitee receive bonus bingo cards. He explains why the double-sided incentive is psychologically superior to simple 'tell a friend' schemes, details the surprisingly complex implementation including Facebook sharing integration, and candidly shares a production bug where a database migration accidentally triggered 30,000 MailChimp API calls. The post balances optimism about the approach with honest uncertainty about whether it will work for his non-technical user base.
quotes.key_insight: Two-sided referral incentives transform spam-like 'tell a friend' schemes into genuine gift-giving by rewarding both parties, and the best implementations use rewards with near-zero marginal cost.

quotes.key_insight: Two-sided referral incentives transform spam-like 'tell a friend' schemes into genuine gift-giving by rewarding both parties, and the best implementations use rewards with near-zero marginal cost.

quotes.quote: Software systems are also, overwhelmingly, killed by their moving parts.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive guide for small companies to build reliable web services without dedicated ops teams. He argues that most downtime comes from predictable, preventable causes—especially operator error—rather than black swan events. Drawing from Toyota's manufacturing philosophy, he emphasizes that systems fail at their 'moving parts': dynamic components, databases, and human interventions. The post covers risk identification, failure mitigation through decoupling and automation, monitoring and notification systems, and root cause analysis using Five Whys. His core message is that reliability is achievable for small operators through checklists, automation, decoupling external dependencies, and disciplined post-failure analysis.
quotes.key_insight: Reliability for small companies isn't about preventing every possible failure—it's about identifying your predictable moving parts (especially yourself), decoupling and automating what you can, and building the discipline to learn from every failure that gets through.

quotes.quote: Today I killed a few hours getting my Rails environment working on my brand new shiny 64 bit Windows Seven laptop.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a concise technical guide for setting up a Ruby on Rails development environment on Windows 7 64-bit. The post documents his solution after spending hours stuck in dependency issues, particularly around getting MySQL to work with Ruby on Windows. The key trick is copying a specific DLL file to Ruby's bin directory to resolve a common mysql_api.so loading error. It's a brief, practical troubleshooting post aimed at experienced Rails developers who found themselves on Windows.
quotes.key_insight: A brief troubleshooting guide documenting the non-obvious DLL copy needed to resolve MySQL dependency issues when running Rails on Windows 7.

quotes.quote: CrazyEgg is really one of my favorite secrets of being a metrics junkie, simply because it makes problems look so freaking obvious, when they could be buried if you relied on quantified analysis.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares how CrazyEgg's visual heatmap tool revealed a conversion problem on his AdWords landing page that no amount of quantitative analytics could have surfaced. Users were clicking on non-active sidebar text instead of the intended call-to-action button. The fix was trivial — making the bullet points into hyperlinks — but the insight required visual analysis. He emphasizes that CrazyEgg's value lies in making problems visually obvious rather than requiring deep data drilling.
quotes.key_insight: Visual analytics tools like heatmaps reveal conversion-killing UX problems that no amount of quantitative data drilling can surface, and the fixes are often trivially simple once you can see the problem.

quotes.quote: The key insight about time is that software lets us take the old saying about how 'Everyone gets the same 24 hours per day' and break it open like a pinata.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares practical strategies for running a software business alongside a demanding full-time job as a Japanese salaryman, drawing on four years of experience with Bingo Card Creator. He introduces the framework of 'time assets' versus 'time debt' as the key mental model for part-time entrepreneurs, arguing that building systems, processes, and automation is far more valuable than squeezing more hours out of each day. The post covers scope management, freelancer relationships, avoiding deadlines, documentation, and choosing problems suited to part-time constraints. McKenzie emphasizes that code is only about 10% of a software business, with marketing and operations consuming the rest.
quotes.key_insight: Part-time entrepreneurs should focus on building 'time assets' — reusable systems, processes, and automation — rather than working more hours, because stored time compounds while spent time rots.

quotes.quote: Every line of code you write is time debt: it is another line that has to be debugged, another line that has to be supported, another line that may require a rewrite later.

quotes.quote: I'm quitting my day job as of March 31st.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces he is quitting his day job effective March 31st to go full-time on his software businesses. He describes the mix of exhilaration and unease that comes with the transition. He updates readers on his current activities: running six simultaneous A/B tests on Bingo Card Creator, working with designers on mini-sites and button redesigns, and mentally committing to a new Twilio-based application. The post captures a pivotal moment in his transition from side-project entrepreneur to full-time independent software developer.
quotes.key_insight: The transition from side-project to full-time entrepreneurship is built on a foundation of relentless incremental optimization — testing your way to victory one small lift at a time.

quotes.quote: The bug is all of one character long.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discloses a significant one-character bug in his open-source A/B testing framework A/Bingo that affected z-score calculations. The bug could potentially change whether test results were reported as statistically significant. He fixed it remotely from a Japanese cafe while stuck in Nagoya and pushed the fix to the repository. After re-running confidence tests on all his historical data, no experimental results actually changed. He commits to writing unit tests for the statistics code to prevent similar issues.
quotes.key_insight: A one-character bug in statistical code can potentially invalidate A/B test results, underscoring why statistical calculations need unit tests even when the rest of your framework works correctly.

quotes.quote: There is a key point about A/B testing: trust your data, not somebody else's data.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his experience testing the trendy 'madlibs-style' signup form that swept the internet after a viral case study. Rather than blindly adopting the design fad, he ran his own A/B test on Bingo Card Creator's registration form. The madlibs style resulted in a 22% decrease in conversion rates compared to the standard form, significant at the 95% confidence level. He emphasizes that other people's successful experiments should inspire you to run your own tests, not to copy their results wholesale. The original viral test changed seven variables simultaneously, making it impossible to attribute the improvement to the madlibs format specifically.
quotes.key_insight: Other people's A/B test results should inspire you to run your own tests, not to blindly copy their implementations — context matters more than creativity.

quotes.quote: True diversity, of course, is the 5-member iStockPhoto of attractive twenty-somethings sitting on the college quad who check different boxes on the demographic inventory and think alike in every way that matters.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that Silicon Valley's diversity problem is really a symptom of deeper structural pathologies: the startup model itself is a terrible value proposition for most intelligent people regardless of gender. Rather than focusing on demographic headcounts, the Valley should fix its broken fundamentals — overwork culture, VC dependency, geographic concentration, and building products nobody pays for. These fixes would naturally make startups more attractive to a broader population, including women, while also producing better businesses.
quotes.key_insight: Silicon Valley's gender imbalance is a symptom of a fundamentally broken startup model that rational people of any gender should reject — fix the model and the demographics will follow.

quotes.quote: A quick check of the server showed that this was indeed the case. Six thousand. Oof.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents a production outage where his Delayed::Job workers died during an Ubuntu server upgrade, leaving 60 users unable to print bingo cards for over 12 hours. He walks through his incident response: personally apologizing to affected paying customers, building a targeted in-app messaging system to notify trial users without spamming everyone, and conducting a Toyota Five Whys root cause analysis. The investigation reveals cascading failures in his processes — skipping checklist steps due to fatigue, incomplete monitoring, commented-out process management, and poor documentation. He credits his Japanese engineering day job for teaching him the discipline of written deploy procedures, checklists, and post-incident analysis.
quotes.key_insight: Rigorous operational discipline — checklists, monitoring, documentation, and structured post-incident analysis — is not bureaucratic overhead but a net time-savings over the emergency response and customer apologies that inevitably follow its absence.

quotes.quote: The 5,000 or so people seeing A/B tests on my site on Valentine's Day are almost certainly less than 1% of the beneficiaries of the software now.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces the official 1.0.0 release of A/Bingo, his open-source Rails A/B testing framework. The release includes several community-requested features: a default dashboard, the ability to stop experiments, named conversions, Redis support, and a proper test suite. He establishes a versioning policy for the growing user base and closes with his characteristic evangelism for A/B testing as an essential business practice.
quotes.key_insight: Open-source developer tools succeed when they shift from solving the author's problems to systematically addressing community friction points like documentation, dashboards, and testing expectations.

quotes.quote: They're seriously my favorite $19 to pay every month, even when I don't actually use them, because some day I know I'll get the itch again and then bam actionable insights into what my customers are doing.
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains how to use CrazyEgg's heatmap tracking on pages that require authentication, which normally blocks CrazyEgg's screenshot bot. The solution involves detecting CrazyEgg's user-agent string and bypassing the login procedure for their bot. He created a dedicated trial account for CrazyEgg's bot to authenticate as, enabling heatmap data collection on logged-in application pages. He acknowledges this approach has security implications but argues it's proportionate to his threat model.
quotes.key_insight: You can extend analytics tools like CrazyEgg behind login screens by detecting their bot's user-agent and auto-authenticating it as a dedicated account, calibrating security tradeoffs to your actual threat model.

quotes.quote: If your metrics aren't giving you actionable insights to make decisions that matter to your business, well, you might as well go play WoW for all the good you're doing.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes the custom dashboard he built for his Bingo Card Creator business, explaining how it consolidates customer support, sales tracking, A/B test results, and system health monitoring into a single view. The dashboard's design philosophy centers on minimizing repetitive work and surfacing actionable information rather than vanity metrics. He details specific features like the Omnibox for customer lookup, ghost login for debugging customer issues, and a daemon that automatically detects known bugs in print jobs. He argues that aggregate conversion rate is misleading because it conflates traffic mix changes with product changes, and that metrics only matter if they drive decisions.
quotes.key_insight: A well-designed business dashboard should ruthlessly prioritize actionable information and workflow shortcuts over comprehensive metrics, because time spent admiring numbers without acting on them is time wasted.

He details specific features like the Omnibox for customer lookup, ghost login for debugging customer issues, and a daemon that automatically detects known bugs in print jobs. He argues that aggregate conversion rate is misleading because it conflates traffic mix changes with product changes, and that metrics only matter if they drive decisions.
quotes.key_insight: A well-designed business dashboard should ruthlessly prioritize actionable information and workflow shortcuts over comprehensive metrics, because time spent admiring numbers without acting on them is time wasted.

quotes.quote: Detailed, actionable advice can be yours if you just ask for it.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares results from a user survey he ran on Bingo Card Creator using Wufoo, revealing demographic data, feature preferences, and complaints. He A/B tested survey incentivization and found that offering free bingo cards more than doubled response rates versus altruistic appeals. The survey confirmed his customer base is mostly female teachers over 40 and revealed that customers value features he considered too obvious to mention. The number one complaint was that the software isn't free, which he uses as a springboard to explain why charging money actually makes the product better for everyone, including non-paying users.
quotes.key_insight: User surveys are cheap, don't hurt conversions, and reveal both what customers actually value and how to talk about your product in their language — but only if you incentivize participation.

quotes.quote: The fact that I didn't know this off the top of my head, and that this is the first time I've thought about individual publishing companies in my entire life, should be a preview of coming attractions for you as regards to which company I am backing in this fight.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie writes four open letters to participants in the book industry's digital transition, centered on the Kindle pricing dispute between publishers and Amazon. As a voracious reader spending thousands annually on books, he makes clear that publishers have zero brand loyalty from consumers and that delaying Kindle releases to protect hardcover sales will simply drive readers to competing authors. He warns authors that siding with publishers over readers' preferences is a losing strategy. The post concludes with dismissive one-liners to bookstores and effusive support for Amazon, illustrating the power shift from publishers to platforms.
quotes.key_insight: Publishers who delay digital releases to protect legacy formats misunderstand that their brand is invisible to consumers—readers are loyal to convenience and content, not publishing houses.

quotes.quote: I do very little backwards facing analysis ('Did that work?') and concentrate mostly on forward facing analysis ('What opportunities can I exploit now?').
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie answers detailed followup questions about the SEO strategies behind his Bingo Card Creator business. He explains his freelancer workflow for content creation, his preference for forward-facing analysis over backward-facing metrics, and how he reuses product data across multiple marketing channels. Most notably, he describes a conversion optimization technique where new trial users get pre-initialized accounts based on the landing page that brought them in, yielding a 20% improvement in funnel success.
quotes.key_insight: The highest-leverage marketing optimization isn't more content or better analytics — it's connecting the marketing promise to the product experience so users land in a personalized context that matches why they showed up.

quotes.quote: You are roughly 20% more likely to successfully download a set of customized bingo cards if I give you the 'personalized' treatment than you are if I drop you at a blank dashboard and expect you to fight your way through.

quotes.quote: Algorithms have no moral status.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that startups need a deliberate SEO strategy that goes beyond tactical page-level optimizations to core business decisions about content creation and marketing. He explains why startup SEO differs from large company SEO due to limited budgets, low domain trust, and cultural aversion, but argues startups have advantages in technical skills and access to link-rich communities. The core strategy is to become the best resource on the Internet for as many topics as possible that matter to paying customers, using scalable content generation approaches. He advocates for evergreen content, leveraging proprietary data, open source software as linkbait, and focusing on long-tail search terms where showing up is often enough to win.
quotes.key_insight: Startups should treat SEO as a strategic content investment — becoming the definitive resource on long-tail topics their customers care about — rather than a tactical optimization exercise or something to be culturally avoided.

quotes.quote: I could get your startup ranked for [fried squirrels with wasabi] by the end of the day, but unless you're selling a book of very eclectic recipes that probably won't do you much good.

quotes.quote: Programmers live in a world where data is available in infinite abundance, and hence we often assume that data is valueless.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes how he built a cheap, effective user survey system for Bingo Card Creator using Wufoo forms, A/B testing, and clever incentivization. Despite running his business for 3.5 years, he realizes he still doesn't have solid data on who his actual users are beyond his initial customer hypothesis of elementary schoolteachers. He details the specific questions asked, the implementation using Rails and his A/B testing framework, and how he incentivized survey completion by offering users extra free cards at zero marginal cost. He also argues controversially that users don't actually care about privacy despite claiming otherwise, and that businesses should prioritize what users demonstrably want over stated privacy preferences.
quotes.key_insight: You can build surprisingly effective user research systems by combining free tools, smart incentives that cost you nothing but have real perceived value to users, and lightweight integration into existing workflows.

quotes.quote: That was my customer hypothesis, to use a buzzword much remarked on lately, and 3.5 years later I'm still not really sure if it is accurate.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes how he built a cheap, effective user survey system for Bingo Card Creator using Wufoo forms, A/B testing, and clever incentivization. Despite running his business for 3.5 years, he realizes he still doesn't have solid data on who his actual users are beyond his initial customer hypothesis of elementary schoolteachers. He details the specific questions asked, the implementation using Rails and his A/B testing framework, and how he incentivized survey completion by offering users extra free cards at zero marginal cost. He also argues controversially that users don't actually care about privacy despite claiming otherwise, and that businesses should prioritize what users demonstrably want over stated privacy preferences.
quotes.key_insight: You can build surprisingly effective user research systems by combining free tools, smart incentives that cost you nothing but have real perceived value to users, and lightweight integration into existing workflows.

quotes.quote: The code for this took 10 minutes. Reasoning my way through a deployment took, hmm, 3 hours or so. I am a programmer not a sysadmin, what can I say.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes building a personal phone secretary using Twilio, Sinatra, and Ruby to manage calls from US contacts while living in Japan. The app checks the local time and either forwards calls or politely tells callers it's the middle of the night. He provides a step-by-step deployment guide for running Sinatra on Ubuntu with Apache reverse proxying. He concludes by marveling at how APIs like Twilio let individual developers build telephony systems that once required enterprise budgets.
quotes.key_insight: APIs like Twilio democratize capabilities that once required enterprise-scale investment, letting solo developers build real-world telephony systems in minutes for almost nothing.

quotes.quote: Sometimes a profit beats an exit.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie wrote a parody rap (to the tune of Eminem's 'The Real Slim Shady') celebrating solo software founders who build and market products without cofounders or VC funding. The song pushes back against Silicon Valley orthodoxy that startups require teams, funding, and exits to succeed. He argues that solo developers can handle both coding and marketing, that niche products with paying customers are legitimate businesses, and that profit can beat exits. The post also casually reveals he has quit his day job to go full-time on his software business. It's a humorous manifesto for the bootstrapped, one-person software company.
quotes.key_insight: Solo founders who combine technical skills with marketing ability can build profitable software businesses without cofounders, funding, or Silicon Valley's permission.

quotes.quote: I tend to work in spurts when I have inspiration.
quotes.summary: McKenzie visualizes his SVN commit history to understand his own work patterns, creating graphs of commits per month and lines of code over time. He discovers he works in spurts driven by inspiration, prefers shipping v1.0 quickly and iterating, and sometimes loses momentum when his day job demands energy. He also graphs cumulative lines of code, showing steady growth over years. The exercise reveals that code output alone doesn't capture the full picture of running a software business.
quotes.key_insight: Visualizing your own work patterns reveals that running a software business involves far more than writing code, and that shipping quickly and iterating beats perfectionism.

quotes.quote: I prefer to ship v1.0 of something and iterate like mad rather than spending more time shipping something more ambitious to start with.

quotes.quote: We're sitting on top of more software and programmer brain juice than Saudi Arabia has oil, and when we deploy it to maximize our sales… we build marginal features that nobody has asked for and that nobody will see.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that engineers should redirect their programming skills from building marginal features toward building systems that improve marketing outcomes. He draws from his own experience where a client/server feature attracted only 22 users while simpler marketing-focused engineering like A/B testing and automated content generation drove significant revenue. The post catalogs specific engineering-to-marketing conversions: A/B testing frameworks, scalable content generation, lifecycle email automation, automated ad creative generation, and process friction removal. He credits Thomas Ptacek with crystallizing this insight and uses examples from his own Bingo Card Creator business and Facebook's retention engineering to illustrate the approach.
quotes.key_insight: Engineers should stop building features and start building automated systems that multiply marketing effectiveness — A/B testing, lifecycle emails, content generation, and friction removal yield far more revenue per hour than new product features.

quotes.quote: Aside from the dropped calls, poor call quality, times when they call and I am connected to an irate Chinese man wondering what happened to his wife, and service interruptions, they pretty much do what they say on the tin.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his experience discovering Twilio's phone call API as a solution to his problem of having family in Chicago call him in Japan. His previous forwarding service was unreliable, and he had no way to handle calls arriving at inappropriate hours. Using Twilio, he built a smart call routing system that checks the time in Japan and either forwards calls, routes to his cell, or warns callers it's the middle of the night. The entire solution required minimal code — five lines of XML for v1.0 and about 30 lines of Sinatra for the full version. He closes by noting Twilio is cheaper and more capable than his previous solution, and hints at wanting to use it for a future application.
quotes.key_insight: When powerful APIs make previously complex infrastructure trivially programmable, individual developers can build custom solutions in minutes that no existing product on the market actually provides.

quotes.quote: I'm currently making more than enough money to live off of on Bingo Card Creator (I live rather simply in a rural prefecture in Japan — think Kansas with rather less white people)
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews Bingo Card Creator's 2009 performance, reporting a 110% increase in profits to $18,525 on $31,156 in sales. The defining event was launching a web version of his desktop application, which doubled conversion rates and reduced support costs. He details successes including price increases, building the A/Bingo testing framework, email marketing via Mailchimp, and a network of holiday bingo mini-sites. He also candidly recounts failures including a $3,500 CSS bug, wasted development on unused desktop features, and ongoing AdWords frustrations. The post closes with his plan to quit his day job in 2010 and go full-time on the business.
quotes.key_insight: A small software business can reach life-changing profitability through relentless conversion optimization, strategic use of web apps over desktop, and cheap re-engagement channels like email — but only if you talk to your users before building features.

quotes.quote: It costs me about 24 cents to get a trial download or trial signup through AdWords at the margin, but only 2 cents to remind someone that their account still exists and that it would be perfect for the Halloween Thanksgiving Christmas festivities around the corner.

He details successes including price increases, building the A/Bingo testing framework, email marketing via Mailchimp, and a network of holiday bingo mini-sites. He also candidly recounts failures including a $3,500 CSS bug, wasted development on unused desktop features, and ongoing AdWords frustrations. The post closes with his plan to quit his day job in 2010 and go full-time on the business.
quotes.key_insight: A small software business can reach life-changing profitability through relentless conversion optimization, strategic use of web apps over desktop, and cheap re-engagement channels like email — but only if you talk to your users before building features.

quotes.quote: They need the Microsoft Word of Internet marketing, a simple pick-up-and-go tool that you can install, employ, and benefit from without needing cooperation from the operations, marketing, and engineering teams which you either a) don't have or b) can't get to do your bidding.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reviews Visual Website Optimizer (VWO), a new A/B testing tool from Indian startup Wingify that makes split testing accessible to non-technical small business owners. He explains how founder Paras Chopra pivoted from building an enterprise analytics system to creating an elegantly simple WYSIWYG A/B testing interface after discovering customers needed simplicity, not power. McKenzie praises the product's UI as vastly superior to Google Website Optimizer for non-technical users, while offering candid advice about the startup's Basecamp-inspired design and pricing strategy. He frames VWO as bringing a powerful technique to the 'Fortune 5 Million' — small businesses that couldn't previously access A/B testing.
quotes.key_insight: The biggest opportunity in software tooling is making powerful techniques accessible to the millions of small businesses that lack technical teams, not building more powerful features for the technically sophisticated.

quotes.quote: The quality is quite good — I'm especially impressed at their oral descriptions of the screenshots and graphs that were crucial to understanding some of those posts.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers that a startup called HearABlog has been converting his blog posts into audio podcasts without his prior knowledge. He evaluates the quality positively, noting their skill at describing visual elements like screenshots and graphs. Rather than objecting to the unauthorized derivative work, he grants explicit blanket permission for them to continue. He later discovers they had actually tried to contact him but the email got lost during a busy period.
quotes.key_insight: When someone creates something cool with your work, default to granting permission and supporting them rather than enforcing copyright — but gently suggest they ask first next time.

quotes.quote: 200 lines of negacode (code that you don't have to write) means 200 lines of code that you don't have to read, test, maintain, or debug.
quotes.summary: McKenzie demonstrates how Ruby metaprogramming can eliminate boilerplate code when storing object preferences, using his Bingo Card Creator's PrintJob class as a real-world example. Rather than adding database columns for each new property (requiring migrations) or using ugly has_many associations, he serializes options to a JSON hash and uses metaprogramming to auto-generate getter/setter methods. This approach saved roughly 200 lines of code, required zero controller or schema changes when adding new features, and still works with Rails validations and form helpers. He argues this pattern is widely applicable to any one-to-many relationship where the 'many' side is only accessed through its parent.
quotes.key_insight: Metaprogramming's real value isn't cleverness — it's eliminating boilerplate so that adding new features requires changing only the parts that actually differ, leaving controllers, schemas, and tests untouched.

quotes.quote: The less boilerplate code you have to write, understand, read, test, debug, and maintain, the more time you can spend creating new features for your customers, doing marketing, or doing other things of value for your business.
