quotes.quote: I have a confession to make: I often trust code that I thinks works.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes tracking down a subtle bug where his analytics and A/B testing data was corrupted for months because a deterministic srand() call in his bingo card printing code was polluting the random seed used to generate unique visitor IDs. Trial users printing cards would reset the random seed to a fixed value, causing subsequent visitors to receive identical 'random' IDs, which Mixpanel then treated as duplicate events. The bug caused roughly 60% of analytics data to be silently discarded. He discovered it only when a massive discrepancy between his signup logs and Mixpanel's reported numbers became impossible to ignore during a Halloween promotion analysis.
quotes.key_insight: Shared mutable process-level state like random seeds creates invisible coupling between unrelated features, and the most dangerous bugs are the ones that produce plausible-looking wrong data rather than obvious failures.

quotes.quote: I knew my conversion code worked and it wasn't displaying zeroes, so I mentally pushed it out of mind.

quotes.quote: Don't use srand(constant) anywhere in your production code, even if it is the easiest way to get what you want, because sooner or later you will use rand() in some unrelated code called by the same process and now you have extraordinarily subtle bugs caused by reuse of random numbers.

quotes.quote: I thought this was such an obviously beneficial change that it was not worth taking on extra coding complexity to keep it the old way. Stupid, stupid, stupid.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recounts how a seemingly trivial CSS change—replacing a standard HTML submit button with a graphical one—caused the sign-up and login buttons to vanish in IE6/IE7, going undetected for over a month during his busiest sales season. The text-indent CSS trick that hid button text also hid the background image in older IE browsers, effectively removing the ability to register. He estimates the bug cost him $3,750 in lost sales, more than his monthly salary. Despite multiple warning signals—shifting signup ratios, declining AdWords conversions, unusual support tickets—he rationalized each away individually. The incident illustrates how small, 'obviously safe' changes can have catastrophic effects and how confirmation bias prevents you from connecting dots you aren't looking for.
quotes.key_insight: Small changes that seem too trivial to test rigorously can silently destroy your business metrics, and without automated sanity-check alerts, you will rationalize away every individual warning signal until the damage is catastrophic.

quotes.quote: Visualizations are wonderful for seeing problems that you already know to look for. However, they are poor for informing you of problems that aren't visible in the default visualization, since you won't spend the time to slice the data to see the problem if you don't know it exists.

quotes.quote: Many software/web-app developers naively assume that demand for their product is roughly static year-round, despite the fact that this is true for very, very few industries.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie outlines three tactical approaches for software businesses to capitalize on seasonal demand spikes: mini-sites optimized for holiday keywords, targeted PPC campaigns with seasonal landing pages, and strategic email marketing to existing trial users. Drawing from his experience selling bingo card software to teachers, he demonstrates how October/Halloween drives his best month annually. He emphasizes building reusable infrastructure for rapid campaign deployment and explains the powerful economics of trading digital IP for conversions. The post is a practical playbook showing that even niche software businesses have seasonal patterns worth exploiting.
quotes.key_insight: Software businesses should identify their seasonal demand spikes and build reusable infrastructure for mini-sites, PPC campaigns, and email promotions that can be deployed rapidly at near-zero marginal cost.

quotes.quote: The notion that working longer hours is correlated to better business results is a pernicious social pathology.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that working longer hours is a counterproductive strategy for business success, particularly for startups and small software companies. Drawing on his experience as both a Japanese salaryman working 70-hour weeks and a side-project entrepreneur averaging four hours weekly, he demonstrates that productivity should be measured by business value created, not hours invested. He introduces the concept of a 'pseudo-wage' to evaluate whether tasks are worth doing personally, then presents three concrete techniques: outsourcing low-value work, automating processes, and eliminating unproductive time. His freelancer content strategy generates $1,300/month in sales from five minutes of monthly oversight, proving that leverage beats effort.
quotes.key_insight: Business productivity should be measured in value created per hour, not hours worked, and the highest-leverage move is replacing low-value labor with outsourcing and automation rather than simply working longer.

quotes.quote: Take it from a Japanese salaryman, guys: it is a disease so vicious that in addition to hobbling businesses it damages society itself.

quotes.quote: It can save and load files from the clooooooooooooud.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces the upcoming release of Bingo Card Creator 3.0 during a five-day Japanese holiday weekend. He requests beta testers for Windows and Mac versions and outlines his ambitious weekend agenda including the release, new features, a major article, and A/B testing. The new version adds cloud save/load, PDF export for registered users, and a streamlined registration process that eliminates the need for most users to manually enter registration keys.
quotes.key_insight: Reducing user friction in registration and leveraging web services to avoid complex native coding are pragmatic ways to improve both the customer experience and developer productivity.

quotes.quote: I like this because it is more visually engaging and contains the sidebar, which I can stuff full of reasons they should sign up without impacting the readability of the main form.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes A/B testing a redesigned signup page for Bingo Card Creator, replacing a spartan Wufoo-derived form with a more visually engaging design featuring a sidebar for conversion arguments. He also addresses the jarring transition users experience when moving from themed mini-sites (like Halloween bingo) to the main site by customizing the signup page to match the referring mini-site's branding and theme.
quotes.key_insight: Maintaining visual continuity ('scent') between landing pages and signup pages prevents users from bouncing when the design shift makes them doubt they're in the right place.

quotes.quote: There is no customer support experience so good as avoiding the problem in the first place.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie explains why he's abandoning desktop software development in favor of web applications, based on concrete data from running both versions of Bingo Card Creator simultaneously. The web version converts trial users to paying customers at nearly double the rate of the desktop version, despite having fewer features and only two months of development versus three years. Web apps eliminate the lengthy shareware download funnel, drastically reduce support burden, enable real-time A/B testing and analytics, and make piracy essentially impossible. The cumulative advantages in conversion rates, customer acquisition costs, development velocity, and per-user customization make web apps strictly dominant for his business.
quotes.key_insight: Web applications structurally outperform desktop software for small businesses because they compress the sales funnel, enable rapid experimentation, and produce data that reveals counterintuitive truths about customer behavior.

quotes.quote: Next time, incorporating a pun into the name is fine, but incorporating a pun more limiting than the actual feature set is perhaps not a good idea.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie briefly acknowledges the growing attention his A/Bingo Rails split testing framework has received from prominent Ruby on Rails community sites. He reflects that naming the tool with a pun tied to his bingo card business may have been limiting, since the framework's capabilities extend well beyond that niche. He invites users to reach out with questions or feature requests.
quotes.key_insight: When naming a product, a clever pun can backfire if it constrains people's perception of what the product actually does.

quotes.quote: Before I go to one of the body shopping sites and get inundated with proposals by folks offering to clone eBay for $25, I thought I would put the word out here.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie posts a freelance writing job listing for his Bingo Card Creator business. He needs someone to write content for 13 seasonal mini-sites targeting holiday bingo activities like Halloween and Christmas bingo. The role involves writing warm, grammatically flawless copy aimed at teachers, plus basic WordPress configuration. He offers $100 per site and emphasizes his reputation as a prompt-paying, no-nonsense client.
quotes.key_insight: A well-scoped freelance project with clear deliverables, reference implementations, and prepared infrastructure minimizes friction and attracts better talent than open-ended marketplace postings.

quotes.quote: Since Bingo Card Creator is powered by Rails, ideally I'd be using a Rails blog platform, but frankly, they're all vastly inferior to WordPress.
quotes.summary: McKenzie walks through the technical process of running a WordPress blog as a subdirectory of a Rails application using Nginx as a reverse proxy. He argues that despite Rails being the main app framework, WordPress is the superior blogging platform and worth integrating. The key architectural decision is placing the blog on a subdirectory rather than a subdomain for SEO benefits. The setup involves creating a WordPress theme from the Rails app's design, installing WordPress on a private URL, and configuring Nginx to proxy requests.
quotes.key_insight: Pragmatic integration of the best tool for each job — WordPress for blogging, Rails for the app — beats ideological purity about using a single stack, especially when SEO demands subdirectory placement.

quotes.quote: I could have slapped up a WordPress blog on a subdomain and called it a day, but I always advise people to put blogs on subdirectories for SEO reasons, so I thought I'd do it properly.

quotes.key_insight: The entrepreneurial habit of registering domains for every business idea quietly accumulates into a surprisingly expensive portfolio of unrealized projects.

The post is a lighthearted aside about the hidden costs of being a serial project starter who accumulates domains for various business ideas.
quotes.key_insight: The entrepreneurial habit of registering domains for every business idea quietly accumulates into a surprisingly expensive portfolio of unrealized projects.

quotes.quote: I am going hog wild with A/B testing now that I have an easy way to do it.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares early results from A/B testing on Bingo Card Creator after building his own testing framework (A/Bingo). He finds that removing guest login and hiding email permission checkboxes had no significant effect on trial signups. However, reordering buttons to prioritize the trial registration call-to-action over downloads increased conversion from 11.9% to 19.8%. He also previews two ongoing tests around purchasing page design and landing page copy.
quotes.key_insight: Most A/B tests produce null results, but the simple act of reordering calls-to-action to prioritize what you actually want users to do can yield dramatic conversion improvements.

quotes.quote: I'm cautiously optimistic that the next $50,000 won't take 3 years.
quotes.summary: McKenzie celebrates reaching $50,000 in cumulative sales of Bingo Card Creator after three years, while noting approximately $27,000 in pre-tax profits. He reports strong momentum heading into the school year sales season, with the online version converting at 1.7% of trials. New ad campaigns are posting his highest conversion rates ever, and his first email newsletter via Mailchimp generated a 30x return on investment.
quotes.key_insight: A solo software business can compound steadily over years, with each new channel (web version, email marketing, better ads) accelerating growth beyond what the initial trajectory suggested.

quotes.quote: Setting up a test takes one line of code. It is so easy you'll find yourself doing it automatically when writing copy or making new features.
quotes.summary: Patrick announces A/Bingo, his open-source A/B testing framework for Ruby on Rails, built to replace Google Website Optimizer. He found GWO too cumbersome to set up, code, and maintain, so he spent a week building his own solution. A/Bingo requires just one line of code to set up tests and track conversions, includes built-in statistical significance testing, and is optimized for performance. He considers it the most technically impressive code he's ever written and expects it to dramatically increase his testing velocity.
quotes.key_insight: The biggest barrier to A/B testing isn't understanding its value but the friction of existing tools — reducing test setup to one line of code transforms testing from an occasional effort into a default habit.

quotes.quote: It does statistical significance testing for you, producing human-readable output which tells you exactly how confident you can be that the test results are accurate.

quotes.quote: I am almost embarassed how well this is working.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports that his landing page redesign dramatically improved AdWords conversion rates from ~21-22% to 28%. The redesign focused on a clear 'One True Goal' button that captured over 180 clicks per 1,000 visitors. This improvement effectively reduces his customer acquisition cost by about 25%. He anticipates Google's Conversion Optimizer will respond by increasing his CPC bids, which should drive even more impressions and conversions.
quotes.key_insight: A focused landing page redesign with a single clear call-to-action can dramatically improve conversion rates, creating a compounding effect through lower acquisition costs and algorithmic bid optimization.

quotes.quote: It is probably slightly more technically involved than most uISVs would care for, but the funnel tracking is solid gold.

He highlights Mixpanel's funnel tracking as particularly valuable despite being more technically demanding than most micro-ISV owners might prefer. He notes that the analytics insights have already led to changes in Bingo Card Creator, and mentions he's on an optimization kick before the busy school year season.
quotes.key_insight: Funnel tracking tools like Mixpanel provide outsized value for conversion optimization even when they require more technical setup than typical small-business analytics.

quotes.quote: Landing pages should have one goal.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares practical tips for designing effective landing pages, drawing from his experience redesigning Bingo Card Creator's landing page which increased AdWords conversions from 18% to 28%. He advocates for ruthless focus on a single goal, stripping away all navigation and visual elements that don't serve that goal. The post covers trust markers like testimonials, money-back guarantees, pleasing Google's landing page quality signals, and maintaining continuity between ad copy and landing page messaging. He emphasizes that users skim rather than read, and that the entire flow from ad click to conversion should feel like one continuous experience.
quotes.key_insight: A landing page should ruthlessly serve a single goal, with every element — from stripped navigation to edited testimonials to ad-matched copy — aligned to move the visitor toward that one action.

quotes.quote: I have as convincing evidence for the existence of Google technical support as I have evidence for the existence of Santa Claus.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers his Google AdWords campaigns have been essentially turned off for over a week, dropping from 10,000 daily impressions to 20, with no notification from Google. After filing a support request and receiving an unhelpful automated response, he investigates and finds a possible Rails bug that prevented conversion tracking for his web application's trial signups. He's uncertain this was the actual cause since his conversion metrics remained near historical norms. The post is a detailed account of the frustration of depending on an opaque advertising platform with effectively non-existent customer support.
quotes.key_insight: Depending on an opaque advertising platform for revenue is terrifying because when automated systems break, the lack of meaningful human support means you're left debugging a black box with no recourse.

quotes.quote: Google hates dealing with customers. We're little annoying things that don't scale well when we can't be handled perfectly algorithmically like all the world's information.

quotes.quote: Clicking on it currently brings up a maximized version of the screenshot in a lightbox. The lightbox is a wonderful effect, but what is the user supposed to do with the maximized version?
quotes.summary: McKenzie identifies a conversion flaw on his own site: clicking the large homepage screenshot opens a lightbox with no call-to-action, creating a dead end. He fixes this by adding download buttons directly inside the lightbox, eliminating an unnecessary click from the conversion path. The change was inspired by advice he gave someone else about maintaining clear user direction on every page element. He considers the improvement obvious enough to skip A/B testing.
quotes.key_insight: Every interactive element on your page should have a clear next step toward conversion — if a click leads to a dead end, you're wasting the user's intent.

quotes.quote: That is two more clicks, bringing to three the total of clicks they have made to my page! Clearly, there is an opportunity to eliminate a click.

quotes.quote: Every step is crucial because the effects are multiplicative: a 5% improvement anywhere essentially results in 5% straight to the bottom line.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares practical conversion optimization tips for his Bingo Card Creator software, drawn from analyzing CrazyEgg heat maps collected over several years. He identifies that users on his purchasing page frequently click navigation links that take them backward in the funnel, and removes those distracting options. He also fixes a misleadingly-labeled 'Information' button and adjusts his purchase page copy to work for both his downloadable and online product versions. The post emphasizes that small improvements at each funnel step have multiplicative effects on revenue, and that all changes should ultimately be validated through A/B testing rather than intuition alone.
quotes.key_insight: Conversion optimization is about systematically finding and fixing the places where your funnel leaks attention, and every improvement compounds multiplicatively across the entire purchase flow.

quotes.quote: I love giving users free things that make them happy, but there is a time and place for it, and that time and place is not right in the middle of me asking them for money.

quotes.quote: Confidence loses to evidence every day and twice on Sunday.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares practical conversion optimization tips for his Bingo Card Creator software, drawn from analyzing CrazyEgg heat maps collected over several years. He identifies that users on his purchasing page frequently click navigation links that take them backward in the funnel, and removes those distracting options. He also fixes a misleadingly-labeled 'Information' button and adjusts his purchase page copy to work for both his downloadable and online product versions. The post emphasizes that small improvements at each funnel step have multiplicative effects on revenue, and that all changes should ultimately be validated through A/B testing rather than intuition alone.

quotes.quote: I'm a heavy subscriber to the notion that you should release your application as early as it possibly provides value to anyone, then start iterating based on customer feedback.
quotes.summary: McKenzie advocates for releasing software early and iterating based on customer feedback and usage statistics rather than waiting for perfection. He shares early metrics from the web version of Bingo Card Creator, showing strong engagement (3,138 cards printed, 200 trial signups) but zero sales in the first week. He notes that summer is a natural dead zone for his education-focused market and remains optimistic that engaged users will convert to paying customers when the school year begins.
quotes.key_insight: Ship early, measure what users actually do rather than what they say, and don't mistake a seasonal lull for product failure when your engagement metrics are strong.

quotes.quote: Since it isn't always practical to get feedback, and since customers do not always know what they want, I'd also consider stats about what they do to be among the most useful forms of feedback.

quotes.quote: Since an absolutely ginormous portion of my business is seasonal, this will let me get in touch with the thousands of users who will sign up before Halloween again at Halloween, and remind them that they can have a great activity ready for only $30.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his initial foray into email marketing for Bingo Card Creator's web application. He chose MailChimp for its API and integrated it using the hominid Ruby gem in about 4 hours. His strategy includes monthly newsletters timed to seasonal demand peaks and a two-email onboarding sequence for new signups. He sets a clear profitability threshold of one sale per thousand emails at 2 cents each.
quotes.key_insight: Email marketing turns a free web app's user registrations into a remarketing channel, letting you re-engage seasonal users at minimal cost with a clear, measurable ROI threshold.

quotes.quote: The emails run me 2 cents apiece, so I'll be looking to see if they generate enough marginal business (about 1 sale per every thousand) to justify.

quotes.key_insight: Transitioning from desktop to web software unlocks deeper analytics capabilities around customer lifecycles and behavior that drive better business decisions.

quotes.quote: He has a much better eye for design issues, including UI and usability issues, than I do.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a brief progress update on the web version of Bingo Card Creator after spending a day working with a local designer. The redesign covers most screens except the main editing interface. He shares his remaining to-do list before launch, including password recovery, analytics, and purchase integration. The post is a short development log typical of his early blogging style.
quotes.key_insight: Hiring a designer to complement your technical skills produces better results than trying to do everything yourself.

quotes.quote: It was ugly, underfeatured, and bug-ridden. Now, it is ugly, underfeatured, and bug-ridden… with AJAX!
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces the web application version of Bingo Card Creator, three years after the original desktop release. The new version uses AJAX for live preview of bingo cards as users type, though it lacks some power-user features from the downloadable version. He details the technical architecture involving Prototype, Rails, Prawn PDF generation, and ImageMagick, estimating his server can handle about 20 simultaneous users. The post serves as both a progress update and a request for beta feedback, with a candid acknowledgment that significant work remains on copy, design, and purchasing funnel integration.
quotes.key_insight: Investing heavily in responsive user feedback — even at the cost of half the total development time — is justified because immediate visual results drive engagement and conversion in consumer software.

quotes.quote: Once again, cron decided that it would stop deleting my Rails cached files after I upgraded to Hardy.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes a frustrating cron job failure on his Rails server after upgrading to Ubuntu Hardy. Cached files for seasonal content (Mother's Day, Cinco de Mayo) weren't being deleted despite logs showing the commands ran successfully. He could manually delete them but the automated process silently failed. It's a brief venting post about the maddening nature of server administration.
quotes.key_insight: Server automation that appears to work according to logs can still silently fail, and OS upgrades are a common culprit for breaking previously reliable cron jobs.

quotes.quote: The logs say that the rf -rm happened at the scheduled times for the last two weeks, and I was able to successfully do it by sudoing into the appropriate user, but the files were still on disk.

quotes.quote: "I'm going to start a backup any day now" ranks right up there with "Hmm, well, we haven't heard from that freaky serial killer in a while. I think I'll stay in the house."
quotes.summary: Patrick recounts how his Vista laptop's hard drive began failing, and how Dropbox saved his important files despite his procrastination on backups. He had signed up for Dropbox casually after a coworker mentioned it but hadn't seriously used it for backups. After fellow software developers suffered hard drive failures, he at least started backing up his servers, but his laptop remained unprotected. When the inevitable crash came, he managed to boot into safe mode and stream his critical files to Dropbox just in time.
quotes.key_insight: Even technically savvy people procrastinate on backups, making frictionless cloud sync tools like Dropbox valuable precisely because they work passively before disaster strikes.

quotes.quote: Most of my critical business data is already off of my laptop — its at a Google server farm, or chilling on a Slicehost server, or over at Paypal, but there is still quite a bit of sentimental value in my photos.

quotes.quote: If you symlink a to b, and are wondering why you can't access a/c, a/d, and a/e despite the preferences on a, c, d, and e all being right, check to make sure you've chmod-ed b/. properly.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a brief productivity update on his Rails development work. He resolved a permissions error related to symlinks and chmod, made his first push to GitHub, and published a fork of Delayed::Job that supports multiple workers. The post reflects his transition from SVN to git while noting the friction of Rails development on Windows.
quotes.key_insight: Small debugging victories and incremental tooling improvements — like moving to git and contributing open source extensions — compound into meaningful productivity gains for solo developers.

quotes.quote: It can interact with the server to save user's word lists in the cloooooooooooud. Which is apparently the $10 buzzword for a client-server application these days.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports on a productive but frustrating three-day programming sprint working on Bingo Card Creator 3.0. The update adds cloud-based word list storage and server-side PDF generation, but he spent significant time fighting Java's limitations and Linux permissions issues. He's integrating Delayed Job to handle heavy PDF generation asynchronously rather than blocking web requests. The web version of the software still needs to be built, and he's looking for beta testers.
quotes.key_insight: Real product development is mostly fighting unglamorous technical problems — Java standard library gaps, Linux permissions, and request queue bottlenecks — rather than building the features themselves.

quotes.quote: I'm wrapping up day two out of the five-day vacation I have for Golden Week, and have not quite gotten as much accomplished on getting the new version out as I had hoped.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares his task list for Bingo Card Creator 3.0 development during Japan's Golden Week vacation, acknowledging he hasn't made as much progress as hoped. The post reveals the full scope of work needed to ship both a desktop update and a new web application version. He uses public accountability as a motivational tool, publishing his incomplete task list to embarrass himself into working harder. The tasks span client-server architecture, licensing, PDF generation, user workflows, and marketing infrastructure.
quotes.key_insight: Solo software developers can use public transparency about their progress and task lists as an accountability mechanism to stay motivated.

quotes.quote: Task list to embarass myself into working hard tomorrow:

quotes.quote: I've got better behavior out of it in 8 hours of playing around then I've been able to coax out of BCC in 2 years.
quotes.summary: Patrick reports major progress on Bingo Card Creator 3.0's web application, specifically the PDF generation for printing bingo cards. After years of struggling with Java's primitive printing facilities, he found the Ruby Prawn library produced better results in 8 hours than he achieved in 2 years with Java. He outlines remaining work including user management, copy rewriting, and integration challenges, while noting his biggest concern is whether non-technical users will pay for a web application.
quotes.key_insight: Choosing the right technology stack can compress years of painful workarounds into hours of productive development, but the hardest problems in software products are often commercial rather than technical.

quotes.quote: There is still some ugly spaghetti code hiding in the bowels of it, but its literally a quarter of the size of the stuff in BCC itself, and much, much more comprehensible.

quotes.summary: Patrick reports major progress on Bingo Card Creator 3.0's web application, specifically the PDF generation for printing bingo cards. After years of struggling with Java's primitive printing facilities, he found the Ruby Prawn library produced better results in 8 hours than he achieved in 2 years with Java. He outlines remaining work including user management, copy rewriting, and integration challenges, while noting his biggest concern is whether non-technical users will pay for a web application.
quotes.key_insight: Choosing the right technology stack can compress years of painful workarounds into hours of productive development, but the hardest problems in software products are often commercial rather than technical.

quotes.quote: About a week ago I noticed a deficiency in my understanding of how my shopping cart actually worked for purchases of 2+ units.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes a cascading bug in his shopping cart pricing logic where a quick fix for multi-unit purchases introduced a worse problem: customers being charged $20 instead of $29.95. After multiple failed attempts to patch the issue involving interactions between e-junkie, Google Checkout, and his own code, he reverted to the original system that worked correctly for 99.9% of customers. The experience cost him roughly $120 in lost revenue plus potential scared-off customers.
quotes.key_insight: Fixing a rare edge case in pricing logic can easily introduce worse bugs, and when money is on the line, reverting to a known-good state beats iterating on broken fixes.

quotes.quote: Not quite sure why. So I fixed it, by making the default item be a single copy at $29.95 and only having the pricing logic apply when buying through my cart.

quotes.quote: Bingo Card Creator is going all web 2.0 and storing its information in the clooooooooooooud. Just kidding.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie outlines the features planned for Bingo Card Creator 3.0, a modest update to his small software business. The update includes Vista compatibility fixes, a cloud storage feature for saving/opening files across locations, and an improved import workflow. He hints that the cloud storage feature will support an upcoming new product. The post captures a typical solo developer sprint planning session with realistic time management challenges.
quotes.key_insight: Strategic product development means building features that serve immediate user needs while simultaneously laying infrastructure for future products.

quotes.quote: B2Cでも、値段が０円であるOSSに勝てます。
quotes.summary: This is Patrick McKenzie's Japanese translation of his English post 'How To Successfully Compete With Open Source Software,' prepared because Japanese blogs had linked to the original. The translated portion covers how commercial B2C software can compete with free OSS alternatives, using his own Bingo Card Creator as a case study. He argues that marketing is the primary advantage commercial software has over OSS, since 99% of users don't think of their problem as a software problem. The translation was left incomplete after the marketing section, with a note that the design section would follow the next day.
quotes.key_insight: Commercial software beats free OSS not on features or price, but by marketing to the user's actual problem rather than treating software as the starting point.

quotes.quote: ユーザには問題があります。ソフトは問題を解決するツールです。と言っても、ユーザは「ソフト不足」との問題を意識していません。

quotes.quote: When customers have a 'stupid' question, they don't spend time searching for your contact information. They just bounce.
quotes.summary: McKenzie uses a personal anecdote about failing to buy socks in Japan due to a language ambiguity to illustrate how customers abandon purchases when they encounter confusing information but feel too embarrassed to ask for help. He argues that developers who dismiss non-technical users as idiots are leaving money on the table, because customers with 'stupid' questions simply leave rather than seek clarification. The post provides concrete tactics for preemptively answering customer confusion through formatting, plain language, FAQs, and welcoming support tone. The core message is that the obstacle between your customer and a sale is often a simple question they're too embarrassed to ask.
quotes.key_insight: The biggest sales killer isn't customers who ask stupid questions — it's the customers who have stupid questions and leave without asking them.

quotes.quote: I'm a college-educated professional who has been speaking Japanese for about eight years now, I do technical translation for engineering specs on Big Freaking Enterprise Software, I am generally not a stupid person: and yet here I am, able to read the words right in front of me but unable to make sense of their import.

quotes.quote: he would cookie everyone who came to the blog, so that you could show different views of it to people who had been following for years and people who just arrived fresh off a Google search
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares his favorite WordPress plugin, 'What Would Seth Godin Do?', which cookies blog visitors to show different content to returning readers versus first-time visitors. He explains the marketing logic of treating these two audiences differently, noting that someone arriving from a Google search has completely different needs than a long-time follower. He places the differentiated content subtly below posts rather than above them, since most of his traffic comes from search queries about bingo cards rather than repeat blog readers.
quotes.key_insight: Smart marketers show different content to first-time visitors versus returning readers, because a stranger arriving from Google has completely different needs than a loyal follower.

quotes.quote: I don't need the distraction for the (several thousand people) who get here for some variant of I want to pay someone money to make bingo cards please tell me oh great Google where I can do this

quotes.quote: Thanks to my new expense tracking feature on the BCC website and the magic of TaxAct, for literally the first time in my life I have my taxes done well ahead of schedule.
quotes.summary: Patrick reports completing his 2008 taxes early, sharing his Bingo Card Creator business financials for the year. The business generated $21,720 in sales with $12,318 in expenses, yielding $8,798 in profit. He credits his own expense tracking feature and TaxAct for making the process smoother. He also notes setting up estimated quarterly tax payments for the coming year, anticipating higher earnings.
quotes.key_insight: Transparent financial reporting of a side software business shows that even modest profits require real tax planning, especially for Americans living abroad.

quotes.quote: I also ended up scheduling the IRS to take four nice big bites out of my checking account during the year because the amount of taxes I expect to owe next year is going to be a few thousand more.

quotes.quote: Less than 1% of customers perceive their problem as a software problem.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that commercial B2C software can successfully compete with open source alternatives by excelling in areas OSS typically neglects. Drawing from his experience selling Bingo Card Creator alongside free OSS alternatives, he identifies six key advantages: marketing that speaks to user problems rather than software features, attractive visual design, superior user experience from download through daily use, non-technical language, accessible customer support, and technical execution. His core argument is that 99% of OSS projects are not Firefox-level polished products, and most customers don't even realize they have a software problem—they have a domain problem that software happens to solve.
quotes.key_insight: Commercial software beats open source not through technical superiority but by solving the 90% of the business that happens outside the IDE—marketing to real user problems, designing attractive interfaces, and making the entire experience from download to daily use frictionless.

quotes.quote: Fun stuff, DNS, fun stuff.
quotes.summary: Patrick briefly explains that he misconfigured his DNS records when migrating his blog, accidentally creating a CNAME record pointing to Google instead of an MX record. This caused the non-www version of kalzumeus.com to go down. He notes it will take about a week for the TTL to expire and the fix to propagate. He also set up a redirect via Google Webmaster Console as a stopgap.
quotes.key_insight: DNS misconfigurations during blog migrations are easy to make and slow to fix due to TTL propagation delays.

quotes.quote: This brings BCC sales for February to $2,925.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports that his side business Bingo Card Creator earned $2,925 in February 2009, surpassing his monthly salary of $2,890 as a Japanese salaryman. He tempers the milestone by noting that Japanese salaries include biannual bonuses and that self-employment would increase his tax burden. He also reveals that BCC had $995 in expenses that month. The post hints at a dramatic difference in hours worked between his day job and his side business.
quotes.key_insight: When a side project's monthly revenue surpasses your salary despite requiring far fewer hours, it forces a serious reckoning about the value of traditional employment.

quotes.quote: I won't tell you how many hours are required to get either of these numbers… principally because the difference is too depressing for words.

The post hints at a dramatic difference in hours worked between his day job and his side business.
quotes.key_insight: When a side project's monthly revenue surpasses your salary despite requiring far fewer hours, it forces a serious reckoning about the value of traditional employment.

quotes.quote: The main reasons is to make me the captain of the blog's destiny, as opposed to WordPress.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents his migration from WordPress.com to a self-hosted WordPress installation after two years of planning. He provides a step-by-step technical guide covering domain registration, DNS changes, content export/import, and post-migration setup. The move was motivated by wanting full control over plugins, themes, and site customization as his business expanded beyond just blogging about Bingo Card Creator. He notes practical details like the importance of maintaining WordPress.com's 301 redirects for SEO continuity and the immediate spam problem that hits self-hosted blogs.
quotes.key_insight: When migrating a blog with established SEO value, preserving link equity through proper 301 redirects is worth far more than the nominal cost.

quotes.quote: WordPress will 301 redirect to your new domain IF AND ONLY IF you continue paying them the $10 a year — its a steal, do it.

quotes.quote: In the last 30 days I have had 118 sales of Bingo Card Creator. (Wow, that number shocks me.)
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports the results of a month-long A/B test comparing a natively compiled version of Bingo Card Creator (using Excelsior JET) against the standard Java version. Of 75 trackable purchases, 44 came from the JET-compiled version versus 31 from Java, which is statistically significant at the 8% level using a binomial probability test. He walks through the tracking methodology using properties files, Google Analytics cookies, and query string parameters. Despite a Vista compatibility bug with the JET version, he concludes the native compilation helps sales enough to make it the default for all Windows users.
quotes.key_insight: Native compilation of a Java desktop application measurably increased sales conversion rates, demonstrating that perceived software quality and installation experience directly affect purchasing decisions.

quotes.quote: If we assume hypothetically that it doesn't matter which version you were using, we would expect that 50% of the total number of conversions would turn out each way.

quotes.quote: Sales for 2008 (less returns): $21,116.65
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces a revamped public stats tracking page for Bingo Card Creator, consolidating all business metrics into one location. While working on 2008 taxes, he provides a detailed financial breakdown showing $21,117 in sales, $11,783 in expenses, and $9,334 in profit (roughly 45% margin). Advertising, primarily Google AdWords, was his largest expense category. He notes that pre-2008 expense data is incomplete due to difficulty accessing old credit card statements.
quotes.key_insight: Radical financial transparency — publishing exact revenue, expenses, and profit margins — builds trust and credibility with an audience of fellow software entrepreneurs.

quotes.quote: I hate load screens. When I see them I think "This is me, losing money".
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes an A/B test on his Bingo Card Creator homepage, replacing a standard Lightbox screenshot popup with an iBox that includes a download link. He explains his reasoning: loading screens cost money because any friction loses potential customers. He previously tried adding instructional text to lightboxed images but it failed because clicking the text dismissed the image. Now he's testing whether embedding a real download call-to-action within the screenshot overlay improves trial downloads, while worrying it might trap users and increase bounces.
quotes.key_insight: Every point of friction in a user's journey — even a two-second loading screen on a screenshot preview — is a measurable leak in your conversion funnel that should be tested and optimized.

quotes.quote: If two seconds to load the shopping cart cost me half of my potential sales then 2 seconds to look at a screenshot must likewise be suboptimal.

quotes.quote: I will learn to not, under any circumstances, commit any code to the trunk which is not ready for immediate deployment.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recounts a deployment mistake where he committed unfinished code to trunk, then deployed a quick fix that accidentally broke the lightbox on his front page for three days. He laments his recurring failure to follow the discipline of only committing deployment-ready code. The silver lining is that the outage provided yet another natural A/B test confirming that lightboxes significantly outperform direct image links for conversions.
quotes.key_insight: The discipline of only committing deployment-ready code to trunk matters because quick unrelated fixes will inevitably pull half-finished work into production.

quotes.quote: I inevitably do "just a quick deploy to fix up that unrelated mistake" and then, boom, the lightbox on the front page silently fails for three days.

quotes.quote: Some customers put Registration Key into Lookup My Registration Key form. This looks up transactions by order number and email address, thus failing to find any matching records, then informs customer to email me to find their Registration Key. This is suboptimal.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents a specific customer support optimization in his sales system. He discovered that some customers were entering their Registration Key into the 'Lookup My Registration Key' form, which searches by order number and email, causing it to fail and unnecessarily directing them to email support. His fix detects when input resembles a Registration Key and either redirects the customer to visual instructions for using their key, or tells them to email if the key doesn't exist.
quotes.key_insight: The best customer support improvements come from observing how users actually misuse your systems and engineering the confusion away rather than answering the same emails repeatedly.

quotes.quote: If input into LMRK form resembles a Registration Key, search for a line item which had that Registration Key assigned.

quotes.quote: Suffice it to say that if I told you the improvement I am seeing you would not believe me. I'm not even sure I believe me.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports a dramatic increase in February 2009 sales for Bingo Card Creator, with projections exceeding $3,000. He attributes the growth to multiple simultaneous improvements: a new shopping cart showing dramatic A/B test results, an optimized Check for Updates page that upsells existing users, native compilation testing with Excelsior JET, UI changes that clarify the product's key value proposition, and a soft timed trial mechanic that nudges long-term free users toward purchasing.
quotes.key_insight: Compounding multiple small conversion optimizations — better cart, smarter upsell timing, clearer UI communication of value, and psychologically-framed trial nudges — can produce dramatic revenue gains that exceed any single change.

quotes.quote: I wrote up approximately everything I know about shopping carts, plus everything I learned from my recent experience rewriting one, and made a proper article about it.
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces a comprehensive article about shopping cart development published on his Bingo Card Creator site. The article covers high-level theory, real statistics from his business, and includes the complete cart code released into the public domain. He explains the strategic decision to publish it on his product site rather than his blog, both for technical integration with live data and as a deliberate SEO play to attract inbound links.
quotes.key_insight: Publishing high-value technical content on your product domain rather than a personal blog is a deliberate SEO strategy to attract inbound links that boost your business site's authority.

quotes.quote: Hello, Google searcher! I spent 2 hours so you don't have to!
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents fixing a scrolling bug in the iBox JavaScript overlay library that affected Safari, Chrome, and Firefox 3. He shares the specific code fix for Google searchers, then updates readers on three bugs in his new shopping cart integration with e-junkie. He reflects on JavaScript's potential for improving user interaction while lamenting the pain of cross-browser development, and prepares to A/B test his new cart against the existing one.
quotes.key_insight: Documenting solutions to obscure technical problems creates valuable SEO content while genuinely helping other developers, turning debugging frustration into marketing.

quotes.quote: I use this feature to move the pricing logic from the e-junkie servers to my Javascript, where it gets "bugs in your teeth fast" speed improvements.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents completing an 8-hour sprint to build a custom shopping cart for Bingo Card Creator, replacing the previous e-junkie-hosted solution. The new cart moves pricing logic client-side for speed, uses a lightbox overlay instead of page navigation, and eliminates unnecessary UI elements like 'Continue Shopping' and 'Update Cart' buttons. He acknowledges remaining bugs but is pleased with the performance and UX improvements, planning to A/B test the new cart against the old one and eventually release it for other micro-ISVs.
quotes.key_insight: Owning your checkout experience by moving logic client-side and stripping away unnecessary UI conventions like the shopping cart metaphor can meaningfully reduce purchase friction.

quotes.quote: I'm starting to chafe at a few of the necessary restrictions from using a third party solution.
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces plans to build a custom shopping cart to replace e-junkie's hosted solution for Bingo Card Creator, despite his long advocacy for the service. His motivation is eliminating friction points he believes hurt conversions: redundant zip code entry for CD purchases and perceived slowness of the third-party cart. He notes his sales are on track to hit $3,000/month with 73% year-over-year growth, driven by AdWords near holidays, organic SEO improvements, and subtle product tweaks.
quotes.key_insight: Even small UX friction in your checkout flow — redundant fields, loading delays — compounds into meaningful lost revenue, making custom solutions worth building once you can measure the cost.

quotes.quote: 86 clicks, or 10.4% of clicks on the page, on that one stinking extra button.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes how a simple CrazyEgg heatmap analysis led him to add a second download button on his main page, resulting in a 20% conversion increase. After noticing he hadn't used his $20/month CrazyEgg subscription in months, he identified that users reading his benefits list had no nearby call-to-action. Adding one button captured 10.4% of page clicks and translated to roughly $160/month in additional revenue. The post illustrates how small, data-driven UX changes yield outsized business results.
quotes.key_insight: Placing a call-to-action immediately after your persuasive content — rather than forcing users to scroll or backtrack — is an obvious but commonly neglected optimization that can meaningfully increase conversions.

quotes.quote: The idea is to see whether the tradeoff of vastly increased download size of the native version makes up for eliminating the Java dependency.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents how he reduced the download size of his Bingo Card Creator native executable by 4.5 MB through a combination of Excelsior JET optimizations and Inno Setup packaging. He was running a split test comparing a self-contained native executable against a Java-dependent version, trading download size for eliminating the Java installation requirement. The process involved removing unused Java packages, using a specialized JAR compression tool, and carefully testing that detached packages weren't needed at runtime. He shares his complete Inno Setup script and notes his philosophy of minimizing installer decision screens for non-technical users.
quotes.key_insight: Every friction point in the download-to-install funnel — including download size and installer prompts — is a potential conversion killer, so obsessively optimizing these details pays off for small software businesses.

quotes.quote: Launch4j will prompt people to download Java if they don't have a sufficient version installed, but that is one of those decision points where people might stop using the app.

quotes.quote: After hours of fruitlessly trying to solve the problem in some clean way, I eventually dirty hacked my way around it.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes squashing a bug in Bingo Card Creator after spending hours trying to find a clean solution. He eventually resorted to a dirty hack that touched only 2 lines instead of the clean solution's six files. The impact was minimal, affecting only 8 out of 560 word lists and only noticeable with non-default settings. He accepts the pragmatic tradeoff and commits to shipping replacement CDs to six affected customers.
quotes.key_insight: Sometimes the 'dirty' two-line fix that ships is better than the 'clean' six-file refactor that doesn't.

quotes.quote: Ironically, the "clean" solution required touching six files, the "dirty" solution required touching 2 lines.

quotes.quote: This is about the worst thing that could happen to me at 1:30 in the morning, short of a crash bug. Without the capability to print, the application is useless.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers a critical bug in Bingo Card Creator at 1:30 AM where printing crashes silently when a card has exactly as many words as spaces. The bug makes the application effectively useless since printing is its core function. Despite extensive testing, the edge case was missed because his test methodology rarely produced the exact condition. He rolls back to a previous version using Capistrano and plans to send replacement CDs and apology emails to affected users.
quotes.key_insight: The most dangerous bugs hide in the gap between how developers test and how customers actually use software.

quotes.quote: Printing is always the thing I spend the most time testing, but I typically test by loading up Wizards and when picking randomly. I rarely get one that has exactly twenty-four words in it.

quotes.quote: I have the absolute right to change anything I want to solve problems for myself and the customer, and oh is that intoxicating.
quotes.summary: McKenzie contrasts the satisfaction of solving customer problems in his own software business with the frustrations of his day job. A customer who purchased Bingo Card Creator in December 2006 contacts him about poor Mac support, and McKenzie discovers the purchase date through his Rails console before cheerfully resolving the issue. He reflects on how owning a business gives him complete authority to solve problems directly, unlike his corporate job where he fights for authority over basic decisions. The post culminates in a hilarious anecdote about an outsourcing team filing a bug report about a typhoon warning displayed on a login screen, which was actually intended behavior.
quotes.key_insight: The freedom to directly solve problems without bureaucratic permission is what makes running your own business intoxicating compared to corporate work.

quotes.quote: Your time is too valuable to use on translation is a lovely thing to hear, but I'm closer to the opinion that my time is too valuable to spend the next 6 months dealing with code that is totally orthogonal to the customer's actual needs.

quotes.quote: I'm also interested in seeing whether natively compiled .exes beat Java-requiring versions in a head to head test.
quotes.summary: Patrick announces the release of Bingo Card Creator 2.5, which includes 400+ new cards, improved functionality, and most-requested features. He thanks community members who helped beta test, particularly Mac users. He shares a cautionary tale about accidentally breaking his free trial download for four days due to an SVN deployment mistake. He also announces an upcoming price increase and reflects on balancing his day job with his software business.
quotes.key_insight: Even minor deployment oversights — like an SVN update that touches more than intended — can silently break critical conversion paths for days, underscoring the need for deployment discipline in solo software businesses.

quotes.quote: if you guys didn't see it that is my fault.
quotes.summary: McKenzie posts a final call for beta testers for Bingo Card Creator 2.5, listing all reported bugs and his responses to each. He categorizes issues into fixed, by-design, and deferred, showing pragmatic triage of a solo developer shipping cross-platform software. The post reveals the friction of building a Java desktop app that must work on both Windows and Mac, with platform-specific UI expectations creating constant tension. He closes with a technical tip for Ant users about zip file permissions on Mac.
quotes.key_insight: Effective bug triage for a solo software business means fixing what impacts paying customers, owning UX failures, and deferring edge cases without guilt.

quotes.quote: I have, for a long time, tracked whether people get to my purchasing page via coming from the trial or not — but I have never tracked from where in the trial.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reveals that for years he tracked whether users reached his purchase page from the trial version, but never tracked which specific trial restriction prompted the purchase. After realizing this oversight, he added tracking in just 8 minutes. The post illustrates how small, obvious instrumentation gaps can persist for years and cost valuable business intelligence.
quotes.key_insight: The most valuable analytics instrumentation is often trivially easy to implement but goes unbuilt for years because no one thinks to ask the question.

quotes.quote: It would be very, very helpful for me to know which of those restrictions are most effective at driving the sales… but I never thought of tracking it before, at least not enough to spend the 8 minutes it took me to add that in.

quotes.quote: I'm in the very unenviable position of having to release Mac software without a) having touched a Mac in 10 years and b) without having access to a Mac to test on.
quotes.summary: McKenzie requests beta testers for the latest build of Bingo Card Creator after fixing errors that broke the Mac and native Windows builds. He highlights the challenge of developing Mac software without access to a Mac, relying on Java's cross-platform capabilities. The update includes Windows shell integration with .BCF files and under-the-hood improvements. This is a routine development update showing the realities of solo software development with limited resources.
quotes.key_insight: Solo developers often ship software for platforms they cannot directly test on, relying on cross-platform tools and community beta testers to bridge the gap.

quotes.quote: On Friday a customer reported a bug with UTF-8 files, which had creeped in literally months ago. Since that was blocking her from getting work done I had a fire lit under my behind this weekend, and spent all three days of it coding like mad.
quotes.summary: Patrick details a highly productive weekend of coding on Bingo Card Creator version 2.5, triggered by a customer's UTF-8 bug report. The update includes several feature improvements: consistent column placement for words, a reworked Wizards menu supporting 500+ word lists, automatic update functionality for word lists, and smarter registration key handling. He also announces a partnership with Excelsior to test whether eliminating the Java dependency increases sales. The post serves as both a development log and a call for beta testers.
quotes.key_insight: A single customer bug report can spark a burst of development that compounds existing investments — turning 507 paid word lists and freelance writing spend into significantly more customer value through better software delivery.

quotes.quote: Several hundred people have started downloading Football Bingo all of a sudden. Not that I mind, of course, but I can't square it with what I know about my target users.
quotes.summary: McKenzie notices an unexpected spike in downloads for Football Bingo cards and can't identify a referral source, concluding it must be organic search traffic. He discovered the anomaly while researching long tail search patterns for an upcoming blog post. The brief post illustrates how niche software can attract surprising audiences through search alone.
quotes.key_insight: Organic search traffic can surface unexpected demand in niches you never anticipated, which is exactly the long tail phenomenon at work.

quotes.quote: The trouble with getting into an uber-crowded market, in a niche which is essentially a commodity service, is making yourself heard above the din.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discusses how entering overcrowded software markets (backup apps, photo organizers, project management) is a losing strategy unless you find a differentiating angle. He advocates for niching down to serve a specific, addressable audience rather than competing as a generic commodity. He highlights ProjectThingy as an example of smart differentiation: project management software that can be embedded in your own website, appealing to technically disinclined small businesses. The creator's portfolio of embeddable apps demonstrates how a single differentiating concept can be applied across multiple products.
quotes.key_insight: In crowded software markets, the winning strategy is not to build a better generic product but to find a specific differentiating angle that makes your product uniquely valuable to an addressable niche.

quotes.quote: The $1,100 paid to a freelancer might stick out in the expenses — I assure you, it is like paying for straw so you can spin it into gold.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews Bingo Card Creator's 2008 performance, reporting $20,707 in sales against ~$10,000 in expenses for ~$10,700 profit, exactly hitting his goal of doubling the previous year's revenue. The biggest win was paying a freelancer $1,100 to create niche bingo card content that dramatically improved SEO and organic traffic. Google AdWords Conversion Optimizer continued to be effective despite consuming $5,850. His side project Widget Bakery stalled due to exhausting day job hours, and ChristmasBingoCards.com underperformed expectations. He sets goals of $30,000 in sales, releasing a second product, and potentially going full-time in 2009.
quotes.key_insight: A small software business can double revenue by investing modestly in niche content creation that captures long-tail search traffic, even while the founder works a demanding full-time job.

quotes.quote: PDFs aren't for whitepapers, they are for flypapers.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents a failed $600 Christmas marketing experiment where he created a mini-site to give away free Christmas bingo cards, hoping parents would link to it and boost his SEO, driving teacher customers to his paid product. The experiment failed on multiple fronts: PDFs acted as 'flypaper' trapping users who never interacted further with the site, the SEO timeline was impossibly aggressive for a seasonal promotion, and paid advertising on StumbleUpon and AdWords generated traffic but no valuable links or conversions. He recovered roughly $50 in marginal sales against $600 in costs, nearly producing his first monthly loss. The post-mortem extracts six concrete lessons for future iterations.
quotes.key_insight: Marketing experiments fail when you optimize individual tactics (high CTR, free content, paid traffic) without a coherent funnel connecting attention to conversion, especially when your content format and timeline work against you.

quotes.quote: I thought, hey, why stop at blogging — it would probably work better with a dedicated website, some linkbait, a little Flickr integration, and the like.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces a programmer-focused charity drive he co-created with Andy Brice. The project involved building a dedicated website with linkbait and Flickr integration to support the cause. He highlights the amusing international nature of the collaboration — a UK developer working with an American in Japan for beneficiaries in India. The site sells programmer t-shirts for charity and includes an essay on how open source principles can be applied to charitable giving.
quotes.key_insight: The same marketing and web development skills used to sell software can be repurposed effectively for charitable causes, applying linkbait and community engagement to fundraising.

quotes.quote: In the annals of offshoring, this may well be the first time somebody from the UK "hired" an American in Japan on the behalf of "clients" in India.

quotes.quote: my office will let me wear a T-shirt the day after I get elected Prime Minister of Japan
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie promotes a charitable Christmas drive organized by Andy Brice, who is selling programmer t-shirts with proceeds going to charity. The two benefiting charities are Jaipur Foot, which provides low-cost prosthetics in India, and Sightsavers, which treats preventable blindness. McKenzie offers a personal matching grant of $30 per person who participates and shares a photo, capped at ten participants. The post is a lighthearted call to action that combines programmer culture humor with genuine charitable giving.
quotes.key_insight: Software community influencers can leverage their platforms and matching grants to amplify charitable giving, even with modest individual contributions.

quotes.quote: I'd make a mostly non-commercial mini-site focused like a freaking laser on giving people exactly what they wanted, and making it as easy to link to as humanly possible.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents his experiment with exact match domain names as an SEO strategy, purchasing christmasbingocards.com for $149 to capture seasonal search traffic for Christmas bingo cards. He outlines a seven-step process for creating a focused mini-site designed to rank quickly for specific keywords, from domain purchase through WordPress setup to content creation. The strategy prioritizes link acquisition over direct sales, reasoning that an attractive, purpose-built free resource will naturally attract links from parents while converting teachers into paying customers. He frames this as a test of Aaron Wall's teachings about exact match domains outranking older, more established sites.
quotes.key_insight: A purpose-built mini-site on an exact match domain, designed to be the definitive free resource for a narrow niche, can acquire links and search rankings far more effectively than trying to rank a commercial site directly.

quotes.quote: It is fairly difficult (though by no means impossible) to get people to link directly to a commercial site.

quotes.summary: McKenzie documents his experiment with exact match domain names as an SEO strategy, purchasing christmasbingocards.com for $149 to capture seasonal search traffic for Christmas bingo cards. He outlines a seven-step process for creating a focused mini-site designed to rank quickly for specific keywords, from domain purchase through WordPress setup to content creation. The strategy prioritizes link acquisition over direct sales, reasoning that an attractive, purpose-built free resource will naturally attract links from parents while converting teachers into paying customers. He frames this as a test of Aaron Wall's teachings about exact match domains outranking older, more established sites.

quotes.quote: Caching is a wonderful tool. You can use it to solve just about any performance problem. It solves it by replacing the problem with a cache expiration problem.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes discovering that a caching bug had been silently killing his content-driven SEO strategy for months. A cron job running under the wrong Unix user failed to clear cached pages, making his dynamic bingo card site appear static and dead to Google. The bug was masked by his development deploys, which incidentally cleared the cache. After fixing the five-minute bug, Google reopened the 'long-tail floodgates' and his traffic more than doubled. The post is a cautionary tale about testing infrastructure with the same rigor as application code.
quotes.key_insight: The infrastructure that silently delivers your content to search engines deserves the same testing rigor as your application code, because a trivial ops bug can dwarf the impact of any feature or marketing initiative.

quotes.quote: This fact isn't very well documented and I've seen folks who have sold $100,000+ with Paypal tell you it is impossible.
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains that PayPal does allow customers to purchase without a PayPal account, despite widespread belief to the contrary among even experienced sellers. He walks through how PayPal's checkout flow presents different interfaces based on whether the customer has a PayPal cookie. He then explains the financial incentives behind this design: PayPal maximizes profit by steering existing users toward balance or ACH payments (which cost less than credit card interchange fees) while still allowing guest checkout to avoid losing conversions from non-members.
quotes.key_insight: PayPal's checkout flow is engineered around their interchange fee economics, which incidentally means it's also optimized to maximize conversions for sellers by automatically presenting the right purchasing option to each customer.

quotes.quote: You have to understand that Paypal's primary source of revenue is interchange fees. And their primary expense? Also interchange fees.

quotes.quote: Recognize that you will be blogging until you stop blogging. That sounds simple, but many people start out with a burst of post-every-day fever, which they cannot sustain over the long haul. Pick a pace which is predictable and sustainable.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reviews 'Blog Blazers,' a book he contributed a chapter to, which collects interviews with 40 successful bloggers including Seth Godin and Jeff Atwood. He highlights the fascinating diversity of answers to identical questions about blogging success, and notes areas where the authors disagree with each other. His own chapter focuses on practical blogging tips for small businesses, and he playfully acknowledges disagreeing with his marketing idol Seth Godin on the value of list posts. He recommends the book as a valuable resource for small software businesses trying to figure out how blogging fits into their marketing strategy.
quotes.key_insight: The best blogging advice comes from studying both aspirational successes and near-peers, and the real skill is extracting principles from common tactics rather than blindly copying formats like list posts.

quotes.quote: I personally can't stand the 'Top Ten Ways To Write A Blog Post' type articles, as aside from being boring and aesthetically unpleasant they turn your blog into a commodity provider of lists.

quotes.quote: I stupidly upgraded to 2.1 on a whim, thinking that I hadn't written any significant code yet so incompatibilities wouldn't bother me — true enough, the only incompatibility was with my mental model of how ActiveRecord worked.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents a frustrating 2.5-hour debugging session caused by Rails 2.1's new partial updates feature silently breaking serialized column saves. When modifying a hash stored in a serialized column, Rails didn't detect the change as 'dirty,' causing database writes to skip the updated data. He presents both a hacky workaround (cloning and reassigning the hash to trigger the dirty bit) and a cleaner solution (disabling partial updates on the model). The post critiques Rails' 'opinionated software' philosophy for introducing breaking changes that are on by default.
quotes.key_insight: Framework upgrades can silently break your mental model of how things work, and 'opinionated' defaults that change behavior without obvious signals create insidious bugs that waste hours of debugging.

quotes.quote: The fact that this is on by default appears to be one of those opinionated software practices where 'opinionated' means 'it is our opinion that you should be as familiar with the change log as the core team before you install a new production release'.

quotes.quote: Word to the wise, kiddies: despite it being so obviously necessary you'd expect them to do it for you, Rails will not automatically slap database indexes on your id columns, and then you get to do full table scans every time you pull in linked objects.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie introduces Widget Bakery, a new project developed during a 30-day sprint, which allows users to create embeddable widgets for third-party websites. He shares progress from an intensive design session with his designer, showing the front page design and a screenshot of a functioning widget. The project involves complex Rails, JavaScript, and iframe integration to inject widgets into any webpage. He notes significant remaining work including GUI development, caching, error handling, and CSS fixes before launch.
quotes.key_insight: Launching a new product during a time-boxed sprint forces pragmatic tradeoffs between showing progress and building robust infrastructure, with database performance gotchas lurking beneath seemingly simple frameworks.

quotes.quote: I honestly think that it is impossible, flat out impossible, for any one person to be expert at all the technologies which are implicated by a trivial web application running on a modern web stack.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that open source software is a powerful strategic asset for proprietary software developers, not a threat. He demonstrates how OSS dramatically reduces development time by letting developers leverage thousands of man-months of existing work while focusing on their unique value-add. He outlines multiple business benefits: lower development costs, SEO advantages from community engagement, and a 'quality cliff' that acts as a barrier to entry against competitors not using OSS. The post reframes the typical proprietary-vs-open-source debate as a false dichotomy, showing how commercial developers can strategically contribute to and consume OSS simultaneously.
quotes.key_insight: Open source software is not a threat to proprietary developers but a force multiplier — the real competitive advantage lies in the skill of integrating diverse OSS projects into a coherent product that competitors cannot easily replicate.

quotes.quote: I'm probably literally using several thousand man months worth of software, adding two man months worth of glue and secret sauce, and then if all goes according to plan making a bit of money off of it.

quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that open source software is a powerful strategic asset for proprietary software developers, not a threat. He demonstrates how OSS dramatically reduces development time by letting developers leverage thousands of man-months of existing work while focusing on their unique value-add. He outlines multiple business benefits: lower development costs, SEO advantages from community engagement, and a 'quality cliff' that acts as a barrier to entry against competitors not using OSS. The post reframes the typical proprietary-vs-open-source debate as a false dichotomy, showing how commercial developers can strategically contribute to and consume OSS simultaneously.
quotes.key_insight: Open source software is not a threat to proprietary developers but a force multiplier — the real competitive advantage lies in the skill of integrating diverse OSS projects into a coherent product that competitors cannot easily replicate.

quotes.quote: Sadly, it did not resolve itself to my benefit, but it is no longer hanging in the air, which means I can get on with life.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports that a stressful personal situation has resolved, though not in his favor. The two weeks lost to worrying have put his 30-day development sprint behind schedule, making it unlikely he'll have a saleable application by the deadline. He's resetting his goal to have something publicly demonstrable by the 1st, with an actual release to follow later. He explains why he can't just ship a half-finished product: since widgets live on third-party sites, bugs could break other people's websites.
quotes.key_insight: When your product runs on other people's websites, you can't ship fast and break things — their reputation is on the line, not just yours.

quotes.quote: I would happily launch with a half-finished app that doesn't even have billing integrated, but the nature of widgets is that they exist on third party sites, and half-finished could very well mean that widgets break on third party sites, which is just not good behavior.

quotes.quote: I spent most of the day fighting old, outdated versions of Capistrano and Deprec to let me get Rails to a deployable state from my Vista workstation to my shiny new 512 MB Slicehost slice.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents a productive day working on his new SaaS product during a 30-day development sprint. He spent most of the day wrestling with deployment tooling (Capistrano, Deprec) to get Rails running on a new Slicehost VPS. He outlines a 12-item implementation agenda for the product, which appears to be a widget platform designed to help users achieve viral distribution. The post captures the unglamorous reality of early-stage product development where deployment infrastructure consumes most of a 'productive' day.
quotes.key_insight: Even highly productive development days are mostly consumed by infrastructure and deployment plumbing rather than building the actual product features.

quotes.quote: I have the artistic ability of paint. Not MSPaint, no, just paint.
quotes.summary: Patrick shares an early proof of concept for an embeddable widget feature for Bingo Card Creator during his 30-day development sprint. The widget allows content to be distributed across external sites like teacher blogs. Despite its rough appearance, he sees the strategic value: viral distribution that spreads content at near-zero marginal cost, replacing expensive CPC advertising. The next step is generalizing the widget so non-technical users can deploy it without Rails programming knowledge.
quotes.key_insight: Embeddable widgets turn your existing customers into a zero-cost distribution channel, replacing expensive paid advertising with viral content that deepens relationships while acquiring new users.

quotes.quote: It propagates itself, for no marginal cost whatsoever, spreads the content I spent so much time and money creating, and gets me new visitors and eventually customers at a bare fraction of the cost of CPC advertising.

quotes.quote: I remember back when Andrey was still comparing his sales to mine to see if he was on a good track.
quotes.summary: McKenzie notes that the Antair team, fellow micro-ISV entrepreneurs he has followed, are moving up to bigger operations with new offices. He reflects on how Andrey from Antair used to benchmark his sales against McKenzie's, but has since surpassed him significantly. The brief post serves as a collegial shout-out within the small software business community.
quotes.key_insight: In small software entrepreneurship communities, founders openly benchmark against peers, and watching colleagues outgrow you is both humbling and motivating.

quotes.quote: Suffice it to say that he's since made it necessary for me to play a wee bit of catchup.
