quotes.quote: I really, really can't stand the name — it says absolutely nothing positive to my customers about my business.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a practical guide for micro-ISV owners using e-junkie as their payment processor to hide the e-junkie branding from customers. He covers three specific issues: redirecting customers to your own thank-you page after checkout, fixing email deliverability problems caused by SPF record mismatches, and using DNS CNAME records to mask e-junkie URLs. The post reflects his philosophy that professional branding matters even for small software businesses. He emphasizes security considerations when implementing URL-based parameter passing and notes that these small details affect conversion rates and customer trust.
quotes.key_insight: Small software businesses should invest effort in hiding third-party service branding from their checkout flow, because professional presentation directly affects customer trust, email deliverability, and conversion rates.

quotes.quote: Nice update interface, very user friendly — a pity about it taking 400 clickthroughs to complete installation (I kept track).
quotes.summary: McKenzie recounts a day where his planned prototype work was derailed by helping college friends migrate an old VBulletin forum to his VPS. He describes the horror of the forum's previous security state, including outdated PHP, MySQL running as root, and a trivially guessable password. He pivots to praising VPS hosting over shared hosting for technically skilled developers, noting he runs multiple sites cheaply on Slicehost. The post closes with his hope that his new project will need serious infrastructure scaling.
quotes.key_insight: The best problem a software entrepreneur can have is needing to scale, and cheap VPS hosting makes it easy to run multiple small businesses affordably while maintaining control.

quotes.quote: I wanted to cry when I saw its previous state — using an outdated PHP and MySql running as root, naturally, with a four letter password the same as the name of the previous site owner.

quotes.quote: I *love it* when other people do my work for me.
quotes.summary: Patrick reports progress on his 30-day sprint to build a widget product, having solved a key technical challenge around making widgets viral using a modified Lightbox library. He has a working JavaScript front-end prototype but needs to build the back end. His weekend plan is to create a Bingo Card Creator-specific widget as a proof of concept that can drive immediate business value while he continues building the full widget creation platform.
quotes.key_insight: When building a platform product, start by creating a specific instance for your own existing business to validate the concept and generate immediate value before tackling the general case.

quotes.quote: Plus, score, it will start driving business value for me even without having the widget creator website up.

quotes.quote: Your program name is also your USP. Sweet deal.
quotes.summary: McKenzie highlights 'Don't Wreck My PC' as the best app name among participants in a development sprint. He praises the name for its brandability and ability to serve as both a product name and unique selling proposition. He also compliments the logo's bright, colorful design and recommends skinning the app to match. He shares that making his own app look more 'Fisher Pricey' improved conversions by about 50%.
quotes.key_insight: The best product names simultaneously serve as a unique selling proposition, and matching your app's visual design to a bright, approachable aesthetic can dramatically improve conversion rates in B2C markets.

quotes.quote: Bright and colorful does great things in B2C.

quotes.quote: I love it when people do things I was meaning to get to… less time typing means more time…. typing.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a brief update on the 30 Day Sprint challenge, pointing readers to a fellow participant's summary chart of all participants. He outlines his own modest agenda for the evening: watching BSG, setting up a Rails environment, and building a first proof-of-concept RSS widget.
quotes.key_insight: Start with the simplest possible proof of concept and let community members handle organizational overhead so you can focus on building.

quotes.quote: This was much easier last time, when I was at the job which got out at 4:30 and has a 20 minute commute, rather than the one that got out at 9:00 with the 2 hour commute…
quotes.summary: McKenzie reflects on the difficulty of maintaining a side project development schedule while working a demanding day job with long hours and a brutal commute. He describes an intellectually satisfying day at work where he wrote 800 lines of elegant code solving a complex constraint satisfaction problem, comparing it to a zoo exhibit placement puzzle. Despite making no progress on his own projects, he finds satisfaction in the craftsmanship of his day job work, while acknowledging the tension between employment demands and entrepreneurial ambitions.
quotes.key_insight: The tension between a demanding day job and side project ambitions is real, and the intellectual seduction of hard engineering problems at work can further delay entrepreneurial progress.

quotes.quote: I did manage to put some of the finishing touches on the best 800 lines of code I've ever written.

quotes.quote: After getting out of work at 8 PM (early) and back home at 12 PM (typhoon) I find myself with not an amazing amount of productive energy for today.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports a low-productivity day during his 30-day development sprint, having only sketched some interface screens after a long workday and typhoon-delayed commute. He highlights other participants in the 30-day challenge, particularly a poker graphing utility, noting his curiosity about marketing in a spam-saturated niche. The post is a brief daily check-in reflecting the reality that not every day in a sprint produces meaningful output.
quotes.key_insight: Even during focused development sprints, the reality of a day job and life circumstances means some days produce little more than throwaway sketches, and that's acceptable.

quotes.quote: I pay about twenty two cents to get my software in front of people. If you had said that to an AOL exec in the nineties, he would have paid me a few hundred million and told me to print enough CDs to wallpaper every home in Africa.
quotes.summary: McKenzie kicks off a 30-day sprint to build a new product: a widget-building platform for small businesses. He argues that while the Internet made spreading ideas cheap, the actual mechanics of sharing — linking — remain surprisingly hard for non-technical people. He proposes a service that lets businesses create embeddable widgets without programming expertise, combining data sources and presentations through a simple interface. The business model is subscription-based, with tiered pricing tied to feature access. He sets deliberately modest growth goals: one customer, then ten, then a hundred, then a few hundred paying subscribers — enough to support him indefinitely.
quotes.key_insight: The Internet made idea-spreading cheap but left the actual mechanics of sharing — embedding rich, interactive content — inaccessibly hard for non-technical businesses, creating a market opportunity for a widget-building platform.

quotes.quote: After all that effort spent in getting your fans to care about you, we consistently fail at giving them the tools to show the world they care.

quotes.quote: And what did mankind come up with once it started embracing widgets? Well, a lot of Would You Survive The Zombie Apocalypse quizzes and What Kind of My Little Pony Are You badges. But there is the potential for so much more.
quotes.summary: McKenzie kicks off a 30-day sprint to build a new product: a widget-building platform for small businesses. He argues that while the Internet made spreading ideas cheap, the actual mechanics of sharing — linking — remain surprisingly hard for non-technical people. He proposes a service that lets businesses create embeddable widgets without programming expertise, combining data sources and presentations through a simple interface. The business model is subscription-based, with tiered pricing tied to feature access. He sets deliberately modest growth goals: one customer, then ten, then a hundred, then a few hundred paying subscribers — enough to support him indefinitely.

quotes.quote: I was sort of expecting maybe one or two folks to actually get back to me about the 30 day June cooperative development sprint. There are, hmm, substantially more than two folks interested.
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces unexpectedly strong interest in his proposed 30-day cooperative development sprint for June 2008. What he expected to attract one or two participants has drawn a substantial group of indie software developers. He and co-organizer Sohail set up a unified RSS feed to aggregate participants' blog posts about the challenge, keeping administration minimal. The post lists roughly a dozen participants and invites others to add their feed URLs.
quotes.key_insight: Public commitment and lightweight community structures can generate surprising momentum for indie software development sprints.

quotes.quote: It requires programming effort, it is really difficult for a technically disinclined person, it affects a lot of link-chucking people, it gets in the way of some people making money, it has a solve-once apply-anywhere model to it.
quotes.summary: Patrick announces he's identified a new software product opportunity while working on Bingo Card Creator. The problem he encountered meets his criteria for a viable micro-ISV product: it requires technical skill, affects many non-technical users, and has a solve-once-apply-everywhere model. He commits to a 30-day development sprint starting June 1st, inspired by the Business of Software forums' 30 Day Challenge, with a goal of offering the product to paying customers by July 1st. He invites other developers to join him in an informal parallel development effort, blogging about progress together.
quotes.key_insight: A viable micro-ISV product opportunity reveals itself when you encounter a problem that is technically solvable, painful for non-technical users, widely applicable, and blocks people from making money.

quotes.quote: I have a simpler explanation: there is no pay premium for engineering over a normal college degree, which means that the starting salary for a computer programmer is about $24,000, and you're more or less required to work 12 hour days at a minimum.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a casual life update after a month-long blogging hiatus. He describes his day job responsibilities managing Indian colleagues visiting Japan, minimal progress on Bingo Card Creator, and early research on a next project. He offers a pointed aside about Japan's engineering shortage, attributing it to poor pay rather than cultural factors. Most of his free time has been consumed by a Japanese video game rather than his software business.
quotes.key_insight: Japan's engineering talent shortage is fundamentally an economic problem — when engineering pays no premium over other degrees and demands brutal hours, rational people choose other careers.

quotes.quote: Given that I'm the only person at the English who speaks English and doesn't answer to "Mr. President", it looks like I'm going to end up being their manager one way or another.

quotes.quote: Every time I see a good, pretty icon set I get the urge to make an application just to be able to use them.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes feeling the entrepreneurial urge to build something new after a period of focusing on his day job. A coworker's simple question about why he doesn't just build something forces him to confront his own inertia. He acknowledges he has the funds, knowledge, and capability but lacks time due to his demanding job. He's considering a web application that can be built in short sprints, and mentions a recurring dream about an application with a terrible business model.
quotes.key_insight: The gap between knowing you should build something and actually starting is often not resources or knowledge but simple inertia, easily broken by someone asking the right question.

quotes.quote: Us software developers don't typically start with massive amounts of marketing or sales talent, and getting a link is effectively selling someone on the proposition that you're worthy of them spending their time, attention, and social capital on you.
quotes.summary: McKenzie presents a comprehensive guide to link building for small software businesses, drawing from his experience promoting Bingo Card Creator. He argues that getting links is fundamentally a sales problem — convincing webmasters that linking to you serves their audience. The post outlines eight strategies ranging from community building and blogging to creating scalable resources and writing authoritative evergreen content. He emphasizes that commercial sites can overcome the anti-commercial bias of the web by providing genuine value to communities beyond their direct customers. The core philosophy is that generosity, community engagement, and creating genuinely useful resources naturally attract links without explicit solicitation.
quotes.key_insight: Link building for small businesses succeeds not through direct solicitation but by creating genuinely valuable resources and community relationships that make linking to you an obvious choice for others.

quotes.quote: Folks who make money with websites are not that great at explaining the value of linking to that website to people who will not see money from the link.

quotes.quote: One thing folks sometime neglect when making a webapp, and I'm certainly guilty, is making it look gorgeous.
quotes.summary: McKenzie briefly reviews Clicky, a new web analytics tool positioned as an alternative to Google Analytics. He notes it doesn't offer much unique functionality for micro-ISVs but has some usability advantages like automatic tracking of downloads and links. He highlights the importance of visual design in web applications, admitting that Clicky's attractive appearance was the primary reason he signed up.
quotes.key_insight: Even technically sophisticated users are swayed by attractive design, making visual polish a legitimate competitive advantage for web applications.

quotes.quote: If you're just starting out your uISV journey, do as I say and not as I do here: obscure in-jokes which are not easy to spell are probably not the best of ideas.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces moving his blog from WordPress.com to a self-hosted WordPress installation at kalzumeus.com. He explains the origin of the Kalzumeus name — a dragon character from a story he wrote years ago — and acknowledges it's not ideal branding. The bulk of the post provides a detailed technical walkthrough for migrating a WordPress.com blog to a self-hosted domain without losing search engine rankings or breaking existing links. He uses the 301 redirect approach via WordPress's $10/year domain mapping feature as a bridge to preserve SEO juice during the transition.
quotes.key_insight: When migrating a blog to a new domain, preserving your search engine equity through proper 301 redirects is more important than the move itself.

quotes.quote: My front page can potentially take 4 seconds to load (longer than a significant portion of visitors stay!) I didn't want that.
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains how browser download queues were causing his sidebar conversion buttons to load last, hurting conversions. He discovered that browsers maintain a FIFO queue per hostname and only download two assets at a time per queue, meaning large screenshots were blocking his important button images. His solution was to set up multiple subdomains (asset hosts) via Nginx to parallelize downloads, then hand-optimize which images loaded on which domain to prioritize conversion-critical assets. The post demonstrates his characteristic obsession with optimizing every element of the conversion funnel, down to browser rendering order.
quotes.key_insight: Page load performance is a conversion optimization problem — understanding browser download mechanics and prioritizing which assets load first can directly impact whether visitors click your conversion buttons.

quotes.quote: This is to push up the main content area in the document, because search engines treat stuff near the top as more important and I want pages to rank for their own content not the content of pages they happen to link to on the sidebar.

quotes.quote: One of the screenshots isn't even visible when you open the page (it is far below the fold, most people won't even see it!)
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains how browser download queues were causing his sidebar conversion buttons to load last, hurting conversions. He discovered that browsers maintain a FIFO queue per hostname and only download two assets at a time per queue, meaning large screenshots were blocking his important button images. His solution was to set up multiple subdomains (asset hosts) via Nginx to parallelize downloads, then hand-optimize which images loaded on which domain to prioritize conversion-critical assets. The post demonstrates his characteristic obsession with optimizing every element of the conversion funnel, down to browser rendering order.
quotes.key_insight: Page load performance is a conversion optimization problem — understanding browser download mechanics and prioritizing which assets load first can directly impact whether visitors click your conversion buttons.

quotes.quote: In terms of driving downloads, download sites are all but worthless.
quotes.summary: McKenzie addresses the common question of whether download sites matter for micro-ISV promotion. He presents data showing that download sites drive virtually zero confirmed installs for Bingo Card Creator. While he still recommends submitting to download sites for SEO backlink benefits, he concludes they are worthless for driving actual downloads. Users simply Google what they need rather than browsing download directories.
quotes.key_insight: Download sites are worthless for driving actual software downloads because users search Google directly, but they retain marginal value as a source of SEO backlinks.

quotes.quote: It is easy to figure out why if you watch users looking for software: rather than going to www.download.com and searching for it, they just Google it to start out with.

quotes.quote: Since it is technically difficult to split test the header on my current setup I'm going to have to sort of fudge it (i.e. look at the metrics for the next two weeks and see if it causes major changes or not).
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents ongoing graphical updates to his Bingo Card Creator website after the Easter traffic surge. He shows before-and-after screenshots of the site redesign, including new logos and buttons created by Logo Samurai with integration by a contractor. He acknowledges the lack of rigorous A/B testing for the header change due to technical limitations, settling instead for a simple before-and-after metrics comparison.
quotes.key_insight: Even when proper A/B testing isn't feasible, monitoring metrics before and after a change still provides useful directional data.

quotes.quote: If you have done any significant reading on the topics, this e-book will not teach you much that you don't know.
quotes.summary: Patrick reviews Steph Grenier's ebook on generating website traffic, finding it too introductory for anyone already familiar with internet marketing basics. The book covers 11 topics in roughly 11 pages each, resulting in surface-level treatment that won't teach experienced ISV operators anything new. He singles out pages 52-54, a case study of using a free calculator as linkbait, as genuinely excellent and wishes the entire book matched that level of specificity. He also raises concerns about affiliate links embedded within ebook content, warning that the practice blurs the line between genuine advice and paid promotion, and could turn the microISV community into a pyramid of people selling marketing ebooks to each other.
quotes.key_insight: Marketing advice grounded in specific case studies with real data is exponentially more valuable than generic introductory material, and communities should be wary of affiliate-driven content ecosystems that generate revenue from within rather than from external customers.

quotes.quote: There is a point after which optimizing for metrics not measured in dollars or other objective measurements of customer interest ceases to be productive.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recommends several newer micro-ISV (uISV) blogs to his readers, noting he tends to link only to established names. He highlights an A/B testing optimization blog, a tracker following the 'Class of 2007' micro-ISVs, and Planet MicroISV as an aggregator. Along the way, he offers a brief but pointed observation about the diminishing returns of optimizing for vanity metrics rather than revenue.
quotes.key_insight: Optimizing for metrics that don't directly measure dollars or genuine customer interest eventually becomes unproductive, no matter how satisfying the charts look.

quotes.quote: I don't link out to uISV bloggers often enough and when I do it is often to the usual suspects.

quotes.quote: Rewriting old evergreen content to make it better bears some fruits.
quotes.summary: McKenzie highlights his brother's blog optimization experiments for a superhero novel site, noting that clearer header art communicating the blog's unique selling proposition increased reader retention by 25%. He connects this to his own experiments with evergreen content on Bingo Card Creator, where reducing the number of free bingo cards offered on a page paradoxically increased downloads, doubled time on site, and shifted conversions from low-value card downloads to higher-value trial downloads.
quotes.key_insight: Offering less free content can increase both engagement and the quality of conversions by pushing users toward higher-value actions.

quotes.quote: I can vouch for my brother's finding on "adding by subtracting" — decreasing the number of free bingo cards offered on one page greatly increased the percentage of visitors who downloaded.

quotes.quote: It is physically impossible for anyone to get from the sales data to any identifiable information about customers, which would be 101 flavors of bad.
quotes.summary: Patrick announces a new feature for semi-automatically generating monthly sales reports for Bingo Card Creator. He explains that while the more impressive backend features can't be shown publicly due to customer data integration, he has made the monthly sales report completely anonymized. He emphasizes his paranoid approach to customer data privacy, noting the backend code isn't even on the production server.
quotes.key_insight: Sharing business metrics publicly builds trust and community, but customer data privacy must be treated as an absolute constraint rather than a tradeoff.

quotes.quote: The actual backend code is not even uploaded on the production copy of the server. I'm a little paranoid, what can I say.

quotes.quote: I don't want my opinion on what my customers want to calcify and blind me to the actual facts of what they act upon.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his ongoing A/B testing strategy for website buttons on Bingo Card Creator. He commissioned custom button designs from two different freelancers—LogoSamurai and his web designer Gursimran—to test against his existing buttons. He emphasizes letting data drive design decisions rather than personal opinion, and argues that paying freelancers fairly is both ethical and strategically smart for maintaining reliable talent relationships.
quotes.key_insight: Every visual element on your site is a testable hypothesis, and the cost of testing design variations is trivially small compared to the potential revenue gains from even marginal conversion improvements.

quotes.quote: Better data visualizations lead to better decisionmaking.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes adding backend statistics tracking to Bingo Card Creator to supplement Google Analytics. He's teaching himself the Plotkit JavaScript library to create client-side charts that won't burden his server. He shares a proof-of-concept graph of bingo card downloads and outlines two goals: better data visualization for decision-making, and automating monthly sales reporting to reduce manual effort.
quotes.key_insight: Building custom analytics and automating reporting is a form of strategic laziness that both improves decision-making and eliminates recurring manual work.

quotes.quote: I'm sort of at a loss as to what is causing the sales to go down. Its possible that is just natural fluctuation, as all of the pre-sales indicators are way the heck up.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports the results of merging two sites (Daily Bingo Cards and Bingo Card Creator) and redesigning the combined site. Traffic, engagement, and trial downloads all improved significantly, but actual sales dropped from 2 per day to slightly under 1. He hypothesizes this could be natural fluctuation or existing trial users being confused by the new branding. He also shares CrazyEgg heatmap data showing how the redesign focused user attention.
quotes.key_insight: Improving all upstream engagement metrics through a site redesign doesn't guarantee improved sales — brand continuity and the existing user pipeline can cause conversion to temporarily drop even as every other number improves.

quotes.quote: I also might have some folks working through the trial pipeline who remember the old branding and get turned off by the new design — "This isn't the right site!" is a very serious worry in my market.

quotes.quote: One less step in the conversion funnel, and instantaneous recovery of a large portion of the 20% of folks who bounce out of the funnel at the download page.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes a JavaScript technique for streamlining the software download funnel by eliminating a separate download page. The approach uses a setTimeout redirect to send users to a confirmation page after their download initiates, avoiding browser security warnings. He also implements server-side user-agent detection in Rails to serve the correct installer for Mac vs PC users. The post includes practical warnings about Rails send_file blocking and bot crawling issues.
quotes.key_insight: Removing unnecessary intermediate pages from your download funnel and using clever JavaScript timing can recover a significant fraction of users who would otherwise abandon the process.

quotes.key_insight: Removing unnecessary intermediate pages from your download funnel and using clever JavaScript timing can recover a significant fraction of users who would otherwise abandon the process.

quotes.quote: The scam is that if you hit that Continue button or the link right next to it, your credit card gets charged $9 a month until you call up the number on your statement to cancel it.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie discovers that Regsoft, a payment processor for software sales, has adopted the same deceptive practice previously used by SWREG: adding a hidden subscription charge during checkout that bills customers $9/month unless they actively cancel. He notes the irony that the same processor flagged his legitimate purchase for fraud screening due to his Japan IP address and US billing address, while simultaneously running what he considers a scam. The post serves as a warning to fellow micro-ISV developers about predatory payment processor practices.
quotes.key_insight: Payment processors that serve small software developers sometimes exploit the checkout flow with deceptive subscription charges, creating a conflict of interest where the processor profits at the expense of the developer's customers.

quotes.quote: IP address in Japan + billing address in America = fraud screening. I need to remember to use my US proxy server next time, so that my profile looks like a smart crook rather than a dumb, legitimate customer.

quotes.quote: Heck, this is the Internet: you should ALWAYS consider the possibility of folks accessing your site in essentially a random manner.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discusses Google Sitelinks appearing for his small software business, noting that this branding feature previously reserved for major corporations has been democratized to include micro-ISVs. He observes that 70% of his traffic enters through pages other than the homepage, making it critical to design every page as a potential landing page. He also highlights the defensive value of sitelinks in protecting brand searches from download sites and typosquatters who arbitrage off his brand equity.
quotes.key_insight: Every page on your site is a potential landing page, and brand protection in search results matters even for the smallest software businesses.

quotes.quote: The little Marketing Bob angel on my shoulder kept saying 'Patrick, Patrick, where are your credibility markers?' until I gave in.
quotes.summary: McKenzie details three significant changes to his Bingo Card Creator website: adding customer testimonials to the purchasing page, implementing a smart sidebar linking strategy for SEO, and fixing a duplicate content issue caused by capitalized URLs. The testimonials were added after persistent internal prodding about credibility markers. The sidebar linking strategy rotates holiday-themed and random content links to distribute link juice across pages. The URL fix involved 301 redirects from capitalized URLs to their lowercase canonical versions to avoid duplicate content penalties.
quotes.key_insight: Small technical details like URL canonicalization and strategic internal linking architecture have outsized impact on SEO and conversions for small software businesses.

quotes.quote: I have some reservations that it might be a little too hip for my customers.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares his experience getting new logo candidates for Bingo Card Creator after feedback that the existing logo clashed with his site redesign's color scheme. He used LogoSamurai, a service whose art style appealed to him despite concerns it might be too trendy for his target audience. The bulk of the post focuses on his admiration for LogoSamurai's checkout upsell strategy, which offered two additional logos for $50 more on a $67 purchase. He highlights how this upsell reduces buyer risk while increasing average order value.
quotes.key_insight: A well-designed upsell that reduces the buyer's perceived risk while increasing order value is one of the most effective conversion tactics, especially when the customer has already committed to purchasing.

quotes.quote: My name is Patrick McKenzie and I run a little business called Bingo Card Creator, which Google and company send big spikes of traffic to every holiday.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie promotes his Bingo Card Creator's free Saint Patrick's Day bingo cards, using the post as both SEO-driven content marketing and personal storytelling. He shares his personal connection to Saint Patrick, including taking the name at Confirmation, and recounts the history of the saint as a nobleman's son who was enslaved in Ireland and returned voluntarily. The post pivots to a charming anecdote about the St. Patrick's Day parade in Nagoya, Japan, which starts and ends at a Buddhist temple. A Japanese attendee explains the cross-cultural appeal: both nations are islands that love festivals and know how to party.
quotes.key_insight: Holiday-themed content pages are a reliable SEO strategy for capturing seasonal search traffic, and embedding genuine personal narrative makes promotional content feel authentic rather than cynical.

quotes.quote: I really lack design skills. Sure, I can edit HTML and modify CSS if given a template to work with, but visually I'm just totally lost.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents his experience outsourcing web design for Bingo Card Creator through eLance, from writing the project spec through selecting a designer and iterating on the final product. He was flooded with low-quality copy-paste proposals but found one standout designer, Gursimran, whose proposal demonstrated she had actually read his requirements and understood design rather than just listing tools. After several iterations on color schemes and cultural icon adjustments, he received a solid template that he then customized himself. The post serves as a practical guide to outsourcing design work as a technical founder with no visual skills.
quotes.key_insight: When outsourcing creative work, the quality of your project brief determines the quality of proposals you attract, and the best freelancers reveal themselves by engaging with your actual requirements rather than listing their tools.

quotes.quote: Essentially every uISV starts out with a five page site which functions as a product brochure and can't sell their product worth garbage.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews Bob Walsh's e-book 'Micro-ISV Sites that Sell!' despite his strong general dislike of e-books as a format. The book treats marketing concepts like Unique Selling Propositions (USPs) as engineering design patterns, making them accessible and systematic for software developers. McKenzie highlights how the book helped him recognize marketing improvements on his own Bingo Card Creator site, and praises Walsh's case-study approach. While noting some structural issues and arguing the $19 price is far too low for its value, McKenzie gives the book a strong recommendation as a practical marketing resource for software engineers.
quotes.key_insight: Marketing becomes tractable for engineers when treated as a design pattern — something you can name, study, systemize, and iteratively test rather than learn through years of painful trial and error.

quotes.quote: After you've named something, you can study it, and how it interacts with other named things. After you've studied it, you can systemize it. And after you've systemized it, you can replicate the success of your implementation of it.

quotes.quote: I had assumed, having set the compiler to use version 1.3 in Eclipse, that I was getting Java 1.3 class files.
quotes.summary: McKenzie asks readers to help beta test his Bingo Card Creator software after discovering a Java compatibility bug. He had been compiling with Eclipse set to Java 1.3 compatibility, but switching to an Ant build script silently changed the compilation target to Java 1.6. Since he only had Java 1.6 on his own machine, he never noticed the breaking change for users running older Java versions.
quotes.key_insight: Build tool defaults can silently break backward compatibility when you switch toolchains, and you won't catch it if you only test on your own machine.

quotes.quote: I made an ant script and, boom, it stopped working the first time I ran the clear target and Ant used the (defaults to 1.6) javac target to make the new build.

quotes.quote: That suggests to me either a problem customer or a borked executable, which has happened to me on GoDaddy in the past.
quotes.summary: McKenzie asks readers for help testing his software after a customer reported installation failures on five Windows XP machines. He's trying to determine whether the issue is a problematic customer or a corrupted executable, which has happened before on GoDaddy hosting. Recent slow sales add urgency to the investigation. He notes the software works fine on his own Vista machine but needs XP-specific confirmation.
quotes.key_insight: When sales dip unexpectedly, investigate whether customers can even complete the download and installation process before assuming a marketing problem.

quotes.quote: It's so true it hurts, as I have to keep reminding myself every time I slip another place on [bingo cards] (oh no, my traffic numbers!) and gain another place on [need bingo cards for reading class] (oh yes, my wallet!)
quotes.summary: McKenzie recommends Aaron Wall's Search Value Pyramid as the single most important resource for understanding niche marketing. He illustrates the concept with his own experience: losing rankings on broad, generic terms like 'bingo cards' matters less than gaining rankings on specific, high-intent search queries like 'need bingo cards for reading class.' The post is a brief but pointed reminder that search traffic quality matters far more than quantity.
quotes.key_insight: A single ranking gain on a specific, high-intent search query is worth more to your business than ranking on a broad, high-traffic keyword.

quotes.quote: Friends from high school do not routinely drop by Japanese rice fields.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces a brief one-week blogging hiatus because a high school friend is visiting him in rural Japan. He notes the rarity of such visits given his remote location and prioritizes hosting over writing. The post is essentially a short personal note to readers.
quotes.key_insight: A brief personal note revealing McKenzie's life in rural Japan and his commitment to keeping readers informed even during short breaks.

quotes.quote: I don't see blogging as an income generating activity. I think of it more as an investment — do the work now, reap the benefits later.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie explains why he doesn't monetize his blog with affiliate links or ads despite regular suggestions to do so. He argues the blog's value lies in building trust, establishing expertise, opening doors to opportunities, and networking — all of which would be degraded by chasing affiliate revenue. He breaks down the math showing affiliate income would be modest compared to what he earns by directing effort toward his own product. The post articulates a philosophy where long-term relationship capital vastly outweighs short-term monetization.
quotes.key_insight: A blog's greatest monetization is the trust and expertise it builds over time, which opens doors worth far more than affiliate revenue would ever generate.

quotes.quote: How many of you would be interested in reading the Make Patrick Five Bucks By Acting On This Post blog? Yeah, that's right. There is no reason for you to read that blog, whatsoever.

quotes.quote: I am planning on doing some serious Rails-based work on the main site itself, starting with some backend improvements (conversion tracking the way Google *should* be doing it, for starters — "first click wins" not "last click wins")
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes transitioning Bingo Card Creator from GoDaddy shared hosting to a Slicehost VPS as his site outgrows shared hosting. The move is driven by plans to integrate multiple sites and do serious Rails development, including custom conversion tracking and automated marketing experiments. He details the smooth migration of email to Google Apps for Domains and his strategy of serving static HTML to handle traffic spikes. Despite leaving, he recommends GoDaddy as a solid choice for new micro-ISVs.
quotes.key_insight: Infrastructure decisions for a small software business should be driven by what the business needs next, not loyalty to a provider — and custom analytics that match your actual business model beat off-the-shelf defaults.

quotes.quote: stupid Windows Notepad inserting a byte order mark into UTF8 text, stupid Java not reading BOMs in UTF8 despite them being in the standard, stupid me for letting this delay a release by almost a month
quotes.summary: Patrick announces that Bingo Card Creator 2.0 is finally complete after a month-long delay caused by a UTF-8 byte order mark compatibility issue between Windows Notepad and Java. The release adds roughly 200 new bingo card wizards, better international character support, and improved printing. He plans to release it alongside a site redesign and has deployed it to Daily Bingo Cards as an early test. He also notes he forgot to pre-announce a planned price increase and will delay it another month.
quotes.key_insight: Small technical gotchas like character encoding issues can delay releases for weeks, and trial restrictions should be evaluated by whether they actually drive sales rather than just frustrate users.

quotes.quote: One of the reasons to have a blog is that it allows you to establish a reputation as an expert in your field.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that blogging serves as a powerful personal marketing tool by establishing expertise and opening professional doors. He cites being contacted by the CEO of Dzone to repost his content as evidence. He advocates for prioritizing content spread over control, and for creating evergreen content that retains value over years. He also reveals he avoided posting his photo for two years due to fear of age-based judgment.
quotes.key_insight: A blog that demonstrates genuine expertise creates compounding returns by attracting inbound opportunities that would never arise through traditional networking alone.

quotes.key_insight: A brief reader engagement post signaling upcoming SEO content for software businesses with large-scale sites.

quotes.quote: I figured as long as I was going to have to edit every page on a website might as well use the opporunity to make it look pretty.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces he's hiring a web designer to redesign the Bingo Card Creator website. The project involves merging Daily Bingo Cards into the main BCC site to leverage BCC's superior link juice for ranking DBC's content while providing fresh daily content to BCC. He provides detailed design requirements targeting his core demographic of non-technical women in their 30s-40s, specifying a clean Web 2.0 aesthetic. The budget is $300-500 with a two-week timeline.
quotes.key_insight: Strategic site consolidation can simultaneously improve SEO through link equity consolidation, provide fresh content, and justify a design refresh — turning a technical migration into a marketing upgrade.

quotes.quote: You can keep the old one if you don't want the goodies. It will continue working for at least another year, but will receive no new updates.
quotes.summary: Google released a major update to Google Analytics requiring users to update their tracking scripts in exchange for significant new features. The update includes download tracking, dynamic content tracking, improved e-commerce conversion tracking, and new reports. The old version will continue working for at least a year but will receive no new updates. McKenzie notes the migration is slightly more complex than previous Analytics setup but manageable for programmers.
quotes.key_insight: Google Analytics' migration to ga.js offered ISVs meaningful new tracking capabilities — especially download and e-commerce conversion tracking — worth the modest migration effort.

quotes.quote: If you can afford to hire a particular SEO firm, they probably aren't competent enough for your needs.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that hiring an SEO consultant is fundamentally irrational because the incentive structure of SEO means anyone truly skilled at it would make far more money applying those skills to their own projects. He walks through the math using his own bingo card business, showing that a #1 ranking on even a minor search term could generate $10,000-$20,000/month. He systematically dismantles every possible billing arrangement (hourly, upfront, equity, partnership) and shows why none work for a genuinely skilled SEO. The conclusion is that the SEO consulting market suffers from adverse selection: the best practitioners leave to run their own businesses, leaving clients to choose among the unskilled remainder.
quotes.key_insight: The SEO consulting market is broken by adverse selection: anyone skilled enough to deliver results can earn far more applying those skills to their own ventures, so the talent pool available for hire is systematically depleted of its best practitioners.

quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that hiring an SEO consultant is fundamentally irrational because the incentive structure of SEO means anyone truly skilled at it would make far more money applying those skills to their own projects. He walks through the math using his own bingo card business, showing that a #1 ranking on even a minor search term could generate $10,000-$20,000/month. He systematically dismantles every possible billing arrangement (hourly, upfront, equity, partnership) and shows why none work for a genuinely skilled SEO. The conclusion is that the SEO consulting market suffers from adverse selection: the best practitioners leave to run their own businesses, leaving clients to choose among the unskilled remainder.
quotes.key_insight: The SEO consulting market is broken by adverse selection: anyone skilled enough to deliver results can earn far more applying those skills to their own ventures, so the talent pool available for hire is systematically depleted of its best practitioners.

quotes.quote: On an Internet which is chock-full of scam artists and folks wanting to make a fast buck at others' expense, you can stand out pretty easily just by bucking the trend.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes how Google selected his Bingo Card Creator business as a case study for their Conversion Optimizer product. He attributes being chosen to his early adoption, high search rankings for the product name, radical transparency with business statistics, and authenticity. He argues that transparency and authenticity are powerful differentiators for small software businesses because most competitors won't or can't replicate them. He also reports continued strong results from Conversion Optimizer across both content and search campaigns.
quotes.key_insight: Radical transparency and authenticity are among the few truly uncloneable competitive advantages for small software businesses, creating opportunities that competitors with identical products simply cannot access.

quotes.quote: If you have 15 competitors and you're the only one who is radically transparent about your business, then for many opportunities you have no competitors.

quotes.quote: Put your Apache config files in version control because if you bork your redirection code and don't remember how you got it working the first time you will be sorry.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares that his brother's blog post went viral after being featured on Fark, overwhelming their shared VPS hosting. He spent an hour stabilizing the server configuration to handle the traffic spike. The post serves as a brief anecdote about viral content and a practical warning about server administration.
quotes.key_insight: Always version control your server configuration files, because emergency changes under pressure are when you're most likely to break things irreversibly.

quotes.quote: Awareness of how much Google does to upend the expectation of privacy hasn't quite seeped in yet.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a small but meaningful moment: discovering an archived mailing list where educators were enthusiastically recommending Bingo Card Creator for teaching reading. He deliberately doesn't link to the discussion out of respect for the participants' privacy, noting that most people don't realize how much of their online activity is searchable. He reflects that while the income and intellectual challenge are nice, the most satisfying aspect is knowing his software genuinely helps teachers and kids.
quotes.key_insight: The most rewarding signal for a small software business isn't revenue but discovering that real users genuinely love what you've built.

quotes.quote: I'm most happy when folks mention how psyched they are about using it to teach their kids.

quotes.quote: Based on the stunning success of my last call for freelancers, I decided to do this here rather than wasting time and money going through one of the freelancer sites.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie posts a job listing for a freelance web designer to reskin a bingo card template for a new linkbait project. He offers $100 for what he estimates is 1-2 hours of work, payable via check or PayPal. The post reveals his strategy of using topical, viral content tied to bingo cards to drive traffic and links. He leverages his blog audience and reputation to recruit directly rather than using freelancer marketplaces, and sweetens the deal with a footer link that he expects will get tens of thousands of views.
quotes.key_insight: A blog with a trusted audience becomes a more effective recruiting channel than freelancer marketplaces, letting you hire faster, cheaper, and with better cultural fit.

quotes.quote: 2007 was my first full year running Bingo Card Creator, and I had impressive growth over last year (about a factor of four on both profits and sales, looking at my last tax return).
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews his first full year running Bingo Card Creator, reporting 4x growth in both profits and sales to reach $10,500 gross income and ~$6,200 profit. He breaks down expenses including AdWords, hosting, and freelancers, noting his effective hourly rate beats his day job at ~$60/hour. He highlights key wins including SEO improvements, launching Daily Bingo Cards, Google's Conversion Optimizer, and blogging as a marketing channel. He sets ambitious 2008 goals of doubling sales to $20,000-$25,000 and releasing a new product.
quotes.key_insight: A solo software business can achieve meaningful income growth through compounding small improvements in boring fundamentals like SEO, page design, and ad optimization, while blogging creates outsized opportunities far beyond its direct marketing value.

quotes.quote: If you've got it, flaunt it.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie kicks off 2008 with a quick update on his plans for Bingo Card Creator and his blog. He announces version 2.0 with improved printing and 200 new word lists, a price increase, and a pending Google-related announcement. He also teases upcoming blog posts about competitors and product naming, while acknowledging his month-long absence due to work and family visits.
quotes.key_insight: Consistently shipping product improvements, raising prices, and leveraging side projects for content are the unglamorous building blocks of a growing micro-ISV.

quotes.quote: See you in early January for my announcement with Google, the official launch of Bingo Card Creator 2.0, the year-end wrapup for 2007, and (possibly, depending on the amount of time I have) a redesign of the Bingo Card Creator and Daily Bingo Cards web sites.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie posts a brief holiday greeting to fellow micro-ISV developers, noting his travel from Japan to Chicago for family time. He teases upcoming announcements for early January including a Google partnership, the launch of Bingo Card Creator 2.0, and a year-end review. The post also doubles as an SEO play, with a postscript directly targeting Google searchers looking for Christmas and New Year's bingo cards, linking them to his free offerings.
quotes.key_insight: Every blog post is a marketing opportunity — even a holiday greeting can be optimized to capture seasonal search traffic and drive conversions.

quotes.quote: I run a small business which sells software that makes bingo cards, and every time a holiday runs around Google sends me a couple thousand people looking for holiday bingo cards.
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces free Christmas bingo cards available on his site, offered in multiple varieties to accommodate different preferences around holiday celebrations. He splits the cards into 'traditional' (religious Christmas story) and 'seasonal' (Santa and reindeer) versions, plus a combined set. The post doubles as content marketing, driving holiday search traffic to his bingo card business. He also mentions Hanukkah cards and uses the popular cards page as a real-time gauge of which Christmas framing resonates more with users.
quotes.key_insight: Creating free seasonal content timed to predictable search traffic spikes is an effective strategy for driving users into your product funnel.

quotes.quote: Ezell said 'I'll prove it!', and has decided to one-up me in the process, by shaving a few days and dollars off my schedule.
quotes.summary: McKenzie highlights a developer named Ezell who bet a friend he could create a sellable software business in just six days with twenty dollars, inspired by McKenzie's own rapid business creation story. McKenzie endorses the attempt and believes it's achievable. The post identifies baby tracking as a very underserved niche with real business potential. McKenzie also draws a sharp distinction between game development as a hobby versus a viable path to building a software business.
quotes.key_insight: Utility software targeting underserved niches can be launched as a viable business with minimal time and capital, whereas creative products like games have fundamentally higher barriers to minimum viability.

quotes.quote: There is no reason you shouldn't promote things which are useful to your audience for your mutual benefit.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recommends an SEO guide for bloggers and its companion piece revealing the marketing strategy behind it. He argues that content can be both genuinely useful and strategically designed for viral distribution without being cynical, citing Rails as another example of quality work that was also professionally marketed. He also promotes an upcoming blogging book to which he's contributing a chapter, and closes with a plug for his Christmas bingo cards product.
quotes.key_insight: Content marketing works best when the content is genuinely useful — designing something to go viral and designing something valuable are complementary goals, not conflicting ones.

quotes.quote: Just because you're professionally marketed and designed to go viral doesn't mean you have to suck.

quotes.quote: The snowflake queries are loving me and owning a top 10 spot on every possible variation of 'thanksgiving bingo cards' is worth 1.5X owning the 11th spot on [thanksgiving bingo cards] itself.
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces he's making his Daily Bingo Cards website statistics publicly available in real time using a Rails plugin called Sitealizer. He teases strong performance from seasonal search queries around Thanksgiving bingo cards, noting that owning top 10 spots on long-tail variations of a keyword can be worth more than ranking just outside the top 10 for the head term. The public stats are meant to keep him honest when discussing traffic numbers with his audience.
quotes.key_insight: Dominating long-tail keyword variations collectively delivers more value than narrowly missing a top ranking on the competitive head term.

quotes.quote: Don't ask me how you can rank for a phrase that competitive in less than 2 months of work.

quotes.quote: I was (to my knowledge) the only person who backed up the first impressions with numbers, and as a result I attracted just a few more links than the next guy and, wham, the search engines think I'm the expert on the topic.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes how he became the #1 Google result for 'conversion optimizer' despite being a small blogger, by combining early adoption with radical transparency and real numbers. He explains that new niches present outsized SEO opportunities because there are zero pre-existing links and less competition. The key was not just being first, but providing uniquely compelling content—actual performance data rather than repackaged press releases. He argues this 'firstest with the mostest' approach creates a self-reinforcing advantage, as the top result becomes the canonical reference others link to.
quotes.key_insight: In emerging niches, the combination of early adoption and radical transparency with real data creates a self-reinforcing SEO advantage that even much larger competitors cannot easily displace.

quotes.quote: It's not just enough to be there first, you have to be there 'firstest with the mostest.'

quotes.quote: All ten results on the first page are about the article, but the article itself doesn't appear at all.
quotes.summary: McKenzie demonstrates what Google's duplicate content penalty looks like in practice by showing how his own blog post was outranked by every site that linked to or scraped it. Searching the exact title of his article returned ten results about the article, but not the article itself. He explains this is caused by Google mistakenly believing his content was plagiarized from scrapers or social bookmarking sites. He offers diagnostic tests for detecting the penalty and notes that inbound links from trusted sites can cure it.
quotes.key_insight: Google's duplicate content penalty can ironically punish original authors when scrapers and linkers reproduce their content, but the penalty is diagnosable by searching exact titles and curable with trusted inbound links.

quotes.quote: Welcome to the Duplicate Content penalty — Google thinks I am plagiarizing one of those results and, as a result, assumes my blog is not a relevant result for the query.

quotes.quote: Too many projects, too little time.
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces that his Rails SEO tips guide is nearly complete, sharing the table of contents covering on-page and off-page optimization techniques. The guide uses his Daily Bingo Cards site as a case study, covering topics like URL structure, metadata, duplicate content, sitemaps, and linkbait strategies. He notes he's been implementing his own suggestions in his Rails application as he writes about them. The post is a brief progress update requesting feedback and sharing.
quotes.key_insight: Documenting your own SEO implementation process as a guide for other developers serves double duty as both content marketing and a forcing function to actually implement best practices.

quotes.quote: An 'evergreen' puts the lie to blogs being a medium which only covers breaking news and the controversies of the day — they keep producing value forever, typically by ranking highly for search terms of consequence.
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains how high-ranking 'evergreen' blog posts can be strategically leveraged to bootstrap traffic for new products. He describes how his 'Free Bingo Cards' post ranks highly in search engines and generates 2,500 hits per month with zero ongoing promotion. By adding timely, topical content to the top of evergreen posts, he both refreshes their search rankings and redirects traffic to new projects like Daily Bingo Cards. The technique exploits the fact that users disproportionately click the first link in any content.
quotes.key_insight: High-ranking evergreen content can be strategically refreshed with topical additions to both extend its search ranking lifespan and funnel traffic to new products.

quotes.quote: Hard to get visitors without ranking, hard to get ranking without links, hard to get links without visitors — it's a vicious cycle.

quotes.quote: There are a bunch of big names participating (Seth Godin, John Chow), quite a few folks from our little uISV community (Andy Brice, Ian Landsman, etc), and for some strange reason I was asked to contribute a chapter.
quotes.summary: Patrick announces his participation as a contributing author in a book about blogging success, edited by Steph Granger of LandLordMax. The book will feature essays and interviews from notable bloggers and uISV community members including Seth Godin and John Chow. Patrick solicits reader input on what topics to cover in his chapter, noting he's leaning toward technical SEO aspects of blogging since marketing is well-covered by other contributors.
quotes.key_insight: When contributing to a collaborative project, differentiate by covering the technical gaps others won't address rather than duplicating what the bigger names will already provide.

quotes.quote: Given that there are marketing folks aplenty on the author list I was thinking of covering some of the technical nuts&bolts of blogging, probably from an SEO angle, but that is hopefully not the only thing I know how to talk about.

quotes.quote: I run a business which sells software that makes custom printable bingo cards and every time a holiday comes around people come out of the woodwork looking for them.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie promotes free Thanksgiving-themed bingo cards as a seasonal marketing strategy for his Bingo Card Creator software. The post offers free downloadable cards while naturally funneling readers toward his free trial and paid product. He includes practical tips for playing Thanksgiving bingo with families, particularly with young children. The post is a textbook example of content marketing disguised as a helpful holiday resource, creating seasonal SEO-friendly content that drives organic traffic.
quotes.key_insight: Creating free seasonal content that targets holiday search queries is an effective way to drive organic traffic into a software product's conversion funnel.

quotes.quote: Conversion Optimizer makes me bid on Good sites, not on Bad sites. And that is why it works phenomenally, phenomenally well.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie details his experience with Google's Conversion Optimizer for AdWords Content Network advertising. After months of frustration with fraudulent Made-for-AdSense sites draining his budget through deceptive click practices, Conversion Optimizer automatically allocated his spending toward high-converting sites and away from bad actors. The result was a doubling of revenue and profit margin in a single month, with the system acting as an autonomous campaign manager that scaled spending into seasonal traffic spikes like Halloween without manual intervention. He provides concrete before-and-after metrics showing a 50x increase in profit from Content Network advertising.
quotes.key_insight: Algorithmic optimization that targets cost-per-conversion rather than cost-per-click can dramatically outperform manual campaign management by automatically scaling spend toward profitable traffic sources and eliminating fraudulent ones.

quotes.quote: I had my best month ever, beating my previous best month ever (September 2007) by about a factor of two.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his best month ever for Bingo Card Creator, crossing $2,000 in gross sales and $1,000 in profit for the first time. The growth was driven by Google's Conversion Optimizer and an unplanned Halloween advertising campaign. He breaks down expenses including AdWords ($407), freelancer content ($315), and CD fulfillment ($175). He also discusses his Daily Bingo Cards side project's long-tail SEO strategy, where niche educational bingo cards rank quickly for low-competition queries with high conversion rates.
quotes.key_insight: Investing in long-tail content that ranks quickly for niche queries with no competition and high conversion rates is more profitable than competing for high-volume head terms.

quotes.quote: Something had to go and God, family, Galaxy, customer satisfaction, health, and professional duty won out over timely blog updating.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie outlines his planned blog schedule for the upcoming week, listing specific topics for each day. He explains the scheduling is partly to avoid a Sunday crunch where church, family, bug fixes, writing, volleyball, and Mario Galaxy all compete for his time. The post gives a window into the juggling act of running a micro-ISV alongside a full-time life in Japan.
quotes.key_insight: A solo software founder's week is a constant triage between product work, content marketing, personal life, and everything else — and being transparent about that triage is itself good content.

quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie gives a rapid-fire update on his Bingo Card Creator business hitting a major milestone in October 2007. His Halloween bingo promotion drove significant traffic and downloads, while Google's Conversion Optimizer helped double his monthly sales to $2,000. He teases a mysterious offer from Google and notes the amusing reality that cheese bingo is his most popular search query.
quotes.key_insight: Seasonal content marketing combined with automated ad optimization can produce rapid, compounding growth for a small software business.

quotes.quote: If you want to see the distribution of how many of those are due to the top five pages and how many are not, why not mosey on over.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes implementing a 'popular bingo cards' feature on his Daily Bingo Cards site, which generated about 300 downloads across roughly 100 cards with minimal promotion. He references the long tail distribution visible in the download stats, where a few top pages dominate while many others contribute smaller amounts. The post also includes a candid aside about broken caching logic causing slow page loads due to Ruby iterating over hundreds of objects. It's a brief, informal update mixing product development with technical debt acknowledgment.
quotes.key_insight: Even a simple content library with minimal promotion reveals long tail distribution patterns, where a few popular items drive most of the traffic while the rest collectively contribute meaningful volume.

quotes.quote: Yeah, I know, I know, fix the cache or do the math at the DB, but I'm lazy and its late.

quotes.quote: My blog is PR5, has a few hundred inbound links, and has little direct impact on my monthly bottom line. Daily Bingo Cards is PR0, has about two inbound links, and has the potential to double my take-home pay.
quotes.summary: McKenzie previews his in-progress article on SEO for Ruby on Rails, explaining his strategy for using it as linkbait. He deliberately hosts the article on his commercial site (Daily Bingo Cards) rather than his blog, because the commercial site has more revenue potential despite lower PageRank. He also reveals his plan to use a 'Considered Harmful' title variant when posting to social networking sites to generate controversy and engagement. The post is a transparent look at the strategic calculus behind content placement and promotion.
quotes.key_insight: Direct linkbait to the asset with the highest revenue potential, not the one with the most existing authority — SEO efforts should follow the money.

quotes.quote: I Googled it to see if there was any major competition for the term, and yep, there apparently is — me, myself, and I…
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers that his new site Daily Bingo Cards is already ranking for long-tail search queries, and finds that he dominates the entire above-the-fold Google results for one query — competing only against his own other sites. He uses this as a lighthearted illustration of his core SEO strategy: dominating thousands of low-volume 'snowflake' queries that collectively drive meaningful traffic.
quotes.key_insight: A viable SEO strategy is to dominate thousands of obscure long-tail queries rather than compete for a few high-volume terms, because the aggregate traffic from many small wins adds up.

quotes.quote: Multiply this by a couple thousand different snowflakes and you have my marketing strategy for Daily Bingo Cards in a nutshell.

quotes.quote: It is going to be Halloween shortly and Google is sending me hundreds of people looking for free Halloween bingo cards.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie writes a seasonal marketing post targeting Google searches for 'free Halloween bingo cards,' directing traffic to his Bingo Card Creator business. The post offers genuinely useful tips for parents and teachers on how to use Halloween bingo cards with children, including creative suggestions for markers and prizes. He closes with a transparent aside to his regular readers acknowledging the post is SEO-driven content. This is a textbook example of his content marketing philosophy: create useful content that naturally attracts search traffic to your product.
quotes.key_insight: Creating genuinely useful seasonal content that matches high-volume search queries is a low-effort way to funnel organic traffic toward your product.

quotes.quote: There are 10 kinds of people on the Internet: those that link, and those that don't.
quotes.summary: McKenzie details his strategy for creating 'linkbait' content targeting non-technical audiences, specifically elementary school teachers. He built DailyBingoCards.com as a massive free resource site containing hundreds of bingo card activities, designed to attract links from educators rather than the typical Digg demographic. The strategy centers on overwhelming visitors with abundance, using clean URLs for SEO, creating evergreen content that compounds in value over years, and treating the entire free site as a subtle advertisement for his paid product Bingo Card Creator. He contrasts this long-tail approach with the ephemeral nature of Digg-style linkbait, arguing that non-technical audiences offer more durable SEO value.
quotes.key_insight: For non-technical audiences, the most effective linkbait is evergreen free resources at industrial scale — content that compounds in SEO value over years while functioning as a subtle funnel to your paid product.

quotes.quote: It can help answer the big (and befuddling) questions like What Line In My 2,000 Line CSS or HTML File Makes This Box Go Here, which throw non-designer me for a loop.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recommends Firebug, a Firefox addon that lets you inspect and modify CSS properties in real time without page refreshes. He used it to fix long-standing visual bugs on Bingo Card Creator's site that he'd been putting off for over a year, including a 12-pixel alignment issue and undersized header fonts. The post illustrates his broader philosophy of incremental improvement and his self-awareness about lacking design skills.
quotes.key_insight: The right tooling can eliminate psychological barriers that cause small but meaningful improvements to be deferred indefinitely.

quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie celebrates crossing $10,000 in cumulative revenue from Bingo Card Creator, noting that sales are accelerating. He mentions upcoming linkbait content and a version upgrade as further growth drivers.
quotes.key_insight: Crossing $10,000 in cumulative revenue validated that a tiny niche software product could generate real and accelerating income through sustained marketing effort.

quotes.quote: It will be regarding the linkbait I have been working on for a week now, and how you can get linkbait to work for you.
quotes.summary: A brief status update where Patrick apologizes for not posting due to illness. He teases an upcoming post about linkbait strategies he's been developing. The post is essentially a placeholder promising content about SEO link-building tactics. At just 51 words, it's one of his shortest posts and reflects the casual, diary-like nature of his early blogging.
quotes.key_insight: Even brief status updates reflect the early blogging ethos of maintaining regular audience engagement, and hint at the SEO-focused marketing strategies Patrick was actively developing.

quotes.quote: Fourteen months into marketing Bingo Card Creator and quite a bit into becoming an amateur SEO, I finally added a 301 redirect from bingocardcreator.com to www.bingocardcreator.com
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie confesses to forgetting a basic SEO best practice for fourteen months: setting up a 301 redirect from his bare domain to the www version. He discovered through WebsiteGrader that roughly half his inbound links pointed to each variant, meaning search engines treated them as two separate sites rather than combining their authority. The fix took fifteen seconds with a simple .htaccess rule. He shares the exact Apache configuration so others can avoid the same mistake.
quotes.key_insight: Even experienced practitioners can lose significant value by neglecting trivial fundamentals, and the assumptions that let you dismiss a problem ('I'm the only one who links') are often wrong.

quotes.quote: I succeeded far, far beyond my expectations in finding folks willing and able to get the job done.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports being overwhelmed by the response to his outsourcing request for help creating bingo card assets. He posted a job request for his micro-ISV and received far more willing helpers than expected within twenty hours. He teases an upcoming experiment involving dozens of PDF and GIF bingo card files, expecting the investment to be more productive than his AdWords spend. He hints at a weekend rollout, schedule permitting.
quotes.key_insight: Investing in content assets through outsourcing can be a more productive marketing spend than equivalent paid advertising budgets.

quotes.quote: I'm sure that even though I'm going to end up buying a few more bingo cards than I was budgeting for for starters that it will be at least as productive as the giant amount I spent on AdWords last month.

quotes.quote: I am paying something like 24 cents a free trial now for the last week. That implies a cost of less than $12 per additional $24 profit sale.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports crossing the $1,000 monthly sales mark with Bingo Card Creator, though heavy AdWords experimentation cut net profit to $604. He breaks down his traffic sources, conversion funnel, and the economics of paid acquisition versus organic search. The long tail of Google search keywords drives most of his traffic, with thousands of unique queries averaging just three hits each. He highlights the power of content marketing, noting a single blog post generates roughly $35-50/month in revenue. His biggest frustration is the inability to accurately track AdWords ROI due to noisy, limited data.
quotes.key_insight: Even a tiny niche software business can cross $1,000/month by combining long-tail SEO, disciplined paid acquisition experimentation, and content marketing — though accurate attribution remains frustratingly elusive.

quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports crossing the $1,000 monthly sales mark with Bingo Card Creator, though heavy AdWords experimentation cut net profit to $604. He breaks down his traffic sources, conversion funnel, and the economics of paid acquisition versus organic search. The long tail of Google search keywords drives most of his traffic, with thousands of unique queries averaging just three hits each. He highlights the power of content marketing, noting a single blog post generates roughly $35-50/month in revenue. His biggest frustration is the inability to accurately track AdWords ROI due to noisy, limited data.
quotes.key_insight: Even a tiny niche software business can cross $1,000/month by combining long-tail SEO, disciplined paid acquisition experimentation, and content marketing — though accurate attribution remains frustratingly elusive.

quotes.quote: A certain amount of minor irritations and miscommunications are par for the course.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes a customer support interaction where a customer didn't read his detailed email containing their registration key and instead placed a duplicate order. Rather than argue or try to recoup the $5 loss from the extra CD, he simply accommodated the customer gracefully. He uses this as an example of not sweating minor costs and irritations in running a small software business, and closes with a recommendation for SwiftCD's fulfillment service.
quotes.key_insight: Small financial losses from customer miscommunications are worth absorbing gracefully rather than creating friction, because goodwill and smooth operations matter more than recovering a few dollars.

quotes.quote: What is the purpose of RentACoder and similar? To connect money with talent while bridging the trust gap. I've got money, you've got talent, and if you are reading this you presumably know me to be an upstanding sort.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie posts a detailed freelance job listing on his blog, seeking contractors to create educational bingo card word lists and associated PDF/GIF files for his Bingo Card Creator software. Rather than using RentACoder and paying a 10% finder's fee, he leverages his blog audience's trust to find talent directly. He offers $30 for approximately two hours of work, with promises of larger follow-up orders for quality performers. The post doubles as a masterclass in writing clear freelance specifications, with detailed deliverable requirements, quality criteria, and legal terms.
quotes.key_insight: A trusted personal brand can replace freelance marketplaces entirely, eliminating fees and attracting higher-quality talent who self-select through familiarity with your work.

quotes.quote: I know what I want the customers to do, so why not tell them?
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his process for optimizing the conversion funnel on his Bingo Card Creator homepage. He identifies that visitors who click the prominent screenshot often fail to proceed to downloading the free trial, instead closing the browser or wandering the site. His solution is to replace the busy, text-heavy screenshot overlay with a cleaner version containing a clear call-to-action directing users to download the trial. He then walks through setting up a Google Website Optimizer A/B test to measure whether the change increases visits to the Free Trial page.
quotes.key_insight: Every step in a conversion funnel should explicitly tell the customer what to do next, because visitors left without clear direction will simply leave.

quotes.quote: This is the best thing to happen to AdWords since AdWords.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports dramatic early results from Google's new Conversion Optimizer for AdWords, which switches bidding from cost-per-click to cost-per-action on the content network. His daily ad spend dropped 50% while click-through rates improved, cutting his cost per acquisition from 45-50 cents to 25 cents. He walks through the unit economics showing how this doubled his profit margin without any effort. He also flags the strategic risk: by telling Google exactly what a conversion is worth, advertisers hand Google the information needed for perfect price discrimination.
quotes.key_insight: CPA-based advertising aligns incentives across the entire ad ecosystem by rewarding relevant placements, but the tradeoff is handing your monopolist supplier the exact information they need to extract maximum value from you.

quotes.quote: If I pay 45 cents for a trial, then I am paying $18 to make a sale worth approximately $24 to me. I still make money, but Google and the content publisher get most of the value from the sale, and I do all of the work.

quotes.quote: Not a bad ninety minutes, actually, especially for someone who doesn't really know PHP or CSS.
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces that Bingo Card Creator is getting its own product blog, built on WordPress. He details his progress after ninety minutes of work porting the site template, despite limited PHP and CSS knowledge. He outlines remaining tasks including styling, SEO-friendly URL setup, choosing a title optimized for search, and actually writing content. He asks readers to link to the blog once launched to boost its SEO.
quotes.key_insight: Every decision about a product blog—its title, URL structure, linking strategy, and launch timing—should be made with SEO implications in mind.
