quotes.quote: The idea would be pay folks $10 or so for every copy of Bingo Card Creator their site sells as opposed to 10 cents every time someone clicks on over to me.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discusses Google's new Conversion Optimizer feature for AdWords, which allows advertisers to set Cost-Per-Acquisition bids instead of Cost-Per-Click bids. He explains the difference between true CPA (affiliate-style) advertising and this approximation, which uses Google's algorithms to optimize click bidding based on conversion history. He signs up his Content Network campaign to test it, setting a target CPA of 35 cents against his current 41-cent average. The post also marks a milestone of crossing $1,000 in monthly sales for the first time.
quotes.key_insight: Google's Conversion Optimizer represents a meaningful step toward performance-based advertising by letting the platform algorithmically optimize bids toward a target acquisition cost rather than requiring manual per-click bid management.

quotes.quote: Google uses some black magic to decide what a click at a certain day, time, and website is likely to be worth to you, based on your campaign history.

quotes.quote: That couldn't possibly be natural. Remember, an AdSense ad is, by definition, being shown to someone who is at least partially interested in something related to your product but has not expressed any interest in being sold to yet.
quotes.summary: McKenzie exposes a deceptive AdSense technique where publishers use CSS manipulation to make advertisements appear as page content, tricking visitors into clicking ads they mistake for navigation or download links. He discovered the scheme when his Content Network CTR inexplicably jumped from 1% to 15%, leading him to investigate. The technique generates roughly $40 CPM for publishers versus $2.50 for honest sites, effectively stealing from advertisers whose visitors immediately bounce. McKenzie details how this cost him money by converting only 2% of deceived visitors versus 22% of genuinely interested ones, making each sale cost $50 for a $25 product. He provides practical countermeasures including monitoring site placement reports and banning offending domains, while calling on Google to implement algorithmic protections.
quotes.key_insight: Deceptive ad placement that tricks users into clicking ads they mistake for content is more damaging to advertisers than click fraud, because it drains budgets on visitors who will never convert while Google's own optimization algorithms amplify the effect.

quotes.quote: Bamboozling visitors to click on my ads hurts me more than errors ever could.

McKenzie details how this cost him money by converting only 2% of deceived visitors versus 22% of genuinely interested ones, making each sale cost $50 for a $25 product. He provides practical countermeasures including monitoring site placement reports and banning offending domains, while calling on Google to implement algorithmic protections.
quotes.key_insight: Deceptive ad placement that tricks users into clicking ads they mistake for content is more damaging to advertisers than click fraud, because it drains budgets on visitors who will never convert while Google's own optimization algorithms amplify the effect.

quotes.quote: Now tell me, back in my father's day, what were the odds that you would find someone translating the meat of a wee little article about a wee little company run by an American in Japan into Spanish?
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers that a Spanish-language blog has translated and commented on one of his earlier posts about giving a free software license to a confused customer. He shares the original Spanish text and attempts his own translation back into English, demonstrating his multilingual capabilities while noting his Spanish has atrophied after years in Japan. The post highlights how the internet connects small business stories across language barriers in ways that would have been impossible a generation earlier.
quotes.key_insight: The internet enables even the smallest one-person businesses to build international reputations through generous customer service stories that cross language barriers organically.

quotes.quote: One simple conversion trick is to put the goal for the page as the first hyperlink in the main content area.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes how using CrazyEgg heat maps revealed that the first hyperlink on his second-most-trafficked page was sending 10% of visitors to Adobe Acrobat's download page instead of his own free trial. After a one-minute fix to make the first link point to his free trial instead, he estimates gaining roughly 13 extra sales per month. The post illustrates how visual analytics tools can expose obvious conversion leaks that are invisible when just looking at raw data or source code.
quotes.key_insight: Visual analytics tools like heat maps can instantly reveal conversion-killing mistakes that are obvious in retrospect but invisible without instrumentation, turning a minute of work into meaningful revenue gains.

quotes.quote: This largely works because Internet browsers will almost reflexively jump on anything that is underlined in blue.

quotes.quote: Anybody who does that much work for the community deserves links early and often.

The preliminary results cover demographics, with sales data still to come. He encourages other bloggers to link to the survey as a way of rewarding community contributions.
quotes.key_insight: Community members who contribute substantial research deserve active support and promotion from fellow bloggers.

quotes.quote: One of the beauties of the software business is that serving your 307th customer is, quite literally, free. Its that first customer who costs you millions… or in my case, about sixty bucks.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes giving a free license to a confused customer who had actually purchased from a competitor, arguing this is sound business strategy rather than charity. He outlines three mercenary reasons: it costs nothing (marginal cost of software is zero), it ends a potential multi-email support thread quickly, and it creates a passionate advocate far more effectively than paid advertising. He compares the cost-effectiveness to his AdWords spending where 99.4% of ad viewers won't even click, while this gesture likely creates a lifetime evangelist. He closes with softer arguments about karma and supporting the small business ecosystem.
quotes.key_insight: Giving away a zero-marginal-cost product to a competitor's confused customer is vastly more cost-effective at creating a passionate advocate than any amount of paid advertising.

quotes.quote: 99.4% of the people who saw one of these advertisements weren't even willing to part with the five seconds! And it was still a smart business decision to do it.

quotes.quote: I generally don't like posting to say 'Well, I've got nothing to say'.
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains why his blog posting frequency has declined, citing a month-long family visit home, returning to a demanding salaryman job in Japan, and his preference not to post without substance. He notes the business is stable but he continues tweaking things. He responds to being called a 'hero of the MicroISV blogging community' with characteristic humility.
quotes.key_insight: Running a side business while working a demanding Japanese salaryman job means accepting that some things — family, sleep, the day job — simply take priority over content creation.

quotes.quote: A split test is when you randomly send half of all prospects to one version of a page and half to another to determine, in a rigorously scientific and statistical manner, which of two alternatives is better.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his experience setting up Google's Website Optimizer to split test his Bingo Card Creator purchasing page with new PayPal icons. He explains that Google's newer, simpler redirect-based approach is much easier than the old markup-heavy method. However, he identifies a critical SEO risk: having two nearly identical pages could trigger Google's duplicate content penalty. He solves this by blocking the alternate page in robots.txt and plans to use a 302 redirect after the test concludes.
quotes.key_insight: A/B testing tools can inadvertently create duplicate content that harms SEO, so always block test pages from search engine indexing.

quotes.quote: Having two pages on your site with 95% similar HTML is an excellent way to get smacked down by the duplicate content penalty, and that would hurt me oh-so-much more than getting a modest bump in conversions from rigorous testing.

quotes.quote: Now, personally, I kind of liked having the credit card icon logos above and slightly dominating the call to action but, hey, this is what CrazyEgg was made for.
quotes.summary: Patrick describes updating his PayPal payment buttons on his Bingo Card Creator purchasing page to newer, larger, more descriptive versions. He notes the visual improvements and plans to A/B test the old versus new buttons using CrazyEgg. He also updates the Google Checkout button but notes Google's brand guidelines prevent him from resizing it to match the PayPal button.
quotes.key_insight: Even minor UI elements like payment buttons should be deliberately tested rather than changed on instinct alone.

quotes.quote: That is genius and honestly something I would never have expected from Microsoft, because it acknowledges that their competition is beating them soundly.
quotes.summary: Microsoft's AdCenter announced a feature allowing direct import of campaigns from competing paid search programs like Google AdWords. McKenzie sees this as a surprisingly smart move from Microsoft, as it acknowledges their competitive disadvantage while simultaneously making it trivially easy for advertisers to expand to their platform. The feature halves the work required to run campaigns on both platforms.
quotes.key_insight: The smartest competitive move a second-place platform can make is to reduce switching costs from the leader, even if it means acknowledging inferiority.

quotes.quote: The ability to import campaigns directly from Adwords literally halves the amount of work required, AND it means you get to do your work where the work is easy (AdWords' vastly superior interface) and then reap the rewards of lower competition on AdCenter.

McKenzie sees this as a surprisingly smart move from Microsoft, as it acknowledges their competitive disadvantage while simultaneously making it trivially easy for advertisers to expand to their platform. The feature halves the work required to run campaigns on both platforms.
quotes.key_insight: The smartest competitive move a second-place platform can make is to reduce switching costs from the leader, even if it means acknowledging inferiority.

quotes.quote: 100 trial downloads implies 2.5 sales implies about $70 in revenue, and believe me, if I could sustain that I would be sitting pretty.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his best single day of sales for Bingo Card Creator, driven by AdWords campaign changes that increased clicks from 10 to 60 per day. August 2007 brought in $813 in revenue with $157 in expenses for $656 profit. He shares two customer service stories: paying $15 to UPS a replacement CD to an upset customer, and giving away a free CD rather than dealing with a school's purchase order system. Both decisions sacrificed short-term profit for long-term goodwill and word-of-mouth marketing.
quotes.key_insight: Sometimes the most profitable customer service decision is to eat the cost of a sale in exchange for goodwill and word-of-mouth marketing.

quotes.quote: Yeah, that eats most of the profit from the sale, but they're now very pleased with my responsiveness rather than thinking I'm a shiftless shipment-forgetting Internet conman.

quotes.quote: White guys are so rare where I live that letters from the Social Security Administration to Patrick McKenzie @ my prefecture get to me, no address necessary.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces his return to Japan to start a new software consulting contract in Nagoya. The position involves Java server programming and managing an Indian outsourcing team at an enterprise software consultancy. He notes the overwhelming dominance of Toyota in Nagoya's economy and shares that the salary exceeds his Bingo Card Creator income. The post is brief and conversational, serving as a networking update for his blog audience.
quotes.key_insight: McKenzie's early career in Japan involved navigating the intersection of enterprise software consulting and cross-cultural communication in a regional economy completely dominated by a single corporation.

quotes.quote: I'd like to recount what we're doing to market a product that, strictly speaking, doesn't exist yet.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents how he set up a WordPress site for his brother's superhero novel on the same cheap VPS already running his Rails application. The post is a detailed technical walkthrough of configuring Apache virtual hosts, mod_rewrite rules, and WordPress installation on a Slicehost VPS with only 256MB of RAM. He advocates for self-hosted WordPress over WordPress.com for SEO control and future flexibility. The broader context is applying his software marketing mindset to help his brother market a product that doesn't even exist yet.
quotes.key_insight: Start marketing before your product exists by establishing a self-hosted web presence you fully control, because the SEO and platform flexibility benefits compound over time.

quotes.quote: America didn't become the world's largest economy by saying no to greenbacks, and billions of dollars passes through millions of foreigner-controlled accounts every day.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie addresses practical banking questions for micro-ISV (uISV) operators, particularly those running international businesses. He explains that non-US residents can obtain US bank accounts despite common fears about regulations like the Patriot Act, with the key obstacle being Know Your Customer requirements. He recommends E-Trade's brokerage-to-banking pathway as a workaround for international entrepreneurs. For US-based operators, he recommends ING Direct's Electric Orange checking account for its fast ACH processing and interest rates, and advises all uISVs to use cash-back credit cards for expense tracking and savings.
quotes.key_insight: International micro-ISV operators can access US banking by leveraging online brokerages as a backdoor past Know Your Customer requirements, while US-based operators should optimize for fast ACH processing and cash-back rewards.

quotes.quote: Forget what you've heard about the Patriot Act, if you're not on a watchlist this actually isn't that hard, provided you can find a bank which will let you.

quotes.quote: I am indebted to Ms. Rowling for inviting us to share in the magic, and in the very real good she has worked for the cause of children's literacy.
quotes.summary: McKenzie uses the release of Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows as an opportunity to promote his Bingo Card Creator product's free Harry Potter-themed bingo cards. He briefly reviews the book, finding it emotionally moving despite some rushed elements, and credits Rowling for advancing children's literacy. The post is a lightweight marketing tie-in to a cultural moment, directed at teachers and parents.
quotes.key_insight: Smart content marketing means tying your product to major cultural moments, even when the connection is tangential.

quotes.quote: Try imagining the stress of planning a month-long vacation, a job hop, and a move at the same time — now add in the wrinkle that it is all done in a second language.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie explains his reduced blogging frequency due to simultaneously planning a month-long US trip, changing jobs, and moving apartments — all conducted in Japanese. He notes he'll spend the time in the US visiting family and working on Kalzumeus, then return to a new OSS consultancy job in Nagoya at a higher salary. The post is a brief personal update with a humorous footnote about bureaucratic mishaps in a second language.
quotes.key_insight: Even fluency in a second language doesn't prevent bureaucratic chaos when juggling major life transitions abroad.

quotes.quote: Just when you think "Oh, it can't be so bad, I am a qualified translator after all" Murphy's Law says that the travel agent's database will forget you exist, the landlord will try sneaking a rider onto page 8 of the contract to charge 10% extra, and the immigration bureau will erroneously tell you that their visa guidelines have changed to disallow your trip.

quotes.quote: One more resource for uISVs trying to cross the chasm from $0 to $1 in revenue is always a good thing.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie notes that Bob Walsh, author of the definitive book on micro-ISVs, has launched a consultancy called 47hats aimed at helping small software businesses. While McKenzie has some disagreements with Walsh's priorities—particularly around incorporation emphasis and intuition-based website advice—he acknowledges Walsh's contributions to the community. McKenzie sees value in any resource that helps software entrepreneurs cross the gap from zero to first revenue.
quotes.key_insight: The biggest challenge for micro-ISV founders is crossing the chasm from $0 to $1 in revenue, and community resources that help with that transition—even imperfect ones—are valuable.

quotes.quote: Rick wrote Paint.NET, a fairly powerful freeware image editing program which I am a major user of and contributed to, and so we have been unknowingly supporting each other's businesses for months.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discusses the surprising profitability of freeware software, prompted by discovering that Paint.NET creator Rick Brewster has been quietly making good money from his free product. He uses the occasion to reflect on his own financial philosophy, revealing that Bingo Card Creator profits paid off his student loans and that he prioritizes saving over spending. The post offers a glimpse into McKenzie's frugal mindset and his belief that software business income should go toward eliminating debt and building savings rather than lifestyle inflation.
quotes.key_insight: Side business income is most powerful when directed toward eliminating debt and building savings rather than lifestyle inflation, and even freeware can generate surprising revenue through indirect monetization.

quotes.quote: Easily my best month ever, and its summer vacation. I'm really looking forward to the start of the term in August.
quotes.summary: Patrick reports his best month ever for Bingo Card Creator, earning $918.25 gross and $756.35 net profit from 36 sales in June 2007. Despite summer vacation reducing teacher-related search traffic, he grew overall traffic and sales by rewriting a single page to capture long-tail 'snowflake' search queries. The rewritten page doubled its pageviews from 12% to 27% of total site traffic. He attributes his sales growth directly to this SEO content strategy and is eager to expand it further.
quotes.key_insight: A single page rewrite targeting long-tail search queries can offset seasonal traffic declines and drive meaningful sales growth for a small software business.

quotes.quote: The "extra" 3,000 page views were a major part of the reason that, despite the fact traffic on my major teacher-related search terms declined since teachers are out of school, my overall traffic stayed mostly flat while my sales increased markedly.

quotes.quote: A user of mine is reporting a quirky Java error on that operating system but not on another OS X 10.4 box they have access to, and I'm trying to isolate whether it is their machine that has the issue or whether Bingo Card Creator has a hidden incompatibility with something on a particular version of OS X.
quotes.summary: McKenzie asks his blog readers for help testing Bingo Card Creator on Mac OS X 10.3.9. A user reported a Java error specific to that OS version, and McKenzie needs to determine whether the bug is machine-specific or a genuine incompatibility with that version of OS X.
quotes.key_insight: A solo developer's blog audience doubles as a free cross-platform QA team, turning community engagement into a practical debugging resource.

quotes.quote: WebScamInc could never get 'millions of satisfied customers' to authorize the $9 for nothing purchase with their lack of service, so they piggyback on the trust the customer has in you.
quotes.summary: SWREG, a payment processor used by micro-ISVs, introduced a deceptive upsell that silently signed customers up for a $9/month 'Reservation Rewards' subscription through manipulative UI design. McKenzie argues this fundamentally betrays the trust relationship between software developers and their customers, since SWREG is exploiting trust that the uISV earned. He dissects exactly how the dark pattern works — large call-to-action buttons paired with tiny disclosure text — and explains why checkout flow context makes users especially vulnerable. The post includes devastating customer testimonials showing real harm, including a soldier serving in Iraq. McKenzie concludes that payment processors have an inherent conflict of interest with the businesses they serve, and urges developers to evaluate whether their processor would ever sacrifice customer trust for revenue.
quotes.key_insight: Payment processors have an inherent conflict of interest with the businesses they serve because they can monetize the trust that developers painstakingly built with their customers, and developers must vigilantly guard against this betrayal.

quotes.quote: A large block of small text font on a web page, placed against a blue button with a strong call to action, isn't asking to be read. Its asking to be missed.

quotes.quote: Sales for the first year were a hair under $6,500. That wildly exceeds my original expectation/goal, which was about 10 sales a month (~$250).
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on the first anniversary of Bingo Card Creator, his small software business. The product earned just under $6,500 in its first year against roughly $2,500 in costs, far exceeding his original goal of $250/month. He highlights the diverse uses of the software across education, corporate events, and international contexts. Beyond revenue, he values the community of micro-ISVs he joined and the people he inspired to start their own businesses. He notes that costs have dropped as startup expenses are behind him and he's mastered AdWords, and he's optimistic about future growth as the school year resumes.
quotes.key_insight: A tiny niche software product can far exceed modest expectations by serving a real need, and the community and learning gained along the way matter as much as the revenue.

quotes.quote: Every last person who wasn't happy got a refund delivered with a smile on the same day.

quotes.quote: I always had a brief catch in my breath when I saw Thunderbird downloading '1/3 mails on the server'.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes reaching a point where his automated sales notification emails are becoming overwhelming due to increased sales volume. He receives three emails per transaction and is now getting enough sales (26 by the 20th of the month, approaching his best month of 30) that the notifications are interfering with his ability to spot actionable emails. He decides to turn off one of the three notifications, noting he'll reduce to one per transaction if sales increase another 25%. It's a brief, lighthearted milestone update about Bingo Card Creator's growing sales.
quotes.key_insight: Even at micro-scale, growing sales force you to shift from savoring each individual transaction to building systems that let you focus on what requires action.

quotes.quote: Not that I mind every other email I received telling me that I had money, but it was getting harder to spot the mails which actually required action from the ones which were just for my reference.

quotes.quote: Being a landlord sucks.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie introduces Kalzumeus, a web application designed to simplify property management for small landlords by making rent collection as easy as selling items on eBay. The product targets the underserved market of non-professional landlords who own one or two properties and find existing property management software too complex and expensive. He outlines the feature set, pricing model, competitive landscape, and marketing strategy, emphasizing low startup costs and a path to profitability from the first customer. The post also covers his approach to Ruby on Rails development, demo-driven marketing, and his goal of reaching 300 paying customers to potentially quit his day job.
quotes.key_insight: The biggest opportunity in crowded markets is finding the segment that's overserved by complex solutions and building something simple enough for non-specialists.

quotes.quote: Same disclaimers as normally: these were prepared for a blog post, not the IRS, and I did not exactly go to the extra mile to ensure their accuracy.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares his first-year financial and traffic statistics for Bingo Card Creator in visual form. The business generated $6,333 in sales against $1,720 in expenses for $4,613 in profit, with 63,429 visits over the year. He notes traffic spikes from Slashdot and Joel on Software mentions, and observes that the expected summer traffic trough hasn't materialized yet. The post is characteristically transparent about the limitations of his accounting methods.
quotes.key_insight: Radical transparency about business metrics — even modest ones like $4,613 in annual profit from a side project — builds audience trust and creates a valuable record for understanding seasonal patterns and growth trajectories.

quotes.quote: note that that e-junkie cart change made ALL the difference!
quotes.summary: Patrick reports his May 2007 financials for Bingo Card Creator, showing $600 in gross sales and $452 in profit after expenses. Sales were flat due to the school year ending, but he teases that June is already performing better than expected. He highlights that switching to e-junkie's cart system significantly boosted CD sales. The post is a brief monthly accounting update with a forward-looking tease about summer traffic trends.
quotes.key_insight: Small changes to the purchase flow, like switching cart systems, can have outsized effects on conversion and revenue even for a tiny software business.

quotes.quote: You wouldn't think shaving a few seconds here and a few seconds there would really matter that much, but the stats don't lie.
quotes.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically reviews Direct Access, a productivity tool that saves time by replacing multi-step file/program opening sequences with short keyboard abbreviations. He demonstrates that the tool saved him 15 minutes during a 3-hour programming session, roughly a 10% productivity gain. He also highlights the tool's autotext feature for boilerplate code snippets, noting it's less useful in Ruby than Java due to less boilerplate. The post doubles as a self-reminder about salesmanship: showing users concrete ROI data is brilliantly simple marketing.
quotes.key_insight: Small workflow optimizations that save seconds per interaction compound into significant productivity gains, and showing users concrete data about those gains is powerful salesmanship.

quotes.quote: Its a wonderful age we live in where you can buy 95% of the tools you need to run a business for less than $10 a month.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents his experience setting up hosting infrastructure for his new Kalzumeus project, including purchasing TextDrive hosting and wrestling with SVN repository migration and Rails development quirks. He shares practical observations about infrastructure costs for small software businesses, arguing that the common 10% of revenue guideline overstates what a uISV actually needs. The post chronicles a frustrating evening of tool-wrangling with SVN, Netbeans, and Cygwin, followed by debugging Rails' confusingly similar but subtly different object creation methods. He announces plans to launch a separate blog at kalzumeus.com for programming and business content, keeping his Bingo Card Creator blog customer-focused.
quotes.key_insight: Running a small software business requires surprisingly little infrastructure spending, but the real cost is the time lost wrestling with poorly documented tools and frameworks you haven't fully mastered yet.

quotes.quote: I get a huge percentage of my organic search traffic, about 60-70%, from arbitrary organic search queries. The majority of them are never repeated by another person.
quotes.summary: McKenzie breaks down his organic search traffic into three tiers: competitive head terms, niche long-tail queries he targets deliberately, and 'snowflake queries' — unique, never-repeated searches that account for 60-70% of his organic traffic. He proposes a systematic approach to capturing these snowflake queries by data-mining search logs, clustering similar queries, and writing targeted content pages optimized for clusters of related long-tail searches. Rather than building his own clustering tool, he discovers HitTail, a webapp that already solves this problem. He plans to apply this strategy to his next product, Kalzumeus, which operates adjacent to a highly competitive market space.
quotes.key_insight: The majority of organic search value comes not from competitive head terms but from systematically clustering and targeting the vast long tail of unique 'snowflake' queries that collectively dwarf any single keyword.

quotes.quote: I like to mentally think of these as snowflake queries — every one is unique but if you look at a lot of them at once they certainly look a good deal similar.

He proposes a systematic approach to capturing these snowflake queries by data-mining search logs, clustering similar queries, and writing targeted content pages optimized for clusters of related long-tail searches. Rather than building his own clustering tool, he discovers HitTail, a webapp that already solves this problem. He plans to apply this strategy to his next product, Kalzumeus, which operates adjacent to a highly competitive market space.
quotes.key_insight: The majority of organic search value comes not from competitive head terms but from systematically clustering and targeting the vast long tail of unique 'snowflake' queries that collectively dwarf any single keyword.

quotes.quote: I should really pay them the money for the tool and spend my time actually writing the text which will clump up the snowflakes (which only I can do, since I'm the guy who presumably has the domain knowledge) rather than reinventing a solution to a Certified Hard Problem.

quotes.quote: What works in Columbus, Ohio is also very true for life in Central Japan.
quotes.summary: McKenzie draws parallels between startup life in Columbus, Ohio and Central Japan, noting that both regions share similar challenges of being outside major tech hubs. He references an article about what Silicon Valley could learn from Columbus and finds it directly applicable to his experience in Nagoya. He also teases an upcoming long-form piece about how small independent software vendors can maximize web applications.
quotes.key_insight: The challenges and opportunities of building tech businesses outside major hubs are remarkably similar whether you're in the American Midwest or Central Japan.

quotes.quote: If you do not do it, not only are you hurting user experience on third party sites which trusted you with a link, you're squandering PageRank that you've worked so hard to gain.
quotes.summary: McKenzie walks through the technical process of renaming a popular web page for SEO purposes, changing 'Free Resources' to 'Printable Bingo Cards.' He shows the bash commands for bulk-updating HTML files, emphasizes the critical importance of 301 redirects to preserve PageRank and user experience, and warns against over-optimizing filenames with keyword stuffing. The post is a practical tutorial wrapped around a real change he made to his own site.
quotes.key_insight: When renaming web pages for SEO, the 301 redirect preserving your existing PageRank and external links is just as important as choosing the better keyword-rich URL.

quotes.quote: It is ineffective to directly market to a community which you are not a part of, be that a forum community, a mailing list, or a Usenet group, regardless of what you are selling.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that directly marketing to online communities you don't belong to is almost always ineffective and often counterproductive. He compares the return on time from posting ads on forums (1 trial download per hour invested per month) against creating useful content like blog posts and resource pages (which generate 75-200x better returns). The most effective community-driven marketing comes from trusted members recommending your product organically, not from outsiders dropping in advertisements. He counsels that trust within a community is the key asset, and aggressively promoting your product to people who know you will destroy that trust for negligible financial gain. Instead, he recommends focusing on content creation and letting satisfied customers do the marketing for you.
quotes.key_insight: Community trust cannot be manufactured by outsiders, so software marketers should invest their time in creating useful content and serving customers well rather than posting ads in communities they don't belong to.

quotes.quote: That community has its little social rituals, in-jokes, standards of acceptable behavior, shared history, friendships, rivalries, dramas, a whole tapestry of meaning for its members that you, the outsider, know nothing about.

quotes.quote: One of the unfortunate facts of the education market is that it has pretty severe seasonality: sales drop to close to nothing when school is not in session.
quotes.summary: McKenzie notes that his education-focused software business (Bingo Card Creator) experiences severe seasonality, with sales dropping to near zero during summer when schools are out of session. He has gone roughly a week without a sale and expects the drought to last until mid-August when the new school term begins. He plans to use the downtime productively by improving his website and marketing. He also mentions needing to find a job by late July.
quotes.key_insight: Education-market software businesses face severe seasonal revenue drops during summer, making it essential to use downtime for improving the product and marketing ahead of the next buying cycle.

quotes.quote: I have been told that having them other colors has a tendency to confuse people who are used to the convention that anything blue and underlined can be clicked.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes changing his website's link colors from a custom orange scheme back to the default blue to reduce visitor confusion and lower bounce rates. He notes that non-standard link colors, while aesthetically matching his site, may confuse users accustomed to the convention that blue underlined text is clickable. He also reports that his redesigned download buttons are performing well, with the 'Download Windows Free Trial' button generating the most clicks on his entire site according to CrazyEgg heatmap data.
quotes.key_insight: Respecting established web conventions like default link colors can reduce user confusion and improve click-through rates, even when custom colors look better aesthetically.

quotes.quote: That Download Windows Free Trial button is, incidentally, the most incandescent place anywhere on my website.

quotes.quote: Sales have been nicely juiced by my crusade against usability bugs these last two weeks.
quotes.summary: Patrick reports mid-month sales stats for May 2007, showing 15 total sales (10 downloads, 5 CDs) generating about $399 in gross revenue and $312 in profit. His recent focus on fixing usability bugs has noticeably boosted sales. CD sales are increasing but cut into margins since he subsidizes each one by about fifty cents. His AdWords campaign is performing exceptionally well at 22 cents per trial download, well below his 30-cent target, making customer acquisition highly profitable.
quotes.key_insight: When your customer acquisition cost is less than a third of your sale price, the rational move is to scale spending aggressively rather than optimize further.

quotes.quote: I predicted I would be on the front page of Google for the string 'increase software sales' within a week. It actually took about 48 hours and I'm number two.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides updates on several fronts: his SEO article ranked #2 on Google for a competitive search term within 48 hours, demonstrating the blogging-for-SEO strategy he advocates. He discusses redesigning website icons after trademark guideline concerns, a CrazyEgg/e-junkie compatibility bug affecting his sales funnel, and shares good news about a promising job interview with a Japanese software company. The post illustrates the day-to-day reality of running a micro-ISV while job hunting in Japan.
quotes.key_insight: A well-targeted blog post with proper SEO fundamentals can rank on Google's front page for competitive terms within days, demonstrating that content marketing is one of the most accessible growth levers for small software businesses.

quotes.quote: Congratulations on finishing an application that real people think was worth spending money on. That's an accomplishment. It is also the easiest part about running a software business.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive playbook for micro-ISV developers looking to increase software sales beyond their initial launch. He covers seven key strategies: installing analytics, SEO optimization, link building through valuable content, blogging with evergreen content, eliminating checkout barriers, offering money-back guarantees, and optimizing AdWords campaigns. The advice is grounded in his own experience selling Bingo Card Creator and emphasizes writing content in the language customers actually use rather than marketing jargon. He stresses that improving sales is a long-term iterative process of testing and observation, not a quick fix.
quotes.key_insight: Increasing software sales is fundamentally about creating genuinely useful content in the language your customers actually use, then systematically removing every barrier between discovery and purchase.

quotes.quote: Writing evergreens is like investing in yourself — it is a way for today's labor to pay dividends tomorrow and every day thereafter.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive playbook for micro-ISV developers looking to increase software sales beyond their initial launch. He covers seven key strategies: installing analytics, SEO optimization, link building through valuable content, blogging with evergreen content, eliminating checkout barriers, offering money-back guarantees, and optimizing AdWords campaigns. The advice is grounded in his own experience selling Bingo Card Creator and emphasizes writing content in the language customers actually use rather than marketing jargon. He stresses that improving sales is a long-term iterative process of testing and observation, not a quick fix.
quotes.key_insight: Increasing software sales is fundamentally about creating genuinely useful content in the language your customers actually use, then systematically removing every barrier between discovery and purchase.

quotes.quote: When your customers are not the technically sharpest knives in the drawer, sometimes I think a little redundancy can be a good thing.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents his iterative process of redesigning download buttons for Bingo Card Creator, focusing on pixel-perfect alignment and adding explanatory text for non-technical users. He describes spending significant time on per-pixel adjustments to ensure visual consistency between button variants. The post captures the obsessive attention to detail required when optimizing conversion elements for unsophisticated users. He also candidly shares mistakes made during the process, including a flopped layout he didn't catch initially. The post illustrates how seemingly trivial UI elements can consume disproportionate effort when precision matters for conversions.
quotes.key_insight: Optimizing conversion elements for non-technical users requires obsessive attention to visual details and redundant cues that technical people would consider unnecessary.

quotes.quote: You've got to sand down the rough edges in your checkout funnel or they'll bleed you to death.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovered that a usability bug in his e-junkie shopping cart was silently destroying CD sales by requiring an unintuitive extra step (clicking 'update cart' before checkout) that 94% of interested buyers failed to complete. The cart displayed error messages when customers with shipping items clicked checkout without first entering their zip code and updating, causing massive abandonment at each step. He traced the problem through conversion data showing CD order rates had plummeted from 50% to single digits. After reporting the issue to e-junkie, they pushed a fix to all their hosted carts. He also notes the counterintuitive finding that a stepped checkout process (choose product, then choose payment) converts better than presenting all options at once, due to decision paralysis.
quotes.key_insight: Minor usability friction in checkout flows compounds multiplicatively — each small confusion point doesn't just lose some customers, it cascades to destroy nearly all conversions.

quotes.quote: I lose NINETY FOUR PERCENT of the people who have expressed interest in buying a CD.

quotes.quote: Information which was previously available through drilling down is apparently being forced into a new widgetized reporting format, which might be great but seems like it has a learning curve steeper than Mt. Everest.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reacts negatively to Google Analytics' redesigned interface, finding it less usable than the previous version. The new widgetized reporting format buries information that was previously accessible through straightforward drill-downs. He specifically struggles to answer basic business questions like month-over-month conversion rate comparisons. He hopes to eventually master the new interface but is frustrated by the productivity loss in the meantime.
quotes.key_insight: A product redesign that makes core workflows harder to accomplish is a step backward, regardless of how modern the new interface looks.

quotes.quote: Getting to the information which is of paramount interest to me, like "Am I doing better this month than last month on Google, in terms of conversion rates?" seems to be much more difficult than it used to be.

quotes.quote: I've disabled automatic downloading, given the users big bright buttons to find the PC vs Mac trials, and hooked it up to Analytics to see if I'm insane.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie redesigned the download page for Bingo Card Creator during a bout of insomnia. He replaced automatic downloading with explicit PC vs Mac trial download buttons and added Analytics tracking to measure the impact. The post is a brief late-night update sharing the change and asking for reader feedback.
quotes.key_insight: Every page in the conversion funnel deserves deliberate design and measurement, even the download confirmation page.

quotes.quote: Week-long holidays like Easter hurt like crazy — I had exactly one sale during Holy Week.
quotes.summary: Patrick reports April 2007 stats for Bingo Card Creator, showing 26 sales and $544 profit after expenses. Easter week severely hurt sales with only one purchase during the holiday. He breaks down traffic sources, noting AdWords visitors convert best while organic Google traffic converts poorly despite being the largest segment. He observes the long tail effect where roughly 30% of Google traffic and downloads come from the top 10 search terms, with the rest spread across hundreds of niche queries.
quotes.key_insight: For niche software businesses, the long tail of search queries collectively drives as much value as the obvious high-volume terms, and understanding attribution across the customer journey matters more than raw traffic numbers.

quotes.quote: Advertising directly on a competing site did not. Constant improvement on the main campaign did. Content network did, to my surprise.

quotes.quote: 2% more people (1 -> 3) are clicking the Buy Now! button.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports on the results of changing the icons/buttons on his Bingo Card Creator website. The new download button saw a 1% decrease in clicks (from 9.5% to 8.5%), but text links compensated to keep overall conversion steady. The Buy Now button saw a significant increase from 1% to 3% click-through. He notes uncertainty about whether the improvement came from the visual redesign or the copy change from 'Purchase Now' to 'Buy Now!'
quotes.key_insight: Small changes to button design and copy can meaningfully move conversion metrics, though isolating which variable caused the change requires more disciplined testing.

quotes.quote: I'm not entirely sure whether that is a result of just the redesigned buttons or the result of changing Purchase Now to Buy Now! but either way, what can I say, I like it.

quotes.quote: I have since learned about good practices for naming B2C software and that idea strikes me as extraordinarily silly now.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recounts how he originally considered naming his software 'Teacher's Pet' before settling on 'Bingo Card Creator' after finding better domain names taken. He discovers that teachers-pet.org has linked to his site, driving steady referral traffic that converts at 14%, nearly matching his organic search conversion rate. The post reflects on the value of unsolicited inbound links from relevant communities.
quotes.key_insight: Unsolicited links from small but highly relevant community sites can drive traffic and conversions on par with paid advertising, making organic community presence invaluable for niche software businesses.

quotes.quote: Apparently despite the PR2 they've got quite a number of regular users, because they put up a link to me about a week ago and its getting fairly steady traffic at about the same level as my AdWords campaign, and converting pretty well, too.

quotes.quote: That post is an evergreen — it never goes bad.
quotes.summary: McKenzie demonstrates the business value of blogging for micro-ISVs by sharing concrete results from a single blog post. His 'Free Bingo Cards' post generated nearly 1,000 hits in one month, converting to about 100 trials and 5 sales—roughly 20% of his monthly revenue. He emphasizes that the post is 'evergreen' content that continues generating traffic and sales indefinitely through search engines and sharing.
quotes.key_insight: A single piece of evergreen blog content can become a compounding marketing asset that generates a significant share of ongoing revenue with zero marginal cost.

quotes.quote: It continues to pick up search engine hits, links, emails to friends, yadda yadda, and keeps making me money.

quotes.quote: I decided to redo my sort of frumpy Buy Now! and Download Free Trial buttons for Bingo Card Creator, and see if the new versions aren't any more successful.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his process of upgrading the Buy Now and Download Free Trial buttons on Bingo Card Creator's website. He purchased Axialis Icon Workshop on community recommendations to create Web 2.0-style buttons, replacing his earlier Paint.NET creations. The post shows before-and-after comparisons and frames the redesign as an A/B test to see if newer button styles convert better. It's a brief, practical update typical of his early blogging about incremental improvements to his software business.
quotes.key_insight: Even minor visual elements like purchase buttons deserve deliberate design investment and testing, because they sit at the critical point of conversion.

quotes.quote: Obviously, doing it myself would transform me from a highly-paid software developer to a low-paid postal clerk, which is frankly stupid when there are plenty of businesses out there which offer ways to do it without ever having to lick a stamp.
quotes.summary: Patrick discovers Postful, a service that converts emails into physical postal mail, solving a key feature requirement for his Kalzumeus webapp. He had been planning to use Japan Post's NetPost API for automated letter sending, a feature his customers need for legal compliance and would find highly valuable. Postful's simpler interface (send an email, they mail a letter) at a lower cost ($0.99 vs $2.25) eliminates the need to integrate another complex API. The simplicity means the feature can ship in the 1.0 release rather than being deferred to 1.1.
quotes.key_insight: The best technical solution isn't always the most powerful API — a simpler integration that reuses existing code can ship faster, cost less, and reduce failure modes.

quotes.quote: I really hate having to learn another API. It is one more thing that can go wrong with my application, and the consequences of a letter not being sent when my app reports it sent are rather severe.

quotes.quote: Its certainly 25% feature complete but its not 25% of the way ready to actually have users.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a brief progress update on his Kalzumeus side project, noting it has reached 25% feature completion but is far from being user-ready. The interface exists as functional skeletons but needs significant polish before public exposure. He plans to reveal the project publicly at 50% completion, potentially within a week. He mentions spending Japan's Golden Week holiday coding and writing resumes rather than vacationing.
quotes.key_insight: Feature completion percentage is misleading because having functional code is only a fraction of the work needed to make software ready for actual users.

quotes.quote: I have functional skeletons up for most of it but they are too ugly to ever show to people, even if they do have the right form elements on them.

quotes.quote: I had not one but two issues that couldn't be taken care of in a five minute email. Oh no!
quotes.summary: Patrick describes his busiest customer support day, handling two issues: a customer who couldn't receive their registration key despite multiple attempts, and a CD that was never delivered. He shares his approach to handling shipping errors—absorbing the cost to maintain goodwill—and briefly notes that April sales were hurt by Easter but are recovering toward a potential new record.
quotes.key_insight: When shipping errors happen in a small software business, eating the cost to make it right is trivially cheap compared to the customer goodwill it generates.

quotes.quote: Customer error in the input field is the #1 cause of non-delivery, by far, so any time you get a report of non-delivery you should suggest in a non-confrontational way 'Could you just confirm your address for me so I can send this out?'

quotes.quote: Trying to compete with established firms only on price in software is kind of suicidal.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reviews ByteCommerce, a digital download service that pitched him via email, and finds its supposedly innovative pricing structure uncompetitive. The service takes every 20th sale as its fee, effectively a 5% commission, which McKenzie calculates is more expensive than e-junkie's flat $5/month for anyone making over $100/month. He also identifies a practical problem with refunds on sales collected by a third party. The post concludes with broader lessons about why competing on price alone is dangerous without a structural cost advantage.
quotes.key_insight: Competing on price only works as a strategy if you have a structural cost advantage that ensures you can always be the cheapest; otherwise, a flat-fee competitor will inevitably undercut any percentage-based pricing model as customers scale.

quotes.quote: I don't choose business partners based on innovative pricing, I choose them based on cheap pricing, if I consider pricing at all.

quotes.quote: The test has showed me several places where my website was broken where I thought it was working perfectly.
quotes.summary: McKenzie compares CrazyEgg's heatmap tracking with Google Analytics' site overlay for understanding user behavior on his Bingo Card Creator website. He discovers that Analytics' per-URL click tracking was giving him fundamentally misleading data, while CrazyEgg's per-coordinate tracking revealed that his screenshot image was getting clicked by 33% of visitors—a catastrophic bounce risk—and that a text link he thought was performing well had zero clicks. Based on these insights, he installed Lightbox to prevent screenshot bounces, planned to rewrite his page copy, and concluded that CrazyEgg's $9/month price was trivially justified by the conversion improvements it enabled.
quotes.key_insight: Different analytics tools can tell fundamentally different stories about user behavior due to their underlying tracking mechanisms, and choosing the wrong one—or not understanding how it works—can leave you optimizing based on faulty data.

quotes.quote: I find money salves ego pretty well. Its like a cold compress made of cash, and without good customer service you have less cash to compress!
quotes.summary: McKenzie dissects a case study where an RSS reader developer's aggressive product activation system and accusatory customer service email turned a deliriously happy customer into a vocal public critic. The post traces how a paying customer who actively recommended the product was flagged by activation code, then received an email implying license breach, leading to a Digg-amplified PR disaster. McKenzie argues the developer had multiple opportunities to avoid the situation: using better activation methods, accepting some key abuse as a cost of business, or simply responding with trust and grace. He provides example emails showing how to handle activation concerns without accusation, demonstrating that assuming good faith costs nothing while suspicion can cost everything.
quotes.key_insight: Treating paying customers as suspected pirates costs far more in lost goodwill and public reputation than any piracy ever would, so default to trust in every customer interaction.

quotes.quote: Doing that required working around some compatibility quirks in Firefox versus IE, so to save anyone else the trouble of doing it you can just grab it from my site.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a small JavaScript enhancement to the Lightbox library that allows users to dismiss images by pressing the escape key. He encountered browser compatibility issues between Firefox and IE when implementing this feature and shares the fix publicly. The post documents specific JavaScript quirks around keyboard event handling across browsers. It's a brief, practical technical note aimed at other developers using Lightbox on their sites.
quotes.key_insight: Cross-browser JavaScript compatibility issues, even for simple features like keyboard event handling, require careful attention to each browser's distinct event model.

quotes.quote: IE will let you test a keyCode against an integer, including a non-printing ASCII code such as 27 (the escape key). Firefox requires you to test against a constant.

quotes.quote: IE captures non-printing keyboard keys (other than F1-F12, apparently) in the keypressed event, but Firefox only captures them in the keydown event.

quotes.quote: The freakishly high chances that two people in a related field will again do business with each other are as good a reason as anything to be faultlessly polite and fair in all of your business dealings.
quotes.summary: Patrick briefly shares that he nailed a job interview, noting that his current boss's relationship with the prospective employer and a former coworker's hiring authority helped. He uses this as a springboard to make a broader point about professional networking: the surprisingly high probability that people in related fields will cross paths again is reason enough to maintain impeccable professional conduct at all times.
quotes.key_insight: The professional world is small enough that you should treat every business interaction as if you'll encounter that person again — because you probably will.

quotes.quote: Many folks think you have to be superman to navigate the IRS' maze, when they are actually decently taxpayer-focused and charged by regulations to make things easy for someone with a six grade reading level.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie demystifies tax filing for micro-ISV (uISV) owners, arguing that the process is far simpler than most aspiring entrepreneurs fear. He walks through his actual 2006 tax forms for Bingo Card Creator, showing that only four forms were needed and the entire process took about three hours. His Schedule C-EZ shows $2,480 in gross receipts, $1,225 in expenses, and $1,255 in net profit, with self-employment tax of $177. He acknowledges that complex situations like AMT or home offices would warrant hiring an accountant, but for most small software businesses, taxes are straightforward.
quotes.key_insight: Tax filing for a small software business is far simpler than most people fear, requiring only basic arithmetic and a few forms, and should not be a barrier to starting a business.

quotes.quote: These are generally teeny-tiny thumbnails which exist more to demonstrate the fact that there is a real purchasable product than anything else.
quotes.summary: Patrick describes a common usability problem with software screenshot pages: customers click thumbnail images and get lost viewing the full-size image, unable to navigate back. He notes his screenshots had 80%+ bounce rates until he addressed this. He recommends Lightbox, a JavaScript widget that overlays the screenshot on the current page with a grayed-out background, keeping the sales context visible. He chose the simpler Lightbox over Lightbox2, which he found to be a usability nightmare for his non-technical audience.
quotes.key_insight: Every UI element on your sales page should keep customers moving toward purchase, and even something as mundane as screenshot presentation can cause massive bounce rates if it breaks the customer's flow.

quotes.quote: Put in email address, put in password, fill out two line form about page to test, copy one line of provided HTML into page, upload. 30 seconds or less to the shiny.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reviews CrazyEgg, a visual analytics tool that competes with Google's free offerings. While he praises CrazyEgg's exceptional usability and heatmap visualization, he notes that Google Analytics and Google Website Optimizer already provide the core functionality for free. He highlights the fundamental challenge of competing with a free product from Google, even when your user experience is significantly better. The post also explains why proper A/B split testing (randomized allocation) is superior to sequential testing, especially for businesses with cyclical traffic patterns.
quotes.key_insight: A paid product can have vastly superior usability and still lose to a free competitor that's already entrenched — but there's a real opportunity in making powerful-but-painful free tools actually usable.

quotes.quote: I have already climbed Mt. Analytics Learning Curve and can now bask in the awesome view — such as dicing visitors down by where they came from, watching search terms trends, and tracking click paths around my site. For free.

The post also explains why proper A/B split testing (randomized allocation) is superior to sequential testing, especially for businesses with cyclical traffic patterns.
quotes.key_insight: A paid product can have vastly superior usability and still lose to a free competitor that's already entrenched — but there's a real opportunity in making powerful-but-painful free tools actually usable.

quotes.quote: One would think that since essentially every webapp needs this functionality there would be an easy, non-repetitive way to do it in Ruby but since the precise specifics differ for every application I think its probably easiest to roll your own.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports progress on his side project Kalzumeus, having built user authentication, account creation, and login functionality in Ruby on Rails during a sick half-day off work. He discusses the tradeoffs of rolling your own authentication versus using plugins, touches on web security concerns like XSS and SQL injection, and reflects on his struggle to adopt test-driven development. The post also highlights his appreciation for Rails scaffolding as a development tool and ends with a cultural aside about Japanese adoption of English gaming terminology.
quotes.key_insight: Even experienced developers face the recurring tension between building authentication from scratch versus using existing solutions, because the specifics of every application make generic solutions harder to adapt than to rebuild.

quotes.quote: Web application developers who allow that or its close cousin an email with your user name and password written in it need to have 'SMTP is not secure' carved onto their eyeballs so that they will not forget.

quotes.quote: Much more code is actually sticking to the screen and its been a matter of hours since I did a rm -rf * on the project directory.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports progress on his second software project, Kalzumeus, built with Ruby on Rails. He estimates the project at roughly 15 classes, similar in complexity to Bingo Card Creator. He discusses his IDE choice (Netbeans), the tradeoffs between Ruby and Java development workflows, and his plan to save money by using a free open-source web design template instead of hiring a designer. He also briefly entertains the idea of going full-time on his software business but decides it's too risky for now.
quotes.key_insight: Early-stage software entrepreneurs should minimize upfront costs and launch with 'good enough' solutions—free templates, simple architecture—while keeping a conventional income safety net until the business proves itself.

quotes.quote: I've noticed so far that I'm spending much, much less time on nuts&bolts programming than I typically do in Java, and much, much more time on chasing down misnamed variables, typos, and the like.

quotes.quote: Best month ever. Would have done better had I not disabled CD orders inadvertently for most of it.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his best month ever for Bingo Card Creator, with 30 sales generating $760 gross and $673 net profit in March 2007. He notes that accidentally disabling CD orders cost him significant sales, and gives himself 50-50 odds of hitting $1,000 in April. AdWords is performing well at $0.34 per demo download, while SEO gains are bringing organic links from school districts directly to his landing pages. The post also celebrates his blog hitting 100,000 page views and thanks his community of readers.
quotes.key_insight: At low price points, the economics of paid acquisition create a hard ceiling on growth, making organic channels like SEO and content marketing disproportionately valuable.

quotes.quote: Given that literally one thousand readers have come to take a gander at that post over the last 24 hours, most attributable to that email newsletter, I would estimate that librarian's reach at probably in the high five to low six digits worth of extraordinarily dedicated readers.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers that a librarian's email newsletter drove 1,000 visitors to his blog in 24 hours after featuring his customer service post. He reverse-engineers the referral chain from a librarian blog to the newsletter, and uses the unexpected traffic spike to illustrate two points: every software entrepreneur should have a blog, and non-technical professions like librarians represent viable, overlooked software markets.
quotes.key_insight: Software entrepreneurs miss huge markets by assuming non-technical professionals aren't viable customers, when in reality these communities are large, engaged, and reachable through their own thriving online networks.

quotes.quote: Librarians, like school teachers, are quite smart folks who are not typically described as being on the leading edge of the technology curve. quotes.quote: Squidoo is a wonderful technology for Seth Godin and, well, somewhat less wonderful for its users.
quotes.summary: McKenzie experiments with Squidoo, a content platform created by Seth Godin, as a tool for organic SEO linkbuilding. He describes the platform as a hybrid between wikis and blogs that allows users to create 'lenses' on specific topics. While he finds the concept promising for SEO since Squidoo lenses rank well on Google, he criticizes the AJAX-heavy editing interface as frustratingly slow and limiting. He creates a lens on teaching dolch sight words, strategically seeding it with links to his own properties. He notes that the platform's most popular content is dominated by spammy make-money-online schemes.
quotes.key_insight: Content platforms designed for user-generated knowledge inevitably become SEO arbitrage tools, useful primarily as linkbuilding vehicles rather than for their stated educational purpose.

quotes.quote: I've had my best month of sales ever, but only 1 CD in that time. Typically about half of my customers get the CD.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovered that a configuration error in his e-junkie payment links had silently broken CD purchases for weeks, despite having his best sales month ever. When he switched item numbers to accommodate SwiftCD integration, he forgot to update the corresponding links on his site. The bug went undetected because most customers simply opted for the download instead, and the failure mode was invisible to him as the seller. He reflects on the difficulty of monitoring opaque third-party system integrations and praises the SwiftCD/e-junkie automation that eliminated manual CD fulfillment work.
quotes.key_insight: Silent failures in third-party integrations can cost you sales for weeks without triggering any alerts, making regular monitoring of your entire purchase flow essential.

quotes.quote: It is far easier to justify someone paying you $250 if you save them $2000 than it is to justify someone paying you $24.95 if you save them $10-15 a few times a year.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his market selection process for Kalzumeus, his second software product. He identifies a niche in the investment space where small businesses face high-value problems that existing solutions address poorly due to prohibitive startup costs. He plans to build a web application targeting these underserved small fish with simple pricing and lower barriers to entry. His strategic thinking reveals a deliberate shift from hobby software (Bingo Card Creator) toward building a business that could eventually replace full-time employment.
quotes.key_insight: The most promising micro-ISV opportunities exist where incumbents have high fixed costs that exclude small customers, creating an underserved niche that actively complains about unsolved problems.

quotes.quote: Many of the solutions involve calculations on paper, elbow grease, and very expensive professionals doing things that could be done by a trained monkey with a slide rule.

quotes.quote: I am an ardent proponent of the theory that product names are more important for branding purposes than company names as a uISV.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces the name of his new LLC, Kalzumeus Software, ending months of referring to his upcoming venture as 'the second project.' He explains his philosophy that product names matter far more than company names for micro-ISVs, since the company is just a legal fiction for interfacing with governments and corporations. The name comes from a talking dragon in a long-running RPG campaign, chosen partly because it was easy to secure as a domain name due to its uniqueness. He outlines plans to transfer Bingo Card Creator to the new LLC and launch a second product under the Kalzumeus banner.
quotes.key_insight: For a micro-ISV, the company name is just legal plumbing — what matters for branding is the product name, since that's what customers actually encounter and remember.

quotes.quote: After all, the company is just a legal fiction designed to give me an easier way to relate government authorities and other corporations who expect entity-to-entity rather than person-to-person relationships.

quotes.quote: Its very easy to forget, particularly if you're doing your own taxes, but the IRS is offering a credit against your taxes (or a refund if you don't owe taxes) if you paid for long distance service in the US.
quotes.summary: McKenzie alerts readers to a commonly overlooked IRS tax credit called the Telephone Excise Tax Refund, available to Americans who paid for long distance service between 2003 and 2006. He explains the two calculation methods and recommends taking the standard refund since most people don't keep old phone bills. The post walks through the exact lines on the 1040 and 1040EZ where the credit should be entered. He emphasizes that this free money won't appear automatically on your return.
quotes.key_insight: Small tax credits like the Telephone Excise Tax Refund are easy to overlook but free money if you remember to claim them on your return.

quotes.quote: Don't have your phone bills? Yeah, me neither. So take the standard refund.

quotes.quote: I'm having my best month ever.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his best month ever for Bingo Card Creator sales as of mid-March 2007, with 19 sales generating approximately $475 in gross income and $360 in profit. He attributes the success to organic growth and improvements to his AdWords campaign rather than any specific new initiative. He expresses optimism about potentially hitting his $1,000 monthly sales target in March instead of April. He also notes his next project is on hold while he searches for a day job.
quotes.key_insight: Consistent organic growth and small optimizations to paid acquisition can compound into milestone months without requiring dramatic new initiatives.

quotes.quote: Google has since picked up on this and that sends a lot of folks here who are looking for many types of bingo cards.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie created a comprehensive SEO-driven resource page listing free bingo cards available through his product Bingo Card Creator. The page serves as a landing page targeting various long-tail search queries like 'Christmas bingo cards,' 'Harry Potter bingo cards,' and 'sight words bingo cards.' Each section links to free resources while funneling visitors toward downloading the free trial of his software. The post demonstrates his strategy of creating content that matches specific search intent while converting organic traffic into product trials.
quotes.key_insight: Creating free, search-optimized content pages that target specific long-tail queries is an effective strategy for converting organic search traffic into product trial downloads.

quotes.quote: If you assume my average is about 4 minutes an email, which is pretty close to accurate, then I am paying myself roughly $370 an hour, give or take, to support Bingo Card Creator.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie quantifies exactly how much customer support his micro-ISV (Bingo Card Creator) requires, finding it far less burdensome than skeptics assume. Over a six-week period with 600+ installations, 2,000 downloads, and 32 sales, he wrote only 27 emails total. He breaks down the emails by category and calculates his effective hourly rate for support at roughly $370/hour. He argues that even scaling to $100,000 in annual revenue would only mean about 11 emails per day, and suspects support scales sub-linearly with customers.
quotes.key_insight: Customer support for a simple B2C software product is far less burdensome than most people assume, and the real leverage comes from systematically eliminating repeat inquiries rather than just answering them faster.

quotes.quote: If it scaled linearly with customers and I was selling 5,000 units a year (income in excess of $100,000 USD) I'd be writing a backbreaking 11 emails a day.

quotes.quote: Its March 13th in Japan and my profits for 2007 just exceeded my profits for 2006.

He projects March will be his best month ever, with sales between $800-$1,000 against roughly $150 in expenses. This very brief post captures an early milestone in the growth of Bingo Card Creator, his first software product.
quotes.key_insight: Even at modest absolute numbers, rapid year-over-year growth in a solo software business signals compounding momentum.

quotes.quote: I think I'll hit $800 to $1,000 in sales for March, smashing my previous records.
quotes.summary: McKenzie predicts he will hit $800-$1,000 in sales for March, which would smash his previous records. He attributes the growth to natural increases in Google traffic and a higher trial-to-purchase conversion rate, noting downloads are up 10%. He observes that order volume has doubled from the typical rate. He also mentions having an Easter promotion planned for April's $1,000 target but suspects organic growth alone may carry him there.
quotes.key_insight: Sustained investment in SEO and conversion optimization can produce compounding organic growth that eventually outpaces what promotions alone can deliver.

quotes.quote: I had assumed Windows would automatically default to the program directory. This is apparently not the case on some systems.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a technical gotcha with Inno Setup installer software where shortcuts don't automatically set the working directory to the program directory. This caused quirky behavior for some users because Java's facility for locating the working directory was returning unexpected paths on certain systems. The issue was masked during testing because Inno Setup sets a sensible default when launching directly from the installer. He announces a patch release (1.052) to fix the problem.
quotes.key_insight: Cross-platform compatibility fixes can introduce subtle regressions that are invisible during testing but affect real users, especially when the testing environment masks the problem.

quotes.quote: I hadn't noticed this problem because Inno Setup actually does set a sensible default working directory when you execute the program directly from the installer.

quotes.quote: I kind of like the feeling of making more money while spending the evening out than the evening out actually costs.

He reflects on the satisfying feeling of earning passive income while socializing. A customer support request about a corrupted file raises concerns about his hosting provider GoDaddy potentially mangling his executable files again.
quotes.key_insight: A solo software business reaching the point where passive overnight sales exceed your evening's spending is a meaningful early milestone.

quotes.quote: With the frequency that I break my software I can't afford for my hosting provider to break it, too.

quotes.quote: Owing to my carelessness sales are quite down this month.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his February 2007 financial results for Bingo Card Creator, showing $454 in gross sales and approximately $345 in profit. Sales were down due to a self-inflicted technical error that broke the download for most visitors. Despite the revenue setback, website traffic hit an all-time high, and AdWords spending improved. He estimates he could have hit $800-900 in sales without the download bug, and closes with a charming aside about attending an early St. Patrick's Day parade in Nagoya, Japan.
quotes.key_insight: A single careless technical error on your download funnel can cut your revenue in half even when traffic is at an all-time high, underscoring how conversion mechanics matter more than raw visitor counts.

quotes.quote: If I hadn't borked the download most of my visitors were getting and my conversion rate had remained steady I would have hit about $800-900 in sales without breaking a sweat.

quotes.quote: The notion of Japanese people throwing a St. Patrick's Day parade is so charming as to forgive the fact that they miss the actual day by quite a margin every single year.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his February 2007 financial results for Bingo Card Creator, showing $454 in gross sales and approximately $345 in profit. Sales were down due to a self-inflicted technical error that broke the download for most visitors. Despite the revenue setback, website traffic hit an all-time high, and AdWords spending improved. He estimates he could have hit $800-900 in sales without the download bug, and closes with a charming aside about attending an early St. Patrick's Day parade in Nagoya, Japan.
quotes.key_insight: A single careless technical error on your download funnel can cut your revenue in half even when traffic is at an all-time high, underscoring how conversion mechanics matter more than raw visitor counts.

quotes.quote: People who already use Paypal are likely to seek it out as an option when given comparable alternatives.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares early transaction data comparing Google Checkout and PayPal adoption among his Bingo Card Creator customers. From 29 transactions, PayPal slightly edged out Google Checkout (16 vs 13), with the notable finding that no Google Checkout users had preexisting accounts while about 60% of PayPal users did. He interprets this as evidence that existing PayPal users actively seek it out when given comparable alternatives. He also notes that his customer demographics (middle-aged women familiar with eBay) likely skew more PayPal-friendly than the tech community.
quotes.key_insight: Payment provider preference is driven far more by existing habits and familiarity than by conscious evaluation, which means reducing friction at checkout matters more than which provider you offer.

quotes.quote: I suspect many customers are just mashing on any button that they know is in the contextually accurate place for Continue This Transaction Darn It rather than making a conscious decision for or against either provider.

quotes.quote: Correlation != causation and all that aside, it only cost me about $8 so maybe you want to try it too.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares an SEO tip from WebsiteGrader suggesting that domain registrations expiring within a year may be penalized by search engines as potential spam sites. He extended his domain registration by two years and prepaid hosting, noting his Google rankings improved afterward. He honestly acknowledges he cannot prove causation and hasn't seen this tip corroborated by trusted SEO sources. He frames it as a low-risk experiment since the $8 cost would be spent eventually regardless.
quotes.key_insight: Low-cost, low-risk SEO experiments like extending domain registration are worth trying even without definitive proof they work, since the downside is negligible.

quotes.quote: Google and other search engines like to see domains that have been registered for extended periods of time as this shows a committment to the domain name.
