quotes.quote: Volume? Low. Interface? Annoying. Work flow? Terrible. Cost per download? Half of what Google is, and this required no tweaking whatsoever.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports that Microsoft's AdCenter advertising platform, despite its clunky interface and low volume, delivers downloads at half the cost per acquisition of Google AdWords. His simple ad for Bingo Card Creator achieved a 4% click-through rate and under 30 cents per download with zero optimization. He notes that large advertisers like Amazon bid on every keyword, but his targeted ad still performs well. He's cautiously optimistic but uncertain whether the low volume justifies further time investment in optimization.
quotes.key_insight: Less popular advertising platforms can deliver dramatically lower acquisition costs precisely because fewer advertisers compete there, even if the volume and tooling are inferior.

quotes.quote: I'm buried under about four ads for places like Amazon which apparently bid on every word in the English language.

quotes.quote: I was performing an involuntary A-B test: A with the feature, and B without, based on what day you had first downloaded the software. A wins by a longshot.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovered that a critical feature of Bingo Card Creator had been silently broken for two weeks due to a single-character error in his installer script. The bug caused new downloads to lack the Wizards menu, which pre-loads word lists and eliminates the 'empty screen problem' that kills conversions. His sales dropped to 20-30% of normal despite record traffic, essentially running an involuntary A/B test proving the feature's importance. The bug went undetected because his testing always ran on machines that retained the old correct file structure. The experience taught him hard lessons about version-controlling build scripts, testing on clean environments, and listening to customers.
quotes.key_insight: Build artifacts and deployment scripts need the same version control and testing rigor as source code, because a single untracked change can silently destroy your conversion funnel.

quotes.quote: Heck yes I am fanatical about customer service. Are you?
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares an example of proactive customer service email he sent when a customer's credit card had issues with Google Checkout, resulting in triple charges. He canceled the duplicate orders and delivered the software immediately on trust, rather than waiting for payment resolution. He then critiques his own email, noting flaws in its language and tone. The post advocates for taking control of customer interactions when circumstances go wrong, rather than letting process failures damage the customer relationship.
quotes.key_insight: Great customer service means proactively resolving problems in the customer's favor and controlling the narrative, even when the failure is someone else's fault.

quotes.quote: I've drunken deeply from the Rails koolaid and am now afflicted with an acute condition known as programming fever.
quotes.summary: McKenzie outlines his development timetable and budget for his next software project (which would become Kalzumeus), planned alongside his day job. He commits to building a Rails application in 20 hours per week with an April 1st alpha target and summer launch. He details plans to develop semi-publicly, sharing market analysis, specs, and sales figures while keeping the project name private. The total initial budget is estimated at $1,500-$2,000, with monthly costs kept low enough to reach profitability quickly.
quotes.key_insight: A bootstrapped software business can be launched on a shoestring budget of under $2,000 by building the product yourself, sequencing expenses carefully, and bringing in outside help only after the core application is proven.

quotes.quote: I'm decided on developing this semi-publicly — I'll be publishing the market, the spec, the thinking behind it, pretty much everything but the source code.

quotes.quote: What an age to be living in where you can get a package to a little ricefield on the other side of the world in about a week for 84 cents.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares his experience receiving a SwiftCD physical copy of his Bingo Card Creator software, noting the impressive print quality. He documents the shipping timeline from order to delivery in Japan, marveling at the speed and low cost of international shipping for his small software business's physical product fulfillment.
quotes.key_insight: Modern fulfillment services let even the smallest software businesses deliver professional physical products globally at negligible cost.

quotes.quote: Sales for the entire year, 2006: $2,100 or so
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a brief revenue update for his software business Bingo Card Creator, showing that in just the first 45 days of 2007 he has already earned half of his entire 2006 revenue. He sets targets of $1,000/month by April and $10,000 for the full year, noting that profits are tracking similarly well against the prior year.
quotes.key_insight: Tracking and publicly sharing concrete revenue milestones creates accountability and makes growth tangible, even at very small scales.

quotes.quote: I really need to start anticipating holidays and releasing special versions/ads for them, because otherwise my sales tend to decline to zero around them.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a mid-February 2007 financial update for Bingo Card Creator, reporting $294 in gross sales from 11 units with roughly $195 in profit after expenses. Sales dipped around Valentine's Day, highlighting the need to anticipate holidays with special versions and ads. He details his cost structure including CD fulfillment, PayPal fees, AdWords, and hosting. Traffic has grown significantly, averaging 400 weekday visitors compared to 100 in November, and a blog post about problem customers went viral with 5,000 views reaching audiences far beyond his usual demographic.
quotes.key_insight: Even a tiny software business can be consistently profitable when expenses are kept low, and content marketing through blogging can drive traffic growth that far exceeds paid advertising.

quotes.quote: The only thing that matters is you keeping your cool, and this can frequently require having the patience of Job and less ego than a blade of grass.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that software business owners should always refund small amounts to unhappy customers without argument, using a real example from Dropsend where the owner escalated a $5 refund request into a hostile exchange. He contends that suppressing your ego and immediately resolving the complaint costs almost nothing compared to the revenue lost from an angry customer who cancels or files a chargeback. McKenzie advocates adopting a pre-committed strategy of obsequiousness in customer service, drawing on the Japanese concept of tatemae. He dismisses common objections like company refund policies and slippery-slope fears, noting that as a business owner you can override any policy at will. The core message is that you can never win an argument with a customer, so don't start one.
quotes.key_insight: Winning an argument with a customer is impossible — the only winning move is to immediately resolve their complaint, because the cost of ego is always higher than the cost of a refund.

quotes.quote: I did order entry for two years and hate it (love talking to customers, hate typing addresses).
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie documents his switch from cd-fulfillment.com to SwiftCD for physical CD fulfillment of his Bingo Card Creator software. The primary motivation was automating order processing—half his customers were requesting CDs, creating a daily manual data entry burden that quadrupled his support workload. SwiftCD's integration with e-junkie eliminated the need to wake up early and manually copy-paste shipping details. He notes SwiftCD actively courted him (likely for the blog coverage), and he's willing to absorb a small per-unit loss on CDs for the convenience and sleep.
quotes.key_insight: Automate fulfillment operations as soon as manual processes become a daily burden—the time and mental overhead cost far more than the per-unit price difference.

quotes.quote: When folks ask me "So how exactly did your uISV come about?", I generally tell them to go to the start of the blog and start reading.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares a fan-created tool that reorganizes his blog into chronological order. A reader named Rizal built a 'MicroISV on a Shoestring Reader' mashup that displays the blog from oldest to newest, solving the problem that blog software defaults to reverse-chronological order. McKenzie appreciates this because he often directs people to read his blog from the beginning to understand his uISV journey.
quotes.key_insight: A blog documenting a micro-ISV journey is best read chronologically, but blog software defaults work against this, creating an opportunity for reader-built tools.

quotes.quote: I'm going to make it easier for you to make a lot of money. You're going to pay me a trivial amount every month and be happy to do it. We'll be doing business together for YEARS.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces plans to start a new software project over the summer, a subscription-based web app targeting a specific vertical market. He estimates a $1,500 launch budget with modest monthly advertising spend, and projects the app could exceed his salary within a year. He plans to build it on LAMP, hire a lawyer and professional designer, and be as transparent about the process as he has been with Bingo Card Creator.
quotes.key_insight: A subscription web app that demonstrably helps customers make money can command loyal, long-term paying customers even against cheaper competition, provided the value proposition is clearly superior.

quotes.quote: The better option of the two, from my current research, would (with rather modest assumptions made about customer volume) probably exceed my current salary within a year of launch.

quotes.quote: In the last six months I have had several music teachers write in to ask me if Bingo Card Creator can create cards with musical notes on them. The answer is "yes", but the process is rather difficult.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes how to create musical note bingo cards using Bingo Card Creator with a special Bach font. Several music teachers had requested this feature, so he provides both step-by-step instructions for the DIY approach and a pre-made set of 32 cards as a free download. The post serves as both customer support content and a subtle SEO play targeting music teachers searching for musical bingo cards.
quotes.key_insight: Responding to repeated customer requests with free content that solves their problem serves simultaneously as customer support, SEO content targeting a long-tail niche, and a soft funnel back to the paid product.

quotes.quote: As this process is rather arduous for many teachers to perform, I have made 32 musical note bingo cards myself.

quotes.quote: I haven't really spent all that much time on my installer, which is somewhat silly because its the first look people get of my software and I should really want to nail it.
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains his decision to switch from NSIS to Inno Setup for his software installer, arguing that visual appearance matters because the installer is the user's first impression. He draws an analogy to his stock icons making software feel 'bright and happy' and notes that in consumer software, looks sell. He acknowledges the tradeoff that Inno Setup requires two extra clicks but plans to optimize that.
quotes.key_insight: Your installer is your software's first impression — visual polish matters as much there as anywhere else in the product.

quotes.quote: Especially in the consumer market, looks sell software.

quotes.quote: I get $2 in purchases for every $1 on AdWords, as opposed to $0.60-0.80 as of a month ago.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports fixing his underperforming AdWords campaign with two simple changes that dramatically improved economics. By enabling position preference to target the #2-3 ad spots instead of #1, he cut cost per download by 50% while actually improving conversion rates. He also ruthlessly killed keywords with cost-per-acquisition above his expected profit per download. The combined result: spending is down 50%, CPA is halved, and he now earns $2 in purchases for every $1 spent on AdWords, up from $0.60-0.80.
quotes.key_insight: In paid search advertising, the #1 position often has worse unit economics than lower positions — deliberately targeting #2-3 can cut costs while improving conversion quality.

quotes.quote: You know what I really like about the prospect of self-employment? No months of 12 to 16 hour days, and no forgetting what day it is.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recounts losing track of what day it is after a grueling month of 12-16 hour workdays culminating in a major presentation. The presentation went well and resulted in a functional spam filter that will be open-sourced. He reflects on how this experience highlights the appeal of self-employment, where such extreme work schedules wouldn't be necessary. He notes he still likes his day job despite the exhaustion.
quotes.key_insight: Grueling day-job schedules that cause you to lose track of time reinforce the appeal of self-employment and working on your own terms.

quotes.quote: It is more important that your ordering pathway be flawless than your application be the best in its class.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares a link to his interview on StartupSpark, where he discusses his experience as a micro-ISV (uISV) developer. He appreciates the interviewer's bingo-themed pun in the title. The post highlights his belief that the sales and ordering process matters more than product quality for software businesses. He acknowledges he still has room to improve his own ordering pathway.
quotes.key_insight: A flawless purchasing experience matters more to software sales than having the best product, yet most developers neglect their ordering pathway entirely.

quotes.quote: This is useful as backlinks are SEO mana from heaven and you can spy on whether your marketing is having any effect.
quotes.summary: Google has added a backlink viewing tool through its Sitemaps/Webmaster Console, allowing site owners to see who links to their website. McKenzie finds this useful for tracking the SEO impact of his marketing efforts. He notes that most of his backlinks come from download sites or spam blogs exploiting bingo-related keywords to boost casino sites. However, he finds some genuine links from real customers and useful content, including an elementary school linking to his Dolch sight word list page.
quotes.key_insight: Backlink data reveals both the spam ecosystem gaming search engines and the genuine organic links earned by creating useful content for your target audience.

quotes.quote: Remember that everyone speaks on the basis of their own experience.
quotes.summary: McKenzie notes a wave of interviews in the micro-ISV community, including Joel Spolsky's appearance on the MicroISV Show and three recent interviews with McKenzie himself. He uses the occasion to caution readers that all advice is shaped by the speaker's specific circumstances. He contrasts Spolsky's perspective as a multi-million-dollar company head with his own as a humble sole proprietor, arguing that success can take many forms. He closes with self-deprecating humor about his limited entrepreneurial experience.
quotes.key_insight: All business advice is autobiographical — filter every expert's recommendations through their specific circumstances before applying them to your own.

quotes.quote: If any customer were to ask for their money back after that period, I would go to any length of effort up to and including mailing them a personal check to get them their money back.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares his refund rate data from selling Bingo Card Creator, a $24.95 educational software product. Over six months, roughly 5% of customers requested refunds, though he considers this inflated by early software bugs, with the mature version seeing under 2%. He argues strongly for unconditional money-back guarantees, explaining that they actually increase sales by letting uncertain customers externalize risk. The most common refund reason — 'it doesn't do what I want' — proves the guarantee is working as intended, converting hesitant browsers into buyers.
quotes.key_insight: Money-back guarantees are pure upside for software sellers because they convert hesitant prospects into buyers while costing virtually nothing to honor.

quotes.quote: While its utilitarian, it doesn't exactly present a great image of my software or help my technically-disinclined customers through the process of getting the software to work.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes redesigning the CD label image for Bingo Card Creator's physical CD distribution, noting concerns about text density on the new design. He also evaluates switching CD fulfillment providers from cd-fulfillment.com to SwiftCD, weighing automation benefits against higher costs and reduced customization. The post illustrates the mundane operational decisions involved in running a one-person software business.
quotes.key_insight: Even small operational decisions like CD label design and fulfillment provider selection involve meaningful tradeoffs between cost, customer experience, and automation that solo software entrepreneurs must navigate constantly.

quotes.quote: Its about as easy to use as MSPaint, with many, many more features (layers! Full undo capability! Competent resizing algorithms!), and its perfect for the sort of 'I have an app icon but need to make it a CD logo' type chores that you're probably spending too much time on right now.
quotes.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically recommends Paint.NET 3.0 as a free graphics editing tool that bridges the gap between MSPaint's simplicity and Photoshop's power. He highlights its features like layers, full undo, and competent resizing as perfect for the small graphic tasks that micro-ISVs regularly face. He also follows through on a previous promise to donate to the project, tying it to his most profitable month ever.
quotes.key_insight: Micro-ISV operators should invest in efficient, free tools for routine tasks like graphics editing, and support the open-source projects that save them money.

quotes.quote: Yes, Virginia, that IS equivalent to taking $65 out my my wallet and setting fire to it.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his best month yet for Bingo Card Creator, with $553.70 in profit from 28 sales on roughly 6 hours of work. His purchase page redesign successfully drove CD sales, and Google Checkout is saving money on fulfillment costs. However, Google AdWords is performing terribly with only 1 sale attributable to $90 in spend, giving it a heavily negative ROI. He's seeing strong organic growth with downloads hitting 1,100 (up from 200 in September), though visitor-to-download conversion rates are falling as less qualified traffic increases. He outlines priorities for February including fixing AdWords ROI, redesigning the download page, and finally adopting source control.
quotes.key_insight: Even with strong organic growth compounding month over month, every marketing channel must justify its ROI or be cut — paying for traffic that doesn't convert is just burning money.

quotes.quote: Just when I think its safe to push out a new version I find that the Mac code has been severely compromised somehow and minor features like saving, printing, and displaying no longer work.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers that a new version of his software has broken critical features on the Mac platform, including saving, printing, and displaying. The bug resulted in at least one customer refund, which he finds particularly painful since it means losing someone who had already committed to purchasing. He reverts the Mac version and PAD file while working on a fix, crediting a colleague for help debugging.
quotes.key_insight: Paying customers who encounter broken software represent a uniquely painful failure because you've already won their trust and money, then immediately betrayed both.

quotes.quote: Free payment processing is nice.
quotes.summary: McKenzie compares his experience using PayPal and Google Checkout side by side for processing payments on Bingo Card Creator. PayPal offers faster access to funds through instant ATM card spending or 3-4 day ACH transfers, while Google Checkout bundles transactions with longer delays. Google's free payment processing is a significant advantage, especially for cross-border transactions where PayPal charges higher fees. However, Google Checkout's merchant interface is poorly designed, making basic tasks like searching transactions or querying sales totals unnecessarily difficult. Both services create customer communication challenges through email obfuscation or outdated addresses. The overall assessment is that Google Checkout's cost savings are attractive but its tooling reflects a product built by engineers unfamiliar with small business operations.
quotes.key_insight: Google Checkout's free processing makes it financially attractive for small software businesses, but its merchant tools are so poorly designed that basic operations like finding a transaction become absurdly difficult — ironic for the world's leading search company.

quotes.quote: Given that payment processing is my #2 expense every month after AdWords (#3 if you count taxes, I suppose) I'm quite happy seeing it get slashed.

quotes.quote: One thing that is even more critical is that you understand what the data would have looked like had you not made the change, because otherwise you could be changing for a spurious reason.
quotes.summary: McKenzie warns micro-ISV owners against misinterpreting website traffic data by failing to account for natural market cycles. Using his own Google organic traffic as an example, he shows how cyclical patterns like holiday breaks, weekends, and long weekends create noise that can be mistaken for the effects of algorithm changes or your own optimizations. He argues that before attributing any traffic change to a specific cause, you must first establish what the baseline pattern would look like without intervention. He illustrates this with his own recent meta description change, noting he doesn't yet have enough data to draw conclusions.
quotes.key_insight: Before attributing any change in your metrics to a specific cause, you must first establish the baseline pattern your data follows naturally, or you risk optimizing based on noise.

quotes.quote: What is actually happening here is that you are seeing the combination of natural cycles in my market combined with a sustained massive sustained increase in overall traffic since early December.

quotes.key_insight: Before attributing any change in your metrics to a specific cause, you must first establish the baseline pattern your data follows naturally, or you risk optimizing based on noise.

quotes.quote: As of yet, focusing on just the last week of results, I do not have sufficient data to conclude that this change has produced a meaningful push in either direction.
quotes.summary: McKenzie warns micro-ISV owners against misinterpreting website traffic data by failing to account for natural market cycles. Using his own Google organic traffic as an example, he shows how cyclical patterns like holiday breaks, weekends, and long weekends create noise that can be mistaken for the effects of algorithm changes or your own optimizations. He argues that before attributing any traffic change to a specific cause, you must first establish what the baseline pattern would look like without intervention. He illustrates this with his own recent meta description change, noting he doesn't yet have enough data to draw conclusions.
quotes.key_insight: Before attributing any change in your metrics to a specific cause, you must first establish the baseline pattern your data follows naturally, or you risk optimizing based on noise.

quotes.quote: "don't use . here, causes problems" is not a maximally useful comment.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents three specific Java cross-platform compatibility issues he encountered when releasing his Bingo Card Creator software on Mac. The post covers file path handling differences between Windows and Mac JARs, printer service lookup inconsistencies across platforms, and the danger of assuming individually-tested components will work correctly in combination. These are practical lessons learned from shipping real software to customers on multiple platforms.
quotes.key_insight: Cross-platform Java development reveals that individually correct components can combine to produce showstopper bugs, making platform-specific testing essential even for supposedly portable code.

quotes.quote: I would rather live in Baghdad than Tokyo.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares personal facts about himself as part of a blog meme chain in the micro-ISV community. He reveals his intense dislike of Tokyo despite living in Japan, his extensive job history of 16+ positions, his functional illiteracy in written Japanese despite working as a translator, his World of Warcraft guild leadership that preceded founding Bingo Card Creator, and his freakish ability to memorize entire books as a child. The post humanizes him beyond his professional persona and provides early glimpses of the personality traits that would define his later career.
quotes.key_insight: The restless intellectual energy that drove McKenzie through 16 jobs, World of Warcraft guild leadership, and memorizing entire books is the same energy that would eventually make him a successful software entrepreneur — he just needed to find the right outlet.

quotes.quote: I'm dependent on Google. Google properties send me 65% of my web traffic and account for over half of my sales.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reflects on his growing dependency on Google for his Bingo Card Creator business, noting that Google properties account for 65% of his web traffic and over half of his sales. He acknowledges that while this dependency is manageable because he has a day job as a safety net, it's concerning for his long-term goal of making his business his primary income. He evaluates several mitigation strategies including diversifying ad providers, maintaining backup payment systems, and finding non-search customer acquisition channels. The post concludes with the uncomfortable realization that even word-of-mouth referrals ultimately funnel back through Google search.
quotes.key_insight: Platform dependency on a single traffic source is an existential business risk that even diversified acquisition strategies struggle to mitigate when that platform dominates the entire discovery layer.

quotes.quote: I moved the option to buy a CD from purgatory at the bottom of the page to front-and-center. This has greatly increased the number of CDs bought.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports on the results of reorganizing his software purchase page. Moving the CD purchase option from the bottom to a prominent position dramatically increased CD orders, though the margins are razor-thin at $0.50 per disc. He also notes that Google Checkout captured half his sales surprisingly quickly, with most buyers creating new accounts just to complete the purchase, suggesting a longer-than-necessary checkout flow.
quotes.key_insight: Page placement is a more powerful conversion lever than most people realize — moving a purchase option from the bottom to the top of a page can dramatically change buying behavior, even when the underlying product and price remain identical.

quotes.quote: Trust is currency, because without trust I get no currency.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie defends his choice to use PayPal for his software business despite widespread community fears about account freezes. He argues that alternative payment processors like eSellerate, Avantage, and Plimus cost significantly more — hundreds of dollars extra on modest profits — while offering worse customer experiences. He frames PayPal as a calculated risk comparable to depending on any other business infrastructure, and notes that 70% of his customers already have PayPal accounts, making it a trust signal. He concludes with practical risk mitigation strategies like auto-sweeping balances to a bank account.
quotes.key_insight: For a small software business, the concrete costs of alternative payment processors and the trust value of a recognized brand like PayPal far outweigh the speculative risk of an account freeze, especially when that risk can be mitigated to near-zero with simple balance sweeping.

quotes.quote: When phenomenally successful Andy Brice asks 'What am I getting for an extra $880 per thousand transactions?' that is not a hypothetical question.

quotes.quote: I had to code Perl at my day job today… and was productive… and enjoyed it. Ewwwww.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recounts a day where he used Perl both at his day job and for personal projects, finding himself unexpectedly productive and enjoying the experience. At work, he used Perl to process 15,000 emails from mailing list archives for spam filter testing, replacing what would have been a laborious gawk-based approach with a single regular expression. At home, he used Perl to quickly modify JavaScript across his web pages for an e-junkie shopping cart integration. He humorously expresses guilt about enjoying Perl's notoriously unreadable syntax.
quotes.key_insight: Pragmatic tool choice trumps aesthetic preferences — Perl's ugly syntax is worth tolerating when it turns hours of text processing into minutes of work.

quotes.quote: God knows a Paypal customer with a problem has not a friend in the world.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents a Paypal bug where he couldn't withdraw money despite having already confirmed his primary email address. The system simultaneously told him he needed to confirm his email and that it was already confirmed. He found a workaround by adding a new disposable email address to the account, which resolved the withdrawal block without disrupting his payment notifications. He shares the fix publicly hoping Google will surface it for others stuck in the same catch-22.
quotes.key_insight: Payment platforms like Paypal can trap users in absurd catch-22 situations where the only escape is creative workarounds, since customer support is effectively unreachable.

quotes.quote: Unfortunately, I have the web-programming ability of a newborn squirrel, so I was resigned to always having my website look and feel a wee bit frumpy.
quotes.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically reviews e-junkie's new 'Fat Free Cart' feature, which adds a slick AJAX-powered shopping cart overlay to his Bingo Card Creator website. The integration took only 45 seconds via copy-pasted JavaScript, replacing his previous clunky ordering flow. He uses this as an opportunity to drop his secondary payment processor eSellerate, consolidating everything through e-junkie which now supports both PayPal and Google Checkout. He strongly recommends e-junkie for micro-ISV developers as a cheap, effective way to handle payment processing without distraction from core product work.
quotes.key_insight: For solo software entrepreneurs, the best payment infrastructure is whatever gets you back to building your product fastest — optimize for integration simplicity and consolidation, not features.

quotes.quote: The best part: it took me 45 seconds to integrate the cart with my page. Take Javascript code, copy-paste. Update Buy Now URLs. Done.

quotes.quote: From the POV of my customers, Paypal and Google Checkout are distinct. From my point of view, I control both through my e-junkie control panel.
quotes.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically reviews e-junkie's new 'Fat Free Cart' feature, which adds a slick AJAX-powered shopping cart overlay to his Bingo Card Creator website. The integration took only 45 seconds via copy-pasted JavaScript, replacing his previous clunky ordering flow. He uses this as an opportunity to drop his secondary payment processor eSellerate, consolidating everything through e-junkie which now supports both PayPal and Google Checkout. He strongly recommends e-junkie for micro-ISV developers as a cheap, effective way to handle payment processing without distraction from core product work.
quotes.key_insight: For solo software entrepreneurs, the best payment infrastructure is whatever gets you back to building your product fastest — optimize for integration simplicity and consolidation, not features.

quotes.quote: I continue to advise anybody and everybody just starting out on their uISV to use e-junkie. Its cheap ($5/month) and it gets you back to doing your business in a matter of minutes, rather than worrying about boring payment processing trivia.

quotes.quote: January has been going like gangbusters. I've had my two highest sales days ever yesterday and today, and have already sold more in January than the entire month of December.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports that January 2007 is proving to be an exceptionally strong month for his Bingo Card Creator business after a poor December. He has already surpassed December's total sales in the first eleven days of January, driven by either seasonal demand from teachers starting new terms or his achievement of a front-page Google ranking for 'bingo cards.' He notes that another micro-ISV blogger also experiences strong Januaries, suggesting a seasonal pattern. He plans to release a software update incorporating user-requested improvements to printing and layout.
quotes.key_insight: For software serving a specific professional audience like teachers, understanding their seasonal calendar and achieving strong organic search rankings are the two most powerful drivers of sales growth.

quotes.quote: I'm not entirely sure whether this is due to a spike in interest due to the new term starting (which means teachers need a new first day of the term game) or whether it is due to me now being on the front page of the search for bingo cards on Google, which has resulted in a sustained doubling of my traffic.

quotes.quote: You can come to great tax savings if you keep copious records.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie walks through his first year of tax preparation as a solo software business owner, detailing which expenses he deducted and which he didn't for his Bingo Card Creator side business. He reports $2,480.05 in gross income on his Schedule C-EZ and methodically evaluates each potential deduction with the help of a CPA friend. The post reveals the mundane reality of running a tiny software business — penny-pinching over $4.50 tax savings on a WinZip license while honestly acknowledging that his internet connection serves his World of Warcraft habit more than his business. He promises to share the final Schedule C-EZ overview once complete.
quotes.key_insight: Running a micro-ISV means sweating the small stuff on taxes — keeping meticulous records and being honest about personal-versus-business use turns even trivial expenses into legitimate deductions.

quotes.quote: Not a stock, ick, I trust the market to routinely beat the bejeezus out of my picks.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a personal finance tip about saving on brokerage commissions through ShareBuilder's discounted annual pricing. He explains his investment strategy of regularly investing profits from Bingo Card Creator into index fund ETFs, having recently paid off student loans. The post demonstrates his characteristic penny-pinching approach by calculating the long-term compound value of a $40 savings. He also analyzes why ShareBuilder offers the discount as a gift certificate rather than online, connecting it to customer acquisition strategy and platform stickiness.
quotes.key_insight: Small financial optimizations compound dramatically over decades, and understanding why businesses structure their pricing the way they do reveals whether you're the target customer or just subsidizing acquisition of new ones.

quotes.quote: Aside from being great fun and a nice learning experience, thats about $1500 or so that I wouldn't have had otherwise.
quotes.summary: McKenzie celebrates selling 100 copies of Bingo Card Creator, netting about $1,500 which he used to pay off student loans. He lays out detailed business goals for 2007 covering product improvements, website optimization, and financial targets. He aims for $1,000/month in sales by April and $10,000 yearly, while planning to get legally established in both Japan and the US. He also discusses personal goals including finding a new day job in Nagoya and starting a more ambitious software project in consumer finance.
quotes.key_insight: A micro-ISV generating $1,500 from 100 sales validates the business model enough to set ambitious growth targets while forcing the founder to confront real-world complexities like international tax compliance and seasonal revenue dependence.

quotes.quote: Peace be with you and your families this year and always.
quotes.summary: A brief holiday announcement from Patrick McKenzie letting readers know he will be traveling from Japan to the United States for Christmas from December 19-30. He notes that blog posting and email responses will be minimal during this period. He wishes readers peace for the holiday season.
quotes.key_insight: Even a brief holiday notice reveals McKenzie's customer-first mindset, explicitly noting he remains available for customer contact during personal travel.

quotes.quote: November was a bad month for sales, principally due to Thanksgiving I think (I got essentially nothing after the 20th.)
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his November 2006 stats for Bingo Card Creator, noting it was a disappointing month with only 20 sales and $349 net profit. He attributes the slowdown to the Thanksgiving holiday and predicts December will also be weak. A bright spot is that he began ranking for high-volume Google searches including 'bingo cards,' doubling his organic traffic. He provides detailed conversion rate breakdowns across traffic sources and analyzes his purchasing page funnel, noting an impressive 60%+ conversion rate once customers enter the checkout process.
quotes.key_insight: High-volume search rankings can double traffic but dilute conversion rates, making the quality-versus-quantity tradeoff a constant tension for small software businesses optimizing on cost per acquisition.

quotes.quote: I hate guessing and love data.
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces his acceptance into the beta of Google's Website Optimizer, a split-testing product. He explains the concept of A/B testing for readers unfamiliar with it, using his own Bingo Card Creator site as an example. He notes his previous inability to do true split testing, relying instead on sequential experiments that were vulnerable to confounding variables. He plans to use the tool extensively during his Christmas break to optimize his site's conversion rate.
quotes.key_insight: True split testing removes guesswork from conversion optimization by eliminating the confounding variables that plague sequential before/after experiments.

quotes.quote: Split testing, for those that don't know the term, is taking a page you have and making an A version and a B version.

quotes.quote: I categorically refuse to use YSM again ("Like AdWords, except inferior in every way — price, performance, features, reporting, and workflow")
quotes.summary: McKenzie notes a Microsoft adCenter promotion offering $200 in ad credit for a $5 signup fee. He plans to take advantage of this effectively free money to test whether Microsoft's ad platform can deliver positive ROI for his software business. He contrasts this with his negative experience with Yahoo Search Marketing (YSM), which he considers inferior to Google AdWords in every dimension. His plan is to run a controlled $50/month experiment over four months.
quotes.key_insight: When promotional credits make the downside risk trivial, it's rational to run controlled experiments on new advertising platforms to discover potential ROI.

quotes.quote: I think I'll give them 4 months at $50 a month or so to see if I can get positive ROI.

quotes.quote: I could definately see the potential for the business model to work with an application with more mass-market appeal.
quotes.summary: McKenzie highlights the success of Printer Friendly software after being featured on Bits du Jour, a deal-of-the-day site for software. He references his own more modest experience being featured on the same platform and acknowledges that products with broader mass-market appeal are better positioned to capitalize on such promotional opportunities. The post is a brief, generous congratulation to fellow micro-ISV entrepreneurs.
quotes.key_insight: Promotional platforms like deal-of-the-day sites can be powerful sales channels, but their impact scales with how broadly appealing the featured product is.

quotes.quote: Here is the thing about babies: while most people absolutely love their baby they've got negative a billion desire to have anyone else's baby.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that micro-ISV founders waste enormous time worrying about things that don't matter for their business success. He systematically dismantles common fears: license terms nobody reads, competitors stealing code, tax complications, and programming language choice. The core argument is that 90% of a software business is marketing and sales, not development, and founders should allocate their worry accordingly. He grounds each point in practical reality checks — comparing software licensing to buying hamburgers, and tax fears to eBay sellers. The post is aimed at solo developers who over-index on technical and legal concerns while under-investing in what actually drives revenue.
quotes.key_insight: Solo software founders squander their scarcest resource — time — on technical and legal anxieties that have near-zero impact on revenue, when they should be focused on marketing and selling to customers.

quotes.quote: 10% of this business, on the outside, happens in the IDE. The other 90% is where you make your money.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that micro-ISV founders waste enormous time worrying about things that don't matter for their business success. He systematically dismantles common fears: license terms nobody reads, competitors stealing code, tax complications, and programming language choice. The core argument is that 90% of a software business is marketing and sales, not development, and founders should allocate their worry accordingly. He grounds each point in practical reality checks — comparing software licensing to buying hamburgers, and tax fears to eBay sellers. The post is aimed at solo developers who over-index on technical and legal concerns while under-investing in what actually drives revenue.
quotes.key_insight: Solo software founders squander their scarcest resource — time — on technical and legal anxieties that have near-zero impact on revenue, when they should be focused on marketing and selling to customers.

quotes.quote: I've gotten no less than four inquiries from people at taking up various positions at their various software ventures/uISVs/etc, and all of them have cited Bingo Card Creator (the business more than the software) or this blog as the reason for extending the offer.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports that his blog and Bingo Card Creator business have unexpectedly generated four job inquiries from software ventures and micro-ISVs in a single week. He originally started the blog as a potential writing portfolio but is surprised that people are actively seeking him out based on it. He notes he's too busy to take on new projects until August, between his day job, BCC, and an upcoming job search. The post serves as an early, brief observation about how building in public and blogging about business can function as professional marketing.
quotes.key_insight: Writing publicly about running a small software business can generate more professional opportunities than the business itself, turning a blog into an unintentional but powerful career marketing tool.

quotes.quote: When I started this blog I had vague dreams of perhaps eventually using it as a portfolio of sorts in case my next position required writing on a regular basis, but I hadn't ever thought people would actively seek me out over it.

quotes.quote: my printing code is a big ugly hairy beast so when I go to fight with it I intend to come out with a freaking pile of treasure to justify my time
quotes.summary: McKenzie returns to coding after a month away to fix a showstopper printing bug for a customer, which turned out to be much simpler than expected. The bug involved an incorrect assumption that teachers always print to their default printer. He reflects on how procrastinating the fix was a mistake since it took less than an hour. He plans to use an upcoming holiday to release version 1.05 with new features and timely holiday content.
quotes.key_insight: Tasks we procrastinate on for fear of complexity often turn out to be much simpler than expected — just do them.

quotes.quote: I had considered fixing it in 1.04 but thought it would take up too much time to do so. It ended up taking less than an hour.

quotes.quote: e-junkie made an API change for me within 48 hours just because I asked for it. That sets a pretty darn high bar for me in terms of what you would have to provide as a competitor.
quotes.summary: McKenzie evaluates Payloadz's new Google Checkout integration and concludes it's not worth switching from his current e-junkie/PayPal setup. He breaks down the cost comparison, showing Payloadz's tiered pricing is significantly more expensive than e-junkie's flat $5/month. He also weighs customer service quality, branding control, and payment provider trust among his target demographic. The post demonstrates his characteristic approach of evaluating business tools through multiple lenses: cost, customer experience, and market fit. He concludes by noting he'd reconsider if e-junkie itself added Google Checkout support.
quotes.key_insight: Payment provider decisions should be evaluated holistically — pricing, customer service responsiveness, branding control, and your specific customers' trust relationships all matter more than headline features.

quotes.quote: Anybody who clicks to read my license terms has a 50-50 shot of buying from me.
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains how to track outbound link clicks using Google Analytics' urchinTracker function, which was not natively supported at the time. By tagging offsite links with fake local paths, you can see which specific buttons and text elements drive users to complete purchases. He discovered that his customers were more motivated by reassuring text about payment security than by prominently placed buy buttons, leading him to plan repositioning that messaging earlier on the page.
quotes.key_insight: Tracking outbound link clicks reveals that customer trust signals in copy often outperform prominent button placement in driving conversions.

quotes.quote: Its NOT the top-corner button (who knew! I always expected folks would go for the easiest button to reach) but rather the part later on in the text where I describe Paypal as a safe, secure place to shop online.

quotes.quote: I'm largely operating it like a vending machine — I collect the change at the end of the week and, once and a while, write out a two-paragraph email to somebody with a question.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports that despite neglecting active promotion of Bingo Card Creator due to time constraints, his organic search traffic has grown significantly to 100+ daily hits from Google. Sales are down about a third from October due to ceased promotion, but long-tail SEO is compensating as his site ages out of Google's sandbox. He argues that writing natural language content for target audiences is more effective than obsessing over keyword optimization. Recent SERP improvements have driven a spike in downloads that he hopes will convert to sales.
quotes.key_insight: Writing genuinely useful content for your target audience is a more sustainable SEO strategy than chasing individual keyword rankings, because long-tail queries collectively outperform any single optimized phrase.

quotes.quote: 5% of my traffic comes from that extremely common query with me being at number 9/10 on page one, and the rest of it comes from very uncommon queries which I'm an insta #1 on.

quotes.quote: So this is the point where you ask me to sign up for a $500 bundle including $200 of games that you would not otherwise be able to sell and pay all of it as a deposit, right?
quotes.summary: McKenzie recounts a humorous experience pre-ordering a Nintendo Wii at a Japanese mall, presented as a dialogue between himself and a store manager. The exchange highlights the dramatic contrast between Japanese and American retail pre-order practices. In Japan, the process was frictionless: no deposit, no forced bundles, just a name and phone number. McKenzie plays up his disbelief as an American accustomed to exploitative pre-order practices like mandatory expensive bundles and full deposits. The post uses gentle comedy to illustrate cultural differences in consumer retail experiences.
quotes.key_insight: Japanese retail culture treats pre-orders as a simple reservation rather than an opportunity to extract maximum upfront payment from captive customers.

quotes.quote: You can't sell it if people can't get to it!
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his October 2006 stats for Bingo Card Creator, showing $500 net sales and ~$350 profit despite losing nearly a week of sales to hosting problems. He sold 22 downloads and 2 CDs with 3 refunds, narrowly missing his monthly goal by one unit. His AdWords costs are trending down thanks to Quality Score improvements, and his trial-to-purchase conversion rate sits at approximately 2.5%. The Mac version shows remarkably higher conversion rates than Windows, with about 1 in 2 Mac trial users converting versus 1 in 10 for Windows.
quotes.key_insight: Granular per-page revenue tracking reveals that Mac users convert at 5x the Windows rate and that customer support is a significant revenue driver, not just a cost center.

quotes.quote: Hmm, I wonder why I've gone three days without a sale. I don't suppose it could be that my trial version got corrupted again.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers that his trial software installer has been corrupted again on GoDaddy's hosting, explaining a three-day sales drought. He notes this is a recurring problem and plans to contact GoDaddy to resolve the issue, as he's confident the file was intact shortly after upload.
quotes.key_insight: A corrupted trial download is a silent revenue killer that can go undetected for days, making file integrity monitoring essential for software businesses.

quotes.quote: One of the best decisions I made during the creation of Bingo Card Creator was cutting my web site designer out of the budget.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a tip about getting a free PageRank 8 backlink by submitting your website to Open Source Web Design's links page. He advocates for using free open-source web designs over hiring expensive web designers, citing his positive experience with Bingo Card Creator. The post was later updated to note the trick doesn't actually work as described. It reflects his early-stage SEO obsession and bootstrapper mindset of minimizing costs while maximizing marketing value.
quotes.key_insight: Bootstrappers should evaluate every business decision — including web design — not just on direct value but on secondary marketing benefits like SEO backlinks.

quotes.quote: This is my advice to anyone who is wondering how to cut a major expense item out of their budget while not compromising the customer experience much.

quotes.quote: If one file on your web server gets corrupted, it WILL be your trial executable, without fail.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers that his trial executable was silently corrupted on his web server — a single byte changed — causing installation failures for potential customers. He only found out because a user wrote in after downloading it three times. A hex diff confirmed the corruption, and the NSIS installer's checksum caught the bad byte. He reflects on Murphy's Law and the importance of knowing about problems rather than letting them silently cost you customers.
quotes.key_insight: Silent failures in your sales funnel — like a corrupted download — can cost you customers for days before anyone bothers to tell you, because most people just leave.

quotes.quote: One freaking byte on disk got corrupted at some point within the last week and I just found out about it today.

quotes.quote: I can make assumptions, like assuming that she (I can assume she is a she! Sort of a rarity in software development, I would think) is not as comfortable using her computer as I am using mine.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reflects on whether he has too narrowly defined his target market for Bingo Card Creator around elementary school teachers. While the tight focus gives him deep understanding of his core customer, he's discovering unexpected use cases: corporate events, rehabilitation tracking, custom placemats, and even advertising for niche organizations. He considers creating alternate website presentations for non-teacher audiences while maintaining his primary educational focus.
quotes.key_insight: A narrowly defined target market gives you deep customer insight, but paying attention to the customers who buy despite not fitting your target reveals organic expansion opportunities.

quotes.quote: I use Cygwin to have them available on my Windows box, because while Linux is a lovely operating system in many ways I rather enjoy having iTunes and games available.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes using Unix command-line tools for a practical web maintenance task: replacing HTML snippets across multiple static pages. He walks through progressively simpler approaches, starting with a multi-file gawk script, then showing how sed accomplishes the same thing in a single line. The post is a casual tutorial aimed at developers who may not be familiar with Unix text processing utilities. He also takes a lighthearted jab at Perl's notoriously unreadable syntax.
quotes.key_insight: When doing simple text substitutions across files, sed is the right tool — simpler and less error-prone than more powerful alternatives like gawk or Perl.

quotes.quote: I hate currency exchange fees with a burning passion in my soul, having been burned by them plenty.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes expanding Bingo Card Creator to serve international customers by adding native currency pricing. He explains his approach to handling VAT (letting customers choose between eSellerate which collects it and PayPal which doesn't), his pricing strategy of converting USD amounts and rounding to convenient numbers, and his decision to absorb currency conversion fees as a small customer-friendly gesture. He also touches on tax compliance across multiple jurisdictions and the challenges of potentially incorporating in Japan.
quotes.key_insight: Absorbing small costs like currency conversion fees is a trivially cheap way to reduce friction and build goodwill with international customers.

quotes.quote: My experiments have been a bunch of failures, I can't seem to find any CPAN Module which will do this cleanly.
quotes.summary: McKenzie asks for help with a Perl programming problem at his day job. He's running an HTTP server using Perl's HTTP::Daemon module and needs to extract uploaded text files from HTTP POST requests on the server side. Despite weeks of effort, searching CPAN modules, and experimentation, he hasn't found a clean solution. The issue is blocking other deliverables since the server is 98% complete but the remaining 2% is critical for testing.
quotes.key_insight: Even a technically capable developer can get stuck for weeks on seemingly simple integration problems when documentation is poor and the ecosystem doesn't offer clean abstractions.

quotes.quote: Business this month is going substantially stronger than last month.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares his mid-October sales statistics for Bingo Card Creator, his micro-ISV product. He reports 13 units sold with one return, generating $311.59 in net sales against $105.02 in expenses for a net profit of $206.57. He notes business is stronger than the previous month and sets a target of 25-30 copies and $500 profit for the full month. A planned Halloween promotion has been delayed due to his day job commitments.
quotes.key_insight: Even with minimal marketing effort and day-job constraints, a well-positioned micro-ISV can generate consistent and growing monthly profit from a simple software product.

quotes.quote: If you are comfortable administering your own email server (and already do), use SpamAssassin. If you are not, use Popfile.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides practical advice on dealing with email spam, recommending SpamAssassin for those who administer their own email servers and Popfile for everyone else. He explains how Bayesian statistical filtering works in practice, noting both tools achieve over 99% accuracy when properly trained. He walks through Popfile's setup as a local email proxy that classifies messages into customizable buckets. He strongly warns against challenge/response systems, arguing they create more problems than they solve and alienate customers.
quotes.key_insight: Effective spam filtering is a solved problem with freely available Bayesian tools, and the real risk is not spam getting through but legitimate messages being lost.

quotes.quote: There, you're done and didn't even have to pay me my company's consulting rate.

quotes.quote: final acceptance testing was supposed to start on, oh, August 31st? Yep, you could say we're under a weeeee bit of pressure to get things finished.
quotes.summary: McKenzie briefly explains his absence from blogging, citing intense pressure at his day job where deliverables are severely behind schedule. Final acceptance testing was supposed to start in August but the team is still working on completing features in mid-October. His side business (uISV) activity has been reduced to answering a few emails and transferring PayPal earnings. He promises to post his business stats for the first half of October soon.
quotes.key_insight: A well-built software product continues generating revenue even when its creator can only spare minimal attention, underscoring the passive income potential of product businesses.

quotes.quote: Perhaps review is the wrong word to use: that implies objectivity, and I've got none here: I love the program like I love chocolate.
quotes.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically recommends Direct Access, a productivity application he uses constantly throughout his workday. He links to a previous glowing review and notes a 50% off sale on Bits Du Jour. He acknowledges his lack of objectivity about the product and discloses that the plug is unpaid.
quotes.key_insight: Authentic, unpaid endorsements built on genuine daily use carry more weight than formal reviews, and transparent disclosure of bias paradoxically increases credibility.

quotes.quote: Roughly 80% of my customers do not buy software. I don't mean this in the sense that they are pirates. I mean this in the sense that their relationship to their computer is not primarily mediated through applications.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares lessons from selling low-cost software ($24.95) to non-technical users who don't typically buy software — people whose computer use is limited to email, documents, and eBay. He argues that visual polish matters far more than features for this audience, that personal accessibility and responsive customer support are essential for converting hesitant buyers, and that the purchasing process must be streamlined to near-foolproof simplicity. The post challenges the common uISV assumption that the target market is other developers.
quotes.key_insight: Selling software to non-technical users requires prioritizing visual polish, personal accessibility, and a frictionless purchase path over features — because these customers are buying trust and reassurance, not capabilities.

quotes.quote: Its crucial that you be able to account for every dime you spent on your business, so that you can properly claim every dime worth of business expenses.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes the accounting system he set up for his micro-ISV (Bingo Card Creator) to simplify tax filing. He got a dedicated credit card exclusively for business expenses, which consolidates all spending into one statement and makes Schedule C filing trivial. He explains the cost of forgetting deductions ('idiot tax') and how the credit card smooths cash flow between his Japan-based day job income and US-denominated business expenses. He also maintains simple paper and digital files printed monthly to reconstruct income and expenses in minutes.
quotes.key_insight: The simplest accounting system for a solo software business is a dedicated credit card for all expenses plus monthly printed statements — minimizing the 'idiot tax' of forgotten deductions.

quotes.quote: Thats $7.50 worth of idiot tax for forgetting a single transaction. Don't pay the idiot tax, keep copious records in an easy-to-access place!

quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes the accounting system he set up for his micro-ISV (Bingo Card Creator) to simplify tax filing. He got a dedicated credit card exclusively for business expenses, which consolidates all spending into one statement and makes Schedule C filing trivial. He explains the cost of forgetting deductions ('idiot tax') and how the credit card smooths cash flow between his Japan-based day job income and US-denominated business expenses. He also maintains simple paper and digital files printed monthly to reconstruct income and expenses in minutes.
quotes.key_insight: The simplest accounting system for a solo software business is a dedicated credit card for all expenses plus monthly printed statements — minimizing the 'idiot tax' of forgotten deductions.

quotes.quote: With everything on one card, all you have to do is pull your statements online and sum. This ensures that you don't miss a single expense.

quotes.quote: The market aggregates information with efficiency and scale which makes Google look like a single-celled organism.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his approach to investing his small but growing software business earnings after paying off his student loans. He explains his reasoning for choosing index funds over actively managed mutual funds, citing market efficiency and the poor odds of active managers beating the market. He diversifies away from his day job's exposure to the Japanese economy and tech sector by investing in S&P 500 index funds through ING and Sharebuilder. The post is a practical walkthrough of a young micro-ISV owner's first steps into investing, grounded in self-awareness about his limitations as a stock picker.
quotes.key_insight: For small software entrepreneurs, the rational investment strategy is low-cost index funds that diversify away from your existing career risks, not stock picking or expensive active management.

quotes.quote: Paying 2% of my investment every year to somebody who has about a 1 in 4 chance of matching the return of the market does not appeal to me.

quotes.quote: Within 45 seconds of bringing my blog back from the dead I got over 200 spam comment submissions, mostly to old posts.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports that his blog was hit by a massive spam attack within seconds of bringing it back online, receiving over 200 spam comment submissions about gambling. Most of the spam bypassed his existing spam filter. Rather than implementing intrusive anti-spam measures like registration requirements, he opted for a simple keyword ban on three gambling-related terms.
quotes.key_insight: When fighting spam, prefer the simplest targeted fix that preserves user experience over heavy-handed measures like mandatory registration.

quotes.quote: To prevent me getting repetitive stress disorder from hitting the 'mark as spam' button and keep this blog easy to comment on without requiring registration or anything intrusive, I've banned the following three words from comments: poker, casino, and gambling.

quotes.quote: What can I say, I really wish that circa June, 2007 I had known enough about my own advice to take it.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reflects on a mistake he made by hosting his blog on a separate domain from his business site, Bingo Card Creator. Despite consistently advising others to put their blog on their main business domain to share PageRank, he failed to follow his own advice. The result: his blog achieved a higher PageRank (PR5) than his product site (PR4), meaning potential customers find his blog first and must click through to the product, losing traffic along the way.
quotes.key_insight: Host your business blog on your main product domain so that the SEO authority your content earns directly benefits your product pages rather than forcing visitors through an unnecessary redirect.

quotes.quote: The difference between PR4 and PR5 is a whole lot of searches, particularly for low-competition niches.

quotes.quote: I've already got multiple sales (!) and also multiple bug reports that it doesn't work on various versions of Mac OS X.
quotes.summary: McKenzie asks his blog readers for help debugging his Mac version of Bingo Card Creator after receiving bug reports from VersionTracker.com users. Despite having some successful Mac users, two users report the app dies on startup due to Java being unable to find the main class. He suspects an issue with the Mac-specific pinfo.list file but cannot reproduce or diagnose it since he has no Mac experience. The post is a straightforward request for community testing assistance.
quotes.key_insight: Solo software developers can compensate for limited resources by leveraging their blog audience for cross-platform QA testing they cannot do themselves.

quotes.quote: It included an apology, an offer to give me a total refund, a choice between the refund and a $100 credit against further services (smart move: it keeps me invested in my business relationship with them — I took it), and listing the software immediately.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his experience resolving a three-week delay with Tucows after paying for an expedited software submission. After contacting their webmaster on a fellow developer's recommendation, he received excellent customer service including an apology, a refund offer, and a $100 credit option. He chose the credit, recognizing it as a smart retention move, and plans to share his support letter as a template for effective customer communication.
quotes.key_insight: Effective customer service recovery — apology, options, and a retention-smart credit offer — can turn a frustrated customer into an advocate.

quotes.quote: Even business owners are customers roughly half the time, right?

quotes.quote: My first two weeks went like gangbusters, my last two weeks have been pretty limp.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his September 2006 business stats for Bingo Card Creator, his first software product. The month saw 22 sales totaling $533.90 gross, with $313.94 in profit after expenses. The first half of the month performed well but the second half slowed down, partly due to Google AdWords issues. He notes that Text Link Ads were not profitable while Tucows submission proved valuable for both its PageRank link and download traffic.
quotes.key_insight: Even a modest solo software business can turn a profit in its early months, but heavy reliance on a single paid acquisition channel like Google AdWords creates fragility when that channel hiccups.

quotes.quote: 7 in the last two weeks, which oddly enough would have made me ecstatic 2 months ago but after my first two weeks of September is a wee bit disappointing.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reflects on a two-week lull in his Bingo Card Creator business, during which sales slowed to 7 units—a number that would have thrilled him earlier but now feels disappointing given recent momentum. He outlines four tactical plans to get back on track: resolving a payment dispute with Tucows, renegotiating text link advertising, creating seasonal Halloween content with supporting AdWords campaigns, and overhauling his underperforming advertising spend. The post captures the psychological recalibration that happens as a small business owner's expectations shift upward with early success.
quotes.key_insight: Rising expectations are a natural byproduct of early business success, and the tactical response to a sales lull is to double down on seasonal content, advertising optimization, and resolving operational friction rather than panic.

quotes.quote: I am now severely regretting both paying them and paying them via debit card, because if I had paid them with a credit card I could at least charge them back.

quotes.quote: I'm almost leery to mention this because the company approaches the seedier side of the SEO industry.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes his experiment purchasing text links from Text Link Ads as a marketing channel for his software business. After spending $35 on two links, he found the service's inventory poor for his education niche, with links placed on irrelevant or low-quality sites. The service obscures which sites you're buying from to prevent disintermediation, leading to mismatched placements. He concludes the experiment was a loss and he won't continue, though he acknowledges the service might work better in other niches like tech accessories.
quotes.key_insight: Paid link-buying services suffer from adverse selection — the inventory available in niche markets tends to be low-quality sites that exist to profit from ads rather than serve real audiences, making them poor marketing channels.

quotes.quote: Text Link Ads makes it less than transparent to figure out exactly who you're buying the link from, so that you can't contact that person off the site and cut Text Link Ads out of the deal.

quotes.quote: The business has been more or less running itself.
quotes.summary: A brief status update where McKenzie notes his blog has been quiet due to work and social obligations while his Bingo Card Creator business runs on autopilot. He lists near-term priorities including evaluating text link ads, getting listed on Tucows, diagnosing a sharp decline in MSN traffic, and fixing broken Google Analytics tracking code. He notes the Mac version of his software is accounting for half his sales, validating his earlier decision to port it.
quotes.key_insight: A solo software business can largely run itself once the fundamentals are in place, but requires constant small adjustments to traffic sources, analytics, and distribution channels to stay healthy.

quotes.quote: A lot of people who aren't the most technologically-savvy in the world prefer to have physical media, as its a) 'real' in a way the download is not and b) proof against something going wrong on their computer.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes setting up CD fulfillment for Bingo Card Creator after his first physical media sale. He found a third-party CD fulfillment service that handles burning, labeling, and shipping for $4.47 per disc, charging customers $5 for the option. He explains the practical economics of physical media sales for a micro-ISV, including his policy of eating the cost on returns rather than processing physical returns. The post illustrates his pragmatic approach to solving business problems with minimal effort and maximum customer goodwill.
quotes.key_insight: When selling low-cost physical goods, outsource fulfillment to eliminate logistics headaches and absorb return costs rather than process them — the customer goodwill is worth more than the few dollars lost.

quotes.quote: Its certainly not worth sending me out to the video store to buy a CD or a stack of CDs, hand-write a license key on it, and then trudge it out to the post office, buy a padded mailer, and ship it abroad to the US, praying it doesn't get a 25% tariff or get dropped in the freaking ocean like the last CD I sent to America.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes setting up CD fulfillment for Bingo Card Creator after his first physical media sale. The post illustrates his pragmatic approach to solving business problems with minimal effort and maximum customer goodwill.

quotes.quote: Well, that's certainly a sliiiiiight increase from my last two months ($25 and -$2, as I recall).
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares a mid-month financial update for Bingo Card Creator, reporting $332 in net sales and $290 in profit — a dramatic improvement over his previous months of $25 and -$2. He tracks a promising sales progression of 1, 2, 6, 8, 16 sales per two-week interval since launch, while cautioning against assuming geometric growth will continue. He outlines plans to reach $600 in monthly sales through Mac distribution, Halloween-themed SEO content, new advertising campaigns, and a CD version of the product. The post closes with a personal note about using the profits to pay off his student loan early and buy a Nintendo Wii.
quotes.key_insight: Early-stage software businesses can achieve meaningful profit growth through disciplined, incremental marketing efforts even when starting from nearly zero revenue.

quotes.quote: Go through your lists, look for words which are under 1% CTR, and scrub.
quotes.summary: McKenzie warns fellow AdWords advertisers about Google's aggressive pricing response to click-through rate dips. He discovered that even his best-performing keyword had its cost quadrupled after briefly dipping below 1% CTR due to random fluctuations. He advises proactively scrubbing keywords that fall under the 1% CTR threshold, noting that paying inflated costs to rehabilitate a keyword offers no guarantee the minimum bid will decrease.
quotes.key_insight: Google's AdWords algorithm punishes even temporary CTR dips with steep, potentially permanent cost increases, making proactive keyword pruning essential for small advertisers.

quotes.quote: That would put my Mac popularity at about 10% of my windows one, which is pretty phenomenal given that a) my Mac version isn't on the major download sites yet b) I make it almost painfully difficult to get the Mac version and c) 2% of my visitors use Macs.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports that his Mac version is performing surprisingly well, accounting for about 10% of his Windows user base despite minimal promotion. He discovers this through OS tracking he added to update requests. The Mac version isn't listed on major download sites yet, and the download process actively discourages Mac users by defaulting to the Windows installer. Despite these obstacles, Mac adoption significantly outpaces the 2% Mac share of his site visitors, prompting him to improve the Mac download experience with JavaScript-based OS detection.
quotes.key_insight: Measuring actual user behavior through simple instrumentation can reveal that design assumptions are actively hurting a significant segment of your customer base.

quotes.quote: After finally achieving 30 cents per download (where AdWords is actually profitable) and keeping it there for a week my CPA creeped up again to 37 cents.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports that Google AdWords unexpectedly doubled minimum bid prices on many of his keywords, destroying the profitability he had carefully optimized over weeks. After finally achieving a profitable cost-per-acquisition of 30 cents per download, his CPA crept back up to 37 cents due to Google's quality penalty raising minimum bids from 7-9 cents to 15 cents. He finds this particularly frustrating because some of the penalized keywords had strong click-through rates and highly relevant landing pages. He suspects either large portfolio bidders like eBay raised prices across the board, or Google applied a low-quality penalty incorrectly.
quotes.key_insight: Platform dependency creates existential risk for small businesses when algorithmic pricing changes can instantly destroy carefully optimized unit economics with no recourse.

quotes.quote: 505 — 'This message will be archived and *may* be presented to the recipient', surely one of the least informative error messages of all time.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes a recurring pattern of customers not receiving their automated registration key emails after purchase, leading to support issues and even a double purchase. After three incidents in quick succession — a basic support request, a bounced email from a school's spam filter, and a customer who repurchased thinking their first transaction failed — he decides to display registration keys directly on the website rather than relying solely on email delivery.
quotes.key_insight: When the same customer problem recurs three times, stop treating it as individual support incidents and fix the system — the cost of a small engineering improvement is far less than the compounding cost of lost customers and chargebacks.

quotes.quote: Aside from this involving evil Perl code (is there any other kind?), there were some potentially nasty timing issues involved.
quotes.summary: McKenzie praises e-junkie's customer service after experiencing email delivery issues with his registration codes. He describes a technical problem where customers couldn't receive registration emails, and how he faced the prospect of writing complex Perl code to work around it. After requesting a feature to pass registration codes as URL parameters, e-junkie not only responded quickly but implemented the feature within 24 hours, apologizing for the 'delay.' The post serves as a testimonial for responsive vendor support.
quotes.key_insight: Exceptional vendor responsiveness — implementing a custom feature request in 24 hours for a $5/month customer — can turn a minor service into a passionate advocate's recommendation.

quotes.quote: End result of the Bits du Jour promotion: 2 sales, which was exactly what I had been expecting.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports the results of running a Bits du Jour promotional deal for Bingo Card Creator, getting exactly the 2 discounted sales he expected from the promotion. He also had a record 5 regular-price sales the same day, suspecting some customers may have downloaded from Bits du Jour but purchased through the app without using the discount. He debates whether to credit those customers with the promotional price and notes the promotion drove about 60 visits total.
quotes.key_insight: Promotional platforms can drive sales even for niche products, but attribution gaps between discovery channel and purchase channel can obscure the true impact of a promotion.
