quotes.quote: I'm always willing to take total losses on 30 minute experiments.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports on his participation in a Bits Du Jour promotional deal, where Bingo Card Creator was offered at a 40% discount ($14.95 instead of $24.95). He praises the promotional copy written by Ellen, noting how she expanded his target market beyond teachers to parents, daycare providers, and corporate event coordinators. He reflects on the economics of time-limited promotions and smart market segmentation, noting that some use cases become viable at a lower price point. He expresses surprise at an early sale at 2 AM and anticipates the promotion will outperform his conservative expectation of two sales.
quotes.key_insight: Low-risk promotional experiments can reveal untapped market segments, especially when a lower price point makes the product viable for use cases that wouldn't justify the full price.

quotes.quote: Who orders Bingo Card Creator at 2 AM in the morning local time? Apparently, someone who saw the 40% discount and just said 'I gotta have it!'

He praises the promotional copy written by Ellen, noting how she expanded his target market beyond teachers to parents, daycare providers, and corporate event coordinators. He reflects on the economics of time-limited promotions and smart market segmentation, noting that some use cases become viable at a lower price point. He expresses surprise at an early sale at 2 AM and anticipates the promotion will outperform his conservative expectation of two sales.
quotes.key_insight: Low-risk promotional experiments can reveal untapped market segments, especially when a lower price point makes the product viable for use cases that wouldn't justify the full price.

quotes.quote: This whole "do a little work once and keep getting paid for it for a while" passive income thing certainly has its benefits.
quotes.summary: McKenzie celebrates a particularly good sales day for his Bingo Card Creator software, where he earned more money sleeping on a Friday morning than he would teaching an English lesson. He reflects on the economics of his side business, noting that his monthly AdWords costs are covered and everything else is profit. He contrasts the agility of being a one-person company with the bureaucratic overhead of large corporations, where even a simple customer interaction required HR, legal, and VP-level approval.
quotes.key_insight: The fundamental advantage of a one-person software business is that it decouples income from hours worked and eliminates the bureaucratic overhead that prevents larger companies from being responsive.

quotes.quote: This is one of the lovely things about being a teeny-tiny slip of a company: you can outmaneuver the guys who have sixteen levels of managers to report to before anything as significant as a pleasantry can be exchanged.

quotes.quote: Writing a blog about and for your customers, as opposed to about and for your business, is a great marketing investment.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that software businesses should write blogs aimed at their customers rather than at fellow developers or entrepreneurs. He explains the 'Pyramid of Participation' concept — most blog audiences are passive consumers, not creators — and advocates inverting it to convert passive readers into engaged customers. Using Declan Software's Japanese language learning tools as a concrete example, he demonstrates how a customer-focused blog provides genuinely useful content that builds trust, drives organic search traffic, and creates word-of-mouth referrals. The post emphasizes writing in your customers' language, having a clear goal for every post, and offering a mix of content types to sustain engagement.
quotes.key_insight: A business blog should be written for and about your customers — not your industry peers — providing genuinely useful content in their language to build trust, drive organic search traffic, and convert passive readers into buyers.

quotes.quote: Taking level 4 is a waste of your time and money, because nobody cares that you have a piece of paper that says you can order a beer.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares his strategy for passing the Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT), arguing that mastering kanji through daily spaced-repetition practice is the most efficient path to passing. He breaks down the test's scoring structure to show that the listening section is easy enough to coast on, meaning candidates only need slightly better than 50% on the remaining sections. He recommends a specific flashcard software called ReadWrite Kanji for daily drilling, emphasizing that true mastery means instant recall without hesitation. The post is explicitly a proof-of-concept for his advice on writing customer-focused blog content that naturally incorporates product recommendations.
quotes.key_insight: Strategic test preparation means identifying the easiest scoring sections to coast on and concentrating effort on the highest-leverage skill — in this case, kanji mastery through daily spaced-repetition drilling.

quotes.quote: This association of submission before heaven and gambling probably had something to do with the naming of a certain playing card company 任天堂, 'the house of leaving one's fortune to heaven', which is probably better known as the company which went on to make two Italian plumbers household names in every nation on earth.
quotes.summary: This post is a proof-of-concept for a 'customer-focused blog' strategy, demonstrating how to create useful content that naturally incorporates a product recommendation. McKenzie teaches a Japanese kanji (任, meaning 'to entrust' or 'leave up to someone') while plugging ReadWrite Kanji software. The post includes readings, JLPT test vocabulary, and trivia connecting the kanji to Nintendo's name etymology. An editor's note at the end breaks the fourth wall to explain the marketing techniques being demonstrated, including calls to action.
quotes.key_insight: Effective content marketing creates genuinely useful content where the product recommendation feels natural rather than forced, as demonstrated by embedding a software plug within a Japanese language lesson.

quotes.quote: If you are a shareware author, an uISV, or if you just want to take a stand against software piracy, you can do so in thirty seconds.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes a creative SEO-based anti-piracy tactic for his Bingo Card Creator software. He proposes 'Google bombing' piracy-related search terms by linking them to a funny YouTube video instead of actual cracks. He encourages other shareware authors to do the same for their software, creating a collective defense against software pirates. He also notes that crackers eventually updated their keygen for his new version, but treats it as a minor nuisance he can quickly counter.
quotes.key_insight: SEO can be weaponized as an anti-piracy tool by flooding crack-related search terms with legitimate links, exploiting the fact that piracy sites have weak link authority.

quotes.quote: There is no possible way I can or would do the 'full time job on top of a full time job' deathmarch for that long — it nearly hospitalized me the first time.
quotes.summary: McKenzie teases his next software project, which would be significantly more ambitious than Bingo Card Creator, targeting a small but growing community of less price-sensitive customers. He plans a multi-tier pricing model including freemium and subscription options, while keeping costs extremely low by reusing infrastructure from his first product. The timeline is delayed until January because he's studying for the JLPT Level 1 Japanese proficiency exam. He acknowledges the risk of depending on a third-party platform but remains enthusiastic about the planning process.
quotes.key_insight: Even in 2006, McKenzie was already thinking in terms of SaaS pricing tiers and platform risk — foundational business strategy concepts that most indie developers wouldn't internalize for years.

quotes.quote: You will have an easier time conceptualizing your registration scheme if you think of it as primarily a marketing, rather than technical, measure. Its your salesman that encourages folks to pay you money.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive guide to software registration and copy protection for micro-ISVs, arguing that registration systems should be thought of as salesmen rather than security measures. He categorizes users into four types — from perfectly honest to dedicated pirates — and explains that protection schemes should target the middle groups while accepting that determined pirates are unbeatable. The post covers crack types (leaked keys, keygens, patches, cracked executables), defensive strategies (user-specific data, code obfuscation, frequent binary changes), and key generation approaches from public-key crypto to simple modular arithmetic. His core message is that developers should invest proportionally in protection, aiming to deter casual piracy rather than achieve impossible perfection.
quotes.key_insight: Software copy protection is a marketing tool that should convert mostly-honest users into paying customers, not a security system that tries — and inevitably fails — to stop determined pirates.

quotes.quote: Salesmen do not typically kick their prospective customers where the sun doesn't shine, and many registration schemes do.

quotes.quote: Your goal in instituting a protection scheme is not to achieve 0 utilization of your software by the Jolly Rogers of the world.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive guide to software registration and copy protection for micro-ISVs, arguing that registration systems should be thought of as salesmen rather than security measures. He categorizes users into four types — from perfectly honest to dedicated pirates — and explains that protection schemes should target the middle groups while accepting that determined pirates are unbeatable. The post covers crack types (leaked keys, keygens, patches, cracked executables), defensive strategies (user-specific data, code obfuscation, frequent binary changes), and key generation approaches from public-key crypto to simple modular arithmetic. His core message is that developers should invest proportionally in protection, aiming to deter casual piracy rather than achieve impossible perfection.
quotes.key_insight: Software copy protection is a marketing tool that should convert mostly-honest users into paying customers, not a security system that tries — and inevitably fails — to stop determined pirates.

quotes.quote: Defeating the cracker does not make you an appreciable amount of money. You just need to defeat the casual user in most instances, and the casual user does not have access to a debugger nor know how to use one.

quotes.quote: What Sally wants, Sally gets.
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces the release of Bingo Card Creator version 1.04, distributed through Robosoft for automated submission to download sites. The update includes a Mac version, improved visual design with stock icons, and several user-requested features like font selection and single-card centering. He notes a practical tip about creating separate PAD files for Windows and Mac versions. The post reflects his early-stage microISV development process of iterating quickly based on customer feedback.
quotes.key_insight: Early-stage software businesses grow by responding directly to individual customer requests and systematizing distribution to maximize reach.

quotes.quote: The only purpose of this system is to keep honest men honest and to remind folks that there is no presumptive right to free Starbucks.
quotes.summary: McKenzie responds to Seth Godin's commentary on Starbucks' failed email coupon promotion that went viral beyond its intended audience. He proposes a lightweight solution: ask customers for their first name and last four phone digits before honoring the coupon, creating just enough friction to deter mass abuse without actually building a verification system. He argues the real business calculus favors honoring the drinks since the marginal cost is trivial compared to brand damage from visibly canceling a promotion. The post demonstrates his characteristic focus on understanding the actual economics of a business decision rather than the surface-level problem.
quotes.key_insight: When the cost of fraud is trivially small per incident but the brand damage of visibly fighting it is enormous, the right move is to honor the promotion with just enough friction to discourage casual abuse.

quotes.quote: Here's the secret to Starbucks: no matter how much they charge you, making the drink didn't cost more than 10 cents!

quotes.quote: Well, honestly, I thought I was going to post a minor paper profit. Hmm, I guess thats why we write things down.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his August 2006 financial results for Bingo Card Creator, his first month of detailed public accounting. Despite expecting a small profit, he discovers a $1.90 loss after writing everything down, though cash flow remains positive due to timing differences in expense recognition. He breaks down sales ($333.54 net from 14 sales), expenses ($335.44 including advertising, hosting, and one-time costs), and detailed advertising and website traffic statistics. The post demonstrates the granular economics of a one-person micro-ISV in its earliest days.
quotes.key_insight: Radical transparency about the economics of a micro-business reveals that even near-breakeven months contain valuable lessons about channel optimization, conversion tracking, and the importance of actually writing down your numbers.

quotes.quote: The secret: continuous improvement of ad texts and landing pages, plus a recent ruthless culling of every keyword which wasn't performing well enough to justify keeping.

quotes.quote: It makes me probably $50 a week, which is not a bad return for writing a few extra pages about elementary school bingo variants.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that micro-ISV websites with only a handful of pages are leaving money on the table by not creating content that targets long-tail search queries. He demonstrates with his own Bingo Card Creator site that even 200 words about a niche use case like 'icebreaker bingo' can generate consistent weekly revenue through organic search traffic. He explains that content serves three purposes: capturing long-tail searches, ranking for keywords you haven't anticipated, and creating linkable resources that generate natural backlinks. He contrasts his organic search results favorably against his paid AdWords spending, showing organic traffic converting better at lower cost.
quotes.key_insight: Creating useful content for your target audience is the cheapest and most sustainable customer acquisition channel because it captures the long tail of search queries that paid advertising and minimal websites can never reach.

quotes.quote: The rest were, in general, a veritable deluge of once-in-a-lifetime queries… the kind that natural English snaps up like hotcakes and all the SEO in the world won't get you.

quotes.quote: A customer convinced me to throw another one on the barbie, as it were.
quotes.summary: McKenzie lists the feature roadmap for version 1.04 of Bingo Card Creator, prompted by customer feedback. The update includes visual improvements, font choices, word wrap options, page positioning, and calling card printing. He teases readers with a puzzle about development time, noting one feature took 4 hours while another took just 5 minutes. The post illustrates the unpredictable nature of software development effort.
quotes.key_insight: The actual development cost of features is wildly unpredictable and often inversely correlated with how complex they appear to users.

quotes.quote: One of the features on this list took 4 solid hours to implement. Another took 5 minutes. Any guesses which was which?

quotes.quote: Sounds like a complete waste of time, right? Yeah, probably… unless you process 20 million messages a month.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares an amusing example of a Japanese web service that converts text messages into schoolgirl leetspeak, charging about 5 cents per message via phone billing. Despite the service being trivially simple to build—essentially a short Perl script and a translation table—it processes 20 million messages monthly. The company also charges to decrypt the gibberish back into readable Japanese, effectively monetizing both directions of the same problem.
quotes.key_insight: Enormous businesses can be built from trivially simple software when you solve a genuine friction point at scale with frictionless payments.

quotes.quote: For a service which is, approximately, a 15 line Perl script and a 100 line Japanese-to-gibberish table of rules.

quotes.quote: She asked me whether I would have the Mac version done soon and I told her "Well, I certainly hope so". And then she went ahead and bought it, sight unseen.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes porting Bingo Card Creator to Mac OS X, motivated by a dedicated customer who bought the software before the Mac version existed. Rather than learning the Mac toolchain himself, he outsourced the native wrapper creation on RentACoder for $25. The Java codebase required zero lines of code changes for the port. He notes that Mac users represent about 2% of his site traffic but feels committed to the platform given he already has a paying customer. The post also briefly covers progress on version 1.04 with new features and word lists.
quotes.key_insight: Even tiny markets are worth serving when the marginal cost of entry is near zero, and a single passionate customer can justify an entire platform port.

quotes.quote: I tried to refund her money because I can't take money for a product which doesn't officially exist yet but she would have none of it.

quotes.quote: This is the screenshot which directly sells my program more than any other.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shows before-and-after screenshots of his Bingo Card Creator software after purchasing stock icons for $29.95. He demonstrates how the visual upgrade from version 1.03 to 1.04 makes the interface look more colorful and professional. He emphasizes that this particular screenshot is his most important sales asset, placed above the fold on his main page. He also notes a side benefit: changing the card preview to white backgrounds better reflects printed output.
quotes.key_insight: Small, inexpensive visual improvements like stock icons can disproportionately improve how professional and substantial your software appears to potential customers.

quotes.quote: Apologizing literally works magic on people, particularly sincere apologizing (not 'I'm sorry you're too stupid to use our software but thats the way it is').
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that apologizing to customers is one of the most powerful and cost-free tools in customer service. He builds on advice from TryBeta.com about taking blame for all mistakes, expanding it into a broader philosophy: sincere apologies combined with immediate action plans create lasting positive impressions. He advocates minimizing ego involvement, offering refunds proactively (which few customers actually take), and using small scripted apologies to smooth over minor friction. He draws on his experience at both his software business and his day job, plus observations about Japanese customer service culture.
quotes.key_insight: A sincere apology costs nothing but creates lasting goodwill, and proactively offering to make things right builds more loyalty than never making mistakes would.

quotes.quote: Very few people will actually take you up on that offer, but they will remember you making it for the duration they do business with you.

quotes.quote: Its a little thing, but excellence is getting the little things right.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that apologizing to customers is one of the most powerful and cost-free tools in customer service. He builds on advice from TryBeta.com about taking blame for all mistakes, expanding it into a broader philosophy: sincere apologies combined with immediate action plans create lasting positive impressions. He advocates minimizing ego involvement, offering refunds proactively (which few customers actually take), and using small scripted apologies to smooth over minor friction. He draws on his experience at both his software business and his day job, plus observations about Japanese customer service culture.
quotes.key_insight: A sincere apology costs nothing but creates lasting goodwill, and proactively offering to make things right builds more loyalty than never making mistakes would.

quotes.quote: Long time readers of this blog will probably remember me describing MSN as a very cheap date in terms of SEO. Well, perhaps I shouldn't have been so dismissive: she's now my #3 source of hits and my #1 source of sales for last week.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports on unexpected SEO success with MSN search, which has become his top sales source despite initially dismissing it. He notes a sudden spike in Google organic traffic suggesting a PageRank improvement, though conversion from Google searches is poor at 10% due to mismatched search intent. He also addresses users searching for registration keys, turning a potential piracy signal into a customer service opportunity.
quotes.key_insight: A search engine dismissed as unimportant can become your best sales channel when its audience better matches your customer profile, while high-traffic sources with mismatched intent convert poorly.

quotes.quote: My question is what you'd have to be smoking to want to.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that micro-ISV developers should aggressively outsource tasks that aren't core to their business. He presents four heuristics for deciding what to outsource: when customers can't tell the difference, when the cost is trivially low, when outsourced quality exceeds what you could produce, and when the effective hourly rate of doing it yourself is insultingly low. He illustrates with examples from his own Bingo Card Creator business, including paying $5/month for payment notification handling and $29.95 for professional stock icons. He describes a 'cookie jar' technique where you physically pay yourself per hour to internalize the opportunity cost of DIY work on low-value tasks.
quotes.key_insight: Solo software developers systematically undervalue their own time and should outsource any task where the cost is lower than the opportunity cost of doing it themselves.

quotes.quote: I am a weak person and was tempted to ask Sally to be a bit less enthusiastic after the first couple of emails.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes how he resisted the urge to brush off an enthusiastic but demanding customer ('Sally') who sent daily emails with feature requests and questions about Bingo Card Creator. Instead, he followed his own advice about customer service, implemented her feature requests (which took only 4 hours), and sent her a preview of the improved software. Sally turned out to work for a state government agency that oversees hundreds of institutions, and she began evangelizing the product across her entire professional network. The post argues that treating every customer interaction as an opportunity rather than an annoyance can yield outsized returns.
quotes.key_insight: The customer who seems like the biggest nuisance may turn out to be your most valuable evangelist — never dismiss an enthusiastic user out of laziness.

quotes.quote: I have a $23.93 incentive to sell one marginal copy of my software. Any given affiliate has, say, a $5 incentive. $5 doesn't motivate someone to put any investment or thought into actually getting my software in front of people who need it.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes being approached by Bits du Jour, a daily software deal site, to feature Bingo Card Creator during their back-to-school week at a 40% discount. He explains why he generally avoids affiliate programs — the small per-sale incentive doesn't motivate affiliates to do quality marketing — but was persuaded by their low-friction pitch and trust-based approach. He sets modest expectations of roughly 2 sales given his niche product, but agrees to the experiment since it costs him almost nothing. He commits to sharing the results publicly.
quotes.key_insight: Affiliate programs are generally a bad deal for niche software because the small per-sale commission doesn't motivate quality promotion, but low-effort experiments with bounded downside are always worth trying.

quotes.quote: I'd much rather spend the affiliate's commission on AdWords, where I have total control of the experience from beginning to end and only have to deal with myself.

quotes.quote: Programmers are very good at producing software for their own needs… and a lot less good at producing software for other's needs.
quotes.summary: McKenzie debunks myths from both the open source and micro-ISV communities about their supposed incompatibility. He argues that open source and small commercial software vendors coexist naturally because they serve fundamentally different needs and audiences. Most OSS is built by corporate-funded developers for programmer needs, while uISVs serve non-technical niches that OSS ignores. The real competitive moat for uISVs isn't code complexity but marketing, documentation, and user-facing work that OSS developers avoid. He uses his own Bingo Card Creator as a case study, showing how its open source competitor languishes despite being technically viable, because nobody is motivated to do the unglamorous work of reaching actual users.
quotes.key_insight: Open source and commercial software don't compete because OSS developers lack the motivation to do the unglamorous marketing, documentation, and user-support work that makes niche software actually reach the people who need it.

quotes.quote: Most successful uISVs have long since learned the lesson that writing the program is about 10% of the battle: you've then got to get it in front of prospective users, write documentation, help them with their problems, market market and market some more.

quotes.quote: After losing $10,000 of hardware I personally would have, hmm, tried to isolate the cause of the problem before frying additional machines but, hey, thats why I'm an engineer and not management.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recounts returning to his office to find his development machine destroyed by a power surge, the third computer lost in a week to the same cause. Despite losing $10,000 in hardware, management kept plugging machines into the faulty outlet without investigating. McKenzie's suggestion to check the electrical outlet with a voltmeter confirmed dangerous power spikes. The incident left his entire team idled, giving him unexpected free time to blog.
quotes.key_insight: When repeated failures share the same symptoms, the engineering instinct to diagnose root cause before continuing operations is worth more than the management instinct to just keep going.

quotes.quote: The reason we have no computers plugged in on this table is that they are all yakiniku.

McKenzie's suggestion to check the electrical outlet with a voltmeter confirmed dangerous power spikes. The incident left his entire team idled, giving him unexpected free time to blog.
quotes.key_insight: When repeated failures share the same symptoms, the engineering instinct to diagnose root cause before continuing operations is worth more than the management instinct to just keep going.

quotes.quote: Hey, boss, I took an electrical engineering course in college. Maybe the socket here is getting power spikes.

quotes.quote: I really love that feeling: 'Hey Tim, Suzie from Ohio just bought us lunch!'
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie returns from a five-day trip to Kyoto with his brother, noting the costs were offset by automated sales of his Bingo Card Creator software. He reflects on handling customer emails while traveling, reports August sales of $300, and enthusiastically recommends Robosoft's software submission service as a worthwhile $99 investment that paid for itself within a week through increased downloads and SEO benefits.
quotes.key_insight: A software product that generates sales while you sleep transforms travel from a pure expense into a partially self-funding experience, reinforcing the appeal of the microISV lifestyle.

quotes.quote: $99 might sound expensive but it paid for itself in about a week and thats not even counting the increase in organic search rankings that about 100 links will get me.

quotes.quote: If you intend to make money by selling software, you need to limit your trial version. The only question is how.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues forcefully that software developers who want to make money must limit their trial versions, rejecting the popular notion that donation-based models work. He cites Colin Messitt's controlled experiment showing limited versions outsold unlimited ones five to one, and Movable Type's average donation of 38 cents as evidence. He then explains his own approach with Bingo Card Creator, where he allows enough functionality to demonstrate value but not enough to complete the actual task (teaching a class). The post concludes that the right limitation strategy depends on deep knowledge of your target market and how they use your software.
quotes.key_insight: Successful software trial design requires limiting the product just enough that users can verify its value but cannot complete their actual task without paying — and choosing the right limitation requires deep knowledge of how customers use the product.

quotes.quote: The limited version outsold the unlimited one. Five to one. Colin calculates that the experiment cost him $17,000 in sales versus having 100% of the installations be limited.

quotes.quote: Literally every single time I have checked my email the last couple of days its been You've Got Money.
quotes.summary: McKenzie briefly announces he'll be away from blogging for about a week due to his brother visiting Japan and an upcoming business trip for his day job to a remote location. Before leaving, he notes with excitement that his software business has been performing exceptionally well, with sales notifications arriving every time he checks email. He promises to explain what's been driving the unexpected success when he returns.
quotes.key_insight: Even a simple side business can surprise its creator with unexpectedly strong sales momentum once the right pieces fall into place.

quotes.quote: When I've got some time after I get back I'll explain what has been going mostly right and what has been outperforming all of my expectations.

quotes.quote: This whole "get a sale every day" thing is starting to get a little overwhelming.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a day-in-the-life vignette from his dual existence as a Japanese salaryman and indie software entrepreneur. At his day job, he spent an hour helping a Japanese student with a school project about foreign countries, fielding questions about American schools. Meanwhile, his side business Bingo Card Creator continued generating daily sales, customer thank-you notes, and support requests without his active involvement. He marvels at reaching consistent daily sales and realizes the revenue is becoming significant enough to require professional tax and legal advice.
quotes.key_insight: The moment a side project starts generating consistent daily revenue is the inflection point where a hobby becomes a real business requiring professional infrastructure.

quotes.quote: Your heart surgeon doesn't tell you he's billing you $50,000 for three hours' labor to implant 32 cents worth of plastic and a $5 chip made in Taiwan.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues that software developers systematically underprice their products due to psychological biases rather than market reality. He contends that customers don't know or care how long software took to build — they only care whether it solves their problem. Using the analogy of a heart surgeon who charges $50,000 for implanting $5 worth of parts, he demonstrates that value-based pricing is the norm in professional services. He explains that software's near-zero marginal cost means developers can pick any point on the demand curve, and that the demand curve is external to development effort. The core message is that developers compare their shipped product to their original vision and feel it falls short, leading them to underprice — a phenomenon he calls 'coder's remorse.'
quotes.key_insight: Software pricing should be based on the value customers receive, not on development cost or time, and most developers underprice because they compare their product to an imagined ideal rather than to the actual market.

quotes.quote: Everything that happens inside of the computer is black magic to them and you are the shaman they rely on for magical amulets to keep the evil spirits at bay.

quotes.quote: My first two weeks in business I saw one sale. My next two weeks I saw 2 sales. These past two weeks, lets see, 6 sales? I'm going to shoot waaaaaaay past my personal goal for August, which was originally 4 sales.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a mid-month statistics update for his Bingo Card Creator software business, now six weeks old. Sales have tripled each two-week period, from 1 to 2 to 6, putting him well ahead of his monthly goal of 4 sales. His expenses run about $110/month across hosting, advertising on Google and Yahoo, and e-junkie, requiring roughly 4.5 sales to break even. He projects consistent profitability of around $1,500/year with minimal ongoing effort, and breaks down his sales sources as roughly half from Google ads, a quarter from download sites, and a quarter from a single teacher message board.
quotes.key_insight: A micro-ISV can reach sustainable profitability remarkably quickly with low fixed costs, disciplined ad spend, and organic word-of-mouth from a niche community.

quotes.quote: If you write for people with the attention span of an ADHD flea who is hopped up on methamphetamines, then this is who you'll end up with for readers.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues against the prevailing trend of writing list-style blog posts optimized for social media sites like Digg, contending that such content attracts fleeting attention from readers who won't remember your name. He makes the case that investing in quality, in-depth writing builds lasting value through blogroll links (which provide permanent SEO benefit unlike ephemeral social media links), loyal RSS subscribers, and evergreen content that continues attracting readers long after publication. The post reflects his early thinking on content marketing strategy, arguing that building trust through sustained quality writing is the path to monetizing a blog audience. He acknowledges list posts are easier to produce but urges writers to develop genuine craft instead.
quotes.key_insight: Investing in quality writing over clickbait list posts builds compounding returns through permanent blogroll links, loyal RSS subscribers, and evergreen content that continues working for you long after publication.

quotes.quote: You don't get blogrolled after the first visit any more than you get to meet the parents after the first date.

quotes.quote: Write in a way that shows off your own voice, personality, and ideas, the better to stand out against a sea of mediocrity.
quotes.summary: McKenzie argues against the prevailing trend of writing list-style blog posts optimized for social media sites like Digg, contending that such content attracts fleeting attention from readers who won't remember your name. He makes the case that investing in quality, in-depth writing builds lasting value through blogroll links (which provide permanent SEO benefit unlike ephemeral social media links), loyal RSS subscribers, and evergreen content that continues attracting readers long after publication. The post reflects his early thinking on content marketing strategy, arguing that building trust through sustained quality writing is the path to monetizing a blog audience. He acknowledges list posts are easier to produce but urges writers to develop genuine craft instead.
quotes.key_insight: Investing in quality writing over clickbait list posts builds compounding returns through permanent blogroll links, loyal RSS subscribers, and evergreen content that continues working for you long after publication.

quotes.quote: Business is business, but a little bit of humanity goes a long way. However, investments, on the other hand, have neither feelings nor moral standing.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovered that a single keyword he added to his AdWords campaign on a whim was responsible for a 50% increase in his advertising costs over two weeks. The keyword had a low 9% conversion rate despite generating many clicks, dragging down his overall cost per action from $0.40 to $0.60+. Rather than building a landing page for traffic that likely wanted a different product (printable numeric bingo cards for gambling), he killed the ads entirely. He also notes uncertainty about whether AdWords is actually generating completed sales, suspecting a tracking misconfiguration with cross-session cookie attribution.
quotes.key_insight: Ruthless optimization of ad spend means quickly killing campaigns that attract the wrong audience, no matter how effective they are at generating clicks.

quotes.quote: The culprit was one single keyword which I had added two weeks ago on a lark and forgotten about.

quotes.quote: Marketing should be more fun than that.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports results from two experimental AdWords ads for his Bingo Card Creator software. A 'poetry' ad with campy rhyming copy doubled click-through rates versus his optimized control while maintaining similar cost-per-acquisition. A 'white space' ad offering free Dolch sight word lists achieved a remarkable 5.8% CTR by leading with a free resource rather than directly selling software. Both experiments cost under $5 total while generating nearly $48 in revenue, demonstrating that creative ad copy and indirect conversion funnels can outperform conventional approaches.
quotes.key_insight: Creative differentiation in ad copy and offering free value upfront can dramatically outperform conventional optimized ads, and testing these hypotheses costs almost nothing at small scale.

quotes.quote: And, hey, if it didn't work I can always kill it in a week and not lose more than a cup of cocoa.

quotes.quote: It also literally doubled the percentage of my downloads which I can verify were executed.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares two simple installer tweaks that significantly improved his software's activation rate. The first is adding a desktop shortcut in addition to a start menu link, which he implemented in 45 seconds. The second is offering to run the program immediately after installation completes. He argues that non-technical users will get distracted or lost if forced to find the program themselves, and that these micro-optimizations doubled the percentage of downloads that resulted in verified program executions.
quotes.key_insight: Tiny reductions in friction between downloading software and actually running it — like auto-launching after install — can dramatically increase activation and sales.

quotes.quote: I try not to listen to it when actually coding because the tears of laughter make it hard to see semicolons.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares Jonathan Coulton's 'Code Monkey' song as the unofficial anthem of his software business, finding humor and inspiration in its depiction of programmer life. The post is a lighthearted interlude in his business blog, but he slips in a sharp observation about pricing psychology: despite loving the song, he never paid for it because it was free, illustrating how free pricing signals low value and discourages voluntary payment. He notes this lesson is relevant to the micro-ISV community debating pricing strategies.
quotes.key_insight: When you price something at free, even people who genuinely love it and would have paid will default to paying nothing — price is a signal, not just a number.

quotes.quote: After one listen to it I would have gladly paid money for it. However, since its free, I haven't taken time to send the guy so much as a $1 Paypal thanking him for his time.

quotes.quote: This is obviously the worst sort of failure — its a failure I'll never know about and can't recover from.
quotes.summary: McKenzie analyzes the gap between his 1,000+ trial downloads of Bingo Card Creator and the mere 18 confirmed active users detected through update checking. He systematically diagnoses why downloads don't convert to installations — IE security blocking auto-downloads, users failing to install, Java runtime requirements creating friction, and users opting out of update checks. He proposes a 'Bonus Pack' of extra word lists as an honest incentive for users to phone home, giving him better installation metrics while providing genuine value.
quotes.key_insight: The most dangerous conversion failures are the invisible ones — when users silently bounce before ever experiencing your product, you lose both the customer and the data to diagnose the problem.

quotes.quote: Say what you will about Chinese hackers, at the very least they have more amusing pseudonyms than our home-grown miscreants.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares an amusing anecdote about discovering his software (Bingo Card Creator) being reviewed on a Chinese warez/cracking site. The review gives his product 3 out of 5 stars, noting it's light on features but good for its niche of English learners. He finds humor in the Chinese hacker's colorful pseudonym and admits he can only roughly translate the Chinese review by recognizing shared characters with Japanese.
quotes.key_insight: When your software gets pirated and reviewed by international crackers, it's a backhanded sign that your product has enough reach to attract attention beyond your intended market.

quotes.quote: I can just sort of get the gist of it by picking out words in isolation which happen to be the same in Japanese, in much the same manner that you can sort-of understand Portuguese by way of Spanish.

quotes.quote: I had my highest traffic ever today, surpassing even the spike from the pirate kings.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports on the early results of using RoboSoft's auto-submission service to distribute his Bingo Card Creator software to download sites. Traffic hit an all-time high, roughly 25% above his previous peak and double his traffic from a week prior. The increase came not from paid ads or organic search but from direct hits (trial installs phoning home) and referrals from download sites. He estimates more trial installs in 24 hours than in the previous two weeks, validating the auto-submission experiment.
quotes.key_insight: Automated submission to software download sites can produce a dramatic, immediate spike in trial installs that dwarfs both paid advertising and organic search traffic.

quotes.quote: This would have been a valid consideration… in 1998.
quotes.summary: McKenzie responds to a suggestion that he should avoid Java/.NET dependencies by programming in C++ instead. He argues with data from his own analytics that the concern about runtime deployment is outdated: only 10% of his visitors use dialup, 97% already have Java installed, and his target market of educators already uses Java-dependent software. He concludes that the development time savings from using Java far outweigh the tiny number of potential customers who might be lost.
quotes.key_insight: Technology adoption decisions should be based on your actual user data and development velocity, not on outdated conventional wisdom about what customers can or cannot run.

quotes.quote: Don't believe the statistics from 2000 that you read on the Internet about 50% deployment rates — Java, like Flash or .NET or anything, is a very viral bit of software.

quotes.quote: From your perspective thats the most important mouse click in your program there is.
quotes.summary: McKenzie advocates tracking which paths within trial software lead users to click the Buy Now button, arguing this data is essential for optimizing conversions. He presents a simple technique: append query parameters to the purchase page URL from different locations in the app, then use web analytics to see which paths drive the most purchase intent. The method requires no server-side changes since web servers log and ignore parameters on static HTML pages. He notes this takes about 10 seconds to implement and provides actionable data for A/B testing nag screens, exit screens, and UI highlighting.
quotes.key_insight: You can track which paths in your trial software lead to purchases with zero infrastructure by appending query parameters to your static purchase page URL and reading the results in Google Analytics.

quotes.quote: Does a nag screen produce appreciable results? How about an exit screen? Do people click the button more when the menu item is highlighted in blue (*cough* yes *cough*)?
quotes.summary: McKenzie advocates tracking which paths within trial software lead users to click the Buy Now button, arguing this data is essential for optimizing conversions. He presents a simple technique: append query parameters to the purchase page URL from different locations in the app, then use web analytics to see which paths drive the most purchase intent. The method requires no server-side changes since web servers log and ignore parameters on static HTML pages. He notes this takes about 10 seconds to implement and provides actionable data for A/B testing nag screens, exit screens, and UI highlighting.
quotes.key_insight: You can track which paths in your trial software lead to purchases with zero infrastructure by appending query parameters to your static purchase page URL and reading the results in Google Analytics.

quotes.quote: Since GoogleBot isn't spidering your application, you can go hog wild with referrals from there and it will never be the wiser.

quotes.quote: What I bring to the table: I give you software for free, and this lets you attract visitors to your pages, not necessarily in the search for my software but in the search for an aggregator of lots of software.
quotes.summary: McKenzie evaluates the economics of submitting shareware to download sites versus paid advertising channels like Google AdWords. He argues that most download sites offer a poor value proposition by charging listing fees and commissions while providing unverifiable traffic quality. He makes an exception for high-traffic sites like Download.com, which generated significant downloads, and Tucows, despite its slow processing. He ultimately submits to 89 free sites via RoboSoft as a low-risk experiment while expressing clear preference for Google ads where ROI is measurable.
quotes.key_insight: When you can't measure the quality of a marketing channel but can measure an alternative, spend your money where you have data and use the unmeasurable channels only when they're free.

quotes.quote: I would much rather spend the $20~$100 you guys want on Google ads, where I have 100% control of my advertising and can guarantee that I'll get X number of downloads for my dollar.

quotes.quote: I hadn't anticipated anybody would try to do this and so didn't test it… doh?
quotes.summary: McKenzie announces the release of Bingo Card Creator 1.03, which fixes memory issues when printing large batches, adds threaded printing to prevent UI lockups, and eliminates visual flickering. The improvements were directly motivated by his first product return, where a customer cited these issues. He notes the value of customer feedback even when it comes in the form of a refund request, and mentions that PayPal refunds don't cost processing fees.
quotes.key_insight: Customer complaints and returns are among the most actionable forms of product feedback, turning known technical debt into urgent priorities.

quotes.quote: My productivity has not increased so much since I learned to Alt-Tab.
quotes.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically reviews Direct Access, a keyboard shortcut launcher from Nagarasoft that lets users type keywords followed by a hotkey to launch applications, open folders, or insert text snippets. He describes how he configured it to replace tedious navigation through cluttered Windows screens, bookmark lookups, and even IDE code snippets. The post paints a vivid picture of a developer's daily friction—deep folder paths, modal dialogs blocking the desktop, and underused IDE features—and how a simple launcher eliminates it. He concludes that the $40 price, which initially seemed steep, would be cheap at twice the cost given the productivity gains.
quotes.key_insight: The biggest productivity gains often come not from better algorithms or frameworks but from eliminating the small, repeated friction of navigating your own tools.

quotes.quote: I was pleasantly suprised to see a weekly conversion rate in the 60% region, spiking to 80% on many days…
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers suspiciously high conversion rates in his Yahoo Search Marketing dashboard, with some long-tail keywords showing impossible 300% conversion rates. He traces the issue to a bug in Yahoo's conversion tracking that counts multiple hits when users visit the download page more than once, likely due to IE users double-clicking links. This leaves him unable to determine whether YSM is actually performing well or if the data is entirely unreliable.
quotes.key_insight: When ad platform analytics show impossibly good numbers, the most likely explanation is a tracking bug, not actual performance — and unreliable data is worse than no data because it prevents sound spending decisions.

quotes.quote: That is the beautiful thing about systems: systems scale.
quotes.summary: McKenzie draws a parallel between Toyota's Single Minute Exchange of Dies (SMED) manufacturing philosophy and micro-ISV software development. The core argument is that investing in systems — automated order processing, marketing infrastructure, and build pipelines — pays compounding dividends by eliminating repetitive manual work. He illustrates this with his own painful 50-click, 30-minute build process that causes him to batch changes and delay bug fixes. The post advocates for treating your development workflow like Toyota treats its assembly line: optimize the changeover process so you can deploy frequently and fix defects immediately rather than letting them accumulate.
quotes.key_insight: The highest-leverage work a solo developer can do is building systems that eliminate repetitive manual steps, because a painful process doesn't just waste time — it actively prevents you from shipping fixes and improvements.

quotes.quote: Work on your system once and you keep getting value out of it for an extended period of time, without inputting more work.

quotes.quote: Given that almost everybody is using the full character allotment in every line, the white space immediately catches the eye.
quotes.summary: McKenzie documents how the back-to-school season dramatically shifted search behavior for his educational software product, causing his ad conversion rates to plummet. He experiments with two creative approaches to Google AdWords copy: a minimalist white-space ad that triples his click-through rate, and a playful rhyming ad inspired by Japanese poetry. The white-space approach of giving searchers exactly what they want (free DSW lists) while upselling proves surprisingly effective. He observes that most AdWords advertisers write identical, uncreative copy and sees an opportunity in standing out.
quotes.key_insight: Standing out in a sea of identical ads — through unconventional formatting, precise intent matching, and creative copy — can dramatically improve both click-through and conversion rates.

quotes.quote: Thats the difference between support and customer service. As a support incident, that call was a waste of time/money. As an opportunity for demonstrating you've got an unparalleled dedication to customer service, that call is as good an opportunity as every other customer contact you make.
quotes.summary: McKenzie distinguishes between 'support' (a cost center to be minimized) and 'customer service' (a scalable investment in reputation). He argues that every customer interaction is an opportunity to demonstrate excellence, not a drain on resources. The post uses a concrete example where brushing off a low-profit customer gracefully led to hundreds of thousands of dollars in future business. He advocates for making products intuitive to reduce support volume, while treating every remaining interaction as a chance to build loyalty. The core thesis is that promising excellent customer service scales the same way guarantees do — most people won't need it, but everyone is reassured by its existence.
quotes.key_insight: Every customer interaction — even unprofitable ones — is an investment in reputation that compounds over time, making excellent customer service one of the most scalable assets a small software business can build.

quotes.summary: McKenzie distinguishes between 'support' (a cost center to be minimized) and 'customer service' (a scalable investment in reputation). He argues that every customer interaction is an opportunity to demonstrate excellence, not a drain on resources. The post uses a concrete example where brushing off a low-profit customer gracefully led to hundreds of thousands of dollars in future business. He advocates for making products intuitive to reduce support volume, while treating every remaining interaction as a chance to build loyalty. The core thesis is that promising excellent customer service scales the same way guarantees do — most people won't need it, but everyone is reassured by its existence.
quotes.key_insight: Every customer interaction — even unprofitable ones — is an investment in reputation that compounds over time, making excellent customer service one of the most scalable assets a small software business can build.

quotes.quote: Thats my personal metric for customer excellence: Nuns Praying For You (NPFY). Paypal, yeah, there's a NPFY-0 business if I ever saw one.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reminisces about working at Quill Corporation, an office supply company fanatically devoted to customer service. He describes their metrics wall, their policy of crediting individual employees mentioned in customer letters, and his favorite artifact: a prayer card from a convent of nuns who were so impressed by Quill's service they dedicated prayers to the staff. The nuns became so beloved that employees fought over who got to handle their account, and workers would bend rules to ensure the Sisters always received free cookies with their orders. The post illustrates how genuine customer obsession creates loyalty that transcends transactional relationships.
quotes.key_insight: Extraordinary customer service isn't about policies and metrics — it's about building a culture where employees genuinely care so much that customers respond with loyalty that borders on devotion.

quotes.quote: Keep in mind we were earning NPFY60+ for, well, prompt delivery of paper and pens.

quotes.quote: Any time your customer asks to invoke the guarantee, process the refund immediately, then write back to your customer.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares his first experience processing a refund and turns it into a masterclass on customer service for micro-ISVs. Drawing on his formative experience at Quill Corporation, he argues that refunds should be processed immediately and graciously, never contested. He explains the practical reasons (avoiding chargebacks and fees) and the strategic ones (word-of-mouth reputation in niche markets). He illustrates with a Quill anecdote where a small accommodation on a toner order led to a six-figure annual account. The post closes with practical advice on keeping sufficient funds available for refunds and a brief case for why money-back guarantees are worth offering.
quotes.key_insight: Generous, no-questions-asked refund policies are not a cost center but a powerful customer acquisition strategy, because the word-of-mouth from graceful exits far outweighs the price of processing returns.

quotes.quote: There is no question that your customer can get money from you: the question is whether they go through you or they chargeback.

quotes.quote: Turns out I had marked my conversion page as http::/foo instead of http://foo. Haha, regular expression FAILURE.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers a typo in his Google Analytics conversion tracking URL that caused lost data, and separately notes that submitting a PAD file to the Association of Shareware Professionals led to a customer acquisition via a high-PageRank shareware directory. The post captures two small but practical lessons from running a micro-ISV: attention to detail in analytics configuration matters, and low-effort distribution channels like PAD files can punch above their weight for discoverability.
quotes.key_insight: Low-effort distribution tactics like PAD file submissions can deliver real customers for bootstrapped software businesses, making them worth the trivial time investment.

quotes.quote: Nice to get away from marketing for a bit.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes a day of programming work on his Bingo Card Creator application, tackling minor but customer-impacting issues. He focuses on fixing a print-related UI flicker caused by JFrame focus stealing, optimizing memory usage during batch printing for customers with low-end hardware, and adding a custom application icon. These are small polish items that improve the overall user experience.
quotes.key_insight: The unglamorous work of fixing UI quirks and optimizing for low-end hardware is what separates a polished product from a developer's side project.

quotes.quote: I got some orders today and I'm now profitable and will continue to be for the forseeable future.
quotes.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces that Bingo Card Creator has become profitable, exactly one month after launching the business. He had been worried after increasing his advertising budget but received enough orders to cross into profitability. He thanks the Joel on Software community for advice and credits teachers as his core customers, closing with a plug for his icebreaker bingo product.
quotes.key_insight: A tiny software business can reach profitability in just one month with modest advertising spend and a clearly defined niche customer base.

quotes.quote: I also like that its one month to the day after opening my business.

quotes.quote: Profit: $3.77.  Take that, Bill Gates!
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares his first month of business statistics for Bingo Card Creator, reporting $74.85 in gross sales against $68.02 in expenses for a net profit of $3.77. He breaks down his costs across signup fees, website hosting, and advertising, while detailing impressive conversion rates from both paid and organic search. The post reveals the raw economics of a bootstrapped micro-ISV in its earliest days, with surprisingly strong marketing metrics despite minimal revenue. He sets modest goals of 10-20 sales per month for August while planning to expand the product's educational scope.
quotes.key_insight: Radical transparency about the unglamorous early economics of a bootstrapped software business — $3.77 in first-month profit — combined with strong conversion metrics that hint at the scalable foundation underneath.

quotes.quote: If your users and your desire to SEO every come into conflict, you have done something terribly wrong.
quotes.summary: McKenzie provides a practical guide to on-page SEO tactics for small businesses, drawn from his direct experience ranking Bingo Card Creator on Google, Yahoo, and MSN. He covers six core techniques: unique keyword-rich page titles, keyword-optimized URLs, attention to pluralization and search term variations, consistent site navigation templates, and avoiding duplicate content penalties. The post demonstrates each tactic with specific ranking results from his own site, showing how small changes like word order in titles or dashes versus underscores in URLs produce measurable ranking improvements. He explains Google's 'supplemental index' penalty for duplicate content and offers a clever workaround: rewriting pages to be semantically identical but lexically different, exploiting the gap between human comprehension and algorithmic text matching.
quotes.key_insight: Small, systematic on-page optimizations — unique titles, keyword-rich URLs, precise pluralization choices, and non-duplicate content — compound into significant organic search traffic for small businesses without requiring any ethically questionable tactics.

quotes.quote: Going supplemental means your page gets quarantined out of the main search index for Google, and is put in basically a low-quality jail.

quotes.quote: Today was the first day I got more organic hits than this, and they appear to be legitimate (i.e. not from pirates for a change).
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports that his organic search traffic has surpassed his paid search (PPC) traffic for the first time. He discovered that his blog ranked higher than his main site for key search terms, so he added internal links from blog posts to product pages. Within 48 hours of being re-crawled, his site jumped from page 3 to page 1 of search results. He sees significant upside in capturing even a fraction of the organic search volume.
quotes.key_insight: A blog's higher domain authority can be leveraged through strategic internal linking to rapidly boost a product site's organic search rankings, reducing dependence on paid traffic.

quotes.quote: Its not A/B testing but its, shall we say, empirically driven iterative improvements.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares five quick website tweaks that measurably improved his conversion rate for Bingo Card Creator, his software product for teachers. He advocates for empirically-driven iterative improvements over gut feelings, testing changes over multi-day periods. The tweaks range from making download links into clickable button images (100% boost) to ensuring screenshots appear above the fold (25% impact). He emphasizes that every page should have a conversion path, since a significant minority of visitors explore extensively before converting. The post closes with a pitch for analytics as the prerequisite for any data-driven optimization.
quotes.key_insight: Small, testable changes to download button design, link placement, and screenshot positioning can yield dramatic conversion improvements, but only if you have analytics in place to measure the results.

quotes.quote: Make your "download now" link an image. This delivered roughly a 100% boost in my conversion rate. Yeah, you read that right.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares five quick website tweaks that measurably improved his conversion rate for Bingo Card Creator, his software product for teachers. He advocates for empirically-driven iterative improvements over gut feelings, testing changes over multi-day periods. The tweaks range from making download links into clickable button images (100% boost) to ensuring screenshots appear above the fold (25% impact). He emphasizes that every page should have a conversion path, since a significant minority of visitors explore extensively before converting. The post closes with a pitch for analytics as the prerequisite for any data-driven optimization.
quotes.key_insight: Small, testable changes to download button design, link placement, and screenshot positioning can yield dramatic conversion improvements, but only if you have analytics in place to measure the results.

quotes.quote: I finished my workday, went out to bid fairwell to a friend leaving the country, and when I get back to my computer I'm on the front page of WordPress.
quotes.summary: McKenzie recounts an unexpected brush with internet fame after commenting on Matt Cutts' blog during a lunch break, only to find himself on the front page of WordPress by evening. He quickly pivots back to his regular business concerns, noting issues with his AdWords conversion tracking showing 0% and Google Analytics reporting zero traffic for three days, which he attributes to system glitches rather than actual problems.
quotes.key_insight: Unexpected internet attention is a distraction; the real work is getting back to building your business and monitoring your metrics.

quotes.quote: Unfortunately, this is largely a manual process and I missed the critical "copy the new installer from the build directory to the web directory before sync'ing the local copy of my website and the FTP server".
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers he failed to upload the correct installer for Bingo Card Creator after making major changes. The deployment process was manual, and he missed the step of copying the new build to the web directory before syncing to the FTP server. As a result, all users had been downloading the old version for days. He takes the mistake in stride rather than dwelling on it.
quotes.key_insight: Manual deployment processes without checklists or automation will inevitably lead to shipping the wrong build.

quotes.quote: Google algorithmically peaked at all the accounts on the list their agent had developed which they had access to, to see if the mail was marked spam or not.
quotes.summary: McKenzie analyzes a legal filing where Google's lawyers inadvertently revealed that Google checked Gmail users' inboxes to determine whether settlement notification emails had been caught by spam filters. In a class-action click fraud case, Google hired a firm to notify affected advertisers about a settlement, and when emails went to spam folders, Google accessed 75,000+ Gmail accounts to identify and re-send the notices. McKenzie argues this constitutes a privacy violation under Google's own privacy policy, which requires opt-in consent for sharing sensitive personal information. He dissects the privacy policy's exemptions and finds none that legitimately cover this action, particularly since Google's own lawyers admitted the inbox-checking exceeded what the court required.
quotes.key_insight: Google's own court filings reveal they accessed Gmail users' inboxes without consent to check spam filter status, and their privacy policy's exemptions don't cover voluntary actions their lawyers admitted exceeded legal requirements.

quotes.quote: Unhinged rantings of a conspiracy nut? Well, no. Google's lawyers bragged about this in a recent document they filed to the court regarding the settlement.

quotes.quote: Checking for updates is a wonderful thing to enable, both because it makes sure your customers have the best-and-brightest version of your product available, and because it lets you see on your web logs 'Ahah, that install actually succeeded'.
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes a simple technique for implementing update checking in desktop software using static HTML files on a website. Each version number gets its own HTML page, with only the latest version's page showing an 'up to date' message while all others prompt users to update. The approach requires no server-side programming, takes seconds per release to maintain, and doubles as analytics by letting you track successful installations via web logs. It's a pragmatic hack that trades sophistication for speed of implementation.
quotes.key_insight: The simplest possible implementation that ships today — even static HTML files on a web server — beats a sophisticated solution that never gets built.

quotes.quote: The latest one says 'Yep, your software is up to date', has the tracking code, and maybe gives a bit of advice or something (hey, why miss an opportunity to sell to people).

quotes.quote: If the prospect of this doesn't make you smile, you have neither soul nor taste in Disney movies.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares his delight at discovering that a fellow Joel on Software forum regular makes niche software for chimney sweeps. He finds the ultra-specific niche charming and references the Mary Poppins chimney sweep song to underscore the whimsy of the product.
quotes.key_insight: There is real business opportunity and delight in building software for the most unexpected, hyper-specific niches.

quotes.quote: Yes, their interface is terrible. Yes, their three day delay between me putting in a new ad and it showing up makes constant refinement impossible. Yes, their integration into Analytics sucks.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reconsiders his negative opinion of Yahoo Search Marketing (YSM/Overture) after seeing strong performance metrics. Despite the platform's terrible interface, 3-day ad approval delays, and broken analytics integration, his Yahoo ads achieved 29 cents CPA with 40% conversion rate — significantly outperforming his Google AdWords at 50 cents CPA and 20% conversion. He decides to continue spending on both platforms, allocating $30 to Yahoo and $90 to Google for the next month. The post also touches on the accounting complexity of when to recognize ad expenses.
quotes.key_insight: Let performance data override your qualitative judgments about platforms — a terrible user experience for the advertiser can still deliver superior results if that's where your customers are.

quotes.quote: It turns out that I was so successful at minimizing my CPC I am now able to pay for all the clicks my ads would generate in a day, with money left over.
quotes.summary: McKenzie discovers that his Google AdWords optimization has been so effective at minimizing cost-per-click that his daily budget can now cover all available clicks, leaving money unspent. He shares detailed metrics for two ad groups promoting Bingo Card Creator, with strong click-through and conversion rates. He shifts strategy from minimizing CPC to maximizing total conversions, and considers expanding to international English-speaking markets.
quotes.key_insight: Optimizing for the lowest possible cost-per-click eventually hits a ceiling where the real constraint becomes total conversion volume, requiring a strategic shift in ad spending approach.

quotes.quote: Time to see if I can't increase the CTR a bit without increasing my cost per conversion that much…

quotes.quote: Discounting the one-time startup costs (dang international fax that cost $17 or so) I'm currently profitable.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports that his software business (Bingo Card Creator) has become cash-flow positive within its first month of real operation. After discounting one-time startup costs, July sales have covered all monthly expenses including a failed eBay selling experiment. He analyzes his conversion rates, noting roughly 5% when excluding non-target traffic like pirates and blog readers. He outlines plans to shift advertising budget from Overture to Google and scale up spending, confident that four sales per month will cover increased costs.
quotes.key_insight: A one-person software business can reach profitability remarkably fast when overhead is kept minimal and conversion metrics are analyzed honestly by filtering out non-representative traffic.

quotes.quote: If you exclude all downloads from people referred by my weblog (JoSers who are not exactly in the market for elementary reading software) and by the pirates, my conversion rate is, and this is a back of the envelope calculation, about 5%.

quotes.quote: I don't know where my monthly sales are going to stabilize and don't want them stabilizing at a point where I routinely pay 12-15% of sales to somebody doing, well, essentially not a heck of a lot for me.
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains his decision to switch payment processing from Payloadz to e-junkie for his software business. The three driving factors are automatic post-purchase redirection for analytics and onboarding, simpler flat-rate pricing versus Payloadz's tiered model, and full control over customer-facing communications. He notes that neither processor's branding conveys the professional image he wants. He also calculates that building his own solution isn't worth the time given e-junkie's $5/month cost.
quotes.key_insight: When choosing payment infrastructure, optimize for customer experience control and predictable pricing over raw features, because your processor's limitations become your brand's limitations.

quotes.quote: Neither Payloadz nor e-junkie screams 'responsible businessman of the sort I am glad to pay to get educational materials from', which is sort of what I'm going for.

quotes.quote: I figure that will take me 2 hours, and $5 a month is not really worth 2 hours of my time…
quotes.summary: McKenzie explains his decision to switch payment processing from Payloadz to e-junkie for his software business. The three driving factors are automatic post-purchase redirection for analytics and onboarding, simpler flat-rate pricing versus Payloadz's tiered model, and full control over customer-facing communications. He notes that neither processor's branding conveys the professional image he wants. He also calculates that building his own solution isn't worth the time given e-junkie's $5/month cost.
quotes.key_insight: When choosing payment infrastructure, optimize for customer experience control and predictable pricing over raw features, because your processor's limitations become your brand's limitations.

quotes.quote: If you're like me, you can't afford at the moment to actually absorb a click from everyone who wants to click on your AdWords ad.
quotes.summary: McKenzie shares a practical AdWords optimization technique: using Google's Position Preferences feature to deliberately target lower ad positions instead of always competing for the top spot. By excluding top positions, advertisers can dramatically reduce their cost-per-click while stretching limited daily budgets to get more total clicks. He demonstrates with his own data that dropping from position 1.2 to position 2.8 cut his CPC by roughly 70% and reduced his cost per trial download from $0.50 to $0.30.
quotes.key_insight: Budget-constrained advertisers get better results by deliberately targeting lower ad positions, trading top ranking for dramatically more clicks per dollar spent.

quotes.quote: Go to your favorite ad group, check all the keywords, and click Edit Keyword Settings. Now turn Position Preferences on.

quotes.quote: I hereby pledge $20 to each of them 'in the indefinite future, when I have the money to spend', because I've gotten that much use and more out of them and I am feeling in a profoundly appreciative mood to people who save me time at the moment.
quotes.summary: McKenzie lists five free software programs he pledges to eventually donate $20 each to, after one of them saved him from IE-related HTML layout problems. The post is an early, lighthearted reflection on the value of free tools to a bootstrapping solo developer. He describes fixing cross-browser compatibility issues using an HTML cleaner and credits the broader ecosystem of free tools that made his project possible. The tone is self-deprecating and humorous, characteristic of his early blogging voice before he became known for business strategy writing.
quotes.key_insight: Even small free tools that save a solo developer a few hours of work deliver outsized value relative to their cost, though the economics of voluntarily paying for free software remain aspirational for cash-strapped bootstrappers.

quotes.quote: scrapper sites, MFA, and affiliate marketers on the other hand I have rather little sympathy for
quotes.summary: Google's AdWords Quality Score update has eliminated many of McKenzie's competitors by raising their minimum keyword bids dramatically. Sites engaging in ad arbitrage, scraper sites, and affiliate marketers were hit hardest, while his legitimate bingo card business benefited. His click-through rates jumped to 25% due to improved ad positioning, forcing him to actually lower his bids to stay within his $1/day budget. The net effect is lower cost per acquisition for his software.
quotes.key_insight: Platform quality enforcement that punishes low-value advertisers creates a windfall for legitimate small businesses competing in the same keyword space.

quotes.quote: I am really starting to hate web development.
quotes.summary: McKenzie notices his Bingo Card Creator website is broken in Internet Explorer, with the layout appearing left-aligned instead of centered. The post is a brief, frustrated plea for help debugging a cross-browser CSS issue. He notes Firefox renders correctly while IE does not, despite the layout working fine previously. He asks readers using IE to check the site and report back.
quotes.key_insight: Building a real product forces you to confront the unglamorous realities of cross-browser web development, even when your core product has nothing to do with web technology.

quotes.quote: I just noticed that my page looks totally borked in IE — instead of being nice and centered its clumped at the left hand portion of the screen.

quotes.quote: It seems that when I was a good web citizen and made all my pages nice and standards compliant via liberal use of DOCTYPE I apparently instructed IE to please ignore my CSS settings (come again?!)
quotes.summary: McKenzie describes a frustrating CSS rendering bug in Internet Explorer caused by using a standards-compliant DOCTYPE declaration. Despite following web standards best practices, IE ignored his CSS margin:auto centering. The fix requires changing the DOCTYPE to XHTML, which in turn demands updating all self-closing tags to pass validation. It's a brief vent about the cascading annoyances of cross-browser compatibility in the mid-2000s web.
quotes.key_insight: In the mid-2000s web, following standards compliance could paradoxically break your site in Internet Explorer, forcing developers into cascading workarounds.

quotes.quote: Last week I was paying roughly $1 per trial download. Now I'm paying $.50. I hope by next week to be paying about $.30.
quotes.summary: McKenzie reports halving his cost per trial download from $1 to $0.50 in one week through iterative optimization of his advertising and landing pages. He attributes the improvement to dropping non-performing ads, optimizing for conversion rate instead of click-through rate, and tuning landing pages. He shares a specific finding that underlining hyperlinks increased trial downloads by 10%, on top of a previous 100% increase from adding a download button. He projects further cost reduction to $0.30 per trial the following week.
quotes.key_insight: Systematic iteration on small, measurable changes to ads, landing pages, and UI elements can rapidly compound to dramatically reduce customer acquisition costs.
